stem: Which statement about the ACS NSQIP universal Surgical Risk Calculator (SRC) is correct?

options.A: It incorporates intraoperative findings, such as blood loss and operative time, to refine its risk estimate
options.B: It draws on ~20 preoperative patient-level variables plus the CPT code and discriminates 30-day mortality with a c-statistic >0.9
options.C: It replaces the need for institution- and surgeon-specific outcome data when counseling a patient
options.D: It was developed and validated only in pediatric populations and is not applicable to adults

exp: The universal NSQIP SRC uses ~20 preoperative patient-level characteristics plus procedure risk via CPT code, and discriminates 30-day mortality excellently (c-statistic >0.9). Crucially, it is designed for preoperative counseling/consent and does not incorporate intraoperative findings (A), nor can it replace institution- and surgeon-specific performance data (C). It has been adapted for — but was not limited to — pediatrics (D).
hy: NSQIP SRC: ~20 preop variables + CPT, mortality c-stat >0.9, no intraop data.

stem: In the NSQIP Surgical Risk Calculator, preoperative "systemic sepsis" is not a yes/no field but is graded into ordered categories. Which set correctly lists those categories?

options.A: Mild, moderate, severe
options.B: None, SIRS, sepsis, septic shock
options.C: Local, regional, disseminated
options.D: Class I through IV

exp: In the calculator, preoperative systemic sepsis is captured as an ordered category: none, SIRS, sepsis, or septic shock — reflecting escalating physiologic derangement. The other choices (mild/moderate/severe; local/regional/disseminated; classes I–IV) are not the NSQIP sepsis field. Most other NSQIP variables (e.g., ascites, ventilator dependence) are simple yes/no.
hy: NSQIP sepsis field = none / SIRS / sepsis / septic shock.

stem: The Revised Cardiac Risk Index assigns one point each to six predictors. Which of the following is one of the six RCRI criteria?

options.A: Preoperative serum creatinine >2.0 mg/dL
options.B: Age greater than 70 years
options.C: Active tobacco use
options.D: BMI greater than 35

exp: The six RCRI predictors, each worth one point, are: high-risk surgery, ischemic heart disease, congestive heart failure, cerebrovascular disease, insulin-treated diabetes, and preoperative creatinine >2.0 mg/dL. Age >70 (B), smoking (C), and BMI >35 (D) are not RCRI criteria (though they appear in other tools). Patients with 0/1/2/≥3 factors fall into classes I–IV.
hy: RCRI: high-risk surgery, IHD, CHF, CVD, insulin DM, Cr >2.0.

stem: Compared with the NSQIP risk calculator, what is the principal advantage the chapter attributes to the Revised Cardiac Risk Index?

options.A: It accounts for the specific CPT-coded procedure
options.B: Its ease of implementation and relatively objective criteria
options.C: It has the highest discrimination of any published cardiac index
options.D: It eliminates the need for any preoperative ECG

exp: The chapter credits the RCRI with only moderate discriminatory power but praises its ease of implementation and relatively objective criteria — which is why the 2014 ACC/AHA guideline still cites it (while endorsing the NSQIP calculator as an alternative). It does not include the specific CPT procedure (A — that is NSQIP), does not have the highest discrimination (C), and does not remove the need for an ECG (D).
hy: RCRI's edge is simplicity and objectivity, not best-in-class discrimination.

stem: A 78-year-old recalls 2 of 3 words and draws a normal clock during preoperative cognitive screening. What is his total Mini-Cog score and its interpretation?

options.A: 2 — suggests possible impairment
options.B: 4 — suggests no impairment
options.C: 3 — suggests possible impairment
options.D: 5 — suggests no impairment

exp: The Mini-Cog sums three-item recall (0–3, one point per recalled word) and the clock draw (0 or 2 points; a normal clock scores 2, any abnormal/refused clock scores 0). Recalling 2 of 3 words (2 points) plus a normal clock (2 points) totals 4. A total of 3, 4, or 5 suggests no impairment, while 0, 1, or 2 suggests possible impairment — so a 4 indicates no impairment. Option A (2) and option D (5) misstate the score, and option C pairs the score 3 with "possible impairment" when 3 actually falls in the no-impairment band.
hy: Mini-Cog: 0–2 = possible impairment, 3–5 = no impairment; normal clock = 2 points.

stem: Cognitive impairment strongly predicts postoperative delirium, which occurs in roughly half of geriatric patients. Which medication class is specifically called out as categorically best avoided because it independently increases delirium, falls, and cognitive impairment in older adults?

options.A: Beta blockers
options.B: Benzodiazepines
options.C: Proton-pump inhibitors
options.D: Statins

exp: Benzodiazepines are flagged in the AGS Beers Criteria as categorically contraindicated in older adults because they increase cognitive impairment, delirium, falls, and other adverse outcomes; postoperative delirium has ~50% incidence in geriatric patients. Beta blockers (A) and statins (D) are generally continued perioperatively, and PPIs (C) are not implicated as delirium drivers. Avoiding "second-hit" insults like high-risk medications is a core delirium-prevention principle.
hy: Benzodiazepines are categorically avoided in the elderly — major delirium/fall risk.

stem: During functional assessment, the ability to manage one's own medications and handle finances belongs to which category?

options.A: Basic activities of daily living (ADLs)
options.B: Instrumental activities of daily living (IADLs)
options.C: Frailty phenotype criteria
options.D: Components of the Mini-Cog

exp: Instrumental activities of daily living (IADLs) are higher-level functions: using the telephone, shopping, food preparation, housekeeping, laundry, transportation, medication management, and handling finances. Basic ADLs (A) are bathing, dressing, toileting, transferring, continence, and feeding. Frailty criteria (C) and the Mini-Cog (D) are separate constructs.
hy: Managing meds/finances = IADL; bathing/dressing/feeding = ADL.

stem: In the cited study of patients >70 undergoing elective cancer operations, a Timed Up and Go (TUG) time above which threshold was associated with a ~50% rate of major complications (vs ~14% below it)?

options.A: >10 seconds
options.B: >15 seconds
options.C: >20 seconds
options.D: >30 seconds

exp: In the cited multi-institutional study, a TUG time >20 seconds was associated with a ~50% risk of major complications in patients older than 70, versus ~14% for TUG ≤20 seconds. Gait speed (the dominant TUG component) outperformed more elaborate frailty scales for predicting cardiac-surgery mortality. The lower thresholds (10/15 s) and 30 s are not the cited cutoff.
hy: TUG >20 s flags ~50% major-complication risk in the elderly.

stem: In the large ACS NSQIP analysis cited, the preoperative frailty index was more strongly associated with postoperative cardiac arrest and death than which of the following?

options.A: ASA class or a history of myocardial infarction
options.B: Serum albumin
options.C: Operative blood loss
options.D: Surgeon case volume

exp: In the large NSQIP study, the preoperative frailty index was more strongly associated with postoperative cardiac arrest and death than ASA class or a history of myocardial infarction — underscoring that age and single comorbidities are weaker predictors than frailty itself. Albumin (B), blood loss (C), and surgeon volume (D) were not the comparison made.
hy: Frailty beats ASA class and prior MI for predicting cardiac arrest/death.

stem: A patient who can climb a flight of stairs, walk up a hill, or walk on level ground at 4 mph without cardiac symptoms has an estimated functional capacity of at least how many METs — the threshold at which the ACC/AHA algorithm allows proceeding to elective surgery without further cardiac testing?

options.A: 1 MET
options.B: 2 METs
options.C: 4 METs
options.D: 10 METs

exp: The ACC/AHA algorithm allows patients with ≥4 METs and no cardiac symptoms to proceed to elective or urgent surgery without further cardiac testing; 4 METs corresponds to climbing a flight of stairs, walking up a hill, or walking at 4 mph. One MET (A) is self-care/eating; 10+ METs (D) is strenuous sport — neither is the proceed-without-testing threshold.
hy: ≥4 METs (a flight of stairs) and asymptomatic → proceed, no further cardiac testing.

stem: Elective noncardiac surgery is being timed after coronary intervention. According to the ACC/AHA guidance, which minimum delay is correctly paired with its intervention?

options.A: Balloon angioplasty — 30 days
options.B: Bare-metal stent — 14 days
options.C: Drug-eluting stent — ideally 365 days
options.D: Drug-eluting stent — never less than 30 days under any circumstance

exp: Ideally elective surgery is delayed ~1 year (365 days) after a drug-eluting stent because of the dual-antiplatelet requirement (reducible to 180 days if delay risk exceeds stent-thrombosis risk). The correct delays are balloon angioplasty ≥14 days (not 30, A), bare-metal stent ≥30 days (not 14, B); and D is wrong because the DES interval can be shortened to ~6 months in select cases.
hy: Delay: balloon 14 d, BMS 30 d, DES ideally 365 d (≥180 d if needed).

stem: A patient on dual antiplatelet therapy after a drug-eluting stent needs an elective operation. Which statement reflects the chapter's guidance?

options.A: Elective surgery requiring DAPT interruption should be avoided within 12 months of a drug-eluting stent
options.B: Aspirin started fresh in the perioperative period reduces death and MI (POISE-2)
options.C: DAPT may always be stopped 7 days before any operation
options.D: Bare-metal stents require 12 months of uninterrupted DAPT

exp: Elective operations that would require stopping dual antiplatelet therapy should be avoided within 30 days of a bare-metal stent and within 12 months of a drug-eluting stent. The POISE-2 trial showed fresh perioperative aspirin does not reduce death/MI and increases major bleeding (B is the opposite). DAPT cannot simply be stopped 7 days before any case (C), and bare-metal stents need ~30 days, not 12 months (D).
hy: Avoid elective DAPT interruption: <30 d after BMS, <12 mo after DES.

stem: Which statement about perioperative beta blockers matches the ACC/AHA-based guidance in the chapter?

options.A: Beta blockers established before surgery should be continued; if newly initiated they should be started more than 7 days ahead, not acutely
options.B: Beta blockers should be started in the holding area for all intermediate-risk patients
options.C: Beta blockers should be stopped the morning of surgery in chronic users
options.D: They have been proven to reduce overall surgical mortality

exp: Beta blockers that are an established preoperative medication should be continued; if newly indicated they should be initiated >7 days (ideally weeks) before surgery and titrated to a heart rate of 60–80, because acute initiation raises stroke, hypotension, and death. They should not be started in the holding area for everyone (B) or stopped the morning of surgery in chronic users (C), and robust evidence of a surgical mortality benefit is lacking (D).
hy: Continue chronic beta blockers; never start them acutely before surgery.

stem: Which pair of hemodynamic thresholds defines two of the high-risk cardiac lesions the chapter says markedly raise perioperative death?

options.A: Pulmonary artery pressure >25 mm Hg and aortic valve area <1 cm²
options.B: Pulmonary artery pressure >15 mm Hg and aortic valve area <2 cm²
options.C: Ejection fraction <55% and mitral valve area <3 cm²
options.D: Central venous pressure >8 mm Hg and aortic valve area <1.5 cm²

exp: The chapter names significant pulmonary hypertension (pulmonary artery pressure >25 mm Hg) and severe aortic stenosis (aortic valve area <1 cm²), along with moderate-to-severe left/right heart failure, as lesions that markedly increase perioperative death and demand a multidisciplinary risk/benefit discussion. The other threshold sets (B, C, D) do not match the text.
hy: PAP >25 mm Hg and AVA <1 cm² mark high perioperative cardiac risk.

stem: Postoperative pulmonary complications occur in roughly 6% of major abdominal operations. Which statement about their predictors is correct?

options.A: There is a defined spirometric (FEV₁) threshold below which nonthoracic surgery is absolutely contraindicated
options.B: Congestive heart failure — especially with pulmonary hypertension — is a stronger predictor of pulmonary complications than severe COPD
options.C: Obesity by itself is the single strongest documented risk factor for postoperative pulmonary complications
options.D: Regional anesthesia carries a higher risk of clinically relevant pulmonary complications than general anesthesia

exp: Congestive heart failure — particularly when accompanied by pulmonary hypertension — is a considerably stronger predictor of postoperative pulmonary complications than even severe COPD. There is no spirometric threshold that absolutely contraindicates nonthoracic surgery (A); obesity per se has no clear independent association (C, though OSA/OHS do); and general anesthesia carries higher pulmonary risk than regional (D is reversed).
hy: CHF (with pulmonary HTN) outpredicts COPD for postoperative pulmonary complications.

stem: In the ARISCAT risk-score system, which two predictors carry the highest assigned point weight (24 points each)?

options.A: Age 51–80 and emergency procedure
options.B: Preoperative anemia and surgery >2 hours
options.C: Preoperative SpO₂ ≤90% and an intrathoracic incision
options.D: Upper-abdominal incision and respiratory infection in the last month

exp: In the ARISCAT score the heaviest-weighted predictors (24 points each) are a preoperative SpO₂ ≤90% and an intrathoracic incision. Age 51–80 (3) and emergency (8), anemia (11) and surgery >2 h (16), and upper-abdominal incision (15) all carry lower weights. The score's seven readily available predictors make it the simplest of the pulmonary indices.
hy: ARISCAT's top weights: SpO₂ ≤90% and intrathoracic surgery (24 pts each).

stem: Using ARISCAT, a total score of ≥45 points places a patient in which risk band, and what was the approximate observed pulmonary-complication rate for that band?

options.A: Low risk; ~0.7%
options.B: Intermediate risk; ~6%
options.C: High risk; ~44%
options.D: High risk; ~13%

exp: An ARISCAT total ≥45 points is the high-risk band, with an observed postoperative pulmonary complication rate of roughly 44% in the development sample. Low risk is <26 points (~0.7% PPC, option A), and intermediate is 26–44 points (~6%, option B); the ~13% figure (D) was the validation-sample intermediate-band rate, not the high band.
hy: ARISCAT ≥45 = high risk ≈ 44% pulmonary-complication rate.

stem: Among the ACP patient-related risk factors for postoperative pulmonary complications, which laboratory threshold is specifically cited?

options.A: Hypoalbuminemia <3.5 g/dL
options.B: Hemoglobin <8 g/dL
options.C: Creatinine >2.0 mg/dL
options.D: Bicarbonate <22 mEq/L

exp: The ACP patient-related risk factors include age >50, ASA class ≥2, functional dependence, COPD, heart failure, and hypoalbuminemia defined as albumin <3.5 g/dL. Hemoglobin <8 (B), creatinine >2.0 (C), and bicarbonate <22 (D) are not the cited pulmonary-risk thresholds. Albumin <3.5 also predicts general postoperative morbidity/mortality.
hy: Hypoalbuminemia <3.5 g/dL is an ACP pulmonary-risk factor.

stem: A patient screened with the STOP-BANG questionnaire is considered at high risk for moderate-to-severe obstructive sleep apnea once how many of the eight items are positive?

options.A: Two or more
options.B: Three or more
options.C: Five or more
options.D: All eight

exp: STOP-BANG poses eight yes/no items (snoring, tiredness, observed apnea, blood pressure, BMI >35, age >50, neck >40 cm, male sex); five or more positive responses mark high risk for moderate-to-severe OSA, prompting consideration of a sleep study and CPAP for elective cases. Two or three positive (A, B) is below the high-risk cutoff, and all eight (D) is not required.
hy: STOP-BANG ≥5 of 8 = high OSA risk.

stem: How is obesity hypoventilation syndrome defined in the chapter?

options.A: BMI >30 plus an awake PaCO₂ >45 mm Hg
options.B: BMI >35 plus daytime somnolence
options.C: BMI >40 plus a positive sleep study
options.D: BMI >50 plus nocturnal desaturation

exp: Obesity hypoventilation syndrome is defined as BMI >30 together with an awake PaCO₂ >45 mm Hg (chronic hypercapnia). The other BMI/criteria combinations (B, C, D) are not the chapter's definition, though the highest hypoventilation risk is in patients with BMI >50. General anesthesia and opioids can worsen the perioperative course.
hy: OHS = BMI >30 + awake PaCO₂ >45 mm Hg.

stem: A patient with end-stage renal disease on hemodialysis is scheduled for an elective operation. Which principle does the chapter emphasize?

options.A: Dialyze ideally the day before or day of surgery, targeting euvolemia and a normal potassium, because a normal K⁺ is a prerequisite for elective/urgent surgery
options.B: Dialyze immediately before incision to maximize fluid removal and start the case as close to dry weight as possible
options.C: A mildly elevated potassium is acceptable preoperatively because intraoperative medications readily correct hyperkalemia
options.D: Withhold dialysis for 72 hours before the operation to avoid the heparin exposure of the dialysis circuit

exp: Ideal preoperative dialysis is the day before or the day of surgery, targeting near-normal electrolytes and euvolemia (close to dry weight); a normal potassium is a prerequisite for elective/urgent surgery because intraoperative options to counteract hyperkalemia are limited and hyperkalemia can cause intraoperative death. Dialyzing immediately before incision (B) risks hemodynamic instability, a mildly high K⁺ is not acceptable (C), and withholding dialysis for 72 h (D) would worsen volume/electrolyte status.
hy: Dialyze day before/of surgery; a normal K⁺ is mandatory before elective/urgent operation.

stem: Which set of variables makes up the modified Child-Turcotte-Pugh score?

options.A: Bilirubin, creatinine, and INR only
options.B: Bilirubin, albumin, INR, ascites, and encephalopathy
options.C: Albumin, platelets, AST, and ascites
options.D: Creatinine, sodium, bilirubin, and INR

exp: The modified Child-Turcotte-Pugh score uses five variables: total bilirubin, serum albumin, INR (prothrombin time), ascites, and encephalopathy. Bilirubin/creatinine/INR alone (A) describe MELD; AST and platelets (C) and sodium (D) are not CTP components. Class A = 5–6, B = 7–9, C = 10–15 points.
hy: CTP = bilirubin + albumin + INR + ascites + encephalopathy.

stem: A resident contrasts the Child-Pugh and MELD scores. Which statement is correct?

options.A: MELD is composed only of objective measures — total bilirubin, creatinine, and INR — whereas CTP includes subjective items (ascites, encephalopathy)
options.B: CTP is composed only of objective laboratory values
options.C: MELD includes ascites grading
options.D: A MELD >20 is associated with roughly 6% 30-day mortality

exp: MELD is built only from objective measures (total bilirubin, creatinine, INR), whereas CTP incorporates subjective items (ascites grade, encephalopathy) — a key reason MELD has supplanted CTP for many risk-stratification uses. CTP is not purely objective (B), MELD does not grade ascites (C), and a MELD >20 carries >50% (not 6%) 30-day mortality (D).
hy: MELD = objective (bili/Cr/INR); CTP adds subjective ascites & encephalopathy.

stem: The "50-50 criteria" used to predict posthepatectomy liver failure (and ~50% mortality) are met on postoperative day 5 when both of which findings are present?

options.A: PT >50% of normal (INR >1.7) and serum bilirubin >2.9 mg/dL
options.B: Albumin <5 g/dL and lactate >5 mmol/L
options.C: Platelet count <50,000 and creatinine >5 mg/dL
options.D: MELD >50 and ascites >5 L/day

exp: The 50-50 criteria define posthepatectomy liver failure on postoperative day 5 as PT <50% of normal (INR >1.7) and serum bilirubin >2.9 mg/dL (50 µmol/L) — a combination associated with up to 50% mortality. The other paired thresholds (B, C, D) are fabricated and not in the text. The name comes from the two "50" cutoffs falling on day 5.
hy: 50-50: POD5 INR >1.7 (PT <50%) + bilirubin >2.9 mg/dL → ~50% mortality.

stem: What minimum residual (future) liver volume does the chapter consider adequate after hepatic resection in a noncirrhotic patient versus a patient with compensated cirrhosis?

options.A: 20% noncirrhotic; 40% compensated cirrhosis
options.B: 10% noncirrhotic; 20% compensated cirrhosis
options.C: 30% noncirrhotic; 50% compensated cirrhosis
options.D: 40% noncirrhotic; 60% compensated cirrhosis

exp: A residual liver volume of ~20% is generally adequate after resection in a noncirrhotic patient, rising to ~40% for compensated cirrhosis (and posthepatectomy liver failure risk climbs as remnant shrinks). The other percentage pairs (B, C, D) misstate these minimums. Remnant volume is estimated by CT/MRI volumetry against body surface area.
hy: Adequate remnant: ~20% normal liver, ~40% compensated cirrhosis.

stem: For most hemodynamically stable, asymptomatic hospitalized patients (including the critically ill), the AABB recommends withholding RBC transfusion until hemoglobin falls to what level — and which subgroup may instead use an 8 g/dL trigger?

options.A: ≤7 g/dL; patients with cardiovascular disease / cardiac surgery
options.B: ≤10 g/dL; trauma patients
options.C: ≤8 g/dL; all surgical patients
options.D: ≤6 g/dL; septic patients

exp: The AABB recommends a restrictive trigger of ≤7 g/dL for most stable, asymptomatic hospitalized patients, including the critically ill (supported by TRICC and TRISS). A threshold of 8 g/dL may be considered for patients with cardiovascular disease or undergoing cardiac surgery (TRICS III/FOCUS). A 10 g/dL liberal trigger (B) and a universal 8 g/dL (C) or 6 g/dL (D) do not match the guidance.
hy: Restrictive Hb ≤7 g/dL; consider 8 g/dL in cardiac disease/cardiac surgery.

stem: Per unit of RBCs transfused, which adverse event is correctly matched to its approximate risk?

options.A: Fatal hemolysis — about 1 in 250
options.B: Transfusion-related acute lung injury — about 1 in 12,000
options.C: HIV transmission — about 1 in 1,000
options.D: Febrile reaction — about 1 in 1,000,000

exp: Transfusion-related acute lung injury carries an approximate per-unit risk of 1:12,000. Fatal hemolysis is far rarer (~1:1,972,000, not 1:250 — A); HIV transmission is ~1:1,467,000 (not 1:1,000 — C); and febrile reaction is common at ~1:60 (not 1:1,000,000 — D). The table underpins the shift toward restrictive transfusion.
hy: TRALI ≈ 1:12,000; viral transmission ≈ 1 in millions; febrile reaction ≈ 1:60.

stem: Isolated prolongation of the aPTT (with a normal PT) points to a deficiency of which factors — and what is the inheritance pattern of the two most common such disorders?

options.A: Factors VIII, IX, or XI; hemophilia A and B are X-linked recessive
options.B: Factor VII; autosomal dominant
options.C: Factors II, V, and X; autosomal recessive
options.D: Factor XIII; X-linked dominant

exp: An isolated prolonged aPTT with normal PT localizes to the intrinsic pathway — deficiencies of factor VIII (hemophilia A), IX (hemophilia B), or XI — and hemophilia A and B are both X-linked recessive. Isolated PT prolongation points to factor VII (B); combined PT/aPTT prolongation indicates the common pathway (II, V, X, fibrinogen — C); and factor XIII deficiency is not detected by PT/aPTT at all (D).
hy: Isolated long aPTT → VIII/IX/XI; hemophilia A & B are X-linked recessive.

stem: In which subtype of von Willebrand disease is DDAVP contraindicated because it can precipitate thrombocytopenia?

options.A: Type 1
options.B: Type 2A
options.C: Type 2B
options.D: Type 2M

exp: DDAVP is contraindicated in type 2B von Willebrand disease because it can cause thrombocytopenia. Type 1 (A) responds well to DDAVP given ~1 hour before incision; types 2A and 2M (B, D) may show a partial response; and type 3 (complete deficiency) does not respond and needs vWF concentrate.
hy: DDAVP is contraindicated in type 2B vWD (causes thrombocytopenia).

stem: For a patient with von Willebrand disease undergoing major surgery, what von Willebrand factor levels does the chapter recommend?

options.A: 100% preoperatively, with trough daily levels of 50% until wound healing
options.B: 60% preoperatively, with trough levels of 30%
options.C: 30% preoperatively as a single dose
options.D: 80% predelivery with troughs of 30%

exp: For major surgery the recommended target is 100% vWF preoperatively with trough daily levels of 50% maintained until wound healing (~5–10 days). The 60%/30% target (B) applies to minor surgery; a single 60% dose (C) is for dental extractions; and 80%–100% with 30% troughs (D) is the delivery/puerperium target.
hy: Major surgery vWD: 100% preop, trough 50% until healed.

stem: Which statement about perioperative oral anticoagulant management is correct?

options.A: Warfarin (half-life 36–42 h) is stopped 5 days before elective surgery, with an INR check on the day of operation
options.B: Dabigatran is reversed with andexanet alpha
options.C: Rivaroxaban requires a 5-day preoperative hold
options.D: Warfarin should be resumed no sooner than 7 days postoperatively

exp: Warfarin (half-life 36–42 h) is stopped 5 days before elective surgery with an INR checked on the day of operation; if INR >1.5, vitamin K or FFP may be given. Dabigatran is reversed by idarucizumab, not andexanet (B — andexanet reverses factor Xa inhibitors); rivaroxaban (a factor Xa inhibitor) is held ~48 h, not 5 days (C); and warfarin is typically resumed within 12–24 h, not 7 days (D).
hy: Warfarin: stop 5 d preop, INR on day of surgery; idarucizumab reverses dabigatran.

stem: Based on the BRIDGE trial and the chapter's guidance, which statement about bridging is correct?

options.A: For atrial fibrillation, forgoing LMWH bridging does not increase arterial thromboembolism and lowers major bleeding
options.B: All patients on warfarin require LMWH bridging
options.C: Unfractionated heparin bridging should be continued up to incision
options.D: Bridging LMWH should be stopped 2 hours before surgery

exp: The double-blind BRIDGE trial concluded that, for atrial fibrillation, forgoing LMWH bridging does not increase arterial thromboembolism and decreases major bleeding — so routine bridging for AF is no longer recommended. Not all warfarin patients need bridging (B); UFH should be stopped 4–6 h before surgery (C); and LMWH is stopped 24 h before, not 2 h (D). Bridging is reserved for very high-risk patients (recent stroke, mechanical valve, high-risk VTE).
hy: BRIDGE: skip LMWH bridging in AF — no more clots, less bleeding.

stem: A patient on prednisone 4 mg daily for years presents for a major operation. According to the supplemental-glucocorticoid framework, what is appropriate?

options.A: Give the usual daily dose; no stress-dose supplementation is needed (no HPA-axis suppression at <5 mg/day)
options.B: Give 100 mg hydrocortisone before induction and taper over 72 hours
options.C: Cancel surgery until a cosyntropin stimulation test is performed
options.D: Give 50 mg hydrocortisone every 8 hours for 48 hours

exp: Patients taking <5 mg prednisone/day (or equivalent) for any duration are considered to have no HPA-axis suppression and should simply receive their usual daily dose with no stress-dose supplementation. High-dose hydrocortisone regimens (B, D) are reserved for documented/presumed suppression undergoing moderate-to-major stress, and cancelling for a stimulation test (C) is unnecessary at this low dose.
hy: <5 mg prednisone/day → no suppression; give usual dose, no stress steroids.

stem: A patient with a catecholamine-secreting adrenal mass is scheduled for adrenalectomy. What preoperative preparation does the chapter require to avoid life-threatening intraoperative instability?

options.A: α-adrenergic blockade established first, then β-blockade, before the planned operation
options.B: β-blockade alone started the morning of surgery
options.C: High-dose corticosteroids for one week preoperatively
options.D: No specific blockade is needed for urgent cases

exp: Catecholamine-secreting tumors (pheochromocytoma, functional paraganglioma) require α-adrenergic blockade established first, followed by β-blockade, before a planned operation; unopposed β-blockade risks hypertensive crisis from unchecked α-mediated vasoconstriction. β-blockade alone the morning of surgery (B) is dangerous, steroids (C) are irrelevant, and urgent operations without adequate blockade (D) carry high morbidity/mortality and should be avoided.
hy: Pheochromocytoma: α-blockade before β-blockade, always.

stem: A patient with carcinoid liver metastases and flushing/diarrhea is planned for tumor-directed surgery. Which statement is correct?

options.A: Somatostatin-analogue (octreotide) blockade should be established, and clinically significant carcinoid heart disease (e.g., tricuspid regurgitation, pulmonic stenosis) addressed, before tumor surgery
options.B: Beta blockade is the mainstay of perioperative carcinoid management
options.C: Carcinoid heart disease characteristically causes mitral stenosis and aortic regurgitation
options.D: Octreotide should be stopped before induction to avoid bradycardia

exp: Symptomatic carcinoid (flushing/diarrhea) or carcinoid liver metastases should be controlled with somatostatin-analogue (octreotide/lanreotide) blockade, and clinically significant carcinoid heart disease — classically tricuspid regurgitation and pulmonic stenosis/regurgitation — should be addressed before tumor-directed surgery. Beta blockade is not the mainstay (B), the valve lesions are right-sided not mitral/aortic (C), and octreotide must be continued/escalated perioperatively, not stopped (D).
hy: Octreotide blockade + treat right-sided (tricuspid/pulmonic) carcinoid heart disease before tumor surgery.

stem: Using the Nutritional Risk Screen (NRS-2002), at what total (age-adjusted) score is a patient considered nutritionally at-risk, triggering initiation of a nutritional care plan?
exp: An NRS-2002 total (age-adjusted) score ≥3 defines a nutritionally at-risk patient and triggers a nutritional care plan; a score <3 prompts weekly rescreening. Scores of ≥1 or ≥2 (A, B) are below the action threshold, and ≥5 (D) is higher than required. The tool combines impaired nutritional status and severity of disease, adding a point for age ≥70.
hy: NRS-2002 ≥3 = nutritionally at-risk → start a care plan.

stem: A surgeon wants the serum protein marker that best reflects recent (not chronic) nutritional status. Which marker — and its half-life — fits, and why are these markers imperfect perioperatively?

options.A: Prealbumin (half-life ~2 days); both albumin and prealbumin are negative acute-phase reactants that fall with inflammation
options.B: Albumin (half-life ~20 days); it rises during acute inflammation and so tracks recent intake closely
options.C: Transferrin (half-life ~20 days); it reflects recent nutrition and is unaffected by the patient's iron status
options.D: Prealbumin (half-life ~20 days); it is a positive acute-phase reactant that increases with surgical stress

exp: Prealbumin has a short half-life (~2 days) and so reflects recent nutritional status better than albumin (half-life ~20 days, a chronic marker); however, both are negative acute-phase reactants and fall during inflammation, limiting perioperative reliability. Albumin does not rise with inflammation (B is wrong); transferrin's half-life is ~8–9 days and it is affected by iron status (C); and prealbumin is a negative, not positive, acute-phase reactant (D).
hy: Prealbumin (t½ ~2 d) = recent nutrition; albumin (t½ ~20 d) = chronic; both fall with inflammation.

stem: Which statement about postoperative enteral nutrition matches the chapter?

options.A: Early enteral feeding (within 24 h) is a key ERAS component, and mechanical ventilation, vasopressors, and an open abdomen are not strict contraindications
options.B: Objective return of bowel function (flatus or stool) must be confirmed before any enteral feeding is started
options.C: Parenteral nutrition is preferred over the enteral route in the majority of postoperative surgical situations
options.D: An open abdomen is an absolute contraindication to enteral feeding until the fascia is definitively closed

exp: Early enteral feeding (within 24 h) is a key ERAS element and may reduce complications; the true contraindications to enteral feeding are few (obstruction, intestinal ischemia, bowel discontinuity, high-output fistula, severe malabsorption), and mechanical ventilation, vasopressor support, and an open abdomen are not strict contraindications. Awaiting objective bowel function is unnecessary (B), enteral is preferred over parenteral (C), and an open abdomen is not absolute (D).
hy: Feed early/enterally; vent, pressors, and an open abdomen don't forbid it.

stem: A colorectal service implements an Enhanced Recovery After Surgery (ERAS) pathway. Which element is characteristic of these protocols?

options.A: Routine prolonged preoperative fasting with liberal perioperative opioid use for analgesia
options.B: Intraoperative euvolemia with opioid-sparing anesthesia, plus early mobilization and early diet postoperatively
options.C: Mandatory mechanical bowel preparation with strict bed rest maintained until the first passage of flatus
options.D: Withholding all oral intake postoperatively until objective signs of bowel function have returned

exp: ERAS pathways standardize care around preoperative education and prehabilitation, intraoperative opioid-sparing anesthesia with maintenance of euvolemia, and a postoperative pathway of early mobilization, multimodal/opioid-minimizing analgesia, avoidance of volume overload, and early resumption of diet. Prolonged fasting with liberal opioids (A) and bed rest until flatus (C) are the old paradigm ERAS replaced, and ERAS deliberately does not await objective bowel function before feeding (D).
hy: ERAS = euvolemia + opioid-sparing + early mobilization and diet.

stem: An elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy with controlled entry into the biliary tract, no spillage, and no infection is which CDC wound class?

options.A: Clean
options.B: Clean-contaminated
options.C: Contaminated
options.D: Dirty-infected

exp: Clean-contaminated wounds are those entering the respiratory, alimentary, genital, or urinary tracts under controlled conditions without unusual contamination — explicitly including operations on the biliary tract, appendix, vagina, and oropharynx with no infection or major break in technique. A controlled, no-spillage cholecystectomy is therefore clean-contaminated, not clean (A), contaminated (C), or dirty (D).
hy: Controlled biliary/GI/GU entry without spillage = clean-contaminated.

stem: Which scenario is correctly matched to its CDC wound class?

options.A: An operation on a field with established infection or a perforated viscus — dirty-infected
options.B: Gross spillage from the GI tract without prior infection — clean
options.C: An uninfected incision not entering the GI/respiratory/GU tract — contaminated
options.D: Controlled entry into the vagina without spillage — dirty-infected

exp: A dirty-infected (Class IV) wound involves existing clinical infection or perforated viscera, with organisms present in the field before the operation. Gross GI spillage without prior infection is contaminated (Class III), not clean (B); an uninfected incision not entering a hollow tract is clean, not contaminated (C); and controlled vaginal entry without spillage is clean-contaminated, not dirty (D).
hy: Pre-existing infection/perforation = dirty (IV); gross spillage alone = contaminated (III).

stem: Within what window before incision should the first dose of prophylactic antibiotics be given for most agents, and what is the exception for vancomycin or fluoroquinolones?

options.A: Within 60 minutes; vancomycin/fluoroquinolones may begin within 120 minutes
options.B: Within 30 minutes; no exceptions
options.C: Within 2 hours; vancomycin within 30 minutes
options.D: At the time of incision; antibiotics after incision are equivalent

exp: Current data support giving the first prophylactic dose within 60 minutes before incision (the Trial to Reduce Antimicrobial Prophylaxis Errors found lowest infection rates within 30 min or 31–60 min before incision). Agents needing longer infusion — vancomycin and fluoroquinolones — may begin within 120 minutes. A flat 30-minute window with no exceptions (B), a 2-hour rule (C), or post-incision dosing (D) are incorrect.
hy: Antibiotics within 60 min of incision (120 min for vancomycin/fluoroquinolones).

stem: For most clean and clean-contaminated procedures, which agent is recommended first-line, and what substitution is reasonable for a patient colonized with MRSA?

options.A: Cefazolin first-line; add/substitute vancomycin for MRSA colonization
options.B: Vancomycin first-line for all cases
options.C: Metronidazole first-line; add cefoxitin for MRSA
options.D: Ceftriaxone first-line; add clindamycin for MRSA

exp: Cefazolin is recommended for most operations because of its low cost, appropriate spectrum (skin flora — S. aureus, coagulase-negative staph), and duration of activity; for patients colonized with MRSA, vancomycin is a reasonable addition/alternative. Vancomycin is not first-line for all (B), and metronidazole or ceftriaxone are not the default single agents with the MRSA logic described (C, D).
hy: Cefazolin default; add vancomycin for MRSA colonization.

stem: Prophylactic antibiotics should be redosed intraoperatively under which circumstances?

options.A: After roughly every two half-lives of the agent, or with blood loss greater than 1500 mL
options.B: Only if the operation exceeds 6 hours
options.C: Every 30 minutes regardless of agent
options.D: Never — a single preoperative dose always suffices

exp: Intraoperative redosing maintains therapeutic serum levels — generally after roughly every two half-lives of the agent, or when blood loss exceeds 1500 mL. A fixed 6-hour rule (B), an arbitrary every-30-minutes rule (C), and "single dose always suffices" (D) are all incorrect; redosing also shortens for agents with shortened half-lives (e.g., extensive burns).
hy: Redose after ~2 half-lives or with >1500 mL blood loss.

stem: What does the chapter say about the duration of antimicrobial prophylaxis?

options.A: When continued at all, postoperative prophylaxis should be limited to <24 hours — though cardiothoracic surgery may justify up to 48 hours
options.B: Postoperative prophylaxis should routinely continue for 5–7 days to cover the healing period in most operations
options.C: All clean-contaminated cases require a full 72 hours of postoperative antibiotics to prevent surgical site infection
options.D: Prolonging the duration of prophylaxis has no effect on Clostridioides difficile infection or antimicrobial resistance

exp: When postoperative prophylaxis is used at all it should be limited to <24 hours for most procedures, but cardiothoracic surgery is a recognized exception where the Surgical Infection Society and STS endorse consideration of up to 48 hours (evidence that this reduces sternal wound infection). Routine 5–7 day (B) or 72-hour (C) courses are not supported, and prolonged antibiotics do raise C. difficile/resistance risk (D).
hy: Prophylaxis <24 h (cardiothoracic may extend to 48 h).

stem: A preoperative clinic reviews herbal supplements. Which discontinuation recommendation matches the chapter's table?

options.A: Garlic and ginseng — discontinue at least 7 days before surgery (bleeding/platelet effects)
options.B: Ephedra — continue through surgery; it is hemodynamically neutral
options.C: Ginkgo — no need to stop; it has no bleeding effect
options.D: St. John's wort — stop only 24 hours before, as it has no drug interactions

exp: Per the herbal-medicine table, garlic and ginseng (platelet-inhibiting/bleeding effects) should be discontinued at least 7 days before surgery. Ephedra must be stopped ≥24 h (it causes tachycardia/hypertension and arrhythmias, so B is wrong); ginkgo should stop ≥36 h because it inhibits platelet-activating factor (C is wrong); and St. John's wort should stop ≥5 days because it induces cytochrome P450 with major drug interactions (D is wrong).
hy: Garlic & ginseng: stop ≥7 days (bleeding); ginkgo ≥36 h; St. John's wort ≥5 d.

stem: Estrogen and tamoxifen should be held for how long preoperatively, and why?

options.A: 4 weeks, because of thromboembolic risk
options.B: 24 hours, because of bleeding risk
options.C: 1 week, because of adrenal suppression
options.D: They need not be stopped

exp: Estrogen and tamoxifen should be held for 4 weeks preoperatively because of their thromboembolic risk. The shorter intervals tied to bleeding (B) or adrenal suppression (C) are incorrect, and these agents are not safe to continue unchanged through surgery (D). They are also frequently underreported, so the medication history must probe for them.
hy: Hold estrogen/tamoxifen 4 weeks preop (VTE risk).

stem: Per the 2017 ASA fasting guidance, which minimum fasting interval before general anesthesia is correctly matched to the ingested material?

options.A: Clear liquids — 2 hours; breast milk — 4 hours; a light meal — 6 hours
options.B: Clear liquids — 6 hours; breast milk — 2 hours
options.C: Breast milk — 8 hours; nonhuman milk — 2 hours
options.D: A light meal — 2 hours; fried/fatty food — 4 hours

exp: The 2017 ASA fasting guidance permits clear liquids up to 2 hours, breast milk up to 4 hours, infant formula/nonhuman milk/light meals up to 6 hours, and fried/fatty foods or meat 8+ hours before general anesthesia. The other pairings (B, C, D) scramble these intervals. Routine GI stimulants/antacids are not recommended solely to shorten fasting.
hy: Clear liquids 2 h, breast milk 4 h, light meal 6 h, fried/fatty 8+ h.

stem: Which consequence of intraoperative hypothermia does the chapter describe?

options.A: Impaired platelet/coagulation function with nearly 20% greater operative blood loss, plus increased wound-infection risk
options.B: Reduced anesthetic duration of action with faster emergence and quicker drug clearance
options.C: Improved wound oxygenation from cutaneous vasodilation, lowering surgical-site infection risk
options.D: Accelerated correction of metabolic acidosis and electrolyte disturbance during resuscitation

exp: Hypothermia impairs platelet aggregation and the coagulation cascade — associated with nearly 20% greater operative blood loss — and reduces wound oxygenation (via vasoconstriction), raising wound-infection risk. It prolongs (not shortens) anesthetic duration of action (B), impairs rather than improves wound oxygenation (C), and impairs correction of acidosis/electrolytes (D).
hy: Hypothermia → ~20% more blood loss, more SSI, prolonged drug effect.

stem: Which statement about intraoperative thermoregulation is correct?

options.A: The distal esophagus is the best practical core-temperature site in intubated patients, and forced-air warming is the most common effective active method
options.B: Rectal, forehead, and external aural-canal temperatures correlate most reliably with true core temperature
options.C: Warming IV fluids well above core temperature is one of the most effective ways to raise body temperature
options.D: Hypothermia is most drastic at the end of a long case rather than within the first hour after induction

exp: The distal esophagus is the best practical core-temperature site in intubated patients (sublingual, axillary, and bladder also correlate well), and forced-air warming is the most common, effective active-warming method. Skin, forehead, external aural canal, and rectal sites correlate poorly (B is wrong); warming IV fluids above core temperature is ineffective because infused fluid cannot exceed core temperature (C is wrong); and hypothermia is most drastic in the first hour after induction from vasodilation, not at the end (D is wrong).
hy: Distal esophagus for core temp; forced air to warm; IV-fluid warming is ineffective.

stem: In the multi-institutional trial comparing antiseptics for clean-contaminated surgery, chlorhexidine-alcohol reduced SSIs versus povidone-iodine (9.5% vs 16.1%). Which related statement is also correct?

options.A: Hair, when removed, should be clipped rather than shaved, since shaving raises SSI risk
options.B: Alcohol-based preps are preferred on mucosal surfaces
options.C: Shaving the night before lowers infection risk
options.D: Povidone-iodine is clearly superior on intact skin

exp: Hair, when removed at all, should be clipped — not shaved — because shaving increases SSI risk. Alcohol-containing preps should be avoided on mucosal surfaces (B is wrong), shaving the night before raises infection risk (C is wrong), and the trial data favor chlorhexidine-alcohol over povidone-iodine on intact skin, so povidone is not superior (D is wrong). An alcohol-based agent in the prep appears optimal.
hy: Clip, don't shave; alcohol-based prep optimal but not on mucosa.

stem: Which vessels does the chapter say can be safely controlled by monopolar electrocautery alone, versus those requiring ties/clips/energy sealing?

options.A: Capillaries and small veins by cautery alone; vessels >1 mm (including all named vessels) need ties, clips, staples, bipolar, or ultrasonic sealing
options.B: Any vessel up to 5 mm in diameter can be safely controlled and divided by monopolar cautery alone
options.C: Only arteries ever require ligation, whereas veins of any caliber can be controlled by cautery
options.D: All vessels regardless of size require formal suture ligation before division to prevent rebleeding

exp: Capillaries and small veins can be controlled and divided with monopolar electrocautery alone, but vessels >1 mm in diameter — including all named vessels — are best controlled with ties, clips, staples, bipolar electrocautery, or ultrasonic devices, and larger vessels may need suture ligation. Cautery alone does not safely handle 5-mm vessels (B), the size rule applies to veins and arteries alike (C), and not every vessel needs ligation (D).
hy: Cautery alone only for capillaries/small veins; >1 mm (named) vessels need a tie/clip/seal.

stem: When a skin/fascial incision is anticipated to be under significant tension, which closure technique does the chapter highlight, and what is the approach to a heavily contaminated dirty wound?

options.A: Vertical mattress sutures for tension; heavily contaminated dirty wounds are left open to heal by secondary intention
options.B: Subcuticular absorbable suture for tension; heavily contaminated dirty wounds should be closed primarily
options.C: Tissue adhesive used alone for tension; heavily contaminated dirty wounds are closed over prosthetic mesh
options.D: Running monofilament for tension; all dirty wounds receive delayed primary closure at exactly 24 hours

exp: Vertical mattress sutures distribute tension over two depths at each point and approximate the dermis effectively for high-tension incisions, while heavily contaminated dirty wounds should be left open to heal by secondary intention with serial packing. Subcuticular suture is for cosmesis, not tension (B), tissue adhesive alone won't bear significant tension (C), and dirty wounds are not routinely closed primarily at 24 h (D).
hy: Vertical mattress for tension; dirty wounds heal open by secondary intention.

stem: A damage-control abdomen is left open with a planned second look. Which statement about temporary/serial closure is correct?

options.A: Delayed primary fascial closure should be achieved when possible within 7–10 days; a Wittman patch can be serially tightened every 24–48 hours
options.B: Definitive fascial closure must be completed within the first 24 hours or it can never be safely achieved
options.C: An absorbable mesh bridge reliably guarantees full fascial closure without any resulting hernia or loss of domain
options.D: Temporary closure should use an adherent material sutured directly to the bowel to seal the abdomen

exp: When an abdomen is left open, delayed primary fascial closure should be achieved when possible within 7–10 days; if not, serial closure with a Wittman patch (sutured to fascia and tightened every 24–48 h) can progressively approximate the edges. Closure is not an all-or-nothing 24-hour proposition (B), an absorbable mesh bridge predictably leaves a large hernia/loss of domain rather than guaranteeing closure (C), and temporary closure uses a nonadherent bridge, never material sutured to bowel (D).
hy: Aim for delayed primary fascial closure in 7–10 d; Wittman patch tightened q24–48 h.

stem: Which statement about tissue adhesives and fibrin sealant is correct?

options.A: Dermabond (octylcyanoacrylate) gives a watertight, low-infection skin barrier, and fibrin sealant combines thrombin and fibrinogen to replicate the final clotting step independent of the patient's pathway
options.B: Cyanoacrylate adhesives must always be combined with an underlying subcuticular permanent suture line to hold
options.C: Fibrin sealant requires the patient's own intact coagulation cascade to be present in order to form a clot
options.D: Tissue adhesives have been proven clearly superior to sutures for both wound dehiscence and surgeon-rated cosmesis

exp: Dermabond (octylcyanoacrylate) gives a watertight, low-infection, cosmetically favorable skin closure, and fibrin sealant works by combining thrombin and fibrinogen to reproduce the final common step of clotting — so it forms a clot regardless of the patient's intrinsic-pathway status. Cyanoacrylate does not require permanent suture (B), fibrin sealant does not depend on the patient's own cascade (C), and meta-analysis found no clear superiority of adhesives over sutures (D).
hy: Dermabond = octylcyanoacrylate; fibrin sealant supplies thrombin + fibrinogen, cascade-independent.

stem: In monopolar electrosurgery, what distinguishes the "cut" from the "coagulation" waveform?

options.A: A continuous waveform (cut) gives little thermal spread and minimal coagulation, whereas an intermittent waveform delivering current <10% of the time (coag) gives lower energy with greater thermal spread, dehydration, and vessel thrombosis
options.B: Cut mode uses a low-duty intermittent waveform, while coag mode uses a continuous waveform for rapid division
options.C: Both cut and coag modes deliver identical total energy, and only the size of the electrode tip differs between them
options.D: Coag mode produces the least thermal spread of any setting, making it ideal for cutting near vital structures

exp: A continuous ("cut") waveform cuts with little thermal spread and minimal coagulation, whereas an intermittent ("coag") waveform delivers current less than ~10% of the activation time, producing lower thermal energy but greater thermal spread with tissue dehydration and vessel thrombosis. Option B reverses the two modes, C wrongly claims identical energy, and D wrongly says coag has the least thermal spread. The "blend" mode is intermediate.
hy: Cut = continuous (low spread); coag = intermittent (<10% duty, more spread/thrombosis).

stem: Why is bipolar electrosurgery preferred when operating near vital structures or in a patient with an implanted electronic device?

options.A: Current passes only through the tissue held between the two adjacent electrodes, so no grounding pad is needed and thermal/electrical spread is minimal
options.B: It delivers far more energy than monopolar electrosurgery and spreads it over a wide tissue field
options.C: It requires a grounding pad whose return path actively protects any implanted electronic device
options.D: It is incapable of coagulating tissue at all, which is why it is considered inherently safe to use

exp: In bipolar electrosurgery the delivering and returning electrodes sit close together in one instrument, so current flows only through the tissue between them: no grounding pad is required, thermal spread is minimal, and it is safe in patients with implanted electronic devices that monopolar current could disturb. It does not deliver more energy over a wide field (B), needs no grounding pad at all (C), and does coagulate (D).
hy: Bipolar: current confined between the jaws — no pad, safe with implants.

stem: Bipolar fusion devices such as LigaSure and Enseal seal vessels up to approximately what diameter, and by what mechanism?

options.A: Up to ~7 mm, by uniform compression with impedance feedback causing collagen/elastin denaturation and cross-linking
options.B: Up to ~2 mm, purely by mechanical crushing
options.C: Up to ~15 mm, by ultrasonic cavitation
options.D: Up to ~7 mm, by argon-gas plasma coagulation

exp: LigaSure and Enseal are bipolar fusion devices that apply uniform compression and monitor tissue impedance to titrate energy, denaturing and cross-linking collagen and elastin into a natural seal for vessels up to ~7 mm, then divide with an internal blade. They are not limited to ~2 mm by mechanical crush (B), do not seal 15 mm by cavitation (C), and do not use argon plasma (D).
hy: LigaSure/Enseal seal up to ~7 mm via collagen/elastin denaturation.

stem: Saline-cooled radiofrequency dissectors (e.g., TissueLink, Aquamantys) overcome eschar formation in vascular parenchyma by maintaining the tissue contact surface below what temperature?
exp: Saline-cooled radiofrequency dissectors (TissueLink, Aquamantys) direct a steady saline irrigation stream to the contact point to keep the surface temperature <105°C, preventing the dense eschar that limits coagulation and risks delayed hemorrhage in vascular parenchyma. The lower (60°C) and higher (150/200°C) values (B, C, D) are incorrect. The organ capsule is usually divided first to prevent subcapsular steam build-up.
hy: Saline-cooled RF keeps surface <105°C to avoid eschar.

stem: Why does the Cavitron ultrasonic surgical aspirator (CUSA) selectively fragment hepatic parenchyma while sparing vessels and bile ducts?

options.A: Hepatocytes have high water content and are susceptible to oscillatory fragmentation, while low-water-content vessels/ducts are spared — but CUSA confers no vessel sealing itself
options.B: It fragments parenchyma and simultaneously seals vessels up to 7 mm in diameter as it dissects the plane
options.C: It selectively heats and denatures collagen-rich vessels while sparing the water-rich hepatocytes around them
options.D: It requires a grounding pad and coagulates tissue as it cuts, like a monopolar electrosurgical device

exp: The CUSA directs ultrasonic vibration along a hollow titanium tip that fragments high-water-content hepatocytes while sparing low-water-content endothelium and epithelium (blood vessels, bile ducts), creating a precise dissection plane — but it confers no vessel-sealing capability and is used with adjunct hemostasis. It does not seal 7-mm vessels (B), does not target collagen (C), and needs no grounding pad/does not coagulate as it cuts (D).
hy: CUSA fragments water-rich hepatocytes, spares vessels/ducts, but doesn't seal.

stem: For radiofrequency ablation of a solid-organ tumor, which statement is correct?

options.A: Tumors up to ~3 cm can be effectively ablated, with an additional ~0.5 cm margin of healthy parenchyma, but a nearby vessel creates a "heat sink" that limits efficacy
options.B: RFA requires no grounding pad and is completely safe to use in the presence of metallic surgical clips
options.C: RFA efficacy is unaffected by adjacent blood vessels because thermal energy stays confined to the probe tip
options.D: Tumors up to roughly 10 cm in diameter can be reliably ablated by RFA in a single application

exp: RFA can effectively ablate tumors up to ~3 cm, and an additional ~0.5 cm margin of healthy parenchyma should be ablated for complete tumor kill; proximity to a blood vessel disperses thermal energy as a "heat sink," limiting efficacy. Because RFA mirrors electrosurgery it does require a grounding pad and is contraindicated near metallic clips/implants (B is wrong), it is affected by adjacent vessels (C), and 10-cm lesions are not reliably ablated (D).
hy: RFA: ≤3 cm tumors + 0.5 cm margin; heat-sink near vessels; needs grounding pad.

stem: What advantages does microwave ablation hold over radiofrequency ablation?

options.A: A larger, more homogeneous ablation zone, an attenuated heat-sink effect, and no need for a grounding pad (safe around metallic clips)
options.B: It still requires a grounding pad like RFA but completes the ablation faster with a larger zone
options.C: It is the only one of the ablation modalities that can be performed by a percutaneous image-guided approach
options.D: It transmits an electric current through tissue and is therefore contraindicated near metallic implants and clips

exp: Microwave ablation offers a larger, more homogeneous ablation zone, an attenuated heat-sink effect, and shorter ablation time; because it does not transmit electric current, no grounding pad is needed and it is safe around metallic clips/implants — advantages driving its rise over RFA. Options B, C, and D contradict these points (it needs no pad, is not the only percutaneous option, and does not transmit current).
hy: Microwave > RFA: bigger uniform zone, less heat sink, no grounding pad.

stem: Which hazard is specific to the argon beam coagulator, and how is it mitigated?

options.A: Direct targeting of central veins can cause fatal argon-gas embolism and cardiac arrest; keep gas flow low, use an angled approach, and avoid deploying into large central veins
options.B: It penetrates deeply into tissue and easily achieves control of large named arteries during dissection
options.C: The argon gas dissolves readily in blood, which essentially eliminates any risk of gas embolism
options.D: It is a true laser device whose only meaningful hazard is ocular injury, requiring protective eyewear

exp: The argon beam coagulator carries a specific risk: the focused jet of argon gas is insoluble in blood, so directing it into central veins can produce a fatal argon-gas embolism and cardiac arrest. Mitigation is to keep gas flow low, use an angled approach, and avoid deploying argon directly into large veins communicating with the right atrium. It has shallow (not deep) penetration and cannot control large arteries (B), is not soluble in blood (C), and is not a laser (D).
hy: ABC: don't fire into central veins — argon embolism can cause cardiac arrest.

stem: Regarding stapler loads, which staple-height pairing is correct?

options.A: Smaller staples (open ~2 mm / closed ~1 mm) suit vascular closure; larger staples (open ~4.5 mm / closed ~2 mm) suit thick tissue such as stomach or pancreas
options.B: Vascular loads use the tallest staples available, reserving the shortest staples for thick gastric tissue
options.C: A single universal staple height is designed to fit every tissue thickness encountered in the abdomen
options.D: Circular (end-to-end) staplers are used mainly for transecting and closing named vascular structures

exp: Smaller staples (open ~2 mm / closed ~1 mm) are best for vascular closure and transection, whereas larger staples (open ~4.5 mm / closed ~2 mm) suit thick tissues such as stomach or pancreas, with intermediate heights available for in-between tissues. Vascular loads are the shortest, not tallest (B), a single height does not fit all tissues (C), and circular (end-to-end) staplers are for luminal anastomoses, not vascular transection (D).
hy: Vascular staples short (~2 mm open); thick-tissue staples tall (~4.5 mm open).

stem: A patient develops a rising end-tidal CO₂ despite increased minute ventilation, then tachycardia and rigidity during a sevoflurane anesthetic. Which statement about malignant hyperthermia is correct?

options.A: It is triggered by volatile halogenated agents and succinylcholine; a rising end-tidal CO₂ despite increased ventilation is among the earliest signs; the classic genetic defect is in the RYR1 gene
options.B: It is triggered chiefly by propofol and opioids, with hypotension as the first intraoperative manifestation
options.C: Bradycardia and hypothermia with a falling end-tidal CO₂ are the earliest features of a developing crisis
options.D: The causative mutation is typically X-linked recessive, so it affects males almost exclusively

exp: Malignant hyperthermia is a hypermetabolic response triggered by volatile halogenated agents (sevoflurane, isoflurane, etc.) and the depolarizing paralytic succinylcholine; a rising end-tidal CO₂ despite increased minute ventilation is among the earliest intraoperative harbingers, and the classic genetic lesion is an RYR1 mutation (autosomal dominant with incomplete penetrance). Propofol/opioids do not trigger it (B), bradycardia/hypothermia are not early features (the picture is fever/tachycardia/rigidity — C), and the inheritance is dominant, not X-linked recessive (D).
hy: MH: triggered by volatile agents + succinylcholine; early sign = rising ETCO₂; RYR1, autosomal dominant.

stem: Which describes correct management and outcome of malignant hyperthermia?

options.A: Stop the volatile agent, give IV dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg (redose for ongoing signs) and cool the patient; with modern care mortality has fallen to <5%
options.B: Continue the volatile anesthetic and give beta blockers as the first-line treatment for the hypermetabolic state
options.C: Give a single IV dose of dantrolene 0.1 mg/kg and avoid active cooling, which is contraindicated in this setting
options.D: Despite immediate dantrolene and supportive care, mortality from a crisis still remains roughly 60–80%

exp: Treatment of malignant hyperthermia is immediate discontinuation of volatile agents, IV dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg (redosed every 10–15 minutes for ongoing fever/acidosis/rigidity), and active cooling, with ICU observation; modern care has reduced mortality from the historic 60–80% to <5%. Continuing the anesthetic with beta blockers (B), a 0.1 mg/kg single dantrolene dose with no cooling (C), and persistent 60–80% mortality (D) are all wrong.
hy: MH: stop volatile, dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg + cooling; mortality now <5%.

stem: During a vascular case the end-tidal CO₂ abruptly drops and the patient becomes hemodynamically unstable from a suspected venous gas embolus. Which statement is correct?

options.A: An abrupt fall in end-tidal CO₂ with cardiovascular collapse is the first manifestation; mortality is ~20%, and the left-lateral-decubitus position should be used only in the absence of cardiovascular collapse (chest compressions need a supine patient)
options.B: A sharp rise in end-tidal CO₂ is the hallmark first sign of a venous gas embolus during the case
options.C: The patient should always be placed in the left-lateral-decubitus position, including during cardiac arrest
options.D: A patent foramen ovale is protective, shunting trapped gas away from the systemic and cerebral circulation

exp: With air or CO₂ venous gas embolus, an abrupt fall in end-tidal CO₂ with cardiovascular collapse is the first manifestation, and overall mortality is ~20%. The left-lateral-decubitus position may help only in the absence of cardiovascular collapse, because meaningful chest compressions require a supine patient. A rising ETCO₂ is not the hallmark (B), the patient should not be placed lateral during arrest (C), and a patent foramen ovale is a risk for systemic/cerebral embolism, not protective (D).
hy: Gas embolus: ETCO₂ drops abruptly, ~20% mortality; lateral decubitus only if not arresting.

stem: During an elective operation a patient develops hypotension, tachycardia, and hypoxia; transesophageal echo shows right-heart strain. Which management principle applies?

options.A: In suspected intraoperative MI or PE with hemodynamic instability, nonemergent/elective operations should be aborted/terminated; a tension pneumothorax found on exam warrants tube thoracostomy
options.B: Right-heart strain seen on transesophageal echo effectively excludes a clinically significant pulmonary embolism
options.C: Elective operations should always be completed regardless of hemodynamic instability once anesthesia has begun
options.D: Intraoperative MI is best confirmed by obtaining a routine portable chest radiograph before taking any action

exp: In suspected intraoperative MI or PE accompanied by hemodynamic instability, nonemergent/elective operations should be aborted or terminated; transesophageal echo can rapidly show right-heart strain (suggesting significant PE or right-heart failure), and an intraoperative tension pneumothorax found on auscultation/diaphragm inspection warrants tube thoracostomy. Right-heart strain supports rather than excludes PE (B), elective operations should not be completed amid instability (C), and intraoperative MI is corroborated by ECG/troponin, not a routine CXR (D).
hy: Instability from MI/PE → abort elective surgery; right-heart strain on TEE flags PE; tension PTX → chest tube.
src: Sabiston Ch 10, "Myocardial Infarction, Pulmonary Embolus, and Pneumothorax"

stem: Which patient factor is listed as an absolute exclusion (or near-absolute) for surgery in a freestanding ambulatory setting?

options.A: Super-morbid obesity with BMI >60
options.B: A mildly elevated BMI of 25–30
options.C: Well-controlled type 2 diabetes
options.D: ASA class 2 status

exp: Super-morbid obesity with BMI >60 is listed as an absolute exclusion for the freestanding ambulatory setting (whereas obese/morbidly obese patients are at higher risk but not strictly prohibited). A mildly elevated BMI of 25–30 (B) is actually associated with the "obesity paradox," well-controlled diabetes (C) and ASA 2 status (D) are compatible with outpatient surgery. Other organ-specific exclusions include serum potassium >5.5, STOP-BANG ≥5 with anticipated opioids, and severe cardiopulmonary disease.
hy: BMI >60 = absolute exclusion from freestanding outpatient surgery.

stem: Which activity is correctly matched to its approximate metabolic-equivalent (MET) level in the Duke Activity Status Index?

options.A: Walking indoors around the house and light housework such as dusting or washing dishes — about 1 MET
options.B: Climbing a flight of stairs or walking up a hill at a steady pace — about 1 MET
options.C: Strenuous swimming or a game of singles tennis to exhaustion — about 4 METs
options.D: Basic self-care such as eating, dressing, and using the toilet — greater than 10 METs

exp: In the Duke Activity Status Index, ~1 MET covers self-care and very light activity: eating/dressing/toileting, walking indoors, walking a block at 2–3 mph, and light housework like dusting or washing dishes. Climbing stairs or walking up a hill is ~4 METs (B is wrong), strenuous swimming/singles tennis is >10 METs (C is wrong), and basic self-care is ~1 MET rather than >10 (D is wrong). The 4-MET line is the clinically important "proceed without further cardiac testing" threshold.
hy: 1 MET = self-care/light chores; 4 METs = stairs; >10 METs = strenuous sport.

stem: Ranking transfusion adverse events by approximate per-unit risk, which sequence runs correctly from MOST to LEAST common?

options.A: Febrile reaction > transfusion-associated circulatory overload > allergic reaction > TRALI > HIV transmission
options.B: HIV transmission > TRALI > febrile reaction > allergic reaction > circulatory overload
options.C: TRALI > febrile reaction > fatal hemolysis > allergic reaction > hepatitis C
options.D: Allergic reaction > febrile reaction > circulatory overload > hepatitis C > TRALI

exp: The approximate per-unit risks are febrile reaction 1:60, transfusion-associated circulatory overload 1:100, allergic reaction 1:250, TRALI 1:12,000, hepatitis C 1:1,149,000, HIV 1:1,467,000, and fatal hemolysis 1:1,972,000 — so most-to-least is febrile > circulatory overload > allergic > TRALI > HIV. The other sequences invert the common (febrile/TACO/allergic) and rare (viral/hemolysis) ends (B, C, D). Common reactions are non-infectious; infectious transmission is now vanishingly rare.
hy: Common: febrile/TACO/allergic; rare: TRALI then viral/hemolysis.

stem: A patient on a direct factor Xa inhibitor (rivaroxaban or apixaban) needs urgent reversal. Which statement correctly contrasts the targeted reversal agents?

options.A: Andexanet alpha reverses the factor Xa inhibitors, whereas idarucizumab reverses the direct thrombin inhibitor dabigatran
options.B: Idarucizumab reverses the factor Xa inhibitors, whereas andexanet alpha reverses the thrombin inhibitor dabigatran
options.C: Vitamin K reliably reverses both the factor Xa inhibitors and dabigatran within a few hours
options.D: Protamine sulfate is the specific antidote for both dabigatran and the oral factor Xa inhibitors

exp: Andexanet alpha is the targeted reversal agent for oral factor Xa inhibitors (rivaroxaban, apixaban, edoxaban, betrixaban), whereas idarucizumab specifically reverses the direct thrombin inhibitor dabigatran. Option B swaps the two agents; vitamin K reverses warfarin, not the DOACs (C); and protamine reverses heparin, not these oral agents (D). Both DOAC classes have rapid onset and short half-lives, allowing short interruptions.
hy: Andexanet → factor Xa inhibitors; idarucizumab → dabigatran.

stem: On the NRS-2002 "severity of disease" axis (increase in nutritional requirement), which pairing is correct?

options.A: An intensive-care patient on assisted ventilation (APACHE >10) scores 3, while major abdominal surgery scores 2
options.B: Hip fracture or chronic disease with an acute complication scores the maximum of 3 points
options.C: Major abdominal surgery scores only 1 point on the severity-of-disease axis
options.D: A patient with entirely normal nutritional requirements is assigned a baseline score of 2

exp: On the NRS-2002 severity-of-disease axis, an ICU patient on assisted ventilation (APACHE >10) scores the maximum 3, while major abdominal surgery scores 2; hip fracture/chronic disease with acute complications scores 1, not 3 (B is wrong); major abdominal surgery scores 2, not 1 (C is wrong); and normal requirements score 0, not 2 (D is wrong). A total (with the nutritional-status axis and age adjustment) of ≥3 triggers a care plan.
hy: NRS-2002 severity: hip fracture/chronic =1, major abdominal/stroke =2, ICU/head injury/BMT =3.

stem: Which prophylaxis pairing reflects the need for added anaerobic/gram-negative coverage in colorectal surgery versus simple skin-flora coverage elsewhere?

options.A: Colorectal surgery — cefazolin PLUS metronidazole (or cefoxitin/cefotetan); inguinal hernia repair — cefazolin alone
options.B: Colorectal surgery — cefazolin alone; inguinal hernia repair — cefazolin plus metronidazole for anaerobes
options.C: Both colorectal resection and inguinal hernia repair require routine carbapenem prophylaxis
options.D: Colorectal surgery needs no antibiotic prophylaxis, whereas hernia repair requires vancomycin

exp: Colorectal operations breach a heavily colonized lumen, so prophylaxis adds anaerobic/gram-negative coverage: cefazolin plus metronidazole, or cefoxitin/cefotetan (often with oral neomycin + erythromycin/metronidazole and a mechanical prep). A clean inguinal hernia repair encounters mainly skin flora and needs only cefazolin. Option B reverses the two, carbapenems are not routine (C), and colorectal surgery does need prophylaxis while hernia repair does not require vancomycin (D).
hy: Colorectal = cefazolin + metronidazole (anaerobes); clean hernia = cefazolin alone.

stem: Which intraoperative antibiotic-redosing statement matches the guideline dosing table?

options.A: Cefazolin (half-life ~1.2–2.2 h) is redosed about every 4 hours, whereas single-dose agents such as vancomycin and gentamicin are generally not redosed for typical case lengths
options.B: Cefazolin should be redosed every 30 minutes throughout the case regardless of its half-life
options.C: Vancomycin requires routine redosing every 2 hours during any operation of moderate length
options.D: Gentamicin must be redosed every hour intraoperatively to keep serum levels therapeutic

exp: Short-half-life agents are redosed intraoperatively at roughly twice their half-life — cefazolin (t½ ~1.2–2.2 h) about every 4 hours — to maintain therapeutic levels through a long case, whereas single-dose agents like vancomycin and gentamicin are marked "NA" for redosing under typical case lengths. A flat 30-minute cefazolin interval (B), 2-hourly vancomycin (C), and hourly gentamicin (D) all contradict the table. Redosing also shortens with blood loss >1500 mL.
hy: Cefazolin redose ~q4 h (≈2 half-lives); vancomycin/gentamicin single-dose.

stem: Which herbal supplement is correctly matched to BOTH its principal perioperative concern AND its recommended preoperative discontinuation interval?

options.A: Ephedra — sympathomimetic tachycardia/hypertension and arrhythmia risk; discontinue at least 24 hours before surgery
options.B: Garlic — sedation and potentiation of anesthetics; discontinue at least 36 hours before surgery
options.C: St. John's wort — irreversible inhibition of platelet aggregation; discontinue at least 7 days before surgery
options.D: Kava — induction of cytochrome P450 affecting warfarin/cyclosporine; discontinue at least 5 days before surgery

exp: Ephedra acts as a direct/indirect sympathomimetic causing tachycardia, hypertension, and arrhythmias (and depletes catecholamines with long-term use), so it should be stopped at least 24 hours before surgery. Garlic's concern is bleeding via platelet inhibition with a 7-day hold (not sedation/36 h — B); St. John's wort induces cytochrome P450 with a 5-day hold (not platelet inhibition/7 d — C); and kava causes sedation with a 24-hour hold (not P450 induction/5 d — D).
hy: Ephedra = sympathomimetic, stop ≥24 h; garlic/ginseng bleeding ≥7 d; ginkgo ≥36 h; St. John's wort CYP ≥5 d; kava sedation ≥24 h.

stem: A parent asks about fasting before an infant's elective procedure under general anesthesia. Per the ASA fasting table, which set of minimum intervals is correct?

options.A: Breast milk — 4 hours; infant formula — 6 hours; nonhuman milk — 6 hours
options.B: Breast milk — 6 hours; infant formula — 2 hours; nonhuman milk — 4 hours
options.C: Breast milk — 2 hours; infant formula — 4 hours; nonhuman milk — 8 hours
options.D: Infant formula — 2 hours; breast milk — 8 hours; nonhuman milk — 2 hours

exp: The ASA minimum fasting intervals before general anesthesia are clear liquids 2 hours, breast milk 4 hours, infant formula 6 hours, nonhuman milk 6 hours, a light meal 6 hours, and fried/fatty foods or meat 8+ hours — so breast milk 4 h with formula and nonhuman milk each 6 h is correct. The other option sets scramble these intervals (B, C, D). Breast milk clears faster than formula or nonhuman milk, hence its shorter interval.
hy: Clear 2 h, breast milk 4 h, formula/nonhuman milk/light meal 6 h, fried 8+ h.

stem: A patient develops a soft, fluctuant, non-tender subcutaneous fluid collection under a mastectomy flap two weeks postoperatively, without erythema or fever. What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.A: Sterile aspiration with a pressure dressing
options.B: Immediate operative re-exploration and washout
options.C: Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics
options.D: Observation alone, as all seromas resolve without intervention

exp: A seroma is a collection of serum/lymph in a dead space (classic after breast/axillary or hernia surgery); when symptomatic it is managed by sterile aspiration and a pressure dressing, and many asymptomatic seromas simply resorb. Immediate re-exploration is overtreatment for an uninfected collection, antibiotics are reserved for an infected seroma, and not all resolve spontaneously.
hy: Symptomatic seroma → sterile aspiration + pressure dressing.

stem: Using the HAS-BLED score to estimate perioperative bleeding risk, which total score is classified as high bleeding risk?
exp: On the HAS-BLED score 0–1 is low, 2 is intermediate, and ≥3 is high bleeding risk. The acronym is Hypertension, Abnormal renal/liver function, Stroke, Bleeding, Labile INR, Elderly, Drugs/alcohol.
hy: HAS-BLED ≥3 = high bleeding risk.

stem: Per the perioperative discontinuation table, which drug-and-timing pairing is correct before elective surgery?

options.A: Warfarin — discontinue 5 days before surgery
options.B: Clopidogrel — discontinue 24 hours before surgery
options.C: Rivaroxaban — discontinue 7 days before surgery
options.D: Aspirin in a high-cardiovascular-risk patient — always discontinue 10 days before surgery

exp: Warfarin is stopped 5 days before elective surgery; clopidogrel is held 5 days (not 24 h), rivaroxaban ≥24 hours (not 7 days), and aspirin in a high-CV-risk patient is generally continued.
hy: Warfarin and clopidogrel each stop ~5 days preop.

stem: What suture-length-to-wound-length ratio is recommended for proper continuous fascial closure of a midline laparotomy?

options.A: At least 2:1
options.B: At least 3:1
options.C: At least 4:1
options.D: At least 6:1

exp: A suture-length-to-wound-length ratio greater than 4:1 is recommended for continuous fascial closure; lower ratios predispose to dehiscence/incisional hernia.
hy: Fascial closure: suture:wound length >4:1.

stem: The STITCH trial compared small-bite with large-bite fascial closure of midline incisions. What did it find?

options.A: The small-bite technique lowered incisional hernia rates (13% vs 21%)
options.B: The large-bite technique lowered incisional hernia rates (13% vs 21%)
options.C: The two techniques produced identical hernia rates
options.D: The small-bite technique increased the rate of acute dehiscence

exp: STITCH found incisional hernia in 13% (small bites) vs 21% (large bites), showing technical superiority of small bites; it did not increase acute dehiscence.
hy: STITCH: small bites halve incisional hernia (13% vs 21%).

stem: Which statement about acute abdominal wound dehiscence is correct?

options.A: It occurs in 1%–3% of procedures, is most common in the first postoperative week, and carries a mortality up to 35%
options.B: It occurs in roughly 20% of procedures and is most common after the third postoperative week
options.C: It is almost never associated with intraabdominal infection
options.D: Late dehiscence is more dangerous than early dehiscence because suture strength is lowest late

exp: Wound dehiscence occurs in 1%–3%, is most common in the first postoperative week (suture-dependent strength), and carries mortality up to 35%; infection contributes to >50% of cases.
hy: Dehiscence: 1%–3%, first week, mortality up to 35%.

stem: By CDC criteria, within what time frame is an infection still classified as a deep incisional or organ/space SSI when a prosthetic implant has been left in place?

options.A: 30 days
options.B: 90 days
options.C: 6 months
options.D: 1 year

exp: With a prosthetic implant in place, deep incisional and organ/space SSIs are counted up to 1 year (otherwise 30 days); superficial SSI is always within 30 days.
hy: With an implant, deep/organ-space SSI counts for 1 year.

stem: Which surgical wound class is correctly matched to its expected infection rate?

options.A: Clean — 1%–3%
options.B: Clean-contaminated — 20%–25%
options.C: Contaminated — 1%–3%
