options.A: Use of bladder catheters/restraints and sleep disruption are precipitating factors; a postoperative hematocrit <30% is associated with increased delirium risk
options.B: Advanced age is a precipitating factor; a hematocrit >45% increases risk
options.C: Baseline dementia is a precipitating factor; hypernatremia is protective
options.D: Epidural (vs general) anesthesia strongly precipitates delirium

exp: Precipitating factors include bladder catheters/restraints, sleep disruption, infection, medications, hypoxemia, and electrolyte abnormalities; a postoperative hematocrit <30% (along with intraoperative blood loss and transfusion) is associated with increased delirium risk. Notably, no association was found with anesthetic route (epidural vs general), so D is wrong.
hy: Precipitants: catheters/restraints, sleep disruption; hematocrit <30% raises delirium risk.

stem: Which statement about managing postoperative delirium is correct?

options.A: Prevention is best (orientation, mobility, sleep-wake cycle, avoiding deliriogenic drugs); low-dose antipsychotics (e.g., haloperidol) are reserved for patients who are a danger to themselves or others
options.B: Scheduled benzodiazepines are first-line therapy
options.C: General anesthesia clearly causes more delirium than epidural anesthesia
options.D: Physical restraints are the preferred first measure

exp: The best treatment for delirium is prevention (orientation, family at bedside, sensory aids, mobility, normal sleep-wake cycle, avoiding dehydration and deliriogenic drugs); low-dose antipsychotics such as haloperidol are reserved for patients who pose a danger to themselves or others. Benzodiazepines are not first-line (B), no association exists with anesthetic route (C), and restraints should be avoided (D).
hy: Delirium: prevent first; low-dose antipsychotic only for dangerous agitation.

stem: Which statement about postoperative aspiration in older adults is correct?

options.A: Incidence rises almost exponentially with age (patients >80 have a 9–10× higher risk than those 18–29); a 3-ounce bedside swallow test and 30–45° upright positioning help reduce risk
options.B: Aspiration risk is independent of age
options.C: Supine positioning reduces aspiration risk
options.D: A 3-ounce swallow test is used to diagnose pneumonia

exp: Postoperative aspiration-pneumonia incidence rises almost exponentially with age — patients >80 have a 9- to 10-fold higher risk than those 18–29 — and a 3-ounce bedside swallow test plus 30–45° upright positioning (during and after meals) helps reduce risk. Aspiration risk is age-dependent (B), supine positioning worsens risk (C), and the swallow test assesses swallowing, not pneumonia (D).
hy: >80 yr → 9–10× aspiration risk; 3-oz swallow test + 30–45° upright.

stem: Which statement about deconditioning from immobility is correct?

options.A: Functional decline can occur after as little as 2 days of immobility, and the period for functional recovery may be up to 3 times longer than the period of immobility
options.B: Functional decline requires at least 2 weeks of bed rest
options.C: Recovery is always faster than the immobility period
options.D: Bed rest has no effect on bone or muscle

exp: Functional decline can occur after as little as 2 days of immobility, and the period needed for functional recovery may be up to 3 times longer than the period of immobility — underscoring the value of early mobilization. The other options understate or deny these effects.
hy: Decline after 2 days' immobility; recovery up to 3× the immobile period.

stem: Which cardiovascular/respiratory effects of bed rest are listed in the chapter?

options.A: Decreased stroke volume and cardiac output with orthostatic hypotension, and decreased respiratory excursion with increased atelectasis potential
options.B: Increased stroke volume and cardiac output
options.C: Improved oxygen uptake and reduced atelectasis
options.D: No measurable cardiovascular effect

exp: Table 13.3 lists decreased stroke volume and cardiac output with orthostatic hypotension (cardiovascular) and decreased respiratory excursion/oxygen uptake with increased atelectasis potential (respiratory). Options B–D reverse or deny these effects.
hy: Bed rest: ↓stroke volume/CO + orthostasis; ↓respiratory excursion + atelectasis.

stem: Beyond the heart and lungs, which musculoskeletal and other effects of bed rest are listed?

options.A: Decreased muscle strength/blood flow, increased bone loss/decreased bone density, constipation, incontinence, and skin breakdown
options.B: Increased muscle strength and bone density
options.C: Improved gastrointestinal motility
options.D: Enhanced skin integrity

exp: Table 13.3 also lists decreased muscle strength and blood flow, increased bone loss with decreased bone density, gastrointestinal effects (malnutrition, anorexia, constipation), genitourinary incontinence, skin breakdown, and psychological effects (isolation, depression, disorientation). Options B–D contradict these.
hy: Bed rest: ↓muscle/bone, constipation, incontinence, skin breakdown.

stem: Which statement about thyroid nodules/cancer in older adults is correct?

options.A: Malignant nodules tend to have more aggressive phenotypes in the elderly (thyroid-cancer–specific mortality ~8%), and outcomes are better when the surgeon's volume exceeds ~30 thyroidectomies/year
options.B: Thyroid cancers are uniformly indolent in the elderly
options.C: Surgeon volume has no effect on outcomes
options.D: Age is a favorable prognostic factor for thyroid cancer

exp: In older adults, thyroid nodules are common but malignant nodules tend to be more aggressive, with thyroid-cancer–specific mortality ~8%; outcomes in complex older patients are better when the surgeon performs >30 thyroidectomies/year. Thyroid cancers are not uniformly indolent (B), surgeon volume matters (C), and age is a negative prognostic factor (D).
hy: Elderly thyroid cancer: more aggressive (~8% mortality); high-volume surgeon better.

stem: In an older outpatient with hypercalcemia, which statement is correct?

options.A: Primary hyperparathyroidism is the most common cause; surgery is indicated for serum calcium >1 mg/dL above normal, 24-h urine calcium >400 mg, CrCl <60 mL/min, or a T-score below −2.5
options.B: Malignancy is the most common cause of outpatient hypercalcemia
options.C: Surgery is never indicated for asymptomatic disease
options.D: Parathyroidectomy requires the calcium to exceed 15 mg/dL

exp: Primary hyperparathyroidism is the most common cause of hypercalcemia in the older outpatient; surgery is indicated for serum calcium >1 mg/dL above normal, 24-hour urine calcium >400 mg, CrCl <60 mL/min, or bone density >2.5 SD below peak (T-score < −2.5). Malignancy (B) is the more common cause in inpatients, asymptomatic disease can warrant surgery (C), and the calcium threshold is not 15 (D).
hy: HPTH surgery: Ca >1 above normal, urine Ca >400, CrCl <60, T-score < −2.5.

stem: How does primary hyperparathyroidism more often present in older adults?

options.A: With nonspecific neuropsychiatric and constitutional symptoms (fatigue, mental impairment, weakness, bone pain) rather than classic kidney stones
options.B: Almost always with symptomatic nephrolithiasis
options.C: With hypocalcemic tetany
options.D: With no symptoms in the great majority of patients

exp: Primary hyperparathyroidism in older adults more often presents with nonspecific neuropsychiatric and constitutional complaints — fatigue, mental/memory impairment, weakness, bone pain — rather than the classic kidney stones, and these symptoms are frequently misattributed to "old age." Tetany (C) reflects hypocalcemia, and truly asymptomatic disease is a minority (D).
hy: Elderly HPTH: neuropsychiatric/constitutional symptoms, not classic stones.

stem: Across the cited series of older parathyroid-surgery patients, which is true of the "asymptomatic" proportion?

options.A: Only a minority are truly asymptomatic (roughly 0%–20% across series), and symptoms are often wrongly attributed to "old age"
options.B: The majority (>80%) are truly asymptomatic
options.C: None are ever asymptomatic
options.D: Asymptomatic disease is more common in the elderly than in younger patients

exp: Across the cited series (Table 13.4), only a minority of older parathyroid-surgery patients are truly asymptomatic (roughly 0%–20%), and apparent "asymptomatic" status often reflects symptoms wrongly attributed to aging. The majority are not asymptomatic (B), some are (C is wrong), and asymptomatic disease is not more common in the elderly (D).
hy: Few elderly HPTH patients are truly asymptomatic (~0–20%).

stem: Which statement about breast cancer in older women is correct?

options.A: A new breast lump is likely malignant; tumors tend to have favorable biology (more hormone-sensitive) yet often present larger/more advanced, and surgical mortality in reasonable health is low (<1%)
options.B: A new breast lump in an older woman is almost always benign
options.C: Older women's tumors are usually hormone-receptor negative and high grade
options.D: Surgical resection is contraindicated in all women over 70

exp: In older women a new breast lump is likely malignant and should be pursued with biopsy; tumors tend to have favorable biology (more hormone-sensitive, lower grade) yet often present larger/more advanced, and surgical mortality in reasonable health is low (<1%). A new lump is not usually benign (B), the biology is favorable not unfavorable (C), and surgery is recommended for most older patients (D).
hy: Older breast lump = likely cancer; favorable biology but later stage; surgery mortality <1%.

stem: Which screening recommendation is correctly attributed?

options.A: USPSTF: biennial screening for women aged 50–74 (insufficient evidence to recommend for/against beyond 74)
options.B: USPSTF: annual screening with no upper age limit
options.C: ACS: screening stops at age 50
options.D: AGS: no screening after age 70 under any circumstances

exp: Per Table 13.5, the USPSTF recommends biennial screening mammography for women aged 50–74, with insufficient evidence to recommend for or against screening beyond age 74. The USPSTF does not recommend annual screening with no upper limit (B); ACS screens from ~45 (C); and AGS supports screening up to 85 with adequate life expectancy (D).
hy: USPSTF: biennial mammography, ages 50–74.

stem: Which screening recommendation is correctly attributed?

options.A: AGS: offer screening up to age 85 in women with a life expectancy ≥5 years (and to healthy women ≥85 with excellent function)
options.B: ACS: stop screening at 45
options.C: USPSTF: screen all women to age 100
options.D: ACR: screening begins only at age 65

exp: Per Table 13.5, the AGS recommends offering screening up to age 85 in women with a life expectancy ≥5 years (and to healthy women ≥85 with excellent functional status). The other attributions are incorrect (ACS continues as long as health/life expectancy ≥10 y; USPSTF stops the clear recommendation at 74).
hy: AGS: screen to 85 if life expectancy ≥5 years.

stem: How does gastroesophageal reflux disease often present in older adults?

options.A: With attenuated or atypical symptoms (dysphagia, anorexia, anemia, weight loss, vomiting) rather than classic heartburn, owing to decreased esophageal pain sensitivity
options.B: With more severe heartburn than younger patients
options.C: Always with classic retrosternal burning
options.D: Without any risk of Barrett esophagus or adenocarcinoma

exp: GERD in older adults often presents with attenuated or atypical symptoms — dysphagia, anorexia, anemia, weight loss, vomiting — rather than classic heartburn, partly from age-related decreased esophageal pain sensitivity; complications (erosive esophagitis, Barrett, adenocarcinoma) are actually more frequent. Older patients tend to have less severe heartburn (B, C wrong), and complications do occur (D wrong).
hy: Elderly GERD: atypical/attenuated symptoms despite more complications.

stem: What is the current management approach to an asymptomatic paraesophageal hernia in an older adult, and the risk if emergency surgery is needed?

options.A: Watchful waiting is generally recommended (low ~1.1%/yr risk of needing emergency surgery); emergency-surgery mortality is ~5.4%–8%
options.B: Immediate repair is mandatory for all asymptomatic hernias
options.C: Emergency repair carries negligible mortality
options.D: These hernias never require surgery

exp: For asymptomatic paraesophageal hernias in older adults, watchful waiting is now generally recommended (only ~1.1% annual probability of needing an emergency operation), and emergency-surgery mortality is ~5.4%–8% (higher with greater frailty). Immediate repair is no longer mandatory (B), emergency mortality is not negligible (C), and some do require surgery (D).
hy: Asymptomatic paraesophageal hernia: watchful waiting; emergency mortality ~5–8%.

stem: Which statement about peptic ulcer disease in older adults is correct?

options.A: Up to 80% of ulcer-related deaths occur in patients >65, and NSAID users have roughly twice the peptic-ulcer mortality of non-users
options.B: NSAIDs are protective against ulcer complications in the elderly
options.C: Peptic ulcer disease decreases in incidence with age
options.D: Patient age alone is the strongest independent predictor of operative mortality for perforated ulcer

exp: Up to 80% of peptic-ulcer-related deaths occur in patients >65, and NSAID users have roughly twice the peptic-ulcer mortality of non-users (80% of ulcer-related deaths are in NSAID users). NSAIDs increase, not protect against, ulcer complications (B); incidence rises with age (C); and age alone is not an independent predictor of operative mortality — concomitant disease, preoperative shock, and >48 h perforation are (D).
hy: 80% of ulcer deaths are >65; NSAIDs double ulcer mortality.

stem: Gallstone obstruction of the small bowel lumen accounts for what fraction of small-bowel obstructions overall, and of obstructions in older adults without a hernia or prior surgery?

options.A: 1%–3% of all SBOs, but up to ~25% of obstructions in patients >65 without a hernia or prior surgery
options.B: The majority of all SBOs at every age
options.C: Less than 0.1% of obstructions in any group
options.D: Equal proportions in young and old patients

exp: Gallstone obstruction of the small bowel ("gallstone ileus") accounts for 1%–3% of all SBOs, but up to ~25% of obstructions in patients >65 who have no abdominal wall hernia or prior surgery. It is not the majority overall (B), not <0.1% (C), and is disproportionately a disease of older adults (D).
hy: Gallstone ileus: 1–3% of all SBOs, up to 25% in elderly without hernia/prior surgery.

stem: Which statement about biliary disease in older adults is correct?

options.A: Biliary tract disease is the single most common cause of acute abdominal complaints in patients >65, and acute cholecystitis often presents atypically (up to ~25% with no tenderness, one-third with no fever or leukocytosis)
options.B: Biliary disease is rare in older adults
options.C: Acute cholecystitis always presents with classic right-upper-quadrant peritonitis in the elderly
options.D: Gallstone prevalence decreases with age

exp: Biliary tract disease is the single most common cause of acute abdominal complaints in patients >65, and acute cholecystitis often presents atypically — up to ~25% have no abdominal tenderness, one-third have no fever or leukocytosis, and many lack peritoneal signs. Biliary disease is common, not rare (B), presentation is often atypical (C), and gallstone prevalence rises with age (D).
hy: Biliary disease = most common acute abdomen >65; cholecystitis often presents atypically.

stem: Which statement about SBO in older adults is correct?

options.A: Adhesions are the most common cause; about 30% of adhesive SBOs ultimately require surgery; and 30-day mortality for malignant (metastatic) obstruction exceeds ~35%
options.B: Hernias are the most common cause in Western countries
options.C: All adhesive SBOs require immediate operation
options.D: Strangulation is reliably identified by fever, tachycardia, and leukocytosis in the elderly

exp: Adhesions cause the substantial majority of SBOs; about 30% of adhesive SBOs ultimately require surgery (most are managed initially nonoperatively with NG decompression and IV fluids); and 30-day operative mortality for malignant (metastatic) obstruction exceeds ~35%. Hernias are second, not first (B); not all adhesive SBOs need surgery (C); and strangulation signs are notoriously misleading in the elderly (D).
hy: SBO: adhesions #1; ~30% of adhesive SBOs need surgery; malignant obstruction mortality >35%.

stem: Which statement about appendicitis in older adults is correct?

options.A: It accounts for 5%–10% of cases; the classic presentation is seen in <20%; and >50% have perforated appendicitis at operation due to delays
options.B: The classic presentation occurs in the majority of older patients
options.C: Perforation is rare in older patients
options.D: The white-count and temperature are reliably elevated in the elderly

exp: Appendicitis accounts for 5%–10% of cases in old age; the classic presentation is seen in <20% of older patients, and delays mean >50% have perforated appendicitis at operation, with high morbidity/mortality. The classic presentation is uncommon (B), perforation is frequent (C), and WBC/temperature are normal in 20%–50% (D).
hy: Elderly appendicitis: classic picture <20%; >50% perforated at operation.

stem: Which statement about colorectal cancer in older adults is correct?

options.A: Most new cases occur in those ≥65; emergency surgery carries threefold-to-fourfold higher mortality than elective; and sphincter-saving coloanal anastomosis can achieve continence in ~80% of selected older patients
options.B: Colorectal cancer is predominantly a disease of the young
options.C: Emergency and elective resections carry identical mortality
options.D: Older patients are over-represented in adjuvant-therapy trials

exp: Most new colorectal cancers occur in those ≥65 (71% of new cases); emergency surgery carries threefold-to-fourfold higher mortality than elective; and sphincter-saving coloanal anastomosis can achieve continence in ~80% of selected older patients. Colorectal cancer is a disease of aging (B), emergency vs elective mortality differ greatly (C), and older patients are under-represented in adjuvant trials (D).
hy: Colorectal cancer: ≥65 in most; emergency surgery 3–4× mortality; coloanal continence ~80%.
src: Sabiston Ch 13, "Carcinoma of the Colon and Rectum"

stem: Which statement about abdominal aortic aneurysm repair in older adults is correct?

options.A: Elective AAA repair mortality is generally <5% at ≥65; EVAR's early perioperative survival advantage is not sustained long-term; and for ruptured AAA, EVAR carries a lower 30-day mortality (~20%) than open repair (~36%)
options.B: Open repair of ruptured AAA carries a mortality under 10%
options.C: EVAR is contraindicated in all patients over 70
options.D: Elective AAA repair mortality exceeds 30% in all older patients

exp: Elective AAA repair mortality is generally <5% in those ≥65; EVAR's early perioperative survival advantage is not sustained beyond a few years; and for ruptured AAA, EVAR carries a lower 30-day mortality (~19.7%) than open repair (~36.3%). Open ruptured-AAA mortality exceeds 50% in older series (B), EVAR is appropriate for suitable older anatomy (C), and elective mortality is not >30% (D).
hy: Elective AAA <5%; ruptured AAA ~20% (EVAR) vs ~36% (open).

stem: In comparing carotid endarterectomy (CEA) and carotid artery stenting (CAS) in older patients, which periprocedural pattern is correct?

options.A: Stenting carries a higher periprocedural stroke risk while endarterectomy carries a higher MI risk; patients >75 have increased stroke risk with stenting (arch calcium/tortuosity)
options.B: Endarterectomy carries a higher stroke risk and stenting a higher MI risk
options.C: Stenting is uniformly safer than endarterectomy in the very elderly
options.D: Age has no bearing on the choice between CEA and CAS

exp: Comparing CEA and CAS, the periprocedural period shows a higher stroke risk with stenting and a higher MI risk with endarterectomy; patients >75 have increased stroke risk with stenting because of greater aortic-arch calcium and tortuosity, so age should factor into the choice. Option B reverses the stroke/MI pattern, C overstates stenting safety, and D denies the age effect.
hy: CAS → more stroke; CEA → more MI; stenting riskier for stroke in patients >75.

stem: What does the minimum alveolar concentration (MAC) of a volatile anesthetic represent?

options.A: The concentration that prevents movement in response to skin incision in 50% of patients — a higher MAC means a less potent agent
options.B: The concentration that produces amnesia in 95% of patients
options.C: The blood concentration at which 50% of patients stop breathing
options.D: The concentration that guarantees no awareness in any patient

exp: MAC is the concentration of volatile agent that prevents movement in response to a skin incision in 50% of patients — a standard measure of potency. A higher MAC denotes a less potent agent (halothane, low MAC, is potent; nitrous oxide, MAC ~104%, is weak). It does not define amnesia, apnea, or guaranteed lack of awareness.
hy: MAC = conc. preventing movement in 50%; higher MAC = less potent.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Which set of MAC values is correct?

options.A: Halothane ~0.75% (most potent), desflurane ~6%, nitrous oxide ~104%
options.B: Halothane ~6%, desflurane ~0.75%, nitrous oxide ~1%
options.C: All potent volatile agents have a MAC near 50%
options.D: Nitrous oxide has the lowest MAC of any agent

exp: Halothane is the most potent modern volatile agent (MAC ~0.75%), desflurane the least potent hydrocarbon agent (MAC ~6%), and nitrous oxide has a MAC of ~104% (so it cannot reach 1 MAC at sea level alone). The other options invert or flatten these values.
hy: MAC: halothane 0.75%, desflurane 6%, N₂O 104%.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: How does the blood/gas solubility coefficient relate to the speed of induction and emergence?

options.A: Lower-solubility agents (e.g., nitrous oxide, desflurane) give faster induction and emergence; higher-solubility agents (e.g., halothane) are slower
options.B: Higher-solubility agents give faster induction and emergence
options.C: Solubility has no effect on induction speed
options.D: Only potency, not solubility, determines induction speed

exp: Less-soluble agents (lower blood/gas coefficient), such as nitrous oxide and desflurane, equilibrate quickly and give faster induction and emergence, whereas highly soluble agents like halothane are slower; isoflurane and sevoflurane are intermediate. Option B reverses the relationship.
hy: Low blood solubility → fast induction/emergence.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Which agents are suitable for inhalational (mask) induction because of low pungency, and which is not?

options.A: Sevoflurane and halothane are suitable; desflurane is pungent/irritating and unsuitable
options.B: Desflurane is the preferred mask-induction agent
options.C: Isoflurane is the least pungent and ideal for inhalational induction
options.D: Nitrous oxide alone is sufficient for inhalational induction of anesthesia

exp: Low-pungency agents — sevoflurane and halothane — do not irritate the airway and are suitable for inhalational induction, whereas desflurane (highly irritating) and isoflurane (pungent) are unsuitable. Nitrous oxide alone is too weak (MAC 104%) for induction/maintenance.
hy: Sevoflurane/halothane suit mask induction; desflurane/isoflurane are pungent.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Which inhalational agent is associated with marked dose-dependent decreases in blood pressure and cardiac output and marked sensitization of the myocardium to catecholamines?

options.A: Halothane
options.B: Nitrous oxide
options.C: Isoflurane
options.D: Sevoflurane

exp: Halothane causes marked dose-dependent decreases in blood pressure and cardiac output and markedly sensitizes the myocardium to catecholamines (arrhythmogenic). Nitrous oxide has little hemodynamic effect, and isoflurane/sevoflurane cause only minimal catecholamine sensitization.
hy: Halothane: marked BP/CO drop + catecholamine sensitization.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Which statement about desflurane is correct?

options.A: A rapid increase in inspired concentration causes tachycardia and hypertension, and its pungency precludes inhalational induction
options.B: It markedly lowers heart rate at all concentrations
options.C: Its low pungency makes it ideal for mask induction
options.D: It is the most potent of the volatile agents

exp: Desflurane is associated with tachycardia and hypertension when the inspired concentration is increased too rapidly, and its pungent odor precludes inhalational induction; it must be delivered through a specialized heated vaporizer. It does not lower heart rate at all concentrations (B), is unsuitable for mask induction (C), and is the least (not most) potent volatile agent (D).
hy: Desflurane: rapid ↑concentration → tachycardia/HTN; pungent, heated vaporizer.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Nitrous oxide is contraindicated in which setting because it diffuses into closed gas spaces faster than nitrogen diffuses out?

options.A: Pneumothorax, small bowel obstruction, middle ear surgery, or an intraocular gas bubble
options.B: Hypertension and tachycardia
options.C: Asthma and reactive airway disease
options.D: Coronary artery disease

exp: Because nitrous oxide is ~30× more soluble than nitrogen and diffuses into closed gas spaces faster than nitrogen leaves, it expands them — so it is contraindicated with a pneumothorax, small bowel obstruction, middle ear surgery, or an intraocular gas bubble. The other settings are not the closed-gas-space contraindication.
hy: Avoid N₂O with closed gas spaces (PTX, SBO, middle ear, intraocular gas).
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Why is nitrous oxide unsuitable as a sole anesthetic for maintenance of general anesthesia?

options.A: Its MAC is ~104%, so it lacks sufficient potency to be used alone at atmospheric pressure
options.B: It is too soluble in blood, giving very slow induction
options.C: It causes profound myocardial depression
options.D: It is highly pungent and irritating to the airway

exp: Nitrous oxide's MAC is ~104% of inspired gas at sea level, so it cannot reach a full anesthetic concentration alone and is combined with potent volatile agents. It is actually low (not high) in blood solubility (giving rapid kinetics), does not markedly depress the myocardium, and is not pungent.
hy: N₂O MAC ~104% → insufficient potency alone.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Which set of properties describes propofol as an IV induction agent?

options.A: Dose ~1–2 mg/kg; causes hypotension and pain on injection; good bronchodilator; low incidence of postoperative nausea/vomiting
options.B: Raises blood pressure and heart rate; ideal for hypovolemic shock
options.C: Causes adrenal suppression with continuous infusion
options.D: Is the most hemodynamically stable agent, preferred in cardiac dysfunction

exp: Propofol (induction dose ~1–2 mg/kg) is a short-acting agent associated with smooth, low-nausea emergence and good bronchodilation, but it causes pain on injection and blood-pressure reduction (use caution in hypovolemia/severe CAD). It does not raise BP/HR (B), is not the agent that suppresses the adrenal (C — that is etomidate), and is not the most hemodynamically stable (D — that is etomidate/midazolam).
hy: Propofol: 1–2 mg/kg, hypotension + injection pain, bronchodilator, low PONV.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Which statement about ketamine is correct?

options.A: It is the only IV induction agent that raises blood pressure/heart rate and increases cerebral blood flow/ICP; it is a bronchodilator but can cause emergence delirium
options.B: It reliably lowers blood pressure and is ideal for coronary artery disease
options.C: It decreases cerebral blood flow and is preferred in traumatic brain injury
options.D: It causes adrenal suppression and myoclonus

exp: Ketamine produces dissociative anesthesia and is the only IV induction agent that raises blood pressure and heart rate (via sympathetic tone) and increases cerebral blood flow/ICP; it is a bronchodilator and potent analgesic but causes emergence delirium (mitigated by benzodiazepines). It does not lower BP (B), increases CBF/ICP rather than decreasing it (C), and adrenal suppression/myoclonus describe etomidate (D).
hy: Ketamine: ↑BP/HR, ↑ICP/CBF, bronchodilator, emergence delirium.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Which feature makes etomidate a common choice for inducing patients with cardiac contractile dysfunction or hypovolemia, and what is its notable drawback?

options.A: It is hemodynamically stable but causes adrenal suppression (with continuous infusion), pain on injection, and myoclonus
options.B: It markedly lowers blood pressure but provides potent analgesia
options.C: It raises heart rate and is contraindicated in shock
options.D: It produces profound amnesia with no cardiovascular effects

exp: Etomidate produces minimal hemodynamic change and preserves blood pressure, making it useful for inducing patients with cardiac contractile dysfunction or hypovolemia; its drawbacks are burning injection pain, myoclonus, and adrenal suppression (notably with continuous infusion). It does not lower BP markedly (B), is used in shock (C), and does have cardiovascular-sparing but not "no" effects (D).
hy: Etomidate: hemodynamically stable; adrenal suppression, myoclonus, injection pain.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Which statement about thiopental is correct?

options.A: Dose ~2–5 mg/kg; rapid emergence after a single dose is due to redistribution; in hypovolemia/heart failure it can cause severe hypotension
options.B: It raises blood pressure and is the agent of choice in hemorrhagic shock
options.C: It is a potent bronchodilator preferred in asthmatics
options.D: It causes adrenal suppression with single doses

exp: Thiopental (induction dose ~2–5 mg/kg) produces rapid, pleasant induction; after a single dose, rapid emergence results from redistribution from brain to peripheral tissues, and only ~10%/hour is metabolized hepatically. In hypovolemia/heart failure it causes vasodilation and cardiac depression leading to severe hypotension. It does not raise BP (B), is not a preferred bronchodilator (C), and single doses don't cause adrenal suppression (D).
hy: Thiopental 2–5 mg/kg; emergence by redistribution; hypotension if hypovolemic.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Why is midazolam sometimes chosen for induction, and what is a key interaction?

options.A: It gives relatively stable hemodynamics and potent amnesia, but causes synergistic ventilatory depression with opioids
options.B: It raises blood pressure and heart rate
options.C: It has no amnestic effect
options.D: It is contraindicated for premedication of anxious patients

exp: Midazolam offers relatively stable hemodynamics and potent amnesia, which is why it is used for cardiovascular induction and premedication; however, it produces synergistic ventilatory depression when combined with opioids. It does not raise BP/HR (B), does have amnestic effect (C), and is commonly used to premedicate anxious patients (D).
hy: Midazolam: stable hemodynamics + amnesia; synergistic respiratory depression with opioids.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Which statement about opioids in anesthesia is correct?

options.A: Fentanyl is ~100–150× more potent than morphine and reduces the MAC of volatile agents; remifentanil is ultra-short-acting because it is metabolized by serum esterases
options.B: Opioids reliably provide hypnosis and amnesia as sole agents at analgesic doses
options.C: Fentanyl is less potent than morphine
options.D: Remifentanil accumulates markedly during prolonged infusions

exp: Fentanyl is ~100–150× more potent than morphine and (like other opioids) reduces the MAC of volatile agents; remifentanil is ultra-short-acting because it is hydrolyzed by nonspecific serum esterases and does not accumulate with prolonged infusion. Opioids give inconsistent hypnosis/amnesia at analgesic doses (B), fentanyl is more potent than morphine (C), and remifentanil does not accumulate (D).
hy: Fentanyl ~100–150× morphine; remifentanil = esterase-metabolized, non-accumulating.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Which statement about succinylcholine is correct?

options.A: It is a depolarizing agent with rapid onset (~60–90 s) and short duration (~5 min), making it useful for rapid-sequence intubation
options.B: It is a nondepolarizing competitive antagonist
options.C: Its duration of action is ~30 minutes
options.D: It is reversed by neostigmine

exp: Succinylcholine is the only depolarizing agent in use; its rapid onset (~60–90 s) and short duration (~5 min, from plasma pseudocholinesterase metabolism) make it ideal for rapid-sequence intubation. It is not a nondepolarizing antagonist (B), its duration is ~5 min not 30 (C), and it is not reversed by neostigmine (D — anticholinesterase would prolong, not reverse, depolarizing block).
hy: Succinylcholine: depolarizing, onset 60–90 s, duration ~5 min, for RSI.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Which adverse effects are associated with succinylcholine?

options.A: Life-threatening hyperkalemia (burns, paraplegia/quadriplegia, massive trauma), malignant hyperthermia triggering, and bradycardia (especially in children)
options.B: Adrenal suppression and myoclonus
options.C: Prolonged paralysis reversible only by dialysis
options.D: Hypertension and tachycardia as the main risks

exp: Succinylcholine can cause life-threatening hyperkalemia in patients with burns, paraplegia/quadriplegia, or massive trauma (upregulated receptors), can trigger malignant hyperthermia, and commonly causes bradycardia (especially in children) and masseter spasm/myalgias. Adrenal suppression/myoclonus (B) describe etomidate, dialysis-reversal (C) is wrong, and hypertension/tachycardia (D) are not its hallmark risks.
hy: Succinylcholine: hyperkalemia (burns/paralysis/trauma), MH trigger, bradycardia in kids.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Which nondepolarizing muscle relaxant is best suited to a patient with significant renal impairment, and why?

options.A: Cisatracurium, because it is largely eliminated by Hofmann degradation in plasma rather than by the kidney
options.B: Pancuronium, because it is renally cleared
options.C: Vecuronium, because it depends on renal excretion
options.D: Succinylcholine, because it is a nondepolarizer

exp: Cisatracurium is largely metabolized in serum by Hofmann degradation, making it well suited to patients with renal (and hepatic) impairment, whereas pancuronium and vecuronium are partly renally eliminated and would be unsuitable. Succinylcholine is a depolarizer, not a nondepolarizer (D).
hy: Cisatracurium (Hofmann elimination) is best in renal failure.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Regarding nondepolarizing relaxant dosing, which principle is correct?

options.A: Higher doses are required to provide intubating conditions than for surgical relaxation, and potent inhalational agents potentiate their action
options.B: Surgical relaxation requires higher doses than intubation
options.C: Inhalational agents antagonize nondepolarizers
options.D: Chemical paralysis reliably indicates adequate depth of anesthesia

exp: Higher doses of nondepolarizers are needed to provide intubating conditions than for surgical relaxation, and potent inhalational agents dose-dependently potentiate their action (succinylcholine pre-use also lowers subsequent requirements). Surgical relaxation needs less than intubation (B), inhalational agents potentiate rather than antagonize (C), and chemical paralysis can mask inadequate anesthetic depth (D).
hy: Intubating doses > surgical-relaxation doses; volatiles potentiate nondepolarizers.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: How are nondepolarizing neuromuscular blockers classically pharmacologically reversed?

options.A: With an anticholinesterase (neostigmine or edrophonium) plus atropine or glycopyrrolate to counter the muscarinic effects
options.B: With additional succinylcholine
options.C: With naloxone
options.D: With a benzodiazepine

exp: Nondepolarizing blockers are classically reversed with an anticholinesterase (neostigmine or edrophonium) given with atropine or glycopyrrolate to block the muscarinic effects of increased acetylcholine. Additional succinylcholine (B), naloxone (C, for opioids), and benzodiazepines (D) do not reverse nondepolarizing block.
hy: Reverse nondepolarizers: anticholinesterase + antimuscarinic (atropine/glycopyrrolate).
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: On neuromuscular monitoring, what train-of-four (TOF) ratio signifies clinically significant residual blockade, and why does it matter?

options.A: A TOF ratio less than 0.9 indicates residual blockade, which is associated with postoperative respiratory complications and delayed PACU discharge
options.B: A TOF ratio greater than 0.9 indicates residual blockade
options.C: A TOF ratio of 0.5 is the target for safe extubation
options.D: The TOF ratio has no relationship to respiratory complications

exp: A train-of-four ratio below 0.9 signifies clinically significant residual neuromuscular blockade, which is associated with increased postoperative respiratory complications and delayed PACU discharge; adequate recovery requires a TOF ratio >0.9 (ideally 1.0). Option B inverts the threshold, 0.5 is inadequate (C), and the relationship to complications is real (D).
hy: TOF ratio <0.9 = residual blockade → respiratory complications.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: What does the bispectral index (BIS) monitor, and what is its scale?

options.A: A processed EEG estimating anesthetic depth/awareness on a 0–100 scale (100 = awake, 0 = complete suppression); it can reduce intraoperative awareness in high-risk patients
options.B: End-tidal CO₂ on a 0–100 scale
options.C: Neuromuscular blockade depth
options.D: Oxygen saturation

exp: The bispectral index is a processed EEG that estimates anesthetic depth/awareness on a 0–100 scale (100 = fully awake, 0 = complete cortical suppression); meta-analysis shows it can reduce intraoperative awareness in high-risk patients, and BIS <40 for >5 minutes may be associated with increased morbidity. It does not measure ETCO₂ (B), neuromuscular blockade (C), or oxygen saturation (D).
hy: BIS = processed EEG, 0–100, reduces awareness in high-risk patients.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Which factor is associated with a higher rate of medicolegal claims for intraoperative awareness?

options.A: The use of intraoperative muscle relaxants
options.B: The use of inhalational rather than IV agents
options.C: Regional anesthesia
options.D: Use of the bispectral index monitor

exp: Medicolegal claims of intraoperative awareness were more than twice as frequent in patients receiving intraoperative muscle relaxants, because chemical paralysis masks the movement that would otherwise signal light anesthesia. Agent choice (B), regional anesthesia (C), and BIS use (D) are not the cited association.
hy: Muscle relaxants raise awareness claims (paralysis masks light anesthesia).
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: On the Mallampati classification of the airway, which finding predicts a more difficult intubation?

options.A: A class IV airway (only the hard palate visible) predicts greater difficulty than class I (soft palate, uvula, and pillars visible)
options.B: A class I airway predicts the most difficult intubation
options.C: The classification is performed with the patient supine and mouth closed
options.D: Class IV means the uvula and pillars are fully visible

exp: On the Mallampati classification, a class IV airway (only the hard palate visible) predicts greater difficulty with mask ventilation/intubation, whereas class I (soft palate, uvula, and pillars visible) predicts ease. It is performed sitting with the mouth open and tongue protruded (C is wrong), and class IV does not show the uvula (D). It is only one component of airway assessment.
hy: Mallampati IV (hard palate only) = harder airway; class I = easiest.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Which physical measurements are part of a reassuring airway examination?

options.A: Mouth opening and thyromental distance of ~6–8 cm (three to four fingerbreadths), with good cervical mobility
options.B: A thyromental distance under 3 cm
options.C: Mouth opening of 1–2 cm
options.D: A single physical finding reliably predicts airway difficulty

exp: A reassuring airway exam includes a mouth opening and thyromental distance of ~6–8 cm (three to four fingerbreadths) with good cervical mobility and adequate neck extension; single findings have poor predictive value, but combining ≥2 predictors improves sensitivity/specificity. The very small measurements (B, C) indicate difficulty, and a single finding is unreliable (D).
hy: Reassuring airway: mouth opening/thyromental ~6–8 cm; combine predictors.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: A patient with congestive heart failure and unstable angina — severe systemic disease that is a constant threat to life — is which ASA physical status class?

options.A: ASA IV
options.B: ASA II
options.C: ASA III
options.D: ASA V

exp: ASA IV is "severe systemic disease that is a constant threat to life," exemplified by congestive heart failure and unstable angina. ASA II is mild systemic disease, ASA III is severe disease with functional impairment, and ASA V is moribund.
hy: ASA IV = severe disease, constant threat to life (CHF, unstable angina).
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Which description correctly matches an ASA class?

options.A: ASA III — severe systemic disease with functional impairment (e.g., diabetes with vascular complications, prior MI)
options.B: ASA II — a moribund patient not expected to survive
options.C: ASA I — severe systemic disease that is a constant threat to life
options.D: ASA V — mild systemic disease with no functional limitation

exp: ASA III is severe systemic disease producing functional impairment (e.g., diabetes with vascular complications, previous MI, uncontrolled hypertension). ASA II (not II = moribund — B is wrong) is mild disease without limitation; ASA I is a normal healthy patient (C wrong); ASA V is moribund (D wrong).
hy: ASA III = severe systemic disease with functional impairment.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: What is the approximate risk of cardiac arrest attributable to anesthesia, and overall surgical mortality in developed nations?

options.A: Anesthesia-attributable cardiac arrest <1 in 10,000 cases; surgical mortality ~0.4%–0.8%
options.B: Anesthesia-attributable cardiac arrest ~1 in 100 cases; surgical mortality ~20%
options.C: Anesthesia-attributable cardiac arrest ~1 in 50; surgical mortality ~10%
options.D: Both are essentially zero in modern practice

exp: Anesthesia-attributable cardiac arrest occurs in fewer than 1 in 10,000 cases, and in developed nations overall surgical mortality is ~0.4%–0.8% (morbidity ~3%–17%). The much higher figures (B, C) are incorrect, and risk is low but not zero (D).
hy: Anesthesia cardiac arrest <1:10,000; surgical mortality ~0.4–0.8%.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: In the perioperative literature, how is poor functional capacity defined, and why does it matter?

options.A: Poor capacity is <4 METs; patients unable to perform 4 METs of work have increased perioperative cardiac and long-term risk
options.B: Poor capacity is <10 METs
options.C: Functional capacity has no bearing on perioperative risk
options.D: Excellent capacity is defined as <4 METs

exp: Functional capacity is classified as excellent (>10 METs), good (7–10), moderate (4–6), or poor (<4); patients unable to perform 4 METs of daily work have increased perioperative cardiac and long-term risk. Poor capacity is <4 (not <10 — B), capacity does matter (C), and excellent is >10 not <4 (D).
hy: Poor functional capacity <4 METs → increased perioperative risk.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Which patient-associated factor carries the highest relative risk for postoperative pulmonary complications in the cited analysis?

options.A: ASA class greater than II (relative risk ~4.9)
options.B: Obesity (relative risk ~1.3)
options.C: Current smoking (relative risk ~1.3)
options.D: COPD (relative risk ~1.8)

exp: In the cited ACP analysis (Table 14.6), ASA class greater than II carries the highest patient-associated relative risk for postoperative pulmonary complications (~4.9); CHF (~2.9), age >60 (~2.1–3.0), COPD (~1.8), and smoking/obesity (~1.3) are lower.
hy: ASA >II is the top patient pulmonary-risk factor (RR ~4.9).
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Which procedure-associated factors increase postoperative pulmonary complication risk?

options.A: Surgery lasting >3 hours, emergency surgery, and general anesthesia
options.B: Laparoscopic approach and regional anesthesia
options.C: Short operative time and elective scheduling
options.D: Use of a bispectral index monitor

exp: Procedure-associated pulmonary-complication risk factors are surgery lasting >3 hours (RR ~2.1), emergency surgery (~2.2), and general anesthesia (~1.8). Laparoscopic/regional approaches and short elective cases are not risk factors (B, C), and BIS use is irrelevant (D).
hy: Procedure pulmonary risks: surgery >3 h, emergency, general anesthesia.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Per the ASA preoperative fasting recommendations, which minimum fasting interval is correct?

options.A: Clear liquids 2 hours, breast milk 4 hours, infant formula/nonhuman milk/solid food 6 hours
options.B: Clear liquids 6 hours, breast milk 2 hours
options.C: Solid food 2 hours, clear liquids 8 hours
options.D: Breast milk 8 hours, clear liquids 4 hours

exp: The ASA fasting guidelines specify minimum fasts of 2 hours for clear liquids, 4 hours for breast milk, and 6 hours for infant formula, nonhuman milk, and solid food. The other options scramble these intervals.
hy: Fasting: clear 2 h, breast milk 4 h, formula/nonhuman milk/solids 6 h.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Which statement about preoperative fasting is correct?

options.A: Clear liquids are those you can see through without solids/particulates (water, pulp-free juice, black coffee, clear tea, carbonated drinks), and routine GI stimulants/antacids are not recommended
options.B: Milk and orange juice count as clear liquids
options.C: A minimum fast of 12 hours is required for clear liquids
options.D: Routine prokinetics are recommended for all patients

exp: Clear liquids are defined as liquids you can see through without solids/particulates — water, pulp-free fruit juice, black coffee, clear tea, carbonated beverages — and routine GI stimulants, acid blockers, antacids, and antiemetics are not recommended for healthy patients. Milk/orange juice are not clear liquids (B), the clear-liquid fast is 2 h not 12 (C), and routine prokinetics are not advised (D).
hy: Clear liquids = see-through, particulate-free; no routine prokinetics.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: For a patient at highest risk undergoing a dental procedure involving gingival manipulation, what is the recommended oral antibiotic regimen?

options.A: Amoxicillin 2 g orally, single dose 30–60 minutes before the procedure
options.B: Vancomycin 1 g orally daily for 7 days
options.C: Penicillin 500 mg four times daily for 10 days
options.D: No antibiotic is ever indicated for dental procedures

exp: For a highest-risk patient undergoing a dental procedure with gingival manipulation, the recommended regimen is amoxicillin 2 g orally as a single dose 30–60 minutes before the procedure. Prolonged courses (B, C) are not used, and prophylaxis is indicated for highest-risk patients (D).
hy: Dental endocarditis prophylaxis: amoxicillin 2 g, single dose 30–60 min before.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Which cardiac condition is in the highest-risk group for which endocarditis prophylaxis before dental procedures is reasonable?

options.A: A prosthetic cardiac valve (or prosthetic material used for valve repair), previous infective endocarditis, or unrepaired cyanotic congenital heart disease
options.B: Isolated mitral valve prolapse
options.C: A repaired atrial septal defect after 1 year with no residual defect
options.D: Routine genitourinary or gastrointestinal procedures in any patient

exp: The highest-risk cardiac conditions for which dental prophylaxis is reasonable include a prosthetic valve (or prosthetic repair material), previous infective endocarditis, unrepaired cyanotic CHD (including palliative shunts/conduits), repaired CHD with prosthetic material during the first 6 months, repaired CHD with a residual defect at/adjacent to a prosthesis, and transplant valvulopathy. Isolated MVP (B) and a fully repaired ASD after a year (C) are not high-risk, and prophylaxis is not recommended for routine GI/GU procedures (D).
hy: Prophylaxis for: prosthetic valve, prior IE, unrepaired cyanotic CHD, recent prosthetic repair, transplant valvulopathy.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Which statement reflects the ACC/AHA perioperative cardiac medication recommendations?

options.A: Continue chronic beta blockers and statins; do not start beta blockers on the day of surgery; delay elective surgery 14 days after balloon angioplasty, 30 days after a bare-metal stent, and 365 days after a drug-eluting stent
options.B: Start beta blockers on the morning of surgery in all patients
options.C: Stop statins before all surgery
options.D: Coronary revascularization is recommended solely to reduce perioperative cardiac events

exp: The ACC/AHA recommendations are to continue chronic beta blockers and statins, not to start beta blockers on the day of surgery, and to delay elective noncardiac surgery 14 days after balloon angioplasty, 30 days after a bare-metal stent, and 365 days after a drug-eluting stent. Starting beta blockers the morning of surgery (B), stopping statins (C), and revascularizing solely to reduce perioperative events (D) are all contrary to the guideline.
hy: Continue beta blockers/statins; don't start beta blockers day-of; stent delays 14/30/365 d.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Which maximal-dose pairing for major nerve blocks (without epinephrine) is correct?

options.A: Lidocaine 5 mg/kg (7–8 mg/kg with epinephrine); bupivacaine 3 mg/kg
options.B: Lidocaine 1 mg/kg; bupivacaine 10 mg/kg
options.C: Bupivacaine 7 mg/kg; lidocaine 1 mg/kg
options.D: All local anesthetics share a maximal dose of 10 mg/kg

exp: For major nerve blocks, the maximal dose (without epinephrine) of lidocaine is ~5 mg/kg (increasable to 7–8 mg/kg with epinephrine) and of bupivacaine ~3 mg/kg; mepivacaine matches lidocaine and ropivacaine matches bupivacaine. The other pairings are incorrect, and there is no universal 10 mg/kg limit.
hy: Lidocaine 5 mg/kg (7–8 w/ epi); bupivacaine 3 mg/kg.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: How do lidocaine and bupivacaine differ in onset and duration for major nerve blocks?

options.A: Lidocaine has a faster onset (~10–20 min) and shorter duration (~60–180 min); bupivacaine has slower onset (~15–30 min) and longer duration (~180–360 min)
options.B: Bupivacaine has the fastest onset and shortest duration
options.C: Both have identical onset and duration
options.D: Lidocaine lasts longer than bupivacaine

exp: Lidocaine has a faster onset (~10–20 min) and shorter duration (~60–180 min), whereas bupivacaine has a slower onset (~15–30 min) and a much longer duration (~180–360 min), reflecting its higher lipid solubility/protein binding. Option B reverses this and the others are wrong.
hy: Lidocaine: fast/short; bupivacaine: slow/long.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: What is the typical progression of local anesthetic systemic toxicity (LAST)?

options.A: Early perioral numbness/tingling, metallic taste, tinnitus, and visual changes, progressing to slurred speech, seizures, and finally cardiovascular collapse
options.B: Cardiovascular collapse occurs first, before any CNS symptoms
options.C: The first sign is always a fixed dilated pupil
options.D: Toxicity manifests only as a localized skin rash

exp: LAST classically begins with CNS excitation — perioral numbness/tingling, metallic taste, tinnitus, visual disturbance, light-headedness — progressing to slurred speech, disorientation, seizures, and finally cardiovascular collapse (bupivacaine is especially cardiotoxic). Cardiovascular collapse is a late, not first, sign (B), and the other descriptions are incorrect.
hy: LAST: perioral numbness/metallic taste/tinnitus → seizures → CV collapse.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: What is the recommended lipid-emulsion regimen for severe local anesthetic systemic toxicity, alongside airway/oxygen support?

options.A: A 20% lipid emulsion bolus of 1.5 mL/kg (lean body mass) over 1 minute, followed by an infusion of 0.25 mL/kg/min
options.B: A 5% dextrose bolus of 10 mL/kg
options.C: Intravenous bupivacaine to stabilize membranes
options.D: Naloxone 0.4 mg IV

exp: For severe LAST, alongside airway and oxygen support (and benzodiazepines for seizures), the recommended 20% lipid-emulsion regimen is a bolus of 1.5 mL/kg (lean body mass) over 1 minute followed by an infusion of 0.25 mL/kg/min, with the bolus repeatable and infusion doubled for refractory toxicity. The other options are incorrect.
hy: LAST lipid rescue: 1.5 mL/kg 20% bolus, then 0.25 mL/kg/min.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: What are the two classes of local anesthetics and their mechanism of action?

options.A: Aminoesters and aminoamides, which act by dose-dependent blockade of sodium channels in nerve fibers
options.B: Agonists and antagonists, acting on potassium channels
options.C: Depolarizing and nondepolarizing, acting at the neuromuscular junction
options.D: Esters and amides, acting by calcium-channel blockade

exp: Local anesthetics fall into two classes — aminoesters and aminoamides — and act by dose-dependent blockade of voltage-gated sodium channels in nerve fibers. They are not agonist/antagonist potassium-channel drugs (B), are not neuromuscular agents (C), and do not act by calcium-channel blockade (D).
hy: Local anesthetics = esters/amides; block sodium channels.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: How does baricity influence the spread of a subarachnoid block?

options.A: Hyperbaric solutions (e.g., local anesthetic in dextrose) sink with gravity, hypobaric solutions rise, and isobaric (plain) solutions stay near the injection level
options.B: Hyperbaric solutions rise against gravity
options.C: Baricity has no effect on block spread
options.D: Isobaric solutions always ascend within the CSF

exp: Baricity governs spread within CSF: hyperbaric solutions (local anesthetic in dextrose) sink with gravity, hypobaric solutions (prepared in water) rise, and isobaric (plain) solutions remain near the injection level — so patient position determines block level. Option B reverses hyperbaric behavior, C denies the effect, and D misstates isobaric behavior.
hy: Hyperbaric sinks, hypobaric rises, isobaric stays — position matters.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: For subarachnoid block, how do the durations of lidocaine and bupivacaine/tetracaine compare?

options.A: Lidocaine is short-acting (~30–60 min) while bupivacaine and tetracaine last significantly longer (~75–200 min)
options.B: Lidocaine lasts the longest of the three
options.C: All three have identical durations
options.D: Tetracaine has the shortest duration

exp: For subarachnoid block, lidocaine is short-acting (~30–60 min) while bupivacaine and tetracaine have significantly longer durations (~75–200 min). Option B/C/D misstate the relative durations.
hy: Spinal: lidocaine ~30–60 min; bupivacaine/tetracaine ~75–200 min.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Which factors increase the cephalad level of a spinal anesthetic?

options.A: Higher dose/volume, baricity and patient position, and conditions that reduce subarachnoid space volume (obesity, pregnancy, increased intraabdominal pressure)
options.B: Only the brand of needle used
options.C: Lower body temperature alone
options.D: The patient's hair color

exp: The cephalad level of a spinal block is increased by higher dose/volume, baricity together with patient position, and anatomic/physiologic factors that reduce subarachnoid-space volume — obesity, pregnancy, increased intraabdominal pressure, prior spine surgery, and abnormal curvature (elderly patients are also more sensitive). Needle brand (B), temperature (C), and hair color (D) are irrelevant.
hy: Higher spinal level: ↑dose/volume, baricity+position, ↓CSF-space states (obesity/pregnancy).
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Which is a recognized complication of subarachnoid block?

options.A: Hypotension from sympathectomy (responsive to fluids/ephedrine), bradycardia, postdural puncture headache, and urinary retention
options.B: Hypertensive crisis from sympathetic surge
options.C: Hyperthermia and tachycardia
options.D: Immediate respiratory alkalosis

exp: Complications of subarachnoid block include hypotension from sympathectomy (usually responsive to fluids and small pressor doses such as ephedrine), bradycardia, postdural puncture headache, transient radicular neuropathy, backache, and urinary retention. It causes hypotension, not hypertension (B), and the other descriptions are incorrect.
hy: Spinal complications: hypotension (sympathectomy), bradycardia, PDPH, urinary retention.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Which factors increase the incidence of postdural puncture headache?

options.A: Female gender, younger age, and larger needle size
options.B: Male gender and older age
options.C: Smaller (pencil-point) needles
options.D: Use of epidural rather than spinal technique always prevents it

exp: Postdural puncture headache is more common with female gender, younger age, and larger needle size. Male gender/older age (B), smaller pencil-point needles (C, which reduce risk), and the claim that epidural always prevents it (D, an inadvertent dural puncture leaves a larger rent) are all incorrect.
hy: PDPH risk: female, younger, larger needle.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Which statement about epidural anesthesia/analgesia is correct?

options.A: Thoracic epidural analgesia provides superior pain control and pulmonary function than parenteral opioids and is associated with decreased postoperative pneumonia
options.B: Epidural analgesia worsens pulmonary function compared with parenteral opioids
options.C: Lumbar epidural is superior to thoracic for upper-abdominal/thoracic pain
options.D: Epidural anesthesia has no effect on postoperative pneumonia rates

exp: Thoracic epidural analgesia provides superior pain control, less sedation, and better pulmonary function than parenteral opioids and is associated with a decreased incidence of postoperative pneumonia (and, in some analyses, lower 0–30-day mortality). It does not worsen pulmonary function (B), thoracic (not lumbar) epidural speeds GI recovery for upper-abdominal/thoracic surgery (C), and it does affect pneumonia rates (D).
hy: Thoracic epidural: better pain/pulmonary function, less pneumonia.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Which drug class and example are correctly paired for treating postoperative nausea and vomiting?

options.A: Serotonin (5-HT₃) receptor antagonist — ondansetron; corticosteroid — dexamethasone; anticholinergic — scopolamine
options.B: Beta blocker — metoprolol
options.C: Loop diuretic — furosemide
options.D: Calcium-channel blocker — nicardipine

exp: Effective antiemetic classes for PONV include serotonin (5-HT₃) receptor antagonists (ondansetron, dolasetron), corticosteroids (dexamethasone), antidopaminergics (phenothiazines, butyrophenones, metoclopramide), antihistamines, and anticholinergics (scopolamine). Beta blockers, loop diuretics, and calcium-channel blockers (B, C, D) are not antiemetics.
hy: PONV agents: 5-HT₃ antagonists, dexamethasone, antidopaminergics, antihistamines, scopolamine.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Which statement about antiemetic agents is correct?

options.A: Droperidol carries an FDA "black box" warning for QT prolongation requiring ECG monitoring, and dexamethasone can cause glucose intolerance
options.B: Ondansetron's main side effect is extrapyramidal dystonia
options.C: Dexamethasone is contraindicated for PONV
options.D: Antihistamines such as diphenhydramine cause tachycardia, not sedation

exp: Droperidol carries an FDA black-box warning for QT prolongation/arrhythmia requiring ECG monitoring before and after administration, and dexamethasone (a corticosteroid antiemetic) can cause glucose intolerance. Ondansetron's main effect is headache, not dystonia (B); dexamethasone is used for PONV (C); and antihistamines cause sedation, not tachycardia (D).
hy: Droperidol = QT black box; dexamethasone → glucose intolerance.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: What is the recommended approach for patients at high risk of postoperative nausea and vomiting?

options.A: A multimodal approach combining agents from different classes, and use of propofol (which lowers PONV) for induction
options.B: A single high-dose antiemetic for all patients
options.C: Routine deep neuromuscular blockade
options.D: Avoiding antiemetics to prevent QT effects

exp: For patients at high risk of PONV (or with a prior history), a multimodal approach combining agents from different mechanistic classes is most effective, and using propofol for induction (which lowers PONV) helps. A single agent for everyone (B), routine deep paralysis (C), and avoiding antiemetics (D) are not recommended.
hy: High-risk PONV: multimodal antiemetics + propofol.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: How is the perioperative-normothermia performance metric defined, and why does hypothermia matter?

options.A: Core temperature ≥36°C within 30 minutes of PACU arrival (for cases >1 hour); shivering increases oxygen consumption and can trigger myocardial ischemia
options.B: Core temperature ≥38°C at the end of surgery
options.C: Hypothermia improves coagulation and reduces wound infection
options.D: Temperature has no measurable effect on oxygen consumption

exp: Perioperative normothermia is defined by the performance metric as a core temperature ≥36°C within 30 minutes of PACU arrival (for procedures >1 hour); hypothermia matters because postoperative shivering raises oxygen consumption and can trigger myocardial ischemia (and it impairs coagulation and raises infection risk). The other options misstate the target or deny the effects.
hy: Normothermia ≥36°C within 30 min of PACU; shivering → ↑O₂ use/ischemia.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Which is the most effective technique for active intraoperative warming?

options.A: Forced-air (and circulating-water) warmers; IV fluid and airway warmers minimize loss but do not actively warm
options.B: Intravenous fluid warmers alone
options.C: Airway humidification alone
options.D: Lowering the operating-room ambient temperature

exp: Forced-air and circulating-water warmers are the most effective techniques for active intraoperative warming; IV fluid warmers and airway warming devices minimize heat loss but do not actively warm, and preoperative warming helps for short cases. Fluid warmers alone (B) and airway humidification alone (C) are insufficient, and lowering room temperature (D) worsens hypothermia.
hy: Forced-air/circulating-water = most effective active warming.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Which statement about postoperative visual loss (POVL) is correct?

options.A: It is rare (~0.02%), occurs most often after prolonged prone spine or cardiac surgery, and includes ischemic optic neuropathy, retinal artery occlusion, and cortical blindness
options.B: It occurs in roughly 5% of all surgical patients
options.C: It is most common after brief outpatient procedures
options.D: It is always fully reversible within 24 hours

exp: Postoperative visual loss is rare (~0.02%), occurs most often after prolonged prone spine surgery or cardiac surgery, and its causes include ischemic optic neuropathy, central retinal artery occlusion, and cortical blindness; risk factors include obesity, prolonged surgery, high blood loss, and lack of colloid use. It is not common (B), not associated with brief procedures (C), and frequently not reversible (D).
hy: POVL ~0.02%; prone spine/cardiac; ischemic optic neuropathy/retinal occlusion/cortical blindness.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: In the PACU, what is the most common cause of airway obstruction and the initial maneuver?

options.A: Obstruction of the oropharynx by the tongue/soft tissues from residual anesthetic effects; relieved by head-tilt/jaw-thrust ± an oral or nasopharyngeal airway
options.B: Laryngeal tumor; relieved by emergency tracheostomy
options.C: Bronchospasm; relieved by chest compressions
options.D: Vocal cord paralysis; relieved by intubation only

exp: The most common cause of PACU airway obstruction is occlusion of the oropharynx by the tongue or soft tissues from residual anesthetic, opioid, or relaxant effects; it is initially relieved by a head-tilt/jaw-thrust maneuver with or without an oral or nasopharyngeal airway. The other causes/maneuvers are less common or incorrect.
hy: PACU obstruction: usually the tongue; treat with jaw-thrust ± airway.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: What is the recommended management of postextubation laryngospasm in the PACU?

options.A: Apply continuous positive airway pressure; if ineffective, give a small dose of succinylcholine (≈10–20 mg)
options.B: Immediate cricothyroidotomy
options.C: High-dose epinephrine infusion
options.D: Withhold oxygen until the spasm resolves

exp: Postextubation laryngospasm is managed by applying continuous positive airway pressure; if that is ineffective, a small dose of succinylcholine (≈10–20 mg) is given to break the spasm. Immediate cricothyroidotomy (B), high-dose epinephrine (C), and withholding oxygen (D) are inappropriate.
hy: Laryngospasm: CPAP, then succinylcholine 10–20 mg if needed.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Which statement about perioperative aspiration is supported by the chapter?

options.A: Aspiration is at least as common during emergence from anesthesia as during induction
options.B: Aspiration occurs only during induction
options.C: Aspiration never occurs under regional anesthesia
options.D: Less than 1% of suspected aspirations cause any clinical concern

exp: The chapter notes that aspiration is at least as common during emergence from anesthesia as during induction, so vigilance and airway-protective precautions are needed throughout, including at extubation. Aspiration is not limited to induction (B), can occur with regional techniques requiring rescue (C), and a substantial fraction of suspected aspirations cause injury (D).
hy: Aspiration risk at emergence ≈ at induction.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Which principle of analgesic route selection is correct?

options.A: The oral route is preferred when feasible; when parenteral therapy is needed, the IV route is preferred over intramuscular/subcutaneous (which give erratic absorption)
options.B: Intramuscular injection is preferred for reliable, steady blood levels
options.C: The subcutaneous route is first-line for severe acute pain
options.D: Intravenous analgesia should be avoided in all postoperative patients

exp: The oral route is preferred for analgesia when feasible; when parenteral therapy is required, the IV route is preferred over intramuscular/subcutaneous, which give painful, erratic absorption and variable blood levels. IM is not preferred for steady levels (B), subcutaneous is not first-line for severe pain (C), and IV analgesia is appropriate when needed (D).
hy: Analgesia routes: oral preferred; IV over IM/SC when parenteral.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Which intervention has been shown to lower postoperative opioid requirements when given before incision (preemptive analgesia)?

options.A: Continuous ketamine infused before incision and continued through wound closure
options.B: Withholding all analgesia until the patient reports severe pain
options.C: A single dose of an antihistamine
options.D: Routine intramuscular meperidine on arrival in the PACU

exp: Ketamine infused continuously before incision and continued through wound closure significantly reduced postoperative morphine consumption (preemptive analgesia). Withholding analgesia (B), an antihistamine dose (C), and routine IM meperidine (D) do not provide preemptive benefit.
hy: Preemptive ketamine (pre-incision) lowers postoperative opioid use.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Which anesthetic agents trigger malignant hyperthermia, and in whom should they be avoided?

options.A: Volatile halogenated agents and succinylcholine; avoid in patients with muscular dystrophy or a family history of malignant hyperthermia
options.B: Propofol and opioids; avoid in asthmatics
options.C: Nondepolarizing relaxants; avoid in renal failure
options.D: Local anesthetics; avoid in cardiac disease

exp: Malignant hyperthermia is triggered by volatile halogenated agents (sevoflurane, isoflurane, etc.) and the depolarizing relaxant succinylcholine; trigger agents should be avoided in patients with muscular dystrophy or a family history of MH, who should receive a trigger-free anesthetic. Propofol/opioids (B), nondepolarizers (C), and local anesthetics (D) are not MH triggers.
hy: MH triggers: volatile agents + succinylcholine; avoid in MD/FH of MH.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Which set of hazards is specific to regional anesthesia and must be weighed against its benefits?

options.A: Postdural puncture headache, local anesthetic systemic toxicity, high neuraxial block, and peripheral nerve injury
options.B: Malignant hyperthermia and adrenal suppression
options.C: Compound A nephrotoxicity and carbon monoxide production
options.D: Hyperkalemia from depolarizing blockers

exp: Hazards specific to regional anesthesia include postdural puncture headache, local anesthetic systemic toxicity, high (excessive) neuraxial block, and peripheral nerve injury — which must be weighed against its benefits, and an inadequate block may require conversion to general anesthesia. The other hazard sets belong to MH/etomidate (B), volatile agents (C), and succinylcholine (D).
hy: Regional hazards: PDPH, LAST, high block, nerve injury.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Which statement about sevoflurane is correct?

options.A: Its low pungency and relatively low blood solubility make it pleasant and suitable for inhalational induction (e.g., in children), with rapid emergence; compound A formation is of theoretical, not clinically important, toxicity
options.B: Its pungency precludes inhalational induction
options.C: It has the slowest emergence of all volatile agents
options.D: It markedly sensitizes the heart to catecholamines

exp: Sevoflurane's low pungency and relatively low blood solubility make it pleasant and suitable for inhalational induction (notably in children), with rapid emergence and a lower incidence of PACU somnolence/nausea than isoflurane; its compound A metabolite is nephrotoxic in animals but its human toxicity is theoretical, not clinically important. The other statements describe desflurane/other agents.
hy: Sevoflurane: pleasant mask induction (kids), rapid emergence; compound A only theoretical.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Which statement about isoflurane is correct?

options.A: It undergoes minimal metabolism and sensitizes the heart to catecholamines less than halothane, but its pungent odor precludes inhalational induction and it can cause tachycardia
options.B: It is the most potent volatile agent and ideal for mask induction
options.C: It is extensively metabolized (>20%) with significant fluoride release
options.D: It markedly depresses cardiac output more than halothane

exp: Isoflurane undergoes minimal metabolism (<2%) and sensitizes the myocardium to catecholamines less than halothane, but its pungent odor precludes inhalational induction and it can cause a variable tachycardia that raises myocardial oxygen consumption (use care in coronary disease). It is not the most potent agent or good for mask induction (B), is minimally metabolized (C), and depresses cardiac output less than halothane (D).
hy: Isoflurane: minimal metabolism, less catecholamine sensitization than halothane, pungent (no mask induction), tachycardia.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: Which combination is typically used to deliver total intravenous anesthesia (TIVA)?

options.A: Propofol combined with an opioid (and other adjuncts), with propofol also used as an infusion for procedural sedation (e.g., endoscopy)
options.B: Nitrous oxide as the sole agent
options.C: Succinylcholine infusion for maintenance
options.D: A volatile agent delivered intravenously

exp: Total intravenous anesthesia is delivered with propofol combined with an opioid and other adjuncts; propofol is also widely used as a continuous infusion for procedural sedation such as GI endoscopy. Nitrous oxide alone (B), a succinylcholine infusion (C), and a volatile agent given IV (D) are not how TIVA is delivered.
hy: TIVA = propofol + opioid (± adjuncts); propofol also for endoscopy sedation.
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: For a penicillin-allergic patient (able to take oral medication) who needs endocarditis prophylaxis before a dental procedure, which agent is appropriate?

options.A: Cephalexin, clindamycin, azithromycin, or clarithromycin (cephalosporins avoided if prior anaphylaxis/angioedema to penicillin)
options.B: A higher dose of amoxicillin
options.C: Oral vancomycin
options.D: No alternative exists; the procedure must be cancelled

exp: For a penicillin-allergic patient able to take oral medication, appropriate dental endocarditis prophylaxis is cephalexin (or another first/second-generation cephalosporin), clindamycin, azithromycin, or clarithromycin — but cephalosporins are avoided in anyone with a history of anaphylaxis, angioedema, or urticaria to penicillins/ampicillin. A higher amoxicillin dose (B) is contraindicated in allergy, oral vancomycin (C) is not used, and an alternative does exist (D).
hy: Penicillin-allergic dental prophylaxis: cephalexin/clindamycin/azithromycin (avoid cephalosporins if anaphylaxis).
chname: Anesthesiology Principles, Pain Management, and Conscious Sedation

stem: How is the Injury Severity Score (ISS) calculated, and what is its range?

options.A: By summing the squares of the AIS severity codes of the three most severely injured body regions; it ranges from 1 to 75
options.B: By averaging the GCS, systolic blood pressure, and respiratory rate
options.C: By adding the AIS codes of all injured regions; it ranges from 0 to 12
options.D: By multiplying the worst AIS code by the number of injuries

exp: The ISS is calculated by summing the squares of the AIS severity codes of the three most severely injured body regions, yielding a range of 1 to 75; it is the standard anatomic measure of overall injury burden. It is not an average of physiologic variables (B), a simple sum (C), or a multiplication (D).
hy: ISS = sum of squares of the 3 worst AIS regions (range 1–75).

stem: Which ISS value defines "severe" injury?

options.A: ISS greater than 25
options.B: ISS less than 9
options.C: ISS between 9 and 16
options.D: ISS of exactly 15

exp: ISS severity bands are minor (<9), moderate (9–16), serious (16–25), and severe (>25), so an ISS greater than 25 defines severe injury. ISS <9 is minor (B), 9–16 is moderate (C), and a single value of 15 is moderate (D).
hy: Severe injury = ISS >25.

stem: In the Abbreviated Injury Scale, what does the "post-dot" severity code represent?

options.A: A 1-to-6 score of injury severity/risk of death (1 = minimal, 6 = presumably fatal)
options.B: The body region of the injury only
options.C: The number of injured organs
options.D: The patient's Glasgow Coma Scale

exp: The AIS "post-dot" code is a 1-to-6 severity score (1 minimal, 6 presumably fatal) describing the risk of death for each injury, used to cohort injuries and feed the ISS. It does not encode body region alone (B), organ count (C), or GCS (D).
hy: AIS post-dot = severity 1 (minimal) to 6 (fatal).

stem: Which set correctly describes the Glasgow Coma Scale components and range?
