options.B: After 3 weeks of open dressing changes
options.C: Immediately at the first debridement in all cases, regardless of contamination
options.D: Only after the fracture has fully united

exp: When the wound cannot be closed primarily, definitive soft tissue coverage (skin graft or muscle flap) should ideally be achieved within the first 72 hours, before secondary colonization and wound fibrosis develop. Waiting 3 weeks (B) invites infection, immediate first-debridement coverage regardless of contamination (C) is not always feasible, and waiting for union (D) is far too long. Negative-pressure wound therapy bridges the interval.
hy: Aim for soft tissue coverage within ~72 h of injury.

stem: Intraoperative muscle viability during debridement is judged by the "4 C's." Which set is correct?

options.A: Color, contractility, consistency, and capacity to bleed
options.B: Crepitus, contamination, cyanosis, and calcification
options.C: Color, calcium, contracture, and cellularity
options.D: Cold, clammy, comminuted, and closed

exp: Muscle viability at debridement is judged by the "4 C's": color, contractility, consistency, and capacity to bleed. The other letter sets (B, C, D) are fabricated. A recent histopathologic study suggests these criteria may actually lead to debridement of some viable muscle, but they remain the bedside standard.
hy: Muscle viability = the 4 C's: color, contractility, consistency, capacity to bleed.

stem: On the Mangled Extremity Severity Score (MESS), which score consistently predicted the need for amputation, and what is its key test characteristic?

options.A: A score of 7 or higher predicted amputation; the score has high specificity but low sensitivity
options.B: A score of 2 or higher predicted amputation; it has high sensitivity but low specificity
options.C: A score of 10 is required; it perfectly predicts both salvage and amputation
options.D: Any positive score mandates immediate amputation

exp: A Mangled Extremity Severity Score of 7 or higher consistently predicted the need for amputation (limbs scoring ≤6 remained viable), and subsequent studies confirmed the score's high specificity but low sensitivity — meaning a low score reliably predicts salvage, but a high score does not mandate amputation. A cutoff of 2 with high sensitivity (B) is wrong, a perfect score of 10 (C) does not exist as described, and no positive score mandates amputation (D).
hy: MESS ≥7 suggests amputation; high specificity, low sensitivity.
src: Sabiston Ch 19, "Limb Salvage Versus Primary Amputation"

stem: What did the Lower Extremity Assessment Project (LEAP) demonstrate about an insensate (plantar-anesthetic) foot on presentation?

options.A: It is not an absolute indication for amputation; many salvaged limbs regained sensation within 2 years with outcomes equal to those with intact sensation
options.B: It is an absolute indication mandating immediate amputation
options.C: It reliably predicts permanent tibial nerve transection
options.D: It correlates with worse outcomes only in the amputation group

exp: A LEAP subgroup study showed that an insensate (plantar-anesthetic) foot on arrival is NOT an absolute indication for amputation: many salvaged limbs regained sensation within 2 years and had outcomes equal to limbs with intact sensation, overturning the old "absent plantar sensation = amputate" dogma. It is therefore not an absolute indication (B), does not reliably predict permanent tibial nerve loss (C), and was not associated with worse outcomes confined to amputees (D). LEAP also found similar function at 2 and 7 years for salvage vs amputation.
hy: Insensate foot is no longer an absolute amputation indication (LEAP).
src: Sabiston Ch 19, "Limb Salvage Versus Primary Amputation"

stem: Beyond scoring systems, which is an accepted factor favoring primary amputation of a mangled lower extremity?

options.A: A warm ischemia time of more than 6 hours
options.B: A warm ischemia time of more than 30 minutes
options.C: The mere presence of any open fracture
options.D: A single absent pulse that returns after reduction

exp: Among the few accepted factors favoring primary amputation is a warm ischemia time of more than 6 hours, along with a severe crush, a non-reconstructable mangled stump, or a missing extremity. Thirty minutes (B) is far too short, the mere presence of an open fracture (C) is not an indication, and a pulse that returns after reduction (D) argues against amputation. The MESS should supplement — not replace — clinical judgment.
hy: Warm ischemia >6 h favors amputation.
src: Sabiston Ch 19, "Limb Salvage Versus Primary Amputation"

stem: Firearm fractures are classified by projectile velocity. Which threshold and management pairing is correct?

options.A: High-energy ≥2000 ft/s (treated per open-fracture protocol); low-energy <2000 ft/s (often treated as sterile closed fractures)
options.B: High-energy ≥500 ft/s; all treated as closed fractures
options.C: Low-energy ≥2000 ft/s; treated with aggressive debridement
options.D: Velocity is irrelevant; all gunshot fractures are managed identically

exp: Firearm fractures are classified as high-energy (≥2000 ft/s muzzle velocity — most rifles), treated per open-fracture protocol with aggressive debridement and IV antibiotics, versus low-energy (<2000 ft/s — most handguns), often treated as sterile closed fractures with ED irrigation, tetanus prophylaxis, and a short antibiotic course. A 500 ft/s threshold (B) and reversed energy assignment (C) are wrong, and velocity clearly does affect management (D). Close-range shotgun wounds are treated as high-energy regardless.
hy: Firearm: ≥2000 ft/s = high-energy (open-fracture protocol); <2000 ft/s often closed.
src: Sabiston Ch 19, "Fractures Secondary to Firearm Injury"

stem: What is the central pathophysiologic mechanism of acute compartment syndrome?

options.A: Rising compartment pressure (usually from muscle edema) impedes venous outflow, creating a self-perpetuating cycle of congestion and ischemia
options.B: Arterial inflow is the first vessel obstructed, causing immediate pulselessness
options.C: A primary nerve injury causes the muscle swelling
options.D: Lymphatic obstruction is the initiating event

exp: Compartment syndrome begins when rising compartment pressure — most often from muscle edema after trauma or reperfusion — exceeds venous pressure and obstructs venous outflow; the resulting congestion, ischemia, inflammatory mediator release, and increased capillary permeability further raise pressure in a self-perpetuating cycle. Arterial inflow loss with early pulselessness (B) is a late, unreliable feature, a primary nerve injury (C) does not cause it, and lymphatic obstruction (D) is not the mechanism. This is why pulses can be present yet the limb is dying.
hy: Compartment syndrome: edema → venous outflow obstruction → vicious cycle.

stem: When compartment pressures are measured, which threshold is the generally accepted indication for fasciotomy?

options.A: A ΔP (diastolic blood pressure minus compartment pressure) less than 30 mm Hg
options.B: An absolute compartment pressure above 10 mm Hg
options.C: A ΔP greater than 50 mm Hg
options.D: A systolic blood pressure minus compartment pressure less than 10 mm Hg

exp: The generally accepted indication for fasciotomy is a ΔP (diastolic blood pressure minus measured compartment pressure) of less than 30 mm Hg, which has supplanted absolute pressure values because absolute thresholds have a high false-positive rate. An absolute pressure >10 mm Hg (B) is far too low, a ΔP >50 mm Hg (C) is normal, and using systolic pressure (D) is not the convention. The measurement is compared to the patient's own diastolic pressure.
hy: Fasciotomy when ΔP (diastolic − compartment) < 30 mm Hg.

stem: How should compartment pressures be measured technically?

options.A: Measure all compartments (four in the leg, two in the forearm) within 5 cm of the fracture using a side-port needle system
options.B: Measure only the anterior compartment, at least 15 cm from the fracture
options.C: Measure a single compartment with an arterial line, which reads accurately
options.D: Two compartments in the leg and four in the forearm

exp: Pressures should be measured in all compartments (four in the leg, two in the forearm) within 5 cm of the fracture, using a side-port needle and pressure-measurement system such as the Stryker monitor. Measuring only the anterior compartment 15 cm away (B) misses disease, an arterial-line setup (C) tends to artificially elevate the reading, and the leg has four (not two) compartments while the forearm has two (D reversed). Single time-point measurements also carry a notable false-positive rate.
hy: Measure all compartments (4 leg / 2 forearm) within 5 cm of the fracture.

stem: In an awake patient, which clinical finding is the earliest and most reliable sign of compartment syndrome?

options.A: Pain out of proportion to the injury, especially with passive stretch of the involved compartment
options.B: Absent distal pulses
options.C: Pallor and a cold limb
options.D: Complete paralysis of the limb

exp: In an awake patient the earliest and most reliable sign is pain out of proportion to the injury, especially with passive stretch of the muscles in the involved compartment, often accompanied by digital dysesthesias as nerves are compromised. Absent pulses (B), pallor/cold limb (C), and paralysis (D) are late findings that appear after irreversible damage. Repeated, well-documented serial exams are the crux of reliable diagnosis.
hy: Earliest sign: pain out of proportion / pain with passive stretch.

stem: After fasciotomy of the lower leg, how should the skin incisions be managed?

options.A: Leave them open (closing skin can dangerously raise intramuscular pressure); attempt delayed secondary closure after swelling subsides at 3–5 days
options.B: Close both incisions primarily and tightly at the end of the case
options.C: Close one incision and apply a circumferential cast
options.D: Suture the skin and apply continuous traction

exp: Fasciotomy skin incisions are left open because closing the skin — even after fascial release — can dangerously raise intramuscular pressure; delayed secondary closure is attempted once swelling subsides at about 3–5 days, sometimes aided by vessel-loop techniques or a split-thickness skin graft. Tight primary closure (B), closure plus a cast (C), and suturing with traction (D) all risk re-elevating compartment pressure. In the setting of a fracture, the fasciotomy effectively creates an open fracture.
hy: Leave fasciotomy wounds open; delayed closure at 3–5 days.
src: Sabiston Ch 19, "Acute Compartment Syndrome: Surgical Treatment"

stem: Using the historical static definitions of pelvic stability, which translation thresholds define instability?

options.A: Anterior translation >2.5 cm defines anterior instability; vertical translation >1 cm through a sacral fracture or SI joint defines vertical instability
options.B: Anterior translation >5 cm and vertical translation >5 cm
options.C: Any translation greater than 1 mm is unstable
options.D: Vertical translation >10 cm; anterior translation is irrelevant

exp: Historically, a stable pelvis tolerates less than 2.5 cm of anterior translation (through the symphysis or rami), while vertical instability is defined by more than 1 cm of translation through a sacral fracture or the sacroiliac joint, which requires posterior sacroiliac ligament injury. The 5-cm thresholds (B), a 1-mm cutoff (C), and a 10-cm vertical figure ignoring the anterior structures (D) are incorrect. Because the pelvis is a true ring, significant anterior displacement implies posterior disruption.
hy: Pelvic instability: >2.5 cm anterior or >1 cm vertical translation.

stem: A transverse process fracture of the L5 vertebra should raise suspicion for what?

options.A: Pelvic instability from disruption of the iliolumbar ligament
options.B: An isolated, stable injury needing no further workup
options.C: A cervical spine fracture
options.D: A femoral neck fracture

exp: An L5 transverse process fracture should raise suspicion for pelvic instability because it implies avulsion/disruption of the iliolumbar ligament, a key posterior stabilizer. It is not an isolated, benign finding (B), and it has nothing to do with the cervical spine (C) or the femoral neck (D). This "marker" prompts careful assessment of the posterior pelvic ring.
hy: L5 transverse process fracture → iliolumbar ligament injury → suspect pelvic instability.

section: Pelvic Ring Disruption / Young and Burgess
stem: In the Young and Burgess classification, which force produces each pelvic fracture type?

options.A: Type A = lateral compression; Type B = anteroposterior compression; Type C = vertical shear
options.B: Type A = vertical shear; Type B = lateral compression; Type C = anteroposterior compression
options.C: Type A = anteroposterior compression; Type B = vertical shear; Type C = lateral compression
options.D: All three types result from the same lateral compression force

exp: The Young and Burgess classification is mechanism-based: Type A = lateral compression (LC), Type B = anteroposterior compression (APC), and Type C = vertical shear. The other orderings (B, C) misassign the forces, and the three types do not share one mechanism (D). LC and APC are further subdivided I–III by the degree of ligamentous/osseous disruption.
hy: Young-Burgess: A = LC, B = APC, C = vertical shear.

section: Pelvic Ring Disruption / Young and Burgess
stem: Which pelvic injury pattern carries the greatest risk of massive retroperitoneal hemorrhage?

options.A: The APC III ("open-book") injury, which markedly increases pelvic volume
options.B: The LC I injury, which is stable
options.C: An isolated pubic ramus fracture
options.D: A coccyx fracture

exp: The APC III injury — the "open-book" pelvis — has complete anterior and posterior ligamentous disruption that markedly increases pelvic volume, allowing massive blood loss; APC patterns carry the greatest risk of retroperitoneal hemorrhage. The stable LC I (B), an isolated ramus fracture (C), and a coccyx fracture (D) are low-bleeding-risk injuries. By contrast, the most common cause of death in LC injuries is associated closed head trauma.
hy: APC III (open-book) = greatest retroperitoneal hemorrhage risk.

stem: What is the most common source of hemorrhage in a pelvic fracture, and how does initial stabilization help?

options.A: The presacral venous plexus and bleeding cancellous bone; reduction/stabilization decreases pelvic volume and tamponades the bleeding
options.B: The internal iliac artery in the majority of cases; only embolization can help
options.C: The aorta; stabilization has no effect on bleeding
options.D: The femoral vein; stabilization worsens bleeding

exp: Most pelvic-fracture hemorrhage arises from the presacral (posterior) venous plexus and bleeding cancellous bone, so initial treatment focuses on reduction and stabilization, which decreases pelvic volume and tamponades the bleeding. Arterial injury from the internal iliac (B) occurs in only ~10% and is addressed with angioembolization when bleeding is refractory; the aorta (C) and femoral vein (D) are not the sources, and stabilization helps rather than worsens bleeding.
hy: Pelvic bleeding is mostly venous (presacral plexus) → reduce/stabilize to tamponade.

stem: How should a pelvic binder be applied, and to which injuries is its use best limited?

options.A: Centered over the greater trochanters; use should be limited mainly to APC injuries with hemodynamic instability, since it can over-reduce LC patterns
options.B: Centered over the iliac crests; used routinely for every pelvic fracture
options.C: Placed over the lower abdomen; ideal for LC injuries specifically
options.D: Left in place for several days regardless of skin condition

exp: A pelvic binder (or sheet) is applied centered over the greater trochanters, and its use should be limited mainly to APC injuries with hemodynamic instability, because in LC patterns it can over-reduce the fracture and injure intrapelvic structures. Centering over the iliac crests (B) or lower abdomen (C) is incorrect, and binders should be removed as soon as possible to prevent skin breakdown, not left for days (D). Internally rotating the legs assists reduction.
hy: Binder over the greater trochanters; reserve for unstable APC injuries.

stem: In Denis' three-column model, which columns are disrupted in a burst fracture, and how does it differ from a compression fracture?

options.A: A burst fracture involves the anterior AND middle columns; a compression fracture involves the anterior column only
options.B: A burst fracture involves the posterior column only; a compression fracture involves all three
options.C: Both involve only the middle column
options.D: A compression fracture involves the middle and posterior columns

exp: In Denis' three-column model, a burst fracture by definition involves the anterior AND middle columns, whereas a compression fracture involves the anterior column only and is rarely associated with spinal cord injury. Option B reverses the columns, C confines both to the middle column, and D misassigns the compression fracture. Injury to two or three columns produces an unstable segment that puts the cord at risk.
hy: Burst = anterior + middle columns; compression = anterior only.

stem: In the Thoracolumbar Injury Classification and Severity (TLICS) score, which point assignments are correct?

options.A: A burst morphology scores 2, an injured posterior ligamentous complex scores 3, and an incomplete cord injury scores 3
options.B: A compression morphology scores 4 and a distraction injury scores 1
options.C: An intact posterior ligamentous complex scores 3
options.D: A nerve-root injury scores 0 points

exp: On the TLICS score, fracture morphology is scored compression 1 / burst 2 / translational-rotational 3 / distraction 4; the posterior ligamentous complex scores intact 0, indeterminate 2, injured 3; and neurology scores intact 0, root 2, complete cord 2, incomplete cord 3, cauda equina 3 — so a burst = 2, an injured PLC = 3, and an incomplete cord injury = 3. Option B misassigns compression/distraction, C gives an intact PLC 3 points, and D zeroes a nerve-root injury. Higher total scores favor operative management.
hy: TLICS: burst 2, injured PLC 3, incomplete cord 3.

stem: The bulbocavernosus reflex is used to assess spinal shock. Which statement is correct?

options.A: An absent reflex indicates spinal shock; it typically returns within 48 hours, after which neurologic examination is more reliable, and sacral sparing implies partial long-tract continuity
options.B: A present reflex confirms a complete cord transection
options.C: The reflex is tested by a Babinski maneuver on the foot
options.D: Spinal shock, once present, is permanent

exp: The bulbocavernosus reflex (anal wink on tugging the Foley or squeezing the glans) is absent during spinal shock and typically returns within ~48 hours; only after it returns is the neurologic exam a reliable indicator of true deficit, and sacral sparing (intact perianal sensation, rectal tone, or great-toe flexion) implies at least partial long-tract continuity. A present reflex does not confirm complete transection (B), it is not the Babinski (C), and spinal shock is transient, not permanent (D). An ASIA classification is assigned after a full exam.
hy: Absent bulbocavernosus = spinal shock (returns ~48 h); sacral sparing = partial cord continuity.

stem: A trauma patient develops urinary retention, saddle anesthesia, and reduced rectal tone after a thoracolumbar injury. What is the appropriate action?

options.A: Obtain MRI immediately (or CT myelography if MRI is unavailable); confirmed cauda equina syndrome requires emergent surgical decompression
options.B: Discharge with outpatient physical therapy
options.C: Observe for 1 week before any imaging
options.D: Begin bracing and reassess in clinic in 6 weeks

exp: Suspected cauda equina syndrome (urinary retention, saddle anesthesia, reduced rectal tone) demands immediate MRI — or CT myelography if MRI is unavailable/contraindicated — and, once confirmed, emergent surgical decompression. Outpatient physical therapy (B), a week of observation (C), and bracing with a 6-week recheck (D) risk permanent deficit. It can be caused by fractures, herniated discs, tumor, or hematoma.
hy: Cauda equina syndrome → urgent MRI + emergent decompression.

stem: In what direction do most hip dislocations occur, what is the classic limb position, and what is the urgency?

options.A: Posterior in ~90%, with the thigh flexed, internally rotated, and shortened; reduction after 12 hours raises avascular necrosis risk (odds ratio ~5.6)
options.B: Anterior in ~90%, with the thigh abducted and externally rotated; timing of reduction is irrelevant
options.C: Posterior in ~50%, with the leg lengthened; reduction can wait 72 hours
options.D: Inferior in most cases, with no neurovascular risk

exp: About 90% of hip dislocations are posterior, classically leaving the thigh flexed, internally rotated, and shortened; delay matters because a meta-analysis found reduction after 12 hours carried an odds ratio of ~5.6 for avascular necrosis. Anterior dislocation in 90% with abduction/external rotation (B) is wrong, a 50%/lengthened/72-hour description (C) is incorrect, and inferior dislocation with no neurovascular risk (D) is false. The sciatic nerve and femoral head blood supply are both at risk.
hy: Hip dislocations are ~90% posterior (flexed/IR/shortened); reduce <12 h to limit AVN.

stem: Which dislocation-to-at-risk-structure pairing is correct?

options.A: Knee dislocation → popliteal artery; shoulder dislocation → axillary nerve
options.B: Knee dislocation → axillary nerve; shoulder dislocation → popliteal artery
options.C: Hip dislocation → radial nerve; elbow dislocation → sciatic nerve
options.D: Shoulder dislocation → femoral artery; hip dislocation → median nerve

exp: Per Table 19.7, a knee dislocation threatens the popliteal artery, and a shoulder dislocation threatens the axillary nerve (shoulder abduction and sensation over the deltoid). Option B swaps these, and options C and D pair joints with the wrong nerves/vessels (hip → sciatic, elbow → radial/ulnar/median). Knowing the at-risk structure guides the focused neurovascular exam before and after reduction.
hy: Knee dislocation → popliteal artery; shoulder dislocation → axillary nerve.

stem: An anterior shoulder dislocation classically presents with the arm held in which position?

options.A: Externally rotated and slightly abducted, with loss of the normal rounded deltoid contour in thin patients
options.B: Internally rotated and adducted, with the limb shortened
options.C: Fully flexed across the chest with the elbow locked
options.D: Abducted to 90 degrees and internally rotated

exp: An anterior shoulder dislocation classically holds the arm externally rotated and slightly abducted, and in thin patients the normally rounded deltoid contour is visibly lost. Internal rotation with adduction and shortening (B) describes a posterior hip dislocation, a fully flexed locked elbow (C) is not the position, and 90-degree abduction with internal rotation (D) is incorrect. The humeral head sits anterior to the glenoid.
hy: Anterior shoulder dislocation: arm externally rotated/abducted, deltoid contour lost.

stem: Which list correctly enumerates the basic types of vascular injury described in the chapter?

options.A: Intimal injury, complete wall defect (pseudoaneurysm/hemorrhage), complete transection, arteriovenous fistula, and spasm
options.B: Only complete transection and spasm
options.C: Venous thrombosis, lymphedema, and embolism only
options.D: Atherosclerosis, aneurysm, and dissection only

exp: The five basic types of vascular injury are intimal injury (flaps, disruptions, intramural hematomas), complete wall defect (pseudoaneurysm or hemorrhage), complete transection (hemorrhage or occlusion), arteriovenous fistula, and spasm. Listing only transection and spasm (B), venous/lymphatic processes (C), or chronic degenerative lesions (D) is incomplete or wrong. Blunt trauma tends to cause intimal injury, while penetrating trauma can cause the other four.
hy: Five vascular injury types: intimal, wall defect, transection, AV fistula, spasm.
src: Sabiston Ch 19, "Vascular Injuries: Incidence and Recognition"

stem: Which statement about recognizing extremity vascular injury is correct?

options.A: Normal pulses are present in 5%–15% of patients with a vascular injury, so hard signs (pulselessness, expanding hematoma, bruit/thrill) and ABI comparison to the other limb are needed
options.B: A palpable pulse fully excludes a significant vascular injury
options.C: Overt external hemorrhage is the rule in orthopedic vascular trauma
options.D: The ABI is unreliable and should not be used

exp: Recognition is difficult because normal pulses are present in 5%–15% of patients with a vascular injury and overt external hemorrhage is rare in orthopedic trauma; reliance is therefore on hard signs (pulselessness, pallor, paresthesia, paralysis, expanding hematoma, loss of Doppler signal, palpable thrill/audible bruit) and an ABI <0.9 compared with the other limb. A palpable pulse does not exclude injury (B), overt hemorrhage is not the rule (C), and the ABI is in fact useful (D). These clinical findings have >90% sensitivity when compared to the uninjured side.
hy: Pulses are normal in 5–15% of vascular injuries — use hard signs + ABI.
src: Sabiston Ch 19, "Vascular Injuries: Incidence and Recognition"

stem: Why does a popliteal artery injury (e.g., from knee dislocation) threaten the limb more than a superficial femoral artery injury?

options.A: There is poor collateral circulation distal to the popliteal artery, so occlusion risks in-situ thrombosis and amputation, whereas the SFA is bypassed by the rich profunda femoris collaterals
options.B: The popliteal artery is larger and bleeds more
options.C: The SFA has no collateral supply, making it more dangerous
options.D: Both have identical collateral networks, so outcomes are equal

exp: A popliteal artery injury is limb-threatening because collateral circulation distal to the popliteal artery is poor, so occlusion can cause in-situ thrombosis and amputation; by contrast, a superficial femoral artery injury rarely leads to amputation because of the rich profunda femoris collateral network. The popliteal artery is not dangerous merely for its size/bleeding (B), the SFA is not collateral-poor (C), and the two networks are not equivalent (D). Prognosis tracks vessel level and collateral adequacy.
hy: Popliteal artery has poor distal collaterals → high amputation risk; SFA is well-collateralized.

stem: Regarding the timing of definitive femoral shaft fixation, which statement reflects current practice?

options.A: Most patients should undergo definitive fixation within 24–48 hours (early total care); damage control orthopedics (external fixation) is reserved for persistent hypotension, metabolic acidosis, or severe head injury
options.B: All femoral shaft fractures should be definitively nailed within the first hour regardless of physiology
options.C: Damage control orthopedics is the first-line treatment for every femoral shaft fracture
options.D: Definitive fixation should always be delayed beyond 2 weeks

exp: With modern resuscitation, the overwhelming majority of patients should have definitive femoral shaft fixation within 24–48 hours (early total care, ETC), reserving damage control orthopedics — provisional external fixation — for patients with persistent hypotension, metabolic acidosis, or severe head injury. Mandatory nailing within an hour regardless of physiology (B) is wrong, DCO-first for everyone (C) is not supported, and routine delay beyond 2 weeks (D) is incorrect. If DCO is used, wait >5 days before definitive fixation to avoid a "second hit."
hy: Femoral shaft: ETC within 24–48 h; DCO for the physiologically deranged.
src: Sabiston Ch 19, "Common Long Bone Fractures: Femur"

stem: Which statements about tibial shaft fractures are correct?

options.A: The incidence of compartment syndrome is as high as 10%, and reamed intramedullary nailing is the fixation of choice when appropriate
options.B: Compartment syndrome essentially never complicates tibial shaft fractures
options.C: Plate fixation is preferred for diaphyseal fractures because of superior wound healing
options.D: The tibial diaphysis has an unusually rich blood supply, so these fractures heal rapidly

exp: The incidence of compartment syndrome in tibial shaft fractures is as high as 10%, demanding close monitoring, and reamed intramedullary nailing is the fixation of choice when appropriate. Compartment syndrome is not negligible here (B), plate fixation has fallen out of favor for diaphyseal fractures because of wound-healing complications (C), and the tibial diaphysis actually has a tenuous single-nutrient-artery supply that slows healing (D). The medial tibia's thin soft tissue also makes these fractures frequently open.
hy: Tibial shaft: ~10% compartment syndrome; reamed IM nail is the treatment of choice.
src: Sabiston Ch 19, "Common Long Bone Fractures: Tibial Shaft"

stem: Which statement about radial nerve injury with humeral shaft fractures is correct?

options.A: It occurs in ~6%–18%, especially with displaced mid-shaft fractures, and is usually a neurapraxia that recovers spontaneously in ~90% of patients within 3–4 months
options.B: It occurs in ~50% and requires immediate nerve grafting in all cases
options.C: It is caused by the nerve's course along the anterior humerus near the elbow
options.D: It never recovers without surgical exploration

exp: Radial nerve injury complicates roughly 6%–18% of humeral shaft fractures, especially displaced mid-shaft fractures, owing to the nerve's course in the spiral groove along the posterior humerus; most are neurapraxias that recover spontaneously in about 90% of patients within 3–4 months. A 50% incidence requiring universal grafting (B) is wrong, the anterior-near-elbow course (C) is incorrect, and spontaneous recovery is the rule, not surgery (D). A removable wrist/finger splint supports the limb during recovery.
hy: Humeral shaft radial nerve palsy ~6–18%; ~90% recover in 3–4 months.
src: Sabiston Ch 19, "Common Long Bone Fractures: Humerus"

stem: A mid-shaft humerus fracture with the radial nerve trapped between fragments at the distal-third spiral level carries which eponym, and what are the acceptable nonoperative alignment parameters?

options.A: Holstein-Lewis fracture; acceptable alignment is <20° sagittal angulation, <30° coronal angulation, and <3 cm shortening
options.B: Holstein-Lewis fracture; acceptable alignment requires perfect anatomic reduction
options.C: Monteggia fracture; up to 60° of angulation is acceptable
options.D: Galeazzi fracture; shortening up to 10 cm is acceptable

exp: A Holstein-Lewis fracture is a distal-third spiral humeral shaft fracture associated with radial nerve entrapment/injury; acceptable nonoperative alignment for humeral shaft fractures is less than 20° of sagittal-plane angulation, less than 30° of coronal-plane angulation, and less than 3 cm of shortening. Requiring perfect anatomic reduction (B) is too strict for a shaft, and Monteggia (C) and Galeazzi (D) are forearm fracture-dislocations with the wrong tolerances. Nonoperative functional bracing achieves high union rates within these parameters.
hy: Holstein-Lewis = distal-third spiral humerus + radial nerve; accept <20° sagittal, <30° coronal, <3 cm shortening.
src: Sabiston Ch 19, "Common Long Bone Fractures: Humerus"

stem: In the institutional DVT-prophylaxis protocol, what is recommended for outpatient prophylaxis after a femur/hip/acetabulum fracture versus an isolated tibia-or-distal fracture?

options.A: Aspirin 325 mg daily for ~35 days after femur/hip/acetabulum fractures; no routine outpatient prophylaxis for isolated tibia-or-distal fractures
options.B: No prophylaxis after hip fractures; lifelong warfarin after tibia fractures
options.C: Identical 35-day anticoagulation for every lower-extremity fracture
options.D: Aspirin only for tibia fractures and nothing for hip fractures

exp: In the institutional protocol (Table 19.8), femur/hip/acetabulum fractures receive outpatient aspirin 325 mg daily for ~35 days, whereas isolated tibia-or-distal lower-extremity fractures receive no routine outpatient chemoprophylaxis (no clinical benefit shown). Withholding prophylaxis after hip fractures (B) is backwards, identical 35-day anticoagulation for every fracture (C) is not the protocol, and giving aspirin only for tibia fractures (D) reverses the recommendation. The protocol reflects the high VTE risk of proximal injuries.
hy: Femur/hip/acetabulum → ASA 325 ×35 d; isolated tibia/distal → none.

stem: Which orthopedic fractures place a patient at the highest risk of DVT, and for roughly how long does elevated risk persist after major hip surgery?

options.A: Pelvis and femur fractures; risk remains elevated for up to ~35 days after major hip surgery
options.B: Distal radius fractures; risk lasts only 24 hours
options.C: Clavicle fractures; risk persists for a year
options.D: Isolated finger fractures; risk lasts 6 months

exp: Within orthopedic patients, pelvis and femur fractures carry the highest DVT risk, and patients remain at increased risk of DVT/PE for up to ~35 days following major hip surgery — the basis for the American College of Chest Physicians' strong recommendation for 35-day prophylaxis after hip fracture surgery, THA, or TKA. Distal radius (B), clavicle (C), and finger fractures (D) are not the high-risk injuries described. Risk factors include trauma, lower-extremity fracture, immobilization, hospitalization, and recent surgery.
hy: Pelvis/femur fractures = highest DVT risk; elevated up to ~35 days after major hip surgery.

stem: Which statement about fat emboli syndrome (FES) is correct?

options.A: It presents with respiratory distress, altered mental status, and petechiae hours to days after long-bone/pelvic fracture; treatment is mainly supportive, and corticosteroids reduced FES by ~78% in one meta-analysis
options.B: It presents weeks later with isolated leg swelling and is treated with anticoagulation
options.C: It is diagnosed by a positive D-dimer alone and treated with thrombolysis
options.D: Corticosteroids are contraindicated because they worsen FES

exp: Fat emboli syndrome presents with the triad of respiratory distress, altered mental status, and petechiae, appearing hours to days after long-bone or pelvic fractures (incidence ~2%–5% with isolated long-bone fractures, up to ~19% with multiple injuries); treatment is mainly supportive, and a meta-analysis by Bederman found corticosteroids reduced FES by ~78% in patients with multiple long-bone fractures. A delayed isolated leg swelling treated with anticoagulation (B) describes DVT, diagnosis by D-dimer with thrombolysis (C) is wrong, and corticosteroids are not contraindicated (D). FES may represent a subset of ARDS.
hy: FES triad: respiratory distress + AMS + petechiae; supportive care, steroids cut FES ~78%.
src: Sabiston Ch 19, "Pulmonary Failure: Fat Emboli Syndrome and ARDS"

stem: How is ARDS defined in this chapter, and what does early fracture fixation accomplish?

options.A: A Pao₂/Fio₂ ratio <200 with bilateral infiltrates and no heart failure; early (<24 h) fixation reduces FES/ARDS, though chest injury (not the fixation method) is the main driver
options.B: A Pao₂/Fio₂ ratio >400; early fixation increases ARDS in all patients
options.C: A pulmonary artery occlusion pressure >30 mm Hg; fixation timing is irrelevant
options.D: Unilateral infiltrates with congestive heart failure; treated with diuretics alone

exp: ARDS is defined here as a Pao₂/Fio₂ ratio below 200 (regardless of PEEP), a pulmonary artery occlusion pressure ≤18 mm Hg, or bilateral diffuse infiltrates without congestive heart failure; early (<24 h) fracture fixation reduces FES/ARDS, though the evidence indicates that chest injury — not the method of fixation (e.g., IM nailing) — is the main driver. A ratio >400 with fixation worsening ARDS (B), a wedge pressure >30 (C), and unilateral infiltrates with CHF treated by diuretics (D) are incorrect. With severe chest injury plus long-bone fracture, definitive IM fixation may be delayed in favor of temporary external fixation.
hy: ARDS = Pao₂/Fio₂ <200 + bilateral infiltrates, no CHF; chest injury (not fixation method) drives it.
src: Sabiston Ch 19, "Pulmonary Failure: Fat Emboli Syndrome and ARDS"

stem: On the TLICS point system, which fracture morphology is assigned the highest number of points?

options.A: Distraction (4 points), versus translational/rotational 3, burst 2, and compression 1
options.B: Compression (4 points), versus burst 3, distraction 2, and translational 1
options.C: Burst (4 points), versus compression 3, distraction 2, and rotational 1
options.D: All morphologies score an equal 2 points each

exp: On the TLICS score, fracture morphology is graded by increasing mechanical severity: compression 1, burst 2, translational/rotational 3, and distraction 4 — so a distraction injury earns the most points. The other options reassign the points to the wrong morphologies (B, C) or flatten the scale (D). Higher total scores (morphology + posterior ligamentous complex + neurology) push management toward operative stabilization.
hy: TLICS morphology: distraction 4 > translational/rotational 3 > burst 2 > compression 1.

stem: Which family accounts for ~95% of venomous snakebites in the United States, and which features identify it?

options.A: Crotalinae (pit vipers) — triangular heads, elliptical pupils, heat-sensing facial pits, and retractable fangs
options.B: Elapidae (coral snakes) — rounded heads, circular pupils, and no fangs
options.C: Colubridae — rear-fanged, all nonvenomous
options.D: Hydrophiidae (sea snakes) — found throughout the contiguous US

exp: Crotalinae (pit vipers — rattlesnakes, copperheads, cottonmouths) cause ~95% of US venomous snakebites and are identified by a triangular head, elliptical pupils, heat-sensing facial (infrared) pits, and large retractable fangs. Elapidae/coral snakes (B) have rounded heads, round pupils, and no fangs and cause few bites; colubrids (C) are not the major venomous group described; and sea snakes (D) are not indigenous to the contiguous US.
hy: Pit vipers (Crotalinae) = 95% of US venomous bites; triangular head, facial pits, fangs.
src: Sabiston Ch 21, "Venomous Species Indigenous to United States"

stem: How do the venoms of Crotalinae and Elapidae differ?

options.A: Crotalinae venom is hemotoxic (zinc-dependent metalloproteinases → tissue necrosis, capillary leak, coagulopathy); Elapidae venom is neurotoxic (alpha-neurotoxin → respiratory depression)
options.B: Crotalinae venom is purely neurotoxic; Elapidae venom is purely hemotoxic
options.C: Both venoms act only locally with no systemic effects
options.D: Neither venom affects coagulation

exp: Crotalinae venom is hemotoxic — zinc-dependent metalloproteinases disrupt the basement membrane/endothelium, causing tissue necrosis, capillary leak (pulmonary edema, shock), and a consumptive coagulopathy — whereas Elapidae venom is neurotoxic (alpha-neurotoxin at pre-/postsynaptic receptors) causing respiratory depression. Option B reverses the two, C ignores their prominent systemic effects, and D is false because pit-viper venom characteristically causes coagulopathy.
hy: Crotalinae = hemotoxic/coagulopathic; Elapidae = neurotoxic.

stem: A patient bitten by a coral snake (Elapidae) is most likely to show which picture?

options.A: Minimal local findings but neurotoxicity — cranial nerve dysfunction, loss of deep tendon reflexes, progressing to respiratory paralysis over hours
options.B: Massive immediate local swelling and bullae with no systemic effect
options.C: Rapid consumptive coagulopathy with diffuse bleeding within an hour
options.D: An immediate hemorrhagic blistering wound with no neurologic signs

exp: Coral snake (Elapidae) envenomation primarily causes neurotoxicity — cranial nerve dysfunction and loss of deep tendon reflexes that can progress to respiratory depression and paralysis over hours — often with minimal local findings. Massive local swelling/bullae (B) and rapid consumptive coagulopathy with bleeding (C, D) are features of Crotalinae (pit viper) envenomation. Because the therapy differs, distinguishing coral snake from pit viper bites is essential.
hy: Coral snake = neurotoxic with minimal local signs; watch for delayed respiratory failure.

stem: The folk rhyme "red on yellow, kill a fellow; red on black, venom lack" distinguishes which snake?

options.A: The coral snake (Micrurus sp.), whose touching red-and-yellow bands indicate the venomous species
options.B: The copperhead, whose banding predicts envenomation severity
options.C: The cottonmouth, by the color of its terminal rattle
options.D: The rattlesnake, by the number of its rattle segments

exp: The rhyme distinguishes the venomous North American coral snake (Micrurus sp.), in which red bands touch yellow bands ("red on yellow, kill a fellow"), from harmless mimics where red touches black. It does not describe the copperhead (B), cottonmouth (C), or rattlesnake rattle (D). Coral snakes are Elapidae and neurotoxic.
hy: "Red on yellow, kill a fellow" = venomous coral snake.
src: Sabiston Ch 21, "Venomous Species Indigenous to United States"

stem: On the snakebite severity grading scale, which finding corresponds to a grade 0 envenomation?

options.A: A fang mark with local swelling/ecchymosis <2.5 cm, minimal pain, and no systemic symptoms
options.B: Swelling and erythema of 15–40 cm with mild systemic symptoms
options.C: Swelling >40 cm with petechiae, bullae, and disseminated intravascular coagulation
options.D: Coma, shock, and paralysis from multiple envenomation sites

exp: Grade 0 on the snakebite severity scale is a fang mark with local swelling/ecchymosis <2.5 cm, minimal pain and tenderness, and no systemic symptoms. Swelling 15–40 cm with mild systemic symptoms (B) is grade 2, swelling >40 cm with petechiae/bullae/DIC (C) is grade 3, and coma/shock/paralysis from multiple sites (D) is grade 4. The scale guides the need for antivenin.
hy: Grade 0 = fang mark, <2.5 cm swelling, no systemic signs.

stem: On the same grading scale, which features define a grade 4 (most severe) envenomation?

options.A: Multiple envenomation sites with severe systemic signs — coma, shock, bleeding, DIC, and paralysis
options.B: Local swelling <2.5 cm with no systemic symptoms
options.C: Swelling and erythema of 5–15 cm without systemic symptoms
options.D: A fang mark with no pain or swelling

exp: Grade 4 (most severe) is defined by multiple envenomation sites with severe systemic signs — coma, shock, bleeding, DIC, and paralysis. Local swelling <2.5 cm with no systemic symptoms (B) is grade 0, swelling 5–15 cm without systemic symptoms (C) is grade 1, and a fang mark without pain/swelling (D) is grade 0. Higher grades favor antivenin administration.
hy: Grade 4 = severe systemic signs (coma/shock/DIC/paralysis), multiple sites.

stem: Which first-aid measure for snakebite is now considered outdated or harmful and should NOT be used?

options.A: Incision and suction ("X-cut" aspiration), tourniquets/constrictor bands, ice cryotherapy, and electrical stimulation
options.B: Cleaning the wound locally and elevating the limb to heart level
options.C: Marking the leading edge of swelling and reassessing every 15 minutes
options.D: Transporting the patient to a hospital for antivenin assessment

exp: Table 21.1 lists outdated/harmful modalities — incision-and-suction (X-cut aspiration), tourniquets/constrictor bands, ice/cryotherapy, electrical stimulation, prophylactic fasciotomy, steroids, heat — none of which should be used. Cleaning and elevating the limb (B), marking the swelling and reassessing every 15 minutes (C), and transporting for antivenin assessment (D) are all appropriate. The mainstay is removal from danger, local wound care, and rapid transport.
hy: No tourniquet, ice, suction, X-cut, or electrical shock for snakebite.

stem: Why is CroFab dosing based on the estimated degree of envenomation rather than the patient's weight?

options.A: Dosing must supply enough antibody to neutralize the injected venom (antigen), which is independent of patient mass — hence 4–6 vials initially in children and adults alike
options.B: CroFab is weight-based like most drugs, dosed in mg/kg
options.C: The antivenin only works in adults, so pediatric dosing differs
options.D: Venom quantity correlates directly with body weight

exp: Antivenin neutralizes venom by antibody-to-antigen binding, so the dose must match the amount of venom injected — which is independent of the patient's mass — hence the standard 4–6 vials initially in both children and adults. It is therefore not weight-based (B), works in both adults and children (C), and venom quantity does not scale with body weight (D).
hy: CroFab is dosed to the venom load, not patient weight (4–6 vials initially).

stem: After the initial CroFab bolus controls envenomation, what is the recommended maintenance regimen?

options.A: Two vials every 6 hours for three additional doses
options.B: A single repeat vial at 24 hours
options.C: Continuous infusion until coagulopathy resolves
options.D: No further dosing is ever given after the first bolus

exp: After the initial bolus controls envenomation, maintenance dosing is two vials of CroFab every 6 hours for three additional doses (hours 6, 12, and 18). A single 24-hour repeat (B), continuous infusion (C), and no further dosing (D) are not the recommended schedule. The bolus is repeated first if symptoms progress before maintenance begins.
hy: CroFab maintenance: 2 vials q6h × 3 doses after control.

stem: Which statement about antivenin administration is correct?

options.A: It is time-sensitive (earlier is more effective) and carries a risk of anaphylaxis, so it is given where epinephrine and airway support are ready; pregnancy is not a contraindication
options.B: It is equally effective whenever given, so timing does not matter
options.C: Pregnancy is an absolute contraindication to CroFab
options.D: It should be withheld until coagulopathy has fully developed

exp: Antivenin is time-sensitive — earlier administration is more effective — and carries a risk of anaphylaxis, so it must be given where epinephrine, steroids, antihistamine, and airway support are available; pregnancy is not a contraindication to CroFab. It is not equally effective at any time (B), pregnancy does not contraindicate it (C), and it should be started early rather than waiting for coagulopathy (D).
hy: Give antivenin early, with anaphylaxis precautions; pregnancy is not a contraindication.

stem: How should the coagulopathy of Crotalinae envenomation be managed?

options.A: Give antivenin first (it treats the primary cause); reserve blood products for coagulopathy not reversed by antivenin or active hemorrhage, and avoid elective surgery for ~2 weeks
options.B: Transfuse blood products before any antivenin is given
options.C: Coagulopathy never occurs with snakebite, so no monitoring is needed
options.D: Proceed with elective surgery immediately to control bleeding

exp: Antivenin is given first because treating the primary cause (venom) is paramount; blood products are reserved for coagulopathy not reversed by antivenin or for active significant hemorrhage, and because coagulopathy can persist ~2 weeks, elective surgery is avoided during that window. Transfusing before antivenin (B), denying that coagulopathy occurs (C), and rushing to elective surgery (D) are all incorrect.
hy: Antivenin before blood products; coagulopathy lasts ~2 weeks → defer elective surgery.

stem: What is the role of fasciotomy in snake envenomation?

options.A: It is very rarely required (most venom is subcutaneous); prophylactic fasciotomy is disproven, antivenin reduces compartment syndrome, and injured muscle should not be prematurely debrided
options.B: Prophylactic fasciotomy should be performed on every envenomated limb
options.C: Early aggressive muscle debridement improves outcomes because viability is easy to judge
options.D: Fasciotomy replaces antivenin as the primary treatment

exp: Because most venom is deposited subcutaneously, fasciotomy is rarely needed; prophylactic fasciotomy is disproven (and may worsen myonecrosis), antivenin itself reduces compartment syndrome, and injured muscle should not be prematurely debrided because the usual viability signs are unreliable in envenomation. Routine prophylactic fasciotomy (B), early aggressive debridement (C), and fasciotomy replacing antivenin (D) are all wrong. Fasciotomy is reserved for documented/highly likely compartment syndrome despite adequate antivenin.
hy: Snakebite fasciotomy is rare; no prophylactic fasciotomy, don't debride muscle early.

stem: Which animal is responsible for the majority of mammalian bites in the United States?

options.A: Dogs (80%–90% of bites), with pit bulls and Rottweilers accounting for most fatal dog bites
options.B: Cats, accounting for ~80% of bites
options.C: Humans, accounting for the majority of bites
options.D: Horses and cattle, which cause most bite wounds

exp: Dogs are responsible for 80%–90% of mammalian bites in the United States (≈4.7 million dog bites/year), and pit bulls and Rottweilers account for most fatal dog bites. Cats (B) and humans (C) follow dogs in frequency, and although horses/cattle (D) cause most nonhuman mammal deaths, those are from trauma rather than bites. Children, especially boys 5–9, sustain most reported bites.
hy: Dogs = 80–90% of US bites; pit bulls/Rottweilers cause most fatal ones.

stem: Which organism is the most common bacterial pathogen in dog and cat bite wounds?

options.A: Pasteurella species (found in ~50% of dog and ~75% of cat bites)
options.B: Eikenella corrodens, found mainly in dog bites
options.C: Pseudomonas aeruginosa
options.D: Clostridium tetani

exp: Pasteurella species are the most common bacterial pathogen in dog and cat bites, found in ~50% of dog and ~75% of cat bite wounds (infected wounds average five isolates with mixed aerobes/anaerobes). Eikenella corrodens (B) characterizes human bites, and Pseudomonas (C) and Clostridium tetani (D) are not the predominant bite pathogens.
hy: Pasteurella = most common dog/cat bite pathogen (≈50%/75%).

stem: A human bite wound is characteristically contaminated with which organism in addition to the usual flora?

options.A: Eikenella corrodens
options.B: Pasteurella multocida
options.C: Bartonella henselae
options.D: Capnocytophaga canimorsus

exp: Human bite wounds are typically contaminated with Eikenella corrodens in addition to the staphylococci, streptococci, and anaerobes found in other bites. Pasteurella (B) typifies dog/cat bites, Bartonella henselae (C) causes cat-scratch disease, and Capnocytophaga canimorsus (D) is associated with dog bites in asplenic patients — not the characteristic human-bite organism.
hy: Human bites → Eikenella corrodens.

stem: Which approach to closure of bite wounds is correct?

options.A: Primary closure for head/neck wounds seen within 24 h (and low-risk arm/leg/trunk wounds within 6–12 h); delayed primary closure after 3–5 days for wounds seen >24 h
options.B: All bite wounds should be closed primarily regardless of timing
options.C: No bite wound should ever be closed primarily
options.D: Puncture wounds should always be sutured tightly

exp: Primary closure is appropriate for head/neck wounds seen within 24 hours (good cosmesis, low infection) and for low-risk arm/leg/trunk wounds within 6–12 hours; wounds seen after 24 hours (or >6 h with ear/nose cartilage) are dressed and undergo delayed primary closure after 3–5 days. Closing all wounds regardless of timing (B), never closing any (C), and suturing puncture wounds tightly (D) are all incorrect. Puncture wounds are not sutured.
hy: Face <24 h and low-risk trunk/limb <6–12 h → primary closure; later → delayed primary.

stem: How should bite wounds to the hand be managed?

options.A: Generally left open to heal by secondary intention (≈ one-third of dog hand bites become infected), with immobilization, elevation, and often empirical antibiotics
options.B: Always closed primarily to optimize cosmetic outcome
options.C: Treated identically to low-risk facial wounds
options.D: Never given antibiotics, as hand bites rarely become infected

exp: Hand bites have a much higher infection rate (≈ one-third of dog hand bites become infected), so they are generally left open to heal by secondary intention, with immobilization, a bulky dressing, elevation, and often empirical antibiotics. They are not closed primarily for cosmesis (B), not treated like low-risk facial wounds (C), and antibiotics are warranted rather than withheld (D).
hy: Hand bites: leave open, immobilize/elevate, consider antibiotics.

stem: A patient has a laceration over the dorsum of the metacarpophalangeal joint sustained by striking another person's teeth. Which statement is correct?

options.A: This is a clenched-fist ("fight bite") injury treated as such regardless of history, because of extensor tendon/joint capsule injury and oral contamination; deeper/infected wounds need OR exploration and IV antibiotics
options.B: It is a trivial laceration that should be sutured and discharged
options.C: The extensor tendon should be examined only with the hand fully open
options.D: Antibiotics are unnecessary because the mouth has little bacteria

exp: A laceration over the MCP dorsum from striking teeth is a clenched-fist ("fight bite") injury and is treated as such regardless of the history given, because of likely extensor tendon/joint-capsule injury and heavy oral bacterial contamination; deeper or infected wounds need OR exploration, debridement, and IV antibiotics. It is not a trivial laceration to suture and discharge (B); the extensor tendon must be examined in both open and clenched positions because it retracts (C); and the mouth is heavily colonized, so antibiotics are needed (D).
hy: MCP-dorsum laceration = fight bite → assume tendon/joint injury, OR + IV antibiotics.

stem: Approximately what proportion of dog versus cat bite wounds become infected?

options.A: Dog bites 3%–18%; cat bites ~50%
options.B: Dog bites ~50%; cat bites 3%–18%
options.C: Both ~1%
options.D: Both essentially 100%

exp: Roughly 3%–18% of dog bite wounds and approximately 50% of cat bite wounds become infected — cat bites are higher because they produce deep puncture wounds that are difficult to irrigate. Option B reverses these, and neither figure is ~1% (C) or ~100% (D). This drives the lower threshold for antibiotics in cat bites.
hy: Infection: dog bites 3–18%, cat bites ~50%.

stem: Which statement about rabies risk and exposure is correct?

options.A: All wild carnivores must be considered rabid, but birds and reptiles do not contract or transmit rabies; a healthy-appearing domestic animal need not trigger prophylaxis if it can be observed for 10 days
options.B: Birds and reptiles are the major reservoirs of rabies
options.C: Every domestic dog bite mandates immediate prophylaxis regardless of observation
options.D: Provoked attacks are more likely to be from rabid animals

exp: All wild carnivores must be presumed rabid, but birds and reptiles neither contract nor transmit rabies; a healthy-appearing domestic dog/cat does not require prophylaxis if it can be observed for 10 days. Birds/reptiles are not reservoirs (B), not every domestic bite mandates immediate prophylaxis (C), and unprovoked (not provoked) attacks are more likely from rabid animals (D). The local health department is consulted for domestic-animal, rodent, or lagomorph bites.
hy: Wild carnivores = presumed rabid; observe healthy domestic animals 10 days; birds/reptiles don't transmit.

stem: What is the correct rabies postexposure prophylaxis regimen in a previously unvaccinated, immunocompetent patient?

options.A: Passive immunization with rabies immune globulin 20 IU/kg (infiltrate as much as possible around the wound) plus active vaccine on days 0, 3, 7, and 14
options.B: Rabies immune globulin alone, with no vaccine
options.C: A single dose of vaccine on day 0 only
options.D: Vaccine given only after symptoms of rabies appear

exp: Postexposure prophylaxis in a previously unvaccinated, immunocompetent patient is passive immunization with rabies immune globulin 20 IU/kg (infiltrating as much as possible into and around the wound, the remainder IM at a site remote from the vaccine) plus active vaccine on days 0, 3, 7, and 14 (a five-dose schedule adding day 28 for the immunocompromised). RIG alone (B), a single day-0 vaccine (C), and waiting for symptoms (D) are all incorrect. Previously immunized patients need vaccine only on days 0 and 3 and no RIG.
hy: Rabies PEP: RIG 20 IU/kg (around wound) + vaccine days 0/3/7/14.

stem: Which infections can be transmitted specifically by human bites?

options.A: Hepatitis B and C, tuberculosis, syphilis, and HIV (rare, but possible with saliva containing HIV-positive blood)
options.B: Only Pasteurella, never viral pathogens
options.C: Rabies, which is the principal concern with human bites
options.D: Cat-scratch disease and cowpox

exp: Human bites can transmit hepatitis B and C, tuberculosis, syphilis, and HIV; HIV seroconversion is rare but possible when an open wound is exposed to saliva containing HIV-positive blood, prompting baseline and 6-month testing and consideration of prophylaxis. Human bites are not limited to Pasteurella (B), rabies is a concern with animal not human bites (C), and cat-scratch disease/cowpox (D) are animal-associated zoonoses.
hy: Human bites can transmit HBV, HCV, TB, syphilis, and (rarely) HIV.

stem: The black widow (Latrodectus mactans) produces which type of venom and presentation?

options.A: A neurotoxin (alpha-latrotoxin) that enhances neurotransmitter release, causing painful muscle spasms with minimal local findings
options.B: A dermonecrotic venom causing a spreading skin ulcer
options.C: A purely local irritant with no systemic effect
options.D: A hemotoxin causing consumptive coagulopathy

exp: The black widow (Latrodectus mactans) produces a neurotoxic venom whose major component, alpha-latrotoxin, enhances presynaptic neurotransmitter release, causing painful muscle spasms with minimal local findings. A dermonecrotic ulcer (B) is loxoscelism (brown recluse), a purely local irritant (C) does not fit, and a consumptive hemotoxic coagulopathy (D) describes pit-viper venom. The identifying mark is the red ventral hourglass.
hy: Black widow = alpha-latrotoxin → neuromuscular spasm, minimal local signs.

stem: A patient has severe abdominal muscle rigidity and cramping after a spider bite, but no rebound tenderness. Which envenomation does this suggest?

options.A: Latrodectism (black widow) — muscle spasm/abdominal rigidity that can mimic a surgical abdomen, but rebound is absent
options.B: Loxoscelism (brown recluse) — necrotic skin ulcer
options.C: Scorpion envenomation — local burning paresthesias only
options.D: Hymenoptera anaphylaxis

exp: Latrodectism classically produces severe spasm and rigidity of large muscle groups, including abdominal rigidity and cramps that can mimic a surgical abdomen — but rebound tenderness is absent, distinguishing it from peritonitis. Loxoscelism (B) causes a necrotic skin lesion, scorpion stings (C) cause local burning paresthesias, and Hymenoptera anaphylaxis (D) presents with urticaria/airway compromise rather than isolated abdominal rigidity.
hy: Black widow: abdominal rigidity mimics a surgical abdomen, but no rebound.
src: Sabiston Ch 21, "Black Widow Spiders: Clinical Manifestations"

stem: Which statement about treatment of black widow envenomation is correct?

options.A: Narcotics and benzodiazepines relieve muscle pain; IV calcium gluconate has no significant efficacy; antivenom is reserved for severe cases (e.g., pregnancy, age <16 or >60, uncontrolled symptoms)
options.B: IV calcium gluconate is the proven first-line therapy
options.C: Antivenom is given routinely to every patient
options.D: Early wide surgical excision of the bite is required

exp: Narcotics and benzodiazepines are the more effective agents for the muscle pain of latrodectism; IV calcium gluconate (once first-line) has no significant efficacy; and antivenom — which can cause anaphylactoid reactions/serum sickness — is reserved for serious cases (pregnant women, children <16, adults >60, or severe envenomation with uncontrolled hypertension/respiratory distress). Calcium gluconate is not proven first-line (B), antivenom is not routine (C), and surgery has no role (D).
hy: Latrodectism: benzodiazepines/opioids for pain; antivenom only for severe/high-risk cases; calcium gluconate ineffective.

stem: Loxoscelism (brown recluse, Loxosceles reclusa) is mediated chiefly by which venom component, producing what effect?

options.A: Sphingomyelinase D, causing dermonecrosis and hemolysis
options.B: Alpha-latrotoxin, causing neuromuscular spasm
options.C: A sodium-channel neurotoxin causing autonomic storm
options.D: A metalloproteinase causing consumptive coagulopathy

exp: Loxoscelism (brown recluse, Loxosceles reclusa) is mediated chiefly by sphingomyelinase D, a phospholipase that attacks erythrocyte, platelet, and endothelial membranes to cause dermonecrosis and hemolysis. Alpha-latrotoxin (B) is the black widow toxin, a sodium-channel neurotoxin (C) describes scorpion venom, and a metalloproteinase (D) describes pit-viper venom. The identifying mark is the violin-shaped marking on the cephalothorax.
hy: Brown recluse = sphingomyelinase D → dermonecrosis + hemolysis.

stem: Which statement about brown recluse envenomation is correct?

options.A: A central necrotic lesion with eschar may develop over days; systemic loxoscelism (hemolysis, DIC, renal failure) is more common in children and can occur with minimal local findings
options.B: The bite is always immediately painful and easily identified
options.C: Systemic involvement never occurs
options.D: Necrosis is least severe in fatty areas such as the thigh

exp: Brown recluse bites can evolve over hours to days into a central necrotic lesion with an eschar; systemic loxoscelism (hemolysis, DIC, renal failure) is more common in children and can occur with minimal local findings. The bite is often initially painless/unnoticed rather than immediately painful (B), systemic involvement does occur (C), and necrosis is in fact most severe in fatty areas such as the abdomen and thigh (D).
hy: Brown recluse: delayed necrotic eschar; systemic loxoscelism worse in children.
src: Sabiston Ch 21, "Brown Recluse Spiders: Clinical Manifestations"

stem: What is the recommended surgical approach to a brown recluse necrotic lesion?

options.A: Avoid early surgery; manage with cold compresses, elevation, and observation, performing debridement only after eschar margins are defined (1–2 weeks)
options.B: Perform wide excision immediately on presentation
options.C: Apply heat to limit ulcer development
options.D: Excise and skin-graft within the first 24 hours in all cases

exp: Early surgery is avoided because the eventual extent of necrosis cannot be predicted and early excision is overaggressive and can promote pyoderma gangrenosum; management is conservative (cold compresses, elevation, observation) with debridement deferred until eschar margins are defined at 1–2 weeks. Immediate wide excision (B), heat application (which worsens damage, C), and 24-hour excision/grafting (D) are all incorrect.
hy: Brown recluse: avoid early surgery; debride only after eschar demarcates (1–2 wk).

stem: Dapsone has been suggested for severe loxoscelism. Which caution applies?

options.A: It can cause methemoglobinemia and is contraindicated in glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency, so G6PD levels are checked; its risks likely outweigh benefits
options.B: It is fully approved for brown recluse bites and carries no risk
options.C: It is safe in G6PD deficiency
options.D: It is the first-line treatment for all brown recluse bites in children

exp: Dapsone (proposed to limit necrosis via neutrophil inhibition) can cause methemoglobinemia and is contraindicated in G6PD deficiency, so G6PD levels are checked before use; given conflicting efficacy data and its side-effect profile, its risks likely outweigh benefits. It is not approved/risk-free for this indication (B), not safe in G6PD deficiency (C), and not first-line in children (it is not approved for children) (D).
hy: Dapsone for loxoscelism: risk of methemoglobinemia; contraindicated in G6PD deficiency.

stem: Which scorpion is the only potentially dangerous species in the United States, and how does its venom act?

options.A: The bark scorpion (Centruroides sculpturatus); its neurotoxin acts on sodium and potassium channels, causing massive autonomic neurotransmitter release
options.B: The emperor scorpion; its venom is hemotoxic
options.C: The bark scorpion; its venom acts purely locally with no systemic effect
options.D: All US scorpions are equally lethal through enzymatic venom

exp: The bark scorpion (Centruroides sculpturatus), found in Arizona/northern Mexico/southern California, is the only potentially dangerous US scorpion; its neurotoxin acts on sodium and potassium channels, causing massive autonomic neurotransmitter release and possible rapid systemic toxicity. The emperor scorpion (B) is not the dangerous US species, the venom is not purely local (C), and not all US scorpions are equally lethal via enzymatic venom (D) — neurotoxic venoms have little enzymatic activity.
hy: Bark scorpion (Centruroides) = only dangerous US scorpion; ion-channel neurotoxin.

stem: Which statement about bark scorpion stings is correct?

options.A: Young children are at greatest risk for severe stings; systemic envenomation warrants ICU monitoring, and an FDA-approved equine Fab antivenin (Anascorp) reduces systemic symptoms
options.B: Adults are at greatest risk and children are spared
options.C: There is no antivenin available in the United States
options.D: Routine fasciotomy is the treatment of choice

exp: Young children are at greatest risk for severe bark scorpion stings; systemic envenomation warrants supportive ICU monitoring of cardiorespiratory status, and an FDA-approved equine-derived Fab antivenin, Centruroides Immune F(ab′)₂ (Anascorp), reduces systemic symptoms. Adults are not the highest-risk group (B), antivenin is available in the US (C), and fasciotomy has no role (D).
hy: Bark scorpion: kids highest risk; Anascorp Fab antivenin for systemic signs.

stem: What is the correct technique for tick removal?

options.A: Grasp the tick by the body as close to the skin as possible with an instrument and apply gradual, gentle axial traction without twisting or crushing
options.B: Apply a lit match or gasoline to make the tick detach
options.C: Twist the tick rapidly to unscrew it
options.D: Crush the tick in place to kill it before removal

exp: Proper tick removal is to grasp the tick by the body as close to the skin as possible with an instrument and apply gradual, gentle axial traction without twisting; the tick must not be crushed (which can squeeze infectious secretions into the wound). Lit matches/gasoline (B), rapid twisting (C), and crushing (D) are ineffective or harmful. Removal within 24 hours makes transmission very unlikely.
hy: Tick removal: grasp at skin, steady axial traction, no twisting/crushing.

stem: Which describes the erythema migrans rash of early Lyme disease (Borrelia burgdorferi)?

options.A: A round/oval erythematous lesion beginning at the bite, expanding up to ~1 cm/day to a median ~15 cm, often with central clearing, appearing in ≥80% of patients
options.B: A fixed, non-expanding purpuric rash that never clears centrally
options.C: A vesicular rash limited to the palms and soles
options.D: A rash that appears only after neurologic symptoms develop

exp: Erythema migrans is the early Lyme rash: a round/oval erythematous lesion that begins at the bite and expands rapidly (up to ~1 cm/day) to a median ~15 cm, often with central clearing, occurring in at least 80% of patients after a 3–30-day incubation. A fixed non-clearing purpuric rash (B), a palm/sole vesicular rash (C), and a rash appearing only after neurologic symptoms (D) do not describe it. Classic erythema migrans in an endemic area can be treated without serologic confirmation.
hy: Erythema migrans: expanding (~1 cm/day) ~15 cm rash with central clearing in ≥80%.

stem: What is the first-line treatment of early or disseminated Lyme disease without neurologic involvement, and the alternative for children ≤8 or pregnant women?

options.A: Oral doxycycline for 14–21 days; amoxicillin (or cefuroxime axetil) as the alternative
options.B: IV ceftriaxone for all cases regardless of stage
options.C: Oral azithromycin for 3 days
options.D: No antibiotics, as Lyme disease always self-resolves

exp: First-line treatment of early or disseminated Lyme disease without neurologic involvement is oral doxycycline for 14–21 days; amoxicillin (or cefuroxime axetil) is the alternative for children ≤8 years and pregnant women, each curing >90%. IV ceftriaxone (B) is reserved for neuroborreliosis or cardiac manifestations, azithromycin (C) is not first-line, and Lyme disease is not left untreated (D).
hy: Lyme: oral doxycycline 14–21 d; amoxicillin for kids ≤8/pregnancy; ceftriaxone for neuro/cardiac.

stem: Regarding prophylaxis after a tick bite, which statement is correct?

options.A: A single 200-mg oral dose of doxycycline within 72 hours can further reduce the already low (~1.4%) transmission risk
options.B: All tick bites require a full 21-day antibiotic course prophylactically
options.C: Prophylaxis is never beneficial under any circumstances
options.D: The risk of transmission after a tick bite exceeds 50%

exp: A single 200-mg oral dose of doxycycline given within 72 hours of a tick bite can further reduce the already low (~1.4%) transmission risk, making routine full courses unnecessary. A 21-day prophylactic course for every bite (B), the claim that prophylaxis is never beneficial (C), and a >50% transmission risk (D) are all incorrect. The decision remains somewhat controversial.
hy: Lyme prophylaxis: single doxycycline 200 mg within 72 h; baseline risk ~1.4%.

stem: Which manifestations characterize disseminated/untreated Lyme disease and their approximate frequencies?

options.A: Neuroborreliosis in ~15% (e.g., facial nerve palsy), cardiac involvement in ~5% (AV block/myocarditis), and oligoarticular arthritis in ~60%
options.B: Renal failure in ~80% of untreated patients
options.C: Permanent blindness in the majority of patients
options.D: No extracutaneous manifestations ever occur

exp: In disseminated/untreated Lyme disease, neuroborreliosis occurs in ~15% (e.g., uni- or bilateral facial nerve palsy, lymphocytic meningitis), cardiac involvement in ~5% (AV nodal block, myocarditis), and oligoarticular arthritis (especially the knee) in ~60%. Renal failure in ~80% (B), majority blindness (C), and no extracutaneous disease (D) are incorrect. These findings may resolve with or without treatment but can progress to chronic Lyme.
hy: Untreated Lyme: neuro ~15%, cardiac ~5%, arthritis ~60%.

stem: Which statement about Hymenoptera (bees, wasps, ants) stings in the United States is correct?

options.A: They cause more human fatalities than snake and mammalian bites combined, most often from anaphylaxis
options.B: They are essentially never fatal in the United States
options.C: They kill more people than all other causes via direct venom toxicity, not allergy
options.D: Fire ants cause most fatalities through direct neurotoxicity

exp: In the United States, Hymenoptera (bees, wasps, yellow jackets, hornets, stinging ants) cause more human fatalities than snake and mammalian bites combined, most often through anaphylaxis. They are not essentially nonfatal (B); the deaths are predominantly allergic/anaphylactic rather than from direct venom toxicity (C); and winged Hymenoptera, not fire ants alone, drive most fatalities (D).
hy: Hymenoptera kill more in the US than snakes + mammals combined — via anaphylaxis.

stem: Which statement about Hymenoptera stinging apparatus and venom is correct?

options.A: Some bees have barbed stingers that detach (allowing only a single sting), while wasps/hornets can sting repeatedly; fire ant venom is mostly nonallergenic alkaloids causing pustules
options.B: All Hymenoptera can sting only once
options.C: Fire ant venom is predominantly allergenic protein
options.D: Bee venom contains no allergenic components

exp: Some bees have barbed stingers that detach (limiting them to a single sting), while wasps/hornets retain their stingers and can sting repeatedly; fire ant venom is mostly nonallergenic alkaloids that release histamine and cause characteristic pustules (allergenic protein is only ~0.1%). Not all Hymenoptera sting only once (B), fire ant venom is not predominantly allergenic protein (C), and bee venom does contain allergens such as melittin and phospholipases (D).
hy: Barbed bee stingers = single sting; fire ant venom = alkaloid (pustules), little allergen.

stem: What is the approximate incidence of bee-sting anaphylaxis in the general population, and its time course?

options.A: 0.3%–3%, responsible for ~40 deaths annually in the US, with symptoms usually within minutes to 1 hour
options.B: ~50% of the population, with deaths occurring after several days
options.C: Essentially 0%, as anaphylaxis does not occur from stings
options.D: ~25%, with symptoms only after 24 hours

exp: Bee-sting anaphylaxis develops in 0.3%–3% of the general population and is responsible for ~40 reported deaths annually in the US, with symptoms usually beginning within minutes and fatalities most often within 1 hour (mostly in adults). A 50% incidence with delayed deaths (B), 0% (C), and 25% at 24 hours (D) are incorrect. A positive IgE skin test helps predict allergic reactions.
hy: Bee-sting anaphylaxis: 0.3–3%, ~40 US deaths/yr, usually within 1 hour.

stem: How is a Hymenoptera-sting reaction managed?

options.A: Remove any stinger quickly; treat anaphylaxis with IM epinephrine (0.01 mg/kg, up to 0.5 mg, 1:1000); a large local reaction gets a 5-day prednisone course; refer patients with systemic reactions for venom immunotherapy
options.B: Epinephrine is given for all isolated local reactions
options.C: Stingers should be left in place to avoid spreading venom
options.D: Immunotherapy is contraindicated after a systemic reaction

exp: Management: remove any retained stinger quickly to limit venom; treat anaphylaxis with IM epinephrine (0.01 mg/kg, up to 0.5 mg, 1:1000) plus antihistamine; treat a large local reaction with a 5-day prednisone course; and refer patients with systemic reactions for venom immunotherapy. Epinephrine is not needed for isolated local reactions (B), stingers should be removed rather than left (C), and immunotherapy is indicated (not contraindicated) after systemic reactions (D).
hy: Sting: remove stinger; IM epinephrine for anaphylaxis; prednisone for large local; refer for immunotherapy.

stem: Which pathogen is of primary concern in Gulf of Mexico marine wound infections, especially in cirrhotic or immunocompromised hosts?

options.A: Vibrio species (e.g., Vibrio vulnificus); Aeromonas species are the analogous concern in fresh water
options.B: Clostridium perfringens
options.C: Pasteurella multocida
options.D: Streptococcus pyogenes exclusively

exp: In the Gulf of Mexico, Vibrio species (notably Vibrio vulnificus) are the primary concern in marine wound infections, especially in cirrhotic or otherwise immunocompromised hosts, while in fresh water the related Aeromonas species are the aggressive pathogen. Clostridium perfringens (B), Pasteurella (C, a bite pathogen), and Streptococcus pyogenes alone (D) are not the characteristic marine concerns. The lab is alerted to use appropriate culture media.
hy: Gulf marine wounds → Vibrio (vulnificus); fresh water → Aeromonas.
src: Sabiston Ch 21, "Marine: Microbiology of Marine-Related Soft Tissue Infections"

stem: Which antibiotic class provides appropriate empirical coverage for marine soft-tissue infections including Vibrio?

options.A: Third- and fourth-generation cephalosporins (with agents such as ciprofloxacin, doxycycline, or TMP-SMX as alternatives)
options.B: Oral penicillin V alone
options.C: Topical bacitracin only
options.D: First-generation cephalosporins as monotherapy

exp: Third- and fourth-generation cephalosporins provide adequate empirical gram-positive and gram-negative coverage for ocean pathogens including Vibrio, with ciprofloxacin, doxycycline, TMP-SMX, gentamicin, or carbapenems as acceptable alternatives (often in combination for significant infections). Oral penicillin V (B), topical bacitracin (C), and first-generation cephalosporin monotherapy (D) do not cover the relevant marine gram-negatives.
hy: Marine infections: 3rd/4th-gen cephalosporins (cover Vibrio); add doxy/cipro/TMP-SMX.

stem: How should most marine-related wounds be closed?

options.A: Generally by secondary intention or delayed primary closure after aggressive irrigation and debridement, given the high infection risk
options.B: Always by immediate tight primary closure
options.C: With cyanoacrylate glue regardless of contamination
options.D: Left completely untreated, as marine wounds rarely become infected

exp: Because of the high risk of serious infection, marine-related wounds are generally managed by secondary intention or delayed primary closure after aggressive irrigation and debridement of nonviable tissue. Immediate tight primary closure (B), glue closure regardless of contamination (C), and leaving wounds untreated (D) all increase infection risk. The cosmetic benefit of closure is balanced against infection risk.
hy: Marine wounds: secondary intention or delayed primary closure after debridement.
src: Sabiston Ch 21, "Marine: General Management / Wound Care"

stem: What is the appropriate initial decontamination for a jellyfish/coelenterate sting, particularly the box jellyfish (Chironex fleckeri)?

options.A: Apply dilute (5%) acetic acid (vinegar) for ~30 minutes to inactivate nematocysts
options.B: Rinse vigorously with fresh water and rub the area to remove stingers
options.C: Apply heat immediately to denature the toxin
options.D: Inject the limb with calcium gluconate

exp: For a coelenterate/jellyfish sting — critically for the box jellyfish (Chironex fleckeri) — dilute 5% acetic acid (vinegar) is applied for ~30 minutes (or until pain relief) to inactivate undischarged nematocysts. Rinsing with fresh water or rubbing (B) makes nematocysts fire, immediate heat (C) is not the standard decontaminant, and injecting calcium gluconate (D) has no role. After decontamination, remaining nematocysts are shaved off.
hy: Jellyfish/box jellyfish: 5% acetic acid (vinegar) ~30 min to disarm nematocysts.

stem: Which action should be AVOIDED when treating a jellyfish sting?

options.A: Rinsing with fresh water or vigorously rubbing the area, which causes undischarged nematocysts to fire
options.B: Removing remaining nematocysts after applying shaving cream and shaving
