The distractors are adjacent negotiation terms not used here for the walk-away: the ZOPA is the overlap range where a deal is possible; "reservation premium" is a made-up term; and the anchor point is an opening figure meant to shape the counterpart's expectations, not your walk-away floor.
hy: BATNA = best alternative to a negotiated agreement (your walk-away).

stem: The chapter cites *Getting to Yes* (Fisher, Ury, and Patton) for the principles of "principled" negotiation. Which set of four principles does it list?

options.A: Separate the people from the problem; focus on interests not positions; invent options for mutual gain; insist on objective criteria
options.B: Win at all costs; never reveal your position; split the difference; accept the first offer
options.C: Anchor high; concede slowly; bluff when necessary; outlast your opponent
options.D: Define, measure, analyze, and improve the deal terms

exp: *Getting to Yes* (Fisher, Ury, and Patton) gives four principles of principled negotiation: separate the people from the problem; focus on interests not positions; invent options for mutual gain; and insist on objective criteria. The distractors are the opposite of principled negotiation: hard-bargaining/positional tactics (win at all costs, bluff, anchor-and-concede) are what the method is designed to replace, and the DMAIC-style "define/measure/analyze/improve" belongs to Six Sigma, not negotiation.
hy: Principled negotiation: people≠problem, interests not positions, options for mutual gain, objective criteria.

stem: First articulated by Michael Porter in a 2010 *New England Journal of Medicine* article, the central concept of value-based care can be summarized by which formulation?

options.A: Maximizing health outcomes achieved per unit of cost
options.B: Maximizing the volume of procedures performed per provider
options.C: Minimizing length of stay regardless of outcome
options.D: Maximizing revenue per relative value unit (RVU)

exp: Value-based care, as Michael Porter framed it in his 2010 *NEJM* article, is maximizing health outcomes achieved per unit of cost — so value rises by improving outcomes, lowering cost, or ideally both. The distractors describe the *volume*-based world value-based care is meant to replace: maximizing procedure volume or revenue per RVU rewards throughput regardless of outcome, and minimizing length of stay alone ignores the outcome side of the equation.
hy: Value = outcomes ÷ cost (Porter, 2010 NEJM).

stem: The chapter expresses value within plastic surgery as a ratio. What constitutes the numerator and the denominator of the value equation?

options.A: Numerator = clinical outcomes plus patient-reported outcomes; denominator = cost of services rendered
options.B: Numerator = total revenue; denominator = number of procedures
options.C: Numerator = cost of services; denominator = patient satisfaction
options.D: Numerator = complication rate; denominator = length of stay

exp: Within plastic surgery the value ratio places clinical outcomes plus patient-reported outcomes in the numerator and the full cost of services rendered (including financial toxicity — lost wages, out-of-pocket and opportunity costs) in the denominator. The distractors invert or corrupt the ratio: putting cost or complications in the numerator, or revenue over procedure count, describes productivity/volume metrics, not value.
hy: Value = (clinical + patient-reported outcomes) ÷ cost.

stem: The traditional US reimbursement environment that the value-based movement seeks to move away from is best described as:

options.A: Fee-for-service, in which payment is tied to volume of services
options.B: Capitation, in which a fixed sum is paid per enrolled patient
options.C: A bundled payment tied to a complete episode of care
options.D: Reference-based pricing benchmarked to Medicare rates

exp: Fee-for-service ties payment to the *volume* of services and is the predominant, misaligned-incentive environment the value movement seeks to move away from toward value-based reimbursement. The distractors are alternative models, not the volume-based status quo: capitation pays a fixed sum per patient, a bundled payment covers a whole episode, and reference-based pricing benchmarks to a set rate — each is a step *toward* value, not the fee-for-service baseline.
hy: Fee-for-service = volume-based reimbursement (the model VBC replaces).

stem: The chapter argues that value-based reimbursement will favor high-volume, specialized centers that harness economies of scale and focus on one condition (e.g., breast cancer, cleft lip/palate). What does it call these centers?

options.A: Integrated practice units (IPUs)
options.B: Accountable care organizations (ACOs)
options.C: Health maintenance organizations (HMOs)
options.D: Ambulatory surgical centers (ASCs)

exp: Integrated practice units (IPUs) are high-volume, specialized centers that harness economies of scale and focus on one condition (breast cancer, cleft lip/palate) rather than offering isolated services, and are favored under value-based reimbursement. The distractors are different organizational entities: ACOs are accountable-care payment networks, HMOs are insurance/managed-care structures, and ASCs are outpatient surgical facilities — none is Porter's condition-focused IPU.
hy: IPUs = condition-focused, high-volume integrated centers.

stem: The chapter emphasizes condition-specific patient-reported outcome measures (PROMs). Which instrument is correctly matched to its domain?

options.A: BREAST-Q for breast surgery, FACE-Q for facial surgery, BODY-Q for body contouring, CLEFT-Q for cleft lip/palate
options.B: FACE-Q for breast surgery and BREAST-Q for facial surgery
options.C: BODY-Q for cleft procedures and CLEFT-Q for body contouring
options.D: The SF-36 generic survey for all plastic surgery conditions

exp: The condition-specific PROMs named in the chapter map cleanly: BREAST-Q for breast (oncologic and aesthetic) surgery, FACE-Q for facial surgery, BODY-Q for body contouring, and CLEFT-Q for cleft lip/palate. The distractors swap the instruments (FACE-Q is not for breast, CLEFT-Q is not for body contouring) or substitute the SF-36, which is a *generic* health survey — the chapter stresses that *condition-specific* PROMs give the granular, valuable outcome detail.
hy: BREAST-Q/FACE-Q/BODY-Q/CLEFT-Q = condition-specific PROMs.

stem: In subdividing the cost of care, the chapter distinguishes fixed from variable costs. Which statement correctly characterizes variable costs?

options.A: They depend on the amount and intensity of care and include implants, medications, and supplies — often preference-sensitive and modifiable for short-term savings
options.B: They are constant regardless of care volume and are renegotiated only rarely
options.C: They consist solely of administrative overhead and real estate
options.D: They cannot be influenced by the clinician under any circumstances

exp: Variable costs depend on the amount and intensity of care — implants, medications, supplies — and because many are preference-sensitive to the clinician, they can be modified by administration to yield short-term savings. The distractors describe fixed costs instead (constant regardless of volume, renegotiated rarely, e.g., overhead/real estate) or wrongly claim the clinician can never influence them — the opposite of the preference-sensitive point.
hy: Variable costs = care-dependent, preference-sensitive (implants/meds/supplies).

stem: To estimate the true cost of an episode of care more accurately than traditional costing, the chapter recommends a method proposed by Robert Kaplan of Harvard Business School. What is this method?

options.A: Time-Driven Activity-Based Costing (TDABC)
options.B: Diagnosis-related group (DRG) weighting
options.C: Relative value unit (RVU) accounting
options.D: Charge-to-cost ratio estimation

exp: Time-Driven Activity-Based Costing (TDABC), proposed by Robert Kaplan of Harvard Business School, measures the actual resources and time staff spend delivering care across an episode, giving more precise cost estimates than traditional methods (validated in cardiac, orthopedic, urologic, and craniofacial surgery). The distractors are coarser, charge-based or unit-based accounting tools: DRG weighting, RVU accounting, and charge-to-cost ratios do not trace true resource consumption the way TDABC does.
hy: TDABC (Kaplan) = time-and-resource-based true cost estimation.

stem: The chapter argues that, from a value-based standpoint, cosmetic surgery inherently achieves high value. What feature is given as the principal reason?

options.A: Prices are completely transparent because patients pay directly, creating a market where they can shop between surgeons
options.B: A third-party payer absorbs most of the cost, shielding patients from price
options.C: Cosmetic procedures are medically urgent and therefore tightly regulated
options.D: Outcomes are unimportant since the procedures are elective

exp: Cosmetic surgery achieves inherently high value because prices are completely transparent — patients pay directly, can shop between surgeons, and surgeons compete to deliver safe, quality care at the lowest cost. The distractors describe the *opposite* dynamics that erode value in insured care: a third-party payer shrouds price and removes shopping, and the claims that cosmetic work is urgent/regulated or that outcomes don't matter contradict the chapter.
hy: Cosmetic surgery = transparent direct-pay market → high value.

stem: The chapter invokes "moral hazard" when a third-party payer covers most costs. What does moral hazard describe in this context?

options.A: Patients are enticed to overutilize health services because they bear little of the financial responsibility
options.B: Surgeons intentionally conceal complication rates from payers
options.C: Insurers deny claims to maximize their own profit
options.D: Patients avoid all care because they fear hidden costs

exp: Moral hazard here means patients are enticed to overutilize services because a third-party payer bears most of the cost, so they feel little financial responsibility (especially once deductibles are met). The distractors describe other problems — surgeons hiding complication data, insurer claim denial, or patients avoiding care from fear of cost — none of which is the overutilization-from-insulation mechanism that defines moral hazard.
hy: Moral hazard = insulation from cost → overutilization.

stem: The chapter describes a process that replaces paternalistic, information-asymmetric care by placing patients on equal footing to weigh risks, benefits, trade-offs, and cost. What is this process, and what concrete tool emerges from it?

options.A: Shared decision-making, which yields validated decision aids
options.B: Informed refusal, which yields liability waivers
options.C: Managed care, which yields utilization-review checklists
options.D: Capitation, which yields per-member budgets

exp: Shared decision-making replaces paternalistic, information-asymmetric care by putting patients on equal footing to deliberate over risks, benefits, trade-offs, and cost, and the concrete tool it yields is the validated decision aid (used in breast-cancer prophylactic-mastectomy/reconstruction and hand-surgery decisions). The distractors pair the wrong process with the wrong tool: informed refusal/waivers, managed care/utilization review, and capitation/per-member budgets are unrelated to the deliberative decision-aid concept.
hy: Shared decision-making → validated decision aids.

stem: Health economists express the cost-effectiveness of an innovation against a "willingness to pay" (WTP) per quality-adjusted life year (QALY). What WTP range per QALY does the chapter cite?

options.A: Approximately $100,000–150,000 per QALY
options.B: Approximately $5,000–10,000 per QALY
options.C: Approximately $500,000–1,000,000 per QALY
options.D: Approximately $25,000–50,000 per QALY

exp: The chapter cites a willingness-to-pay threshold of roughly $100,000–150,000 per QALY as the range health economists consider acceptable to society. The distractors are off by an order of magnitude in either direction ($5–10k or $25–50k are too low; $500k–1M is far too high) and do not match the figure the chapter gives.

stem: In cost-effectiveness analysis, the chapter describes a rare, ideal scenario in which a new technology is "dominant." What does a dominant innovation achieve?

options.A: Better outcomes at reduced cost
options.B: Better outcomes at higher cost
options.C: Equivalent outcomes at the same cost
options.D: Worse outcomes but at a much lower cost

exp: A "dominant" innovation is the rare, ideal case that achieves better outcomes at reduced cost — unambiguously worth adopting. The distractors describe the far more common or undesirable situations: better outcomes at higher cost (the usual trade-off requiring a WTP judgment), equivalent outcomes at equal cost (no value gain), or worse outcomes more cheaply (not "dominant").
hy: Dominant innovation = better outcomes AND lower cost.

stem: The chapter contrasts cost-effectiveness analysis with a related method that evaluates outcomes and costs at the level of an individual health system rather than society. What is this method called?

options.A: Budget-impact analysis
options.B: Time-Driven Activity-Based Costing
options.C: Sensitivity analysis
options.D: Markov modeling

exp: Budget-impact analysis is the variant of cost-effectiveness analysis that evaluates outcomes and costs at the level of an individual health system rather than society at large. The distractors are different analytic tools: TDABC is a costing method, sensitivity analysis tests how results vary with assumptions, and Markov modeling simulates disease-state transitions — none is the health-system-level budget lens described.
hy: Budget-impact analysis = cost-effectiveness at the individual health-system level.

stem: The field dedicated to understanding why providers and systems do or do not adopt current best evidence uses a named framework with multiple domains (intervention, evidence, context, individuals). Which framework does the chapter cite?

options.A: The Consolidated Framework for Implementation Research (CFIR)
options.B: The Plan-Do-Study-Act (PDSA) cycle
options.C: The Donabedian structure–process–outcome model
options.D: The RE-AIM evaluation framework

exp: The Consolidated Framework for Implementation Research (CFIR) is the named, multi-domain framework (the intervention and its evidence, the context, and the individuals involved) the chapter uses to understand why best evidence is or isn't adopted. The distractors are other QI/evaluation models: PDSA is a rapid-cycle improvement loop, Donabedian's structure–process–outcome is a quality-assessment triad, and RE-AIM evaluates dissemination — none is CFIR.
hy: CFIR = framework for understanding evidence adoption.

stem: The chapter gives a concrete "implementation gap" example where routine practice contradicts level I evidence. Which example is it?

options.A: Surgeons routinely prescribe antibiotics after breast prosthesis placement despite evidence that prolonged prophylaxis does not prevent implant infection
options.B: Surgeons routinely omit perioperative antibiotics despite evidence they prevent infection
options.C: Surgeons routinely use drains despite evidence they increase infection
options.D: Surgeons routinely avoid indocyanine green despite proven cost savings

exp: The chapter's implementation-gap example is that many surgeons continue to prescribe antibiotics after breast-prosthesis placement even though level I evidence shows prolonged post-perioperative prophylaxis does not prevent implant infection. The distractors reverse the evidence or substitute unrelated practices: omitting effective perioperative antibiotics, drain-related infection, and indocyanine green cost savings are not the antibiotic-overuse gap the chapter highlights.
hy: Antibiotics after breast implants persist despite level I evidence of no benefit.

stem: The chapter urges plastic surgery societies to identify and "de-implement" services that raise the cost of care with no known benefit (e.g., preoperative testing for low-risk procedures, routine EMG for carpal tunnel). Which national campaign exemplifies this de-implementation effort?

options.A: Choosing Wisely
options.B: Getting to Yes
options.C: The Joint Commission core measures
options.D: Meaningful Use

exp: Choosing Wisely is the national campaign exemplifying de-implementation — identifying tests/treatments that add cost without benefit (e.g., preoperative testing for low-risk procedures, routine EMG for carpal tunnel) — which the chapter urges plastic societies to embrace. The distractors are unrelated: Getting to Yes is a negotiation text, Joint Commission core measures and Meaningful Use are reporting/EHR programs, not low-value-care de-implementation campaigns.
hy: Choosing Wisely = de-implement low-value care.

stem: The chapter encourages participation in physician-developed alternative payment models (APMs) incentivized by a specific statute. Which statute, and roughly what share of Medicare expenditures is now tied to an APM mechanism?

options.A: MACRA (Medicare Access and CHIP Reauthorization Act); nearly 40% of Medicare expenditures
options.B: HIPAA; nearly 75% of Medicare expenditures
options.C: EMTALA; nearly 10% of Medicare expenditures
options.D: The Affordable Care Act; nearly 90% of Medicare expenditures

exp: The statute incentivizing physician-developed alternative payment models is MACRA (the Medicare Access and CHIP Reauthorization Act), and the chapter states nearly 40% of Medicare expenditures are now tied to an APM mechanism, a share expected to grow. The distractors misname the law and the figure: HIPAA governs privacy, EMTALA governs emergency care, and the ACA is insurance reform — and 75%, 10%, and 90% do not match the cited ~40%.
hy: MACRA drives APMs; ~40% of Medicare tied to an APM.

stem: Which set of payment mechanisms does the chapter list as examples of alternative payment models (APMs)?

options.A: Global budgets, accountable care organizations, bundled payments, and reference-based pricing
options.B: Fee-for-service, balance billing, and cash-only pricing
options.C: Health savings accounts, copays, and deductibles
options.D: Relative value units, charge masters, and DRG weights

exp: The APM examples the chapter lists are global budgets, accountable care organizations, bundled payments, and reference-based pricing — mechanisms that tie payment to value/episodes rather than volume. The distractors are the opposite: fee-for-service/balance billing/cash-only are volume- or charge-based; HSAs/copays/deductibles are cost-sharing tools; and RVUs/charge masters/DRG weights are fee-setting units — none is an APM.
hy: APMs = global budgets, ACOs, bundled payments, reference-based pricing.

stem: The chapter singles out bundled payments as the APM most associated with surgical care. Which two core elements characterize a bundled payment?

options.A: An agreed-upon target price covering all services in a care episode, plus an accountable entity bearing the spending and outcome risk
options.B: A per-visit fee with no cap, plus full indemnity for the surgeon
options.C: A capitated monthly premium, plus unlimited specialist referrals
options.D: A discount off charges, plus retrospective fee-for-service reconciliation

exp: A bundled payment is defined by two core elements: an agreed-upon target price (a lump sum or cap) covering all services across the sites of a care episode, and an accountable entity (surgeon or hospital) that bears the spending and clinical-outcome risk. The distractors describe non-bundled arrangements: an uncapped per-visit fee with indemnity, a capitated premium with unlimited referrals, and a charge discount with retrospective fee-for-service reconciliation are not episode-based target-price-plus-risk bundles.
hy: Bundle = target price for the episode + accountable risk-bearing entity.

stem: Underscoring why value matters to surgery, the chapter cites recent estimates of surgical care's share of Medicare spending. Approximately what fraction of all Medicare spending does surgical care account for?

options.A: About 51%
options.B: About 10%
options.C: About 25%
options.D: About 80%

exp: The chapter cites recent estimates that surgical care accounts for about 51% of all Medicare spending, underscoring why value-based care is salient to surgery. The distractors (10%, 25%, 80%) do not match the cited figure; 51% is the specific number tied to surgical care's share.
hy: Surgical care ≈ 51% of Medicare spending.

stem: What figure does the chapter give for US healthcare spending as a share of GDP (2017), in international context?

options.A: Nearly 18% of GDP — nearly double that of any other industrialized country
options.B: About 5% of GDP — comparable to most industrialized countries
options.C: About 35% of GDP — roughly triple the next-highest country
options.D: About 9% of GDP — slightly below the OECD average

exp: The chapter states the US spent nearly 18% of GDP on healthcare in 2017 — nearly double that of any other industrialized country — motivating the push for value. The distractors understate or wildly overstate this: ~5% or ~9% would put the US near or below peers (it is not), and ~35% triples the real figure.
hy: US ≈ 18% of GDP on healthcare (2017), ~double peer nations.

stem: The chapter underscores the personal financial burden of healthcare costs in the US. Which statement does it make about medical bills?

options.A: Financial obligations from medical care are the leading cause of individual bankruptcy in the US
options.B: Medical bills are a negligible contributor to personal bankruptcy
options.C: Medical bankruptcy occurs only among the uninsured
options.D: Bankruptcy from medical bills has been eliminated by the Affordable Care Act

exp: The chapter states that financial obligations from medical care are the leading cause of individual bankruptcy in the US, part of the escalating economic strain motivating value-based reform. The distractors minimize or misattribute this: medical bills are not a negligible contributor, medical bankruptcy is not limited to the uninsured, and it has not been eliminated by the ACA.
hy: Medical bills = leading cause of personal bankruptcy in the US.

stem: According to Fig. 6.1, fairly benchmarking reimbursement for procedural bundling requires that a service line have which characteristics?

options.A: Predictability in both cost and complications, with risk adjustment to attribute downside risk equitably
options.B: The highest possible procedural volume, regardless of complication variability
options.C: A single surgeon performing every step of care
options.D: Complete absence of any postoperative complications

exp: Per Fig. 6.1, fair benchmarking for procedural bundling requires predictability in both cost and complications, with risk adjustment so downside risk is attributed equitably — which is why predictable service lines (cosmetic, routine hand, breast reconstruction) bundle more readily than trauma/burn. The distractors miss this: chasing volume despite variable complications, single-surgeon care, or demanding zero complications are not the cost-and-complication-predictability criterion the figure depicts.
hy: Bundling needs predictability in cost AND complications (+ risk adjustment).

stem: The chapter argues surgical care is especially suited to a value-based framework. Which feature does it cite as supporting this, alongside its episodic nature and high cost variation?

options.A: An established culture of outcomes measurement through registries such as ACS-NSQIP and the STS Cardiac Surgery Registry
options.B: The absence of any interdisciplinary involvement in surgical care
options.C: Reimbursement that is already entirely value-based
options.D: A lack of measurable complications in most operations

exp: The chapter says surgical care suits a value-based framework partly because of an established culture of outcomes measurement through registries such as ACS-NSQIP, the STS Cardiac Surgery Registry, and TOPS — alongside its episodic nature, interdisciplinary delivery, and high cost/outcome variation. The distractors contradict the text: surgery is highly interdisciplinary (not devoid of it), is *not* already fully value-based, and does have measurable complications (the basis for the registries).
hy: Surgery suits VBC: episodic, interdisciplinary, outcomes-registry culture (ACS-NSQIP/STS/TOPS), high variation.

stem: The chapter frames clinical photography's role in plastic surgery in the strongest possible terms. Which statement reflects its stance?

options.A: Photography is the standard of care and a sine qua non for proper practice in plastic surgery
options.B: Photography is an optional adjunct best reserved for aesthetic cases
options.C: Photography is primarily a marketing tool with little documentary value
options.D: Photography is being rendered obsolete by the written operative note

exp: The chapter's synopsis states unequivocally that photography is standard of care and a sine qua non for proper plastic surgery practice, because the specialty is highly visual and depends on accurate representation of form and function to diagnose, plan, treat, and track outcomes. The distractors understate this: photography is not merely optional, not primarily promotional, and is not being replaced by the operative note — the image carries far more information (color, texture, contour) than words can.
hy: Clinical photography = standard of care / sine qua non in plastic surgery.

stem: To standardize magnification, the chapter specifies particular lens equivalents for facial versus body photographs. Which pairing is correct (in 35 mm film terms)?

options.A: Facial photos at the 100 mm lens equivalent; body photos at the 50 mm equivalent
options.B: Facial photos at the 50 mm equivalent; body photos at the 100 mm equivalent
options.C: Both facial and body photos at the 35 mm equivalent
options.D: Facial photos at the 200 mm equivalent; body photos at the 24 mm equivalent

exp: The standard is facial photographs at the 35 mm-film 100 mm-lens equivalent and body images at the 50 mm equivalent, which control magnification and minimize distortion. The distractors swap the values (50 for face / 100 for body is reversed), collapse both to 35 mm, or use extreme focal lengths (200/24 mm) the chapter does not endorse.
hy: Face = 100 mm equiv; body = 50 mm equiv.
src: Neligan I.7 · Standards in capturing images

stem: To avoid motion blur from subject movement, the chapter sets a minimum shutter speed for clinical photographs. What is it?

options.A: 1/60 of a second or faster
options.B: 1/4 of a second or faster
options.C: 1/2 of a second or faster
options.D: 2 seconds or faster

exp: To prevent blur from subject movement, shutter speed should be 1/60 of a second or faster (with adequate lighting to allow it). The distractors are far too slow: 1/4 s, 1/2 s, and 2 s would all produce motion blur in a standing clinical subject.
hy: Shutter speed ≥ 1/60 s.

stem: The chapter advises a particular aperture range to keep adequate depth of field. Which statement is correct?

options.A: An f-stop of 16 or higher (narrow aperture) should be used, as a narrow aperture increases depth of field
options.B: An f-stop of 1.4 (wide aperture) should be used, as a wide aperture increases depth of field
options.C: Aperture has no effect on depth of field, only on shutter speed
options.D: A wide aperture (low f-stop) is preferred to capture the crow's feet and submentum simultaneously

exp: A narrow aperture means a high f-stop, which increases depth of field; the chapter advises an f-stop of 16 or higher and consistency across pre- and postoperative images (darker skin may need a larger f-stop to avoid underexposure). The distractors invert the optics: f1.4 is a *wide* aperture that gives *shallow* depth of field; aperture certainly affects depth of field; and a wide aperture would blur, not sharpen, simultaneous near-and-far features like crow's feet and submentum.
hy: f-stop ≥16 (narrow aperture) → greater depth of field.
src: Neligan I.7 · Lens aperture and shutter speed

stem: Regarding sensor resolution, what does the chapter conclude is needed for clinical plastic surgery photographs?

options.A: More than ~6 megapixels are generally not required, though most modern cameras capture ≥20
options.B: A minimum of 50 megapixels is required for adequate documentation
options.C: Fewer than 1 megapixel is sufficient for all clinical purposes
options.D: Resolution is irrelevant; any phone camera meets the standard of care

exp: The chapter concludes that more than ~6 megapixels are generally not required for clinical photographs, even though most modern cameras capture ≥20 MP (and minimum arrays are now >3000 × 2000 = 6 MP). The distractors overstate the need (50 MP), understate it absurdly (<1 MP), or wrongly claim a phone meets the standard of care — the text says phone cameras remain inadequate.
hy: >6 MP not required (most cameras ≥20).

stem: The chapter specifies the optimal photographic background for the best skin tones and least exposure interference. Which background is recommended?

options.A: Medium blue or 18% gray, featureless
options.B: Pure white, to maximize brightness
options.C: Pure black, for maximum contrast
options.D: Green surgical towels

exp: A medium-blue or 18% gray featureless background gives the best skin tones and least exposure interference (standard blue surgical towels are close enough to ideal). The distractors are explicitly cautioned against: pure white and pure black skew metering and don't render natural skin color, and green surgical towels are specifically called unsuitable.
hy: Background = medium blue or 18% gray.

stem: In the anterior–posterior facial view, how does the chapter say the frame should be bounded superiorly and inferiorly?

options.A: About 2 cm (≈10% of image height) of background above the vertex; the inferior border stops just below the suprasternal notch
options.B: The frame is cropped exactly at the hairline above and the chin below
options.C: The superior border cuts the top of the skull; the inferior border ends at the lips
options.D: The whole body must be included from vertex to feet

exp: In the AP facial view, leave about 2 cm (≈10% of the image's vertical height) of background above the vertex, and stop the inferior border just below the suprasternal notch. The distractors crop too tightly (hairline-to-chin or skull-top-to-lips) or far too widely (whole body), none matching the chapter's vertex-to-suprasternal-notch framing.
hy: AP face: ~2 cm above vertex → just below suprasternal notch.

stem: The chapter uses a specific anatomic reference plane, kept parallel to the floor, to standardize head positioning. Which plane is it, and how is it defined?

options.A: The Frankfort horizontal plane, connecting the infra-orbital rim to the tragus
options.B: The Camper plane, connecting the nasal tip to the earlobe
options.C: The occlusal plane, connecting the upper and lower incisal edges
options.D: The Reid plane, connecting the glabella to the menton

exp: The Frankfort horizontal plane — connecting the infra-orbital rim to the tragus — is kept parallel to the floor to standardize head position. The distractors invent or misdefine planes: "Camper," "occlusal," and "Reid" are named for other landmarks, and only the Frankfort plane (infra-orbital rim ↔ tragus) is the standard used here.
hy: Frankfort plane = infra-orbital rim to tragus, parallel to floor.

stem: What is the standard basic set of facial views the chapter prescribes?

options.A: Five views — anterior–posterior, two obliques (right and left), and two laterals (right and left)
options.B: Two views — anterior–posterior and a single lateral
options.C: Three views — anterior–posterior, right lateral, and posterior
options.D: A single anterior–posterior view supplemented by close-ups

exp: The basic facial set is five views: anterior–posterior, right and left obliques, and right and left laterals. The distractors give incomplete sets (two or three views, or a single AP with close-ups) that omit the standardized oblique/lateral pairs.
hy: Standard face = 5 views (AP, 2 oblique, 2 lateral).

stem: The chapter corrects a common misconception about facial photography. Which statement is correct about perspective distortion?

options.A: Perspective distortion is determined exclusively by subject-to-camera distance; focal length affects subject size in the frame but not perspective
options.B: Perspective distortion is determined exclusively by the lens focal length, independent of distance
options.C: Perspective distortion is eliminated entirely by using a wide-angle lens up close
options.D: Perspective and optical distortion are the same phenomenon

exp: The key correction: perspective distortion is determined *exclusively* by subject-to-camera distance, while focal length only changes the subject's size within the frame — changing focal length affects perspective only *indirectly*, because you must move to keep framing constant. The distractors embody the very misconception the chapter debunks (blaming focal length), claim a wide-angle lens up close removes distortion (it worsens it), or conflate perspective with optical distortion.
hy: Perspective distortion = subject distance, NOT focal length.
src: Neligan I.7 · Focal length and distortion

stem: The chapter distinguishes optical distortion from perspective distortion. Which statement correctly characterizes optical distortion?

options.A: It is a lens artifact (e.g., barrel stretch from wide-angle lenses) that can usually be corrected in software and rarely affects facial distortion at normal distances
options.B: It is caused solely by the subject standing too far from the camera
options.C: It is impossible to correct and always dominates facial photographs
options.D: It is identical to perspective distortion and varies only with subject distance

exp: Optical distortion is a lens artifact — most notably barrel stretch from wide-angle lenses — that can usually be corrected in software and rarely affects facial distortion at normal shooting distances. The distractors misassign it: distance-driven stretch is *perspective* distortion, optical distortion is correctable (not always dominant), and it is not identical to perspective distortion.
hy: Optical distortion = correctable lens artifact (≠ perspective distortion).
src: Neligan I.7 · Focal length and distortion

stem: When imaging the malar eminence, which viewpoint does the chapter say is generally preferred?

options.A: The bird's-eye view is generally preferred over the worm's-eye view
options.B: The worm's-eye view is always preferred over the bird's-eye view
options.C: Only a strict lateral view should ever be used
options.D: The viewpoint is irrelevant for the malar eminence

exp: For the malar eminence, the chapter says the bird's-eye view is generally preferred over the worm's-eye view (unless a particular anatomic feature dictates otherwise). The distractors over-state the worm's-eye view, restrict imaging to a strict lateral, or wrongly call viewpoint irrelevant.
hy: Malar eminence → bird's-eye preferred over worm's-eye.

stem: The chapter explains why a single on-camera flash is problematic for clinical photography. What is the issue, and what is preferred?

options.A: A single on-camera flash causes harsh shadowing in all backgrounds except black; a multiple-flash studio system gives consistent flesh tones
options.B: A single on-camera flash overexposes only black backgrounds; ambient fluorescent light is preferred
options.C: On-camera flash is ideal and a multi-flash system offers no advantage
options.D: Flash should never be used; auto white balance under fluorescent light is most consistent

exp: A single on-camera flash produces harsh shadowing in every background except black, so a multiple-flash system in a dedicated studio is preferred to capture flesh tones consistently (flash also forces smaller aperture, lower ISO, shorter exposure, minimizing ambient-light variation). The distractors get the physics backwards: the problem is shadowing across backgrounds (not just black), on-camera flash is *not* ideal, and auto white balance under pulsating fluorescent light is specifically called a source of image-to-image variation.
hy: Single on-camera flash → harsh shadows; use multi-flash studio.

stem: Why does the chapter caution against pure white or black backgrounds with the default camera metering mode?

options.A: White/black backgrounds skew the default matrix metering, so the mode must be switched to spot metering on the skin in the center of the field
options.B: White/black backgrounds are ideal and require no metering change
options.C: They cause the autofocus to fail completely
options.D: They are forbidden by HIPAA for medical photographs

exp: Pure white or black backgrounds bias the default matrix metering, so the photographer must switch to spot metering on the skin in the center of the field (and even then the sharp contrast won't render natural skin color). The distractors wrongly call white/black ideal, blame autofocus, or invoke HIPAA — none is the metering issue described.
hy: White/black backgrounds → switch to spot metering on skin.

stem: Most digital cameras filter light using a checkerboard color pattern. Which pattern does the chapter name as most common, and how is it arranged?

options.A: The Bayer pattern — every other pixel is green, while one in four is red or blue
options.B: The Foveon pattern — every pixel records all three colors in stacked layers
options.C: The RGB-stripe pattern — equal vertical stripes of red, green, blue
options.D: A pattern in which every pixel is gray and color is added in software

exp: The Bayer pattern is the most common digital color filter array: every other pixel is green, while one in four is red or blue (mimicking the eye's green sensitivity). The distractors describe other or invented schemes: Foveon's stacked full-color pixels, an RGB-stripe arrangement, and an all-gray sensor are not the Bayer mosaic the chapter names.
hy: Bayer pattern = ½ green, ¼ red, ¼ blue.

stem: The chapter contrasts the two main image-sensor technologies. Which pairing is correct?

options.A: CCD (charge-coupled device) and CMOS (complementary metal-oxide semiconductor) — both can capture excellent images
options.B: CCD and LED are the two competing sensor types
options.C: CMOS sensors use light-sensitive silver halides like film
options.D: Only CCD sensors exist in modern cameras; CMOS is obsolete

exp: The two main sensor technologies are the CCD (charge-coupled device) and the CMOS (complementary metal-oxide semiconductor); the chapter notes well-designed cameras of either type capture excellent images. The distractors are wrong: LED is not an image sensor, CMOS does not use silver halides (that is film), and CCD is not the only modern sensor (CMOS is widespread).
hy: Image sensors = CCD vs CMOS (both excellent).

stem: In Table 7.1, the "full frame" sensor format is the reference standard. What crop factor and physical size does the chapter assign it?

options.A: Crop factor 1×, 36 × 24 mm
options.B: Crop factor 1.5×, 23.6 × 15.7 mm
options.C: Crop factor 0.79×, 44 × 33 mm
options.D: Crop factor 2×, 17.3 × 13 mm

exp: In Table 7.1 the full-frame format is the 1× reference, sized 36 × 24 mm (the classic 35 mm-film frame). The distractors are *other* rows of the same table: 1.5× / 23.6 × 15.7 mm is APS-C, 0.79× / 44 × 33 mm is medium format, and 2× / 17.3 × 13 mm is Micro Four Thirds — none is full frame.
hy: Full frame = 1× crop, 36 × 24 mm (Table 7.1).

stem: Based on Table 7.1, how does a sensor's crop factor relate to its physical size?

options.A: Crop factor rises as sensor size falls — e.g., APS-C (≈1.5×) is smaller than full frame (1×), and medium format (0.79×) is larger
options.B: Crop factor rises as sensor size rises — larger sensors have larger crop factors
options.C: All listed formats share the same 1× crop factor
options.D: Crop factor depends only on megapixel count, not physical size

exp: Reading across Table 7.1, crop factor moves *inversely* to sensor size: smaller sensors have larger crop factors (APS-C ≈1.5× is smaller than full frame's 1×), and the larger medium-format sensor has a crop factor below 1 (0.79×). The distractors reverse the relationship, claim all formats share 1×, or attribute crop factor to megapixels rather than physical sensor size.
hy: Smaller sensor → larger crop factor (inverse relationship).

stem: The chapter recommends a particular file format as best for print reproduction, supporting 24-bit color and lossless (LZW) compression. Which format?

options.A: TIFF
options.B: JPEG
options.C: GIF
options.D: RAW

exp: TIFF (Tagged Image File Format) is recommended for print reproduction: a bit-mapped/raster format supporting 24-bit color with header-tagged attributes and safe lossless LZW compression. The distractors differ: JPEG is lossy, GIF uses a limited palette for graphics, and RAW is an unprocessed "digital negative" — none is the print-oriented lossless TIFF.
hy: TIFF = lossless, print reproduction (LZW).

stem: The chapter calls one format "perhaps the most common file type" and notes it is "lossy," with selectable compression that trades file size against image quality. Which format?

options.A: JPEG (JPG)
options.B: TIFF
options.C: PSD (Photoshop)
options.D: RAW

exp: JPEG (JPG) is described as perhaps the most common file type and is "lossy," with selectable compression (none up to ~90%) that trades file size against fidelity — ideal for quick email exchange. The distractors are not the common lossy format: TIFF is lossless print, PSD is Adobe's proprietary layered format, and RAW is the unprocessed digital negative.
hy: JPEG = most common, lossy, selectable compression.

stem: The chapter likens one format to a "digital negative." Which format, and why?

options.A: RAW — it is the truest image, with no white balance, color processing, compression, or sharpening applied, though files are 2–6× larger than JPEG
options.B: JPEG — because it compresses the image like a developed print
options.C: GIF — because it stores a fixed palette like a film negative
options.D: PSD — because it is Adobe's proprietary negative format

exp: RAW is the "digital negative": the truest image, with no white balance, color processing, compression, or sharpening applied, fulfilling the role film negatives once did — at the cost of files 2–6× larger than JPEG and a need for specialized software. The distractors misassign the analogy: JPEG is a compressed/processed format (like a print, not a negative), GIF is a palette format, and PSD is Adobe's editing format, not the unprocessed capture.
hy: RAW = unprocessed "digital negative," 2–6× larger than JPEG.

stem: Modern cameras embed technical capture data (camera, lens, date, shutter, aperture) in a standard image-metadata format. What is it called?

options.A: EXIF
options.B: DICOM
options.C: ICD-10
options.D: CODEC

exp: EXIF is the standard metadata format modern cameras use to record camera type, lens, date, time, shutter, aperture, and other technical capture data. The distractors are different standards: DICOM is the medical-imaging (radiology) standard, ICD-10 is a diagnosis-coding system, and a CODEC is a video coder-decoder — none is the still-image capture-metadata format.
hy: EXIF = camera capture metadata.
src: Neligan I.7 · Image attributes and metadata

stem: Which 1996 federal law does the chapter cite as driving increased emphasis on patient privacy across the medical record, including photographs?

options.A: The Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA)
options.B: The American Recovery and Reinvestment Act
options.C: The Sarbanes–Oxley Act
options.D: The Patient Safety and Quality Improvement Act

exp: The chapter cites the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act of 1996 (HIPAA, PL 104-91) as driving increased emphasis on patient privacy across the entire medical record, including photographs (later extended to business associates by the 2009 ARRA). The distractors are real laws with other purposes: ARRA is the 2009 stimulus that *extended* HIPAA, Sarbanes–Oxley is corporate-accounting reform, and the Patient Safety and Quality Improvement Act addresses error reporting — none is the 1996 privacy law.
hy: HIPAA (1996) = patient-privacy law covering photographs.
src: Neligan I.7 · Image attributes and metadata

stem: Parker et al. documented four basic ways inconsistency is introduced into clinical photography. Which set matches their scheme?

options.A: Photographer-based, publisher-based, combined, and patient-based
options.B: Lens-based, sensor-based, software-based, and printer-based
options.C: Pre-operative, intra-operative, post-operative, and archival
options.D: Lighting, metering, depth of field, and color temperature

exp: Parker et al. classified inconsistency into four sources: photographer-based, publisher-based, combined, and patient-based. The distractors are plausible-sounding but invented groupings (hardware-based lists, phase-of-care lists, or the lighting/metering/depth/color set, which actually belongs to Galdino's indirect-variable scheme) — none is Parker's four-category model.
hy: Parker: photographer / publisher / combined / patient sources of inconsistency.

stem: Beyond the standard five views, the chapter lists additional dedicated eye views. Which set does it specify?

options.A: Closed-eye (superior tarsal sulcus/fold), upward gaze (inferior orbital fat), and squinting views
options.B: Only a single anterior close-up of each eye
options.C: Worm's-eye and bird's-eye views exclusively
options.D: A Frankfort-plane view and an occlusal-plane view

exp: Beyond the standard five views, the dedicated eye views are the closed-eye view (highlighting the superior tarsal sulcus and fold), the upward-gaze view (inferior orbital fat pockets/lower lid), and a squinting view (orbicularis action) — with a tightly-closed-lid view occasionally added. The distractors are inadequate or wrong: a single anterior close-up omits the functional views, worm's/bird's-eye are facial-contour viewpoints, and Frankfort/occlusal planes are positioning references, not eye views.
hy: Eye views: closed-eye, upward gaze, squinting.

stem: The chapter notes that morphing/simulation software carries an important default disclaimer to avoid implying a guaranteed result. Which disclaimer does it cite (from the Mirror Image program)?

options.A: "Simulation only: Actual results may differ"
options.B: "Results guaranteed as shown"
options.C: "For entertainment purposes only"
options.D: "Not a medical record"

exp: The chapter cites the Mirror Image program's important default disclaimer — "Simulation only: Actual results may differ" — to avoid implying a guaranteed outcome when showing morphed postoperative previews. The distractors are the opposite of safe practice: guaranteeing results, calling it entertainment, or denying it is a medical record would all create medico-legal exposure rather than mitigate it.
hy: Simulation disclaimer: "Simulation only: Actual results may differ."

stem: The chapter describes a 3D technology that gives patients a simulated postoperative view of their own anatomy. Which technology does it name?

options.A: Physics-based finite element analysis (FEA) 3D surface imaging
options.B: Two-dimensional matrix metering
options.C: Lossless LZW compression
options.D: Spot-meter morphometry

exp: The chapter names physics-based finite element analysis (FEA) 3D surface imaging as the technology that gives patients a simulated, manipulable postoperative view of their own anatomy from varying angles, improving communication. The distractors are unrelated photographic concepts: matrix metering, LZW compression, and spot metering are capture/file techniques, not 3D simulation.
hy: 3D simulation = physics-based FEA surface imaging.

stem: The chapter discusses computer-based virtual surgical planning (VSP) available from commercial companies. Which downstream technology does it pair with VSP as potentially "revolutionizing" implants?

options.A: 3D printing of customized implants and prostheses
options.B: Floppy-disk archival storage
options.C: Single on-camera flash photography
options.D: GIF-based image compression

exp: Virtual surgical planning (VSP) is paired with 3D printing of customized implants and prostheses, which — by integrating precise anatomical topography into preoperative planning — the chapter says could revolutionize implants in plastic surgery. The distractors are obsolete or unrelated: floppy-disk storage, on-camera flash, and GIF compression have nothing to do with VSP-driven custom implants.
hy: VSP + 3D printing → customized implants.

stem: The chapter warns about AI-generated falsified images created with generative adversarial networks. What term does it use for these realistic fake still images and videos?

options.A: Deepfakes
options.B: Morphs
options.C: Raster artifacts
options.D: Codecs

exp: AI-generated, highly realistic fake still images and videos — created with generative adversarial networks (a neural-network method) — are termed "deepfakes," which the chapter flags as a threat to outcome transparency (falsified results/testimonials). The distractors are different concepts: a "morph" is interpolation between two images, "raster artifacts" are compression flaws, and a "codec" is a video coder-decoder — none is the GAN-generated deepfake.
hy: GAN-generated fake images/videos = deepfakes.

stem: What backup discipline does the chapter recommend for digital image archives?

options.A: At least two copies onsite and another offsite at a remote location, to guard against fire, water, or earthquake damage
options.B: A single copy on the camera's memory card is sufficient
options.C: One copy on a floppy disk, refreshed yearly
options.D: Cloud storage only, with no local copies

exp: The recommended backup discipline is at least two copies onsite plus another offsite at a remote location, to survive water, fire, or earthquake damage (digital files can fail catastrophically, unlike a merely scratched transparency). The distractors are unsafe: a single memory-card copy, a single yearly-refreshed floppy, or cloud-only with no local copy all leave the archive vulnerable to a single point of failure.
hy: Backup = ≥2 copies onsite + 1 offsite.

stem: Modern 3D clinical photography is rooted in stereophotogrammetry. Which statement best describes the principle?

options.A: 3D surface coordinates are estimated from two or more photographs taken from different positions, recreating how horizontally-offset human eyes perceive depth
options.B: A single high-resolution photograph is computationally inflated into a 3D mesh using a generic head model
options.C: A laser rangefinder sweeps the surface and depth is read from time-of-flight, with no cameras involved
options.D: Cross-sectional CT slices are stacked to render the external surface in three dimensions

exp: Modern 3D photography is rooted in stereophotogrammetry — estimating 3D surface coordinates from two or more photographs taken from different positions, recreating how our horizontally-offset eyes perceive depth. This surface technique excels at external contour, which cross-sectional imaging captures poorly.
hy: Stereophotogrammetry = 3D coordinates from ≥2 offset images.
chname: Pre- and Intra-operative Imaging for Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — defines stereophotogrammetry: 3D coordinates from ≥2 photographs at different positions, mimicking binocular vision.
why.B: Wrong — inflating a single image with a generic model is model-based morphing, not stereophotogrammetry's multi-view geometry.
why.C: Wrong — time-of-flight laser ranging is a different depth method; stereophotogrammetry uses cameras, not a rangefinder.
why.D: Wrong — stacking CT slices renders internal anatomy and is poor at external surface; that is cross-sectional imaging, not 3D photography.

stem: A 3D imaging system projects a structured pattern of light onto the face and uses two cameras to capture its reflection. Which approach is this, and what is its main advantage?

options.A: Active stereophotogrammetry — projected patterned light eases identification of corresponding points and is less affected by ambient light
options.B: Passive stereophotogrammetry — it relies on the skin's natural texture and is therefore unaffected by ambient light
options.C: Hybrid stereophotogrammetry — it uses neither structured light nor surface texture, only motion parallax
options.D: Active stereophotogrammetry — its advantage is that it needs only one camera and no computation

exp: Active stereophotogrammetry projects patterned (structured) light onto the surface and captures its reflection with two or more cameras; the projected pattern makes corresponding points easy to identify and renders the system less sensitive to ambient light. Passive systems instead rely on natural skin texture and are more vulnerable to ambient lighting, while hybrid systems combine both.
hy: Active = structured light (ambient-resistant); passive = surface texture.
chname: Pre- and Intra-operative Imaging for Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — structured-light projection with multiple cameras defines active stereophotogrammetry and eases point-matching while resisting ambient light.
why.B: Wrong — describes passive stereophotogrammetry (natural texture), which is actually more affected by ambient light.
why.C: Wrong — hybrid combines structured light AND texture; it is not "neither," and motion parallax is not the mechanism.
why.D: Wrong — active systems use two or more cameras and computation, not a single camera with none.

stem: The 3dMD software suite can overlay cross-sectional imaging on a 3D photograph and simulate soft-tissue procedures. What is its stated limitation?

options.A: It cannot simulate changes to the underlying bony structure
options.B: It cannot capture external surface anatomy at all
options.C: It requires ionizing radiation for every capture
options.D: It works only for breast augmentation and no other region

exp: The 3dMD software suite can overlay cross-sectional imaging onto a 3D photograph and simulate a range of soft-tissue procedures, but it explicitly cannot simulate changes to the underlying bony structure.
hy: 3dMD simulates soft tissue but not bony change.
chname: Pre- and Intra-operative Imaging for Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — the stated limitation is inability to model underlying bony changes.
why.B: Wrong — capturing external surface anatomy is exactly what 3D photography does well.
why.C: Wrong — 3D photography is radiation-free; no ionizing radiation is used.
why.D: Wrong — 3dMD has six hardware systems (face, head, hand, body, foot, modular), not breast only.

stem: Which feature distinguishes the Crisalix 3D system from 3dMD and Vectra?

options.A: It is purely software-based, generating 3D images from ordinary consumer-camera photos plus distance measurements, with no dedicated hardware required
options.B: It is the only system that uses active structured-light projection for sub-millimeter bony detail
options.C: It is a hardware-only system with no simulation software suite
options.D: It is the only one validated to simulate changes to underlying bone

exp: Crisalix is purely software-based: it builds 3D images from photographs taken with any consumer camera plus physical distance measurements, requiring no dedicated hardware, which lowers the barrier to entry (at the cost of sensitivity to photo quality and lighting).
hy: Crisalix = software-only 3D from consumer photos.
chname: Pre- and Intra-operative Imaging for Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — Crisalix needs no hardware, using consumer-camera photos and distance measurements.
why.B: Wrong — that describes active structured-light hardware systems (e.g., 3dMD), not the software-only Crisalix.
why.C: Wrong — Crisalix does offer simulation software suites (breast and facial); it is software, not hardware-only.
why.D: Wrong — no system here reliably simulates underlying bone (3dMD explicitly cannot), and this is not Crisalix's distinguishing feature.

stem: Compared with CT and MRI, which set of advantages does diagnostic ultrasound offer for plastic surgery?

options.A: Low cost, real-time image acquisition, no ionizing radiation, and noninvasive portability
options.B: Superior bony cortical detail and a complete radiation-free survey of deep abdominal viscera
options.C: Operator-independent images with the highest spatial resolution of any modality
options.D: Built-in quantification of flap metabolic rate without any probe contact

exp: Ultrasound's advantages are relatively low cost, real-time image acquisition, absence of ionizing radiation, and noninvasive portability (some probes connect to a smartphone). Doppler ultrasound adds blood-flow detection via the Doppler frequency shift.
hy: US = cheap, real-time, no radiation, portable.
chname: Pre- and Intra-operative Imaging for Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — lists the chapter's stated ultrasound advantages.
why.B: Wrong — bony cortical detail and deep visceral survey are CT strengths; US is poor through bone and gas.
why.C: Wrong — ultrasound is notably operator-dependent and is not the highest-resolution modality.
why.D: Wrong — quantifying flap metabolic rate without contact describes thermography/optical methods, not ultrasound.

stem: Ultrasound is an essential first study in the work-up of breast implant-associated anaplastic large cell lymphoma (BIA-ALCL). What is its specific role?

options.A: Detecting late peri-implant seromas and guiding aspiration of fluid for cytologic analysis
options.B: Staging distant nodal and visceral metastases throughout the body
options.C: Confirming the genetic translocation that defines the lymphoma
options.D: Measuring implant-shell integrity to diagnose silent rupture

exp: In suspected BIA-ALCL, ultrasound is the essential first study to detect late peri-implant seromas and to guide aspiration of the fluid for cytologic analysis.
hy: US in BIA-ALCL = detect late seroma + guide aspiration.
chname: Pre- and Intra-operative Imaging for Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — ultrasound detects the late seroma and guides diagnostic fluid aspiration.
why.B: Wrong — whole-body metastatic staging is a role for CT/MRI/PET, not first-line ultrasound.
why.C: Wrong — the defining pathology is established on cytology/immunostaining of aspirate, not by ultrasound itself.
why.D: Wrong — implant-shell rupture surveillance is an MRI role; ultrasound here targets the seroma.

stem: A 34-year-old man has an open tibia–fibula fracture with diminished distal pulses and is planned for free-flap coverage. The study shown is obtained to map the lower-limb vasculature. Which modality—largely replacing digital subtraction angiography for this role—is shown?

options.A: CT angiography (CTA)
options.B: Digital subtraction angiography (DSA)
options.C: Color duplex ultrasonography
options.D: MR angiography (MRA)

exp: CT angiography has emerged as the frontrunner over digital subtraction angiography: it gives rapid arterial and venous phases, is minimally invasive, costs less, and simultaneously images adjacent bone, soft tissue, and hardware — though it still requires ionizing radiation and IV contrast similar to DSA. The image is an axial CTA of both legs.
hy: CTA replaced DSA: fast, minimally invasive, images bone+soft tissue (still radiation+contrast).
chname: Pre- and Intra-operative Imaging for Plastic Surgery
imgcap: Axial CT angiogram of both legs before free-flap reconstruction after an open tibia–fibula fracture (Neligan Fig 8.1)

why.A: Correct — CTA is the modality shown and the stated frontrunner over DSA for vascular mapping.
why.B: Wrong — DSA is the invasive historical standard (arterial puncture, pseudoaneurysm, contrast nephropathy) that CTA replaced.
why.C: Wrong — duplex ultrasonography is noninvasive but operator-dependent and lacks the simultaneous bony road map shown here.
why.D: Wrong — MRA avoids radiation but is slower, costlier, and metal-incompatible, and is not the axial CT image shown.

stem: As preoperative CTA (e.g., for DIEP flaps) has become routine, incidental findings have risen. Which figures does the chapter cite?

options.A: Incidental findings occur in roughly 12–76% of scans, and about 0.6% of patients are found to have metastatic disease
options.B: Incidental findings occur in under 1% of scans, none of which alter management
options.C: Incidental findings occur in essentially every scan and half prove malignant
options.D: Incidental findings occur in ~30% of scans, of which 50% are metastatic disease

exp: With routine preoperative CTA, incidental findings are reported in roughly 12–76% of scans; most are benign, but about 1–2% alter management and, in pooled analysis, ~0.6% of patients are found to have metastatic disease.
hy: CTA incidental findings 12–76%; ~0.6% metastatic.
chname: Pre- and Intra-operative Imaging for Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — matches the cited 12–76% incidental rate and 0.6% metastatic figure.
why.B: Wrong — understates both the frequency and the management impact the chapter reports.
why.C: Wrong — incidental findings are common but the majority are benign, not 50% malignant.
why.D: Wrong — invents a 30%/50% split not supported by the text.

stem: By what physical principle does MRI generate its cross-sectional images?

options.A: Excitation of hydrogen atoms in a strong magnetic field and detection of energy released as they return to ground state, mapping tissue water and lipid
options.B: Attenuation of a rotating X-ray beam by tissues of differing electron density
options.C: Reflection of high-frequency mechanical sound waves at tissue interfaces
options.D: Detection of positrons emitted by a radiolabeled glucose analogue

exp: MRI excites hydrogen atoms in a strong magnetic field and detects the energy released as they relax to ground state, effectively mapping the body's water and lipid (hydrogen-rich) distribution; this underlies its superb soft-tissue discrimination without ionizing radiation.
hy: MRI = hydrogen excitation/relaxation mapping water & lipid.
chname: Pre- and Intra-operative Imaging for Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — describes MRI physics: hydrogen excitation/relaxation mapping water and lipid.
why.B: Wrong — X-ray-beam attenuation by electron density is the principle of CT, not MRI.
why.C: Wrong — reflection of mechanical sound waves is ultrasound.
why.D: Wrong — positron emission from a radiotracer is PET.

stem: For asymptomatic silicone breast implants, what MRI surveillance schedule for rupture does the chapter cite?

options.A: Begin at 5 years after implantation, then every 2–3 years thereafter
options.B: Begin at 1 year, then annually for life
options.C: Begin at 10 years, then once only
options.D: Begin immediately postoperatively, then every 6 months

exp: For asymptomatic silicone implants, MRI surveillance for rupture is recommended to begin 5 years after implantation and then every 2–3 years thereafter.
hy: Implant MRI: start at 5 yr, then q2–3 yr.
chname: Pre- and Intra-operative Imaging for Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — the cited schedule: begin at 5 years, then every 2–3 years.
why.B: Wrong — annual imaging from year 1 is not the recommended interval.
why.C: Wrong — starting at 10 years (once) is later and less frequent than recommended.
why.D: Wrong — immediate and 6-monthly imaging is far more frequent than the guideline.

stem: Computer-assisted design lets a virtual surgical plan be manipulated with "6 degrees of freedom." What does this refer to?

options.A: Translation along the X-, Y-, and Z-axes plus rotation in yaw, pitch, and roll
options.B: Six separate osteotomy cuts that the software can model simultaneously
options.C: Six cephalometric landmarks that must each be registered before planning
options.D: Six tissue layers (skin to bone) that the software segments independently

exp: "Six degrees of freedom" means a virtual segment can be translated along the X-, Y-, and Z-axes and rotated in yaw, pitch, and roll — the full rigid-body motion CAD/VSP allows when modeling osteotomies.
hy: 6 DOF = XYZ translation + yaw/pitch/roll rotation.
chname: Pre- and Intra-operative Imaging for Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — defines the six rigid-body degrees of freedom.
why.B: Wrong — it refers to motion axes, not a count of osteotomy cuts.
why.C: Wrong — it is not a number of cephalometric landmarks to register.
why.D: Wrong — it is not a count of segmented tissue layers.

stem: What level of accuracy does the chapter cite for virtual surgical planning in orthognathic surgery?

options.A: Less than 1 mm difference between planned and actual cephalometric point locations
options.B: Roughly 5 mm difference between planned and actual positions
options.C: Accuracy equivalent to freehand planning, with no measurable improvement
options.D: Within 1 cm, sufficient only for gross asymmetry correction

exp: VSP in orthognathic surgery achieves less than 1 mm difference between planned and actual cephalometric point locations, reflecting its high precision in aligning mobile bony segments.
hy: Orthognathic VSP accuracy <1 mm.
chname: Pre- and Intra-operative Imaging for Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — cited accuracy is <1 mm planned-vs-actual.
why.B: Wrong — 5 mm overstates the error; VSP is sub-millimeter.
why.C: Wrong — VSP demonstrably improves on freehand, not equivalent.
why.D: Wrong — 1 cm is far coarser than the cited sub-millimeter accuracy.

stem: In the virtual surgical plan shown, the maxillary segment, genioplasty segment, and genioplasty cutting guide are each rendered in a different color. What core capability of VSP does such color-coded modeling represent?

options.A: Mobile bony segments can be repositioned virtually and translated into intraoperative cutting guides and splints
options.B: It records real-time intraoperative blood perfusion of each bony segment
options.C: It measures the metabolic activity of the genioglossus during the simulation
options.D: It substitutes for the actual osteotomy, which is then performed without instruments

exp: The color-coded plan illustrates VSP's core capability: mobile bony segments (maxilla, genioplasty segment) are repositioned virtually with their cutting guides, then translated into physical intraoperative osteotomy guides and occlusal splints.
hy: VSP → virtual segment repositioning → intraoperative cutting guides/splints.
chname: Pre- and Intra-operative Imaging for Plastic Surgery
imgcap: Virtual surgical plan for orthognathic surgery: maxillary segment (green), genioplasty segment (blue), and genioplasty cutting guide (orange) (Neligan Fig 8.4)

why.A: Correct — VSP repositions segments virtually and produces real cutting guides/splints, as the colors depict.
why.B: Wrong — perfusion of segments is an ICG/thermography role, not what VSP color-coding shows.
why.C: Wrong — genioglossus metabolic activity is not measured by VSP; soft-tissue effects are modeled geometrically.
why.D: Wrong — VSP guides the osteotomy; it does not replace performing it with instruments.

stem: Mandibular reconstruction with a fibula free flap (the gold standard since 1989) is technically demanding. Which advantage of applying VSP does the chapter cite?

options.A: Decreased intra-operative time, decreased fibula ischemia time, improved bone-on-bone contact, and more precise construct positioning
options.B: Elimination of the need for a microvascular anastomosis
options.C: Conversion of the procedure into a pedicled, non-microsurgical reconstruction
options.D: Avoidance of any osteotomies of the fibula

exp: Applying VSP to fibula-flap mandibular reconstruction yields decreased intra-operative time, decreased fibula ischemia time, improved bone-on-bone contact, and more precise positioning of the mandibular construct.
hy: VSP mandible: less OR time, less ischemia, better bone contact, precise position.
chname: Pre- and Intra-operative Imaging for Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — lists the cited VSP advantages for fibula mandibular reconstruction.
why.B: Wrong — it remains a free flap; VSP does not remove the microvascular anastomosis.
why.C: Wrong — the fibula flap stays microsurgical; VSP does not convert it to a pedicled flap.
why.D: Wrong — VSP plans the fibular osteotomies precisely rather than avoiding them.

stem: How can a standard abdominal CTA be combined with 3D printing to aid DIEP flap surgery?

options.A: Printing a to-scale tactile model of the intermuscular perforators to facilitate intra-operative dissection
options.B: Printing a custom titanium plate to bridge the donor fascial defect
options.C: Printing a resorbable nerve conduit for the perforator's sensory branch
options.D: Printing a silicone implant sized to the harvested flap

exp: A standard abdominal CTA can be used to 3D-print a to-scale model of the intermuscular DIEP perforators, giving surgeons a tactile, multi-angle aid for the difficult intramuscular dissection.
hy: CTA → 3D-printed perforator model for DIEP dissection.
chname: Pre- and Intra-operative Imaging for Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — the chapter describes printing a to-scale perforator model from CTA to aid dissection.
why.B: Wrong — bridging the donor fascia with titanium is not the described application.
why.C: Wrong — a nerve conduit is unrelated to perforator dissection mapping.
why.D: Wrong — printing a flap-sized silicone implant is not the perforator-model use described.

stem: 3D "powder printing" with a biocompatible calcium-phosphate cement is proposed for custom craniofacial implants. Which property does the chapter highlight?

options.A: It degrades after implantation and has a density similar to bone, so it does not interfere with later imaging
options.B: It is permanent, radiopaque metal that must never be burred in the operating room
options.C: It conducts electricity, allowing intra-operative nerve stimulation through the implant
options.D: It can only be produced over several weeks, precluding same-admission use

exp: 3D powder printing with biocompatible calcium-phosphate cement produces custom craniofacial implants that degrade in vivo over months and have a density similar to bone, so they do not interfere with later imaging; printers reach 0.2 mm resolution and fabricate within 3–4 hours.
hy: Ca-phosphate powder-printed implants: degrade, bone-like density, imaging-neutral.
chname: Pre- and Intra-operative Imaging for Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — degradation, bone-like density, and imaging compatibility are the cited properties.
why.B: Wrong — describes prefabricated titanium splints (permanent, not bone-replacing); these implants can in fact be burred in the OR.
why.C: Wrong — electrical conduction/nerve stimulation through the implant is not a described property.
why.D: Wrong — these are fabricated in hours (within 3–4 h), not weeks.

stem: Which combination of properties makes indocyanine green (ICG) well suited to intra-operative perfusion fluoroscopy?

options.A: Rapid uptake and excretion, confinement to the intravascular space with minimal leakage, low toxicity, and fluorescence detectable through several millimeters of tissue
options.B: Slow hepatic accumulation allowing delayed imaging hours later, with deep tissue penetration of many centimeters
options.C: Avid binding to extravascular collagen, which highlights interstitial edema
options.D: Emission of ionizing radiation that is read by a gamma probe

exp: ICG suits intra-operative fluoroscopy because of rapid uptake and excretion, confinement to the intravascular space with minimal leakage, low toxicity, and near-infrared fluorescence detectable through several millimeters of tissue (with portable detectors such as SPY).
hy: ICG: fast in/out, intravascular, low-tox, fluoresces mm-deep.
chname: Pre- and Intra-operative Imaging for Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — lists ICG's stated favorable properties.
why.B: Wrong — ICG has rapid (not slow) clearance and penetrates only several millimeters, not centimeters.
why.C: Wrong — ICG stays intravascular with minimal leakage; it does not bind extravascular collagen.
why.D: Wrong — ICG fluoresces; it does not emit ionizing radiation for a gamma probe (that is a radiotracer).

stem: In implant-based reconstruction, ICG angiography predicts mastectomy skin-flap necrosis. What performance does the chapter cite?

options.A: Sensitivity ~90% and specificity ~100%, more reliable than clinical examination alone
options.B: Sensitivity ~50% and specificity ~50%, no better than a coin toss
options.C: Sensitivity ~100% but specificity near zero, so it overcalls necrosis
options.D: Performance inferior to clinical examination, so it is not recommended

exp: For mastectomy skin-flap viability in implant-based reconstruction, ICG angiography has ~90% sensitivity and ~100% specificity for predicting skin necrosis and is more reliable than clinical examination alone.
hy: ICG mastectomy flap: ~90% sens, ~100% spec.
chname: Pre- and Intra-operative Imaging for Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — matches the cited 90%/100% performance, superior to clinical exam.
why.B: Wrong — 50/50 grossly understates ICG's demonstrated accuracy.
why.C: Wrong — specificity is ~100%, so it does not indiscriminately overcall necrosis.
why.D: Wrong — ICG outperforms clinical exam here, so it is recommended, not discouraged.

stem: Intra-operative ICG is used to delineate the perfusion zones of a DIEP flap. Which classic relationship does the chapter state?

options.A: Zone I has excellent perfusion and zone IV the poorest, while zones II and III are variable
options.B: Zone IV has the best perfusion and zone I the poorest
options.C: All four zones are equally perfused, so ICG adds nothing
options.D: Only zone III is reliably perfused; zones I, II, and IV are always discarded

exp: In a DIEP flap, zone I (over the pedicle) has excellent perfusion and zone IV the poorest, while zones II and III are variable; ICG helps define which parts of zones II/III are safe to keep.
hy: DIEP: zone I best, zone IV worst, II/III variable.
chname: Pre- and Intra-operative Imaging for Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — states the classic DIEP perfusion gradient.
why.B: Wrong — reverses the gradient; zone IV is the worst, not the best.
why.C: Wrong — zones are not equally perfused; that is why ICG is useful.
why.D: Wrong — zone III is not uniquely reliable; the gradient runs I > II/III > IV.

stem: ICG lymphography in lymphedema reveals characteristic dermal backflow patterns that correspond to disease severity. Which set of patterns does the chapter name?

options.A: Splash, stardust, and diffuse
options.B: Tram-track, target, and halo
options.C: Cobblestone, comedo, and reticular
options.D: Starburst, lattice, and plaque

exp: ICG lymphography identifies dermal backflow patterns — splash, stardust, and diffuse — that correspond to progressively more severe lymphedema and can appear even in subclinical disease.
hy: ICG backflow: splash → stardust → diffuse (worsening lymphedema).
chname: Pre- and Intra-operative Imaging for Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — names the splash/stardust/diffuse backflow patterns.
why.B: Wrong — tram-track/target/halo are not the ICG lymphatic patterns described.
why.C: Wrong — cobblestone/comedo/reticular are dermatologic/morphologic terms, not ICG backflow stages.
why.D: Wrong — starburst/lattice/plaque are invented labels here.

stem: Laser Doppler imaging (LDI) evolved from laser Doppler flowmetry. Which statement is correct?

options.A: LDI scans the target at a distance (non-contact) and, for abdominal perforator mapping, avoids the pitfall of misidentifying subfascial vessels as perforators
options.B: LDI requires direct, sometimes painful probe contact with the skin, unlike flowmetry
options.C: LDI uses ultrasound waves rather than laser light
options.D: LDI measures absolute perfusion values that can be compared directly between patients

exp: Laser Doppler imaging (LDI) scans the target at a distance without probe contact; for abdominal perforator mapping it offers superior sensitivity to standard Doppler and avoids misidentifying subfascial vessels as true perforators (resolution ~3 mm).
hy: LDI = non-contact; avoids subfascial false-perforators.
chname: Pre- and Intra-operative Imaging for Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — LDI is non-contact and avoids the subfascial-vessel pitfall of standard Doppler.
why.B: Wrong — the contact, sometimes painful probe is flowmetry (LDF); LDI was developed to scan at a distance.
why.C: Wrong — LDI uses laser light (shorter wavelength), not ultrasound waves.
why.D: Wrong — perfusion values vary with physiology/artifact, so trends (not absolute cross-patient values) are used.

stem: When LDI is used for postoperative free-flap monitoring, how should its output be interpreted?

options.A: The trend in perfusion values—not absolute numbers—should guide decisions, and LDI can flag compromise 1–3 hours before other clinical signs
options.B: A single absolute perfusion value below a universal cutoff confirms flap failure
options.C: It detects flap compromise only after frank clinical signs are already present
options.D: It is useful preoperatively but provides no postoperative monitoring value

exp: For postoperative flap monitoring, the trend in LDI perfusion values — not absolute numbers — should guide decisions, and LDI can detect microvascular compromise 1–3 hours before other clinical signs appear.
hy: LDI flap monitoring: follow trend; warns 1–3 h early.
chname: Pre- and Intra-operative Imaging for Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — trend-based interpretation and the 1–3 h early-warning window are both cited.
why.B: Wrong — no universal absolute cutoff applies, because values vary between patients.
why.C: Wrong — LDI's value is precisely that it precedes overt clinical signs.
why.D: Wrong — LDI is used for continuous postoperative monitoring, not only preoperatively.

stem: What distinguishes dynamic infrared thermography (DIRT) from static infrared thermography?

options.A: DIRT applies a thermal challenge (e.g., a cooling fan) and observes how the tissue returns to equilibrium temperature, rather than simply observing baseline temperature
options.B: DIRT uses an injected radiotracer, whereas static IRT does not
options.C: DIRT measures absolute blood flow in mL/min, whereas static IRT measures temperature
options.D: DIRT requires direct contact with the skin, whereas static IRT is non-contact

exp: Static infrared thermography simply observes baseline temperature, whereas dynamic IRT (DIRT) applies a thermal challenge (e.g., a cooling fan, heat, or water immersion) and observes how the tissue returns to equilibrium — added information that has made DIRT more widely adopted.
hy: DIRT = thermal challenge + recovery; static = baseline only.
chname: Pre- and Intra-operative Imaging for Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — DIRT's defining feature is the thermal challenge and observed recovery.
why.B: Wrong — IRT uses no radiotracer; it reads emitted infrared heat.
why.C: Wrong — IRT measures temperature/infrared emission, not absolute mL/min flow.
why.D: Wrong — infrared thermography is non-contact for both static and dynamic forms.

stem: How do DIRT and ultrasound compare for abdominal perforator mapping, per the chapter?
