options.A: DIRT "hot spots" reliably localize perforators just beneath the skin while ultrasound identifies them at the muscular fascia, making the two complementary
options.B: DIRT and ultrasound identify perforators at exactly the same depth, so one is redundant
options.C: DIRT localizes deep subfascial vessels that ultrasound cannot, and ultrasound adds nothing
options.D: Neither can localize perforators; both are purely research tools

exp: DIRT "hot spots" reliably localize perforators lying directly beneath the skin, while ultrasound identifies them at the level of the muscular fascia — so the two techniques are complementary rather than redundant.
hy: DIRT (subdermal hot spots) + US (fascial level) = complementary.
chname: Pre- and Intra-operative Imaging for Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — the chapter calls DIRT and US complementary because they localize perforators at different depths.
why.B: Wrong — they identify perforators at different depths, which is why both add value.
why.C: Wrong — DIRT is best for vessels just beneath the skin, not deep subfascial ones.
why.D: Wrong — both do localize perforators; neither is merely a research curiosity here.

stem: For assessing burn depth, how do laser Doppler and DIRT compare?

options.A: Laser Doppler has borne out as the superior method; DIRT loses accuracy beyond post-injury day 3 as wounds granulate
options.B: DIRT is the established superior method and is accurate for weeks after injury
options.C: The two are identical in accuracy and timing
options.D: Neither correlates with healing time, so serial clinical exam is abandoned

exp: Laser Doppler has borne out as the superior method for burn-depth evaluation; DIRT is a useful, easy adjunct but loses accuracy beyond post-injury day 3 as wounds granulate (and is confounded by evaporative and ambient heat loss).
hy: Burn depth: laser Doppler > DIRT (DIRT fades after day 3).
chname: Pre- and Intra-operative Imaging for Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — laser Doppler is the cited superior method; DIRT degrades after day 3.
why.B: Wrong — reverses the comparison and overstates DIRT's temporal accuracy.
why.C: Wrong — they are not identical; laser Doppler is superior for burns.
why.D: Wrong — these tools correlate with healing and supplement (not replace) serial clinical exam.

stem: Surgical navigation systems are broadly image-based or model-based. Which contrast is correct?

options.A: Image-based systems match preoperative imaging to the patient (common in neurosurgery); model-based systems use intraoperative bony landmarks with no preoperative imaging (common in orthopedics)
options.B: Image-based systems use no preoperative imaging; model-based systems are built entirely from preoperative CT
options.C: Both types require intraoperative CT for every case
options.D: The two terms are interchangeable and describe the same workflow

exp: Image-based navigation matches preoperative imaging to the patient's intraoperative position (common in neurosurgery), whereas model-based navigation uses no preoperative imaging and instead computes anatomy from intraoperative bony landmarks (common in orthopedics).
hy: Image-based (preop imaging, neurosurg) vs model-based (intraop landmarks, ortho).
chname: Pre- and Intra-operative Imaging for Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — correctly pairs image-based/neurosurgery and model-based/orthopedics.
why.B: Wrong — it reverses which system uses preoperative imaging.
why.C: Wrong — model-based navigation specifically avoids the need for preoperative (or intraoperative CT) imaging.
why.D: Wrong — the two terms denote genuinely different workflows.

stem: How is surgical navigation classically applied in unilateral orbital and midface trauma?

options.A: The uninjured contralateral side is mirrored to create a model that guides repair and implant shaping on the injured side
options.B: The injured side is scanned and used as the template for the contralateral orbit
options.C: Navigation replaces imaging entirely, relying on palpation alone
options.D: It is used only after hardware placement to document the result

exp: In unilateral midface/orbit trauma, navigation mirrors the uninjured contralateral side to build a model that guides repair and the shaping/placement of the orbital implant on the injured side, yielding a more symmetric reconstruction.
hy: Navigation: mirror uninjured side to guide injured-side repair.
chname: Pre- and Intra-operative Imaging for Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — the uninjured side is mirrored as the template for the injured side.
why.B: Wrong — the injured, comminuted side is not the template; the normal side is.
why.C: Wrong — navigation depends on imaging-derived models, not palpation alone.
why.D: Wrong — it guides the repair itself, not merely post-hoc documentation.

stem: The chapter contrasts virtual reality (VR) and augmented reality (AR). Which description is correct?

options.A: VR (e.g., Vive, Oculus) takes over the entire visual field for an immersive experience, whereas AR (e.g., Google Glass, HoloLens) overlays digital data onto the real visual field
options.B: AR fully replaces natural vision, whereas VR only overlays small data points
options.C: Both VR and AR completely block the user's view of the real world
options.D: VR and AR both overlay data while leaving the real world as the primary focus

exp: Virtual reality (e.g., Vive, Oculus) takes over the entire visual field for an immersive experience meant to supplant natural vision, whereas augmented reality (e.g., Google Glass, HoloLens) overlays digital data onto the user's real visual field, keeping the real world as the focus.
hy: VR = immersive/replaces vision; AR = overlay on real world.
chname: Pre- and Intra-operative Imaging for Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — correctly contrasts immersive VR with overlay AR and their example devices.
why.B: Wrong — reverses the definitions; AR overlays, VR immerses.
why.C: Wrong — AR does not block the real world; only VR is fully immersive.
why.D: Wrong — VR does not overlay onto the real world; it replaces it.

stem: Which paired statement about mixed-reality applications is correct?

options.A: AR overlays can cause "inattention blindness" to unexpected findings, while immersive VR has been used to reduce pain during burn dressing changes
options.B: AR overlays improve detection of all unexpected findings, while VR worsens procedural pain
options.C: VR is used for surgical navigation overlays, while AR fully immerses the patient to block pain
options.D: Neither VR nor AR has any demonstrated patient-care application

exp: Because AR draws attention to overlaid data, it can cause "inattention blindness" and reduced recognition of unexpected findings; separately, immersive VR has been used to divert attention and reduce pain during burn dressing changes and other painful procedures.
hy: AR risk = inattention blindness; VR = burn-pain distraction.
chname: Pre- and Intra-operative Imaging for Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — pairs AR's inattention-blindness risk with VR's analgesic distraction in burns.
why.B: Wrong — reverses both: AR can miss unexpected findings, and VR reduces (not worsens) pain.
why.C: Wrong — swaps the roles of VR and AR.
why.D: Wrong — both have demonstrated patient-care applications in the chapter.

stem: The image shows an eye-tracking heat map over a breast reconstruction result. What does eye-tracking technology measure, and why is it used in plastic surgery?

options.A: It tracks the position and movement of a viewer's gaze to quantify which areas of interest capture attention, making otherwise subjective aesthetic outcomes measurable
options.B: It measures the patient's own visual acuity before and after surgery
options.C: It records the surgeon's hand tremor to grade technical skill
options.D: It maps cutaneous blood flow by following infrared emission from the skin

exp: Eye tracking records the position and movement of a viewer's gaze to quantify which areas of interest capture attention, turning otherwise subjective aesthetic judgments (e.g., distraction by scars or an absent nipple) into measurable data, as the heat map illustrates.
hy: Eye tracking quantifies gaze/attention → objective aesthetic outcomes.
chname: Pre- and Intra-operative Imaging for Plastic Surgery
imgcap: Eye-tracking heat map over a free-flap breast reconstruction, gaze concentrating on the nipple–areolar complex and skin paddle (Neligan Fig 8.8)

why.A: Correct — eye tracking quantifies viewer gaze/areas of interest to objectify aesthetic outcomes.
why.B: Wrong — it tracks an external viewer's gaze, not the patient's own visual acuity.
why.C: Wrong — recording surgeon hand tremor is a different skill-assessment method.
why.D: Wrong — mapping skin blood flow via infrared is thermography, not eye tracking.

stem: The 2000 Institute of Medicine report *To Err Is Human* shaped the modern patient-safety movement. Which principle did it most centrally advance?

options.A: Most adverse events stem from systems that fail to prevent error, rather than from blameworthy individuals
options.B: Adverse events are rare in high-income countries and are mainly a problem of low-resource settings
options.C: The most effective remedy for adverse events is disciplinary action against the responsible providers
options.D: Adverse events are an unavoidable cost of complex care and are not meaningfully preventable

exp: *To Err Is Human* (IOM, 2000) reframed adverse events as the product of systems that fail to prevent error rather than of blameworthy individuals — the systems-thinking foundation of modern patient safety. The same literature shows the problem is neither rare nor confined to poor settings: a systematic review found 9.2% of patients in high-income countries suffer in-hospital adverse events, over 43% of them preventable. A blame-the-individual culture suppresses reporting and leaves the latent system flaws that allow errors untouched.
hy: *To Err Is Human* = adverse events are system failures, mostly preventable.

why.A: Correct — the report's central message was systems failure over individual blame.
why.B: Wrong — review showed 9.2% adverse events / 43% preventable in high-income countries, so it is not rare or low-income-only.
why.C: Wrong — the report explicitly moved away from disciplining individuals toward redesigning systems.
why.D: Wrong — over 43% of events were judged preventable, contradicting "not meaningfully preventable."

stem: The CMS Hospital-Acquired Condition Reduction Program (HACRP) is a value-based-care lever. Beginning in 2015, what happens to hospitals ranking in the worst-performing 25% on hospital-acquired-condition measures?

options.A: Their CMS payments are reduced by 1%
options.B: Their CMS payments are increased by 1% as a quality bonus
options.C: They are excluded from Medicare reimbursement for one year
options.D: They must suspend all ambulatory surgical procedures

exp: HACRP is a flagship example of paying for the value rather than the volume of care: from 2015, hospitals in the worst-performing 25% on hospital-acquired-condition measures had CMS payments cut by 1%. Tying reimbursement to safety performance is meant to pressure systems toward measurable harm reduction. The penalty is a reduction, not a bonus, and it adjusts payment rather than excluding a hospital or shutting its units.
hy: HACRP = worst-quartile hospitals lose 1% of CMS payment (since 2015).

why.A: Correct — a 1% CMS payment reduction for the worst-performing quartile.
why.B: Wrong — it is a penalty, not a quality bonus.
why.C: Wrong — payment is reduced, not Medicare participation revoked.
why.D: Wrong — no suspension of ambulatory procedures is involved.

stem: Hernando-Boussard and colleagues analyzed over 400,000 NIS records of inpatient soft-tissue reconstruction. Which best summarizes their adverse-event findings?

options.A: ~4% overall, highest among pedicled flap/graft patients (~5%) and lowest among breast reconstruction patients (~2%)
options.B: ~4% overall, with breast reconstruction carrying the highest subgroup rate (~5%)
options.C: ~15% overall, with rates essentially uniform across procedure types
options.D: <1% overall, with pedicled flap/graft patients the lowest-risk subgroup

exp: In >400,000 NIS inpatient soft-tissue-reconstruction records, the overall adverse-event rate was 4.06%, higher in pedicled graft/flap patients (5.08%) and lower in breast reconstruction patients (1.97%). The gradient tracks operative complexity, with flap work carrying the greatest physiologic insult. The inflated overall-rate option and the reversed-subgroup option both misstate these figures.
hy: Plastic surgery inpatient adverse events ~4% overall; flaps highest (~5%), breast recon lowest (~2%).

why.A: Correct — matches 4.06% overall, 5.08% flaps, 1.97% breast reconstruction.
why.B: Wrong — reverses the subgroups; breast reconstruction was the lowest, not highest.
why.C: Wrong — the overall rate was ~4%, not ~15%, and rates were not uniform.
why.D: Wrong — flap/graft patients had the highest rate, not the lowest.

stem: A preoperative patient has severe systemic disease that is a constant threat to life but is not moribund. Which ASA physical status class applies?

options.A: ASA IV
options.B: ASA III
options.C: ASA V
options.D: ASA II

exp: ASA IV denotes severe systemic disease that is a constant threat to life — the patient is gravely ill but not yet moribund. ASA III is severe disease that is not a constant threat to life; ASA V is the moribund patient not expected to survive without surgery; ASA II is mild controlled systemic disease. Distinguishing "constant threat to life" (IV) from "moribund" (V) is the classic tested boundary.
hy: ASA IV = severe disease that is a constant threat to life (not yet moribund).

why.A: Correct — "constant threat to life, not moribund" is the ASA IV definition.
why.B: Wrong — ASA III is severe disease without the constant life threat described.
why.C: Wrong — ASA V is the moribund patient, a step beyond this stem.
why.D: Wrong — ASA II is mild systemic disease, far milder than described.

stem: On the ASA physical status scale, which pairing of class to description is correct?

options.A: ASA V = moribund, not expected to survive without surgery; ASA VI = declared brain-dead for organ donation
options.B: ASA V = declared brain-dead for organ donation; ASA VI = a moribund patient
options.C: ASA V = severe systemic disease; ASA VI = mild systemic disease under control
options.D: ASA V = a normal healthy patient; ASA VI = severe disease threatening life

exp: On the six-class ASA scale, ASA V is the moribund patient not expected to survive without the operation, and ASA VI is a patient declared brain-dead whose organs are being procured for donation. ASA I is a normal healthy patient and ASA II mild controlled disease, so options swapping these definitions are incorrect. The V-versus-VI pairing is the high-yield endpoint of the scale.
hy: ASA V = moribund; ASA VI = brain-dead organ donor.

why.A: Correct — pairs moribund (V) with brain-dead organ donor (VI) accurately.
why.B: Wrong — reverses V and VI.
why.C: Wrong — ASA V is moribund and VI is brain-dead, not "severe" and "mild" disease.
why.D: Wrong — describes ASA I and an ASA III/IV-type state, not V and VI.

stem: Foley et al. examined over 2 million ambulatory-surgery patients in NSQIP. What did ASA physical status predict?

options.A: Medical complications, readmission, and an ~89-fold mortality rise in class IV versus class I
options.B: No relationship between ASA class and mortality in the ambulatory setting
options.C: A ~2-fold mortality rise confined to class V patients only
options.D: Complications in inpatients only, with no ambulatory signal at all

exp: In >2 million NSQIP ambulatory patients (Foley et al.), ASA physical status predicted medical complications, readmission, and mortality across specialties — including an ~89-fold mortality increase for class IV versus class I. This validated ASA as a risk stratifier even for outpatient surgery, not just inpatients. The "no relationship," "2-fold class V only," and "inpatient only" options each understate or misplace this finding.
hy: ASA predicts ambulatory outcomes: ~89× mortality class IV vs I.

why.A: Correct — ~89-fold class IV vs I mortality plus higher complications/readmission.
why.B: Wrong — ASA clearly correlated with ambulatory mortality.
why.C: Wrong — the increase was ~89-fold (class IV vs I), not a 2-fold class-V-only effect.
why.D: Wrong — the study's whole point was a strong ambulatory (outpatient) signal.

stem: A surgeon judges a patient a moderate-risk candidate for elective surgery who has modifiable factors and time before the operation. Per the chapter's approach (Algorithm 9.1), what is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Optimize comorbidities and behavior (glycemic/BP/pulmonary control, weight loss, smoking cessation), then reassess
options.B: Proceed directly to surgery, since the risk is only moderate and not high
options.C: Decline the patient for surgery permanently, regardless of modifiability
options.D: Refer every such patient for inpatient admission before any procedure

exp: Algorithm 9.1 routes a moderate/high-risk patient who has modifiable factors, time, and willingness toward optimization — consultations for comorbidities (DM, HTN, pulmonary, heme, rheum) and altered behavior (weight loss, smoking cessation, improved nutrition, medication holds) — then reassessment before proceeding. Optimization is the value-add of the preoperative visit; proceeding straight to surgery wastes a modifiable window, and permanent refusal or universal admission are both disproportionate.
hy: Moderate/high risk + modifiable factors + time → optimize, then reassess.

why.A: Correct — optimize comorbidities/behavior then reassess, per the algorithm.
why.B: Wrong — skips the modifiable-risk optimization the algorithm specifies.
why.C: Wrong — risk here is modifiable, so permanent refusal is unjustified.
why.D: Wrong — routine inpatient admission is not part of the outpatient optimization pathway.

stem: Which statement about the 2005 Caprini score is correct?

options.A: A validated VTE risk tool, recommended by the ASPS and the American College of Chest Physicians, aggregating ~40 risk factors
options.B: An intra-operative bleeding-risk score recommended by anesthesiology societies
options.C: A cardiac risk index intended to replace the ASA classification system
options.D: A wound-healing prognosis score driven by a single dominant risk factor

exp: The 2005 Caprini score is a widely validated VTE risk-stratification tool, formally recommended by the ASPS and the American College of Chest Physicians, that produces an aggregate score from ~40 individual risk factors. It is a clotting-risk instrument, not a bleeding score, a cardiac index, or a wound-healing score, and it complements rather than replaces ASA. Its strength is translating many small factors into an individualized VTE risk.
hy: Caprini (2005) = validated ~40-factor VTE risk score (ASPS/ACCP endorsed).

why.A: Correct — validated ~40-factor VTE score endorsed by ASPS and ACCP.
why.B: Wrong — it stratifies thrombosis risk, not intra-operative bleeding.
why.C: Wrong — it is a VTE tool, not a cardiac index, and does not replace ASA.
why.D: Wrong — it aggregates many factors, not a single dominant one, and is not a wound-healing score.

stem: Among plastic surgery inpatients, the Caprini score stratifies VTE risk across approximately what range?

options.A: An ~20-fold variation, from about 0.3% to 8.5%
options.B: An ~2-fold variation, from about 4% to 8%
options.C: A fixed ~5% risk the score does not meaningfully change
options.D: An ~100-fold variation, from about 0.1% to 10%

exp: Across plastic surgery inpatients the Caprini score reveals an ~20-fold variation in VTE risk, spanning roughly 0.3% to 8.5%. That spread is what lets a surgeon hold a data-driven, individualized conversation about baseline risk and the value of risk reduction. The narrower "2-fold," the "fixed 5%," and the inflated "100-fold (0.1–10%)" options misrepresent the cited range.
hy: Caprini spreads inpatient VTE risk ~20-fold (≈0.3%–8.5%).

why.A: Correct — ~20-fold variation from ~0.3% to ~8.5%.
why.B: Wrong — understates the spread (it is ~20-fold, not ~2-fold).
why.C: Wrong — the score does stratify risk; it is not a fixed 5%.
why.D: Wrong — the cited figures are 0.3–8.5% (~20-fold), not 0.1–10%.

stem: Completing a Caprini score can reveal markers of undiagnosed genetic hypercoagulability that warrant hematology referral. Which finding is such a marker?

options.A: A history of ≥3 lost pregnancies, or a strong family history of VTE (present in ~10–15% of plastic surgery inpatients)
options.B: Hypertension well controlled on a single antihypertensive agent
options.C: A body mass index that falls within the normal range
options.D: A remote single outpatient procedure performed under local anesthesia

exp: A strong family history of VTE (commonly missed, present in ~10–15% of plastic surgery inpatients) and, in women, ≥3 lost pregnancies are markers of possibly undiagnosed genetic hypercoagulability that justify hematology referral, especially for elective surgery. Controlled hypertension, a normal BMI, and a remote uncomplicated local-anesthetic procedure are not flags for an inherited thrombophilia. Catching these histories preoperatively allows panels to be drawn before any prophylactic anticoagulation clouds interpretation.
hy: Strong VTE family history (10–15%) or ≥3 pregnancy losses → consider hematology referral.

why.A: Correct — family history / recurrent pregnancy loss flag possible inherited hypercoagulability.
why.B: Wrong — controlled hypertension is not a thrombophilia marker.
why.C: Wrong — a normal BMI is not a hypercoagulability flag.
why.D: Wrong — a remote uncomplicated local-anesthesia case does not suggest genetic thrombophilia.

stem: A chronically warfarin-anticoagulated patient with atrial fibrillation needs elective surgery. Based on level I evidence from the BRIDGE trial, what is recommended?

options.A: Stop warfarin 5 days before surgery and resume ~24 hours after, without routine LMWH bridging
options.B: Continue warfarin through surgery at the full therapeutic dose
options.C: Stop warfarin the morning of surgery and bridge everyone with therapeutic LMWH
options.D: Stop warfarin 2 weeks before and resume only after suture removal

exp: Level I evidence from the BRIDGE trial supports stopping warfarin 5 days before the procedure and resuming ~24 hours after surgery (or at surgeon discretion), without routine low-molecular-weight-heparin bridging. Continuing full-dose warfarin invites bleeding, stopping only the morning of surgery leaves the patient over-anticoagulated, and a 2-week hold is unnecessarily long. The trial's practice-changing message is "stop and don't routinely bridge."
hy: BRIDGE: stop warfarin 5 d pre-op, resume ~24 h post-op, no routine bridging.

why.A: Correct — matches the BRIDGE protocol (stop 5 days, resume ~24 h, no routine bridge).
why.B: Wrong — full-dose warfarin through surgery is unsafe for bleeding.
why.C: Wrong — same-morning cessation leaves the INR therapeutic, and routine bridging is what BRIDGE refuted.
why.D: Wrong — a 2-week hold over-corrects and is not the trial recommendation.

stem: In the BRIDGE trial, why is routine LMWH bridging discouraged for these patients?

options.A: It did not reduce arterial thromboembolism (0.3% vs 0.4%) but increased major bleeding (3.2% vs 1.3%)
options.B: It halved arterial thromboembolism but tripled the rate of fatal bleeding
options.C: It reduced both clotting and bleeding, but only at a prohibitive cost
options.D: It had no measurable effect on either thrombosis or bleeding outcomes

exp: In BRIDGE, LMWH bridging did not significantly reduce arterial thromboembolism (0.3% vs 0.4%) but did significantly increase major bleeding (3.2% vs 1.3%) — an unfavorable trade-off, so routine bridging is discouraged. The "halved clots/tripled fatal bleeding," "reduced both," and "no effect on either" options misstate that specific risk–benefit result. The teaching point is that bridging adds bleeding without buying thrombosis protection.
hy: BRIDGE: bridging ATE 0.3 vs 0.4% (NS) but major bleeding 3.2 vs 1.3% → don't bridge routinely.

why.A: Correct — no ATE reduction (0.3 vs 0.4%) but more major bleeding (3.2 vs 1.3%).
why.B: Wrong — ATE was not halved and fatal bleeding was not tripled; the figures are as above.
why.C: Wrong — bleeding rose, it did not fall.
why.D: Wrong — there was a clear effect: increased major bleeding.

stem: To let VTE risk from exogenous estrogen return toward baseline around surgery, the chapter suggests discontinuing oral contraceptives or hormone replacement over what window?

options.A: For 3 weeks before and 3 weeks after surgery
options.B: For 48 hours before and 48 hours after surgery
options.C: For 3 months before and 3 months after surgery
options.D: Discontinuation is unnecessary, as these do not affect clot risk

exp: Because exogenous estrogen raises clot risk, discontinuing oral contraceptives or hormone replacement for 3 weeks before and 3 weeks after surgery lets VTE risk drift back toward baseline; copper (non-hormonal) IUDs are an alternative for those who need contraception. A 48-hour hold is far too short for estrogen's prothrombotic effect to resolve, 3 months is longer than recommended, and "no effect" is simply wrong. The risks of stopping (irregular menses, unintended pregnancy, vasomotor symptoms) should be discussed.
hy: Stop estrogen-containing OCP/HRT 3 weeks before and 3 weeks after surgery.

why.A: Correct — 3 weeks before and 3 weeks after is the cited window.
why.B: Wrong — 48 hours is far too short for estrogen's prothrombotic effect to clear.
why.C: Wrong — 3 months overshoots the recommended window.
why.D: Wrong — exogenous estrogen demonstrably raises clot risk.

stem: What does the chapter conclude about surgical safety checklists?

options.A: They improve crisis-simulation adherence and cut complications in low-income settings, but plastic-surgery–specific use still needs further study
options.B: Randomized trials have definitively proven they reduce plastic surgery complications
options.C: They have been shown to be ineffective and are no longer recommended for surgery
options.D: They are useful only in aviation and have no surgical evidence base at all

exp: Checklists improve adherence to gold-standard care in simulated surgical crises and reduce overall and infectious complications when the WHO Surgical Safety Checklist is implemented in low-income settings; however, the chapter notes that rigorous trials in plastic surgery specifically are hard (deliberately allowing errors is unethical), so checklist use for plastic surgery still needs further study. Claiming definitive plastic-surgery RCT proof, or that checklists are useless/aviation-only, overstates in opposite directions. The honest position is "promising, evidence-supported broadly, but study-limited in plastic surgery."
hy: Checklists help broadly (crisis sims, low-income settings) but plastic-surgery–specific data are still limited.

why.A: Correct — captures the demonstrated benefits plus the plastic-surgery evidence gap.
why.B: Wrong — the chapter says well-designed plastic-surgery trials are lacking, not definitive.
why.C: Wrong — checklists are not deemed useless; they help in several settings.
why.D: Wrong — there is a real surgical evidence base, not aviation only.

stem: A patient with a family history of a life-threatening anesthetic reaction is scheduled for surgery. Which agents must be avoided in confirmed malignant hyperthermia susceptibility?

options.A: Succinylcholine and volatile inhalational anesthetics
options.B: Propofol and infiltrated local lidocaine
options.C: Nitrous oxide and intravenous acetaminophen
options.D: Opioids and benzodiazepines

exp: Malignant hyperthermia is an autosomal-dominant disorder in which succinylcholine and volatile inhalational anesthetics trigger uncontrolled skeletal-muscle hypermetabolism. Propofol, nitrous oxide, local lidocaine, IV acetaminophen, opioids, and benzodiazepines are not triggers, so a known-susceptible patient can receive a "non-triggering" anesthetic. Family or personal history mandates preoperative anesthesia consultation.
hy: MH triggers = succinylcholine + volatile anesthetics (autosomal dominant).

why.A: Correct — succinylcholine and volatile agents are the MH triggers.
why.B: Wrong — propofol and local lidocaine are safe (non-triggering) in MH.
why.C: Wrong — nitrous oxide and IV acetaminophen are not MH triggers.
why.D: Wrong — opioids and benzodiazepines do not trigger MH.

stem: During general anesthesia a patient develops rising end-tidal CO₂, tachycardia, and masseter rigidity; temperature is still normal. Besides removing the trigger and supporting the cardiovascular system, what is the most appropriate immediate treatment?

options.A: Intravenous dantrolene
options.B: Intravenous calcium gluconate
options.C: External surface cooling alone, deferring any drug therapy
options.D: Intravenous beta-blockade

exp: Rising end-tidal CO₂ with tachycardia and muscle (masseter) rigidity are the early signs of malignant hyperthermia; a temperature spike is a late sign, so treatment must not wait for it. Management is immediate IV dantrolene plus removal of the offending agent and cardiovascular support. Calcium gluconate, cooling alone, and beta-blockade do not address the underlying hypermetabolic muscle reaction and delay the only definitive drug.
hy: MH = IV dantrolene now (don't wait for the late temperature rise).

why.A: Correct — dantrolene is the definitive immediate treatment.
why.B: Wrong — calcium gluconate treats a different toxidrome, not MH.
why.C: Wrong — cooling is adjunctive; withholding dantrolene is dangerous.
why.D: Wrong — beta-blockade does not reverse the hypermetabolic muscle reaction.

stem: Which intra-operative choice is most protective against postoperative VTE, per meta-analysis?

options.A: Avoiding general anesthesia in favor of regional, MAC, or local when feasible
options.B: Routinely converting regional anesthesia to general anesthesia
options.C: Maximizing total operative time to complete more in a single stage
options.D: Combining several procedures into one longer operation when possible

exp: General anesthesia is itself a recognized VTE risk factor, and meta-analysis shows that avoiding it in favor of regional, MAC, or local anesthesia when feasible is protective against postoperative VTE. Converting regional to general adds risk, and maximizing operative time or combining procedures both lengthen surgery — independent VTE risk factors. The intra-operative lever is "less general anesthesia, less time" when clinically appropriate.
hy: Avoiding general anesthesia (regional/MAC/local) lowers postoperative VTE.
src: Neligan I.9 · Intra-operative VTE risk reduction

why.A: Correct — avoiding general anesthesia is protective per meta-analysis.
why.B: Wrong — converting to general anesthesia adds VTE risk.
why.C: Wrong — longer operative time raises VTE risk.
why.D: Wrong — combined procedures lengthen surgery and increase VTE risk.

stem: What relationship does the chapter describe between operative duration / combined procedures and VTE?

options.A: A linear relationship between OR time and VTE risk, with higher risk for combined versus single procedures
options.B: No relationship between operative time and VTE risk in surgical patients
options.C: Combined procedures carry lower VTE risk by sparing a second anesthetic
options.D: VTE risk falls as operative time lengthens because monitoring is continuous

exp: There is a linear relationship between time in the operating room and VTE risk, and combined procedures (which tend to be longer) carry higher VTE risk than single procedures. Single-stage surgery is more convenient and may cost less, but the longer operative time must be weighed against the increased clotting risk. "No relationship," "combined is lower," and "risk falls with time" all contradict this dose–response.
hy: VTE risk rises linearly with OR time; combined > single procedures.
src: Neligan I.9 · Intra-operative VTE risk reduction

why.A: Correct — linear OR-time relationship and higher risk for combined procedures.
why.B: Wrong — there is a clear linear time relationship.
why.C: Wrong — combined procedures carry higher, not lower, VTE risk.
why.D: Wrong — risk increases, not decreases, with operative time.

stem: Beyond emptying the calf veins, by what additional mechanism do sequential compression devices reduce VTE risk?

options.A: They augment systemic fibrinolysis by increasing tissue plasminogen activator (tPA) and PAI-1 levels
options.B: They thin the blood by mechanically diluting circulating clotting factors
options.C: They deliver subcutaneous heparin transdermally through the calf skin
options.D: They cool the limb to slow the enzymatic coagulation cascade

exp: Beyond mechanically emptying the lower-limb veins (recapitulating the calf-muscle pump), sequential compression devices provide systemic VTE protection by augmenting head-to-toe fibrinolysis, increasing tissue plasminogen activator (tPA) and PAI-1 levels. They do not dilute clotting factors, deliver any drug, or work by cooling. This dual action is why SCDs help even territories the cuffs don't directly compress.
hy: SCDs work mechanically AND by boosting systemic fibrinolysis (↑tPA, PAI-1).
src: Neligan I.9 · Intra-operative VTE risk reduction

why.A: Correct — SCDs raise tPA/PAI-1, augmenting systemic fibrinolysis.
why.B: Wrong — they do not dilute circulating clotting factors.
why.C: Wrong — SCDs deliver no transdermal heparin.
why.D: Wrong — the mechanism is fibrinolytic, not limb cooling.

stem: By what mechanism does venous stasis predispose to deep vein thrombosis?

options.A: Venous dilation causes intimal microtears that serve as a nidus for thrombus formation
options.B: Stasis directly activates platelets through high arterial shear stress
options.C: Stasis raises blood oxygen content, which promotes the clotting cascade
options.D: Stasis destroys venous valves, which by itself is sufficient to cause thrombosis

exp: Venous stasis causes venous dilation, and that dilation produces intimal microtears that act as a nidus for deep vein thrombosis — the link between immobility/pooling and clot initiation. Platelet activation by arterial shear, "raised oxygen content," and valve destruction as a sole cause are not the mechanism the chapter describes. This is why measures that prevent pooling (ambulation, SCDs) are central to prophylaxis.
hy: Stasis → venous dilation → intimal microtears → DVT nidus.
src: Neligan I.9 · Intra-operative VTE risk reduction

why.A: Correct — dilation-induced intimal microtears nucleate thrombus.
why.B: Wrong — high arterial shear is not the venous-stasis mechanism.
why.C: Wrong — stasis does not raise oxygen content to promote clotting.
why.D: Wrong — valve damage contributes but is not described as the sole cause here.

stem: The chapter cautions against routine preoperative anticoagulation. Which finding supports this in the facelift population?

options.A: Preoperative enoxaparin markedly increased bleeding (16.2% vs 1.1%) without proven VTE benefit
options.B: Preoperative enoxaparin eliminated VTE with no change in bleeding rates
options.C: Preoperative enoxaparin actually reduced bleeding compared with no prophylaxis
options.D: Preoperative enoxaparin produced bleeding identical to breast reconstruction (each ~6%)

exp: In facelift patients, preoperative enoxaparin significantly increased bleeding (16.2% vs 1.1%, P<0.003) without a demonstrated VTE benefit; combined with meta-analysis showing no VTE reduction from preoperative anticoagulation, this argues against routine preoperative prophylaxis in the general plastic surgery population. By contrast, the breast reconstruction population did not show a significant bleeding increase (6.1% vs 3.3%, P=0.42). The options claiming eliminated VTE, reduced bleeding, or identical 6% bleeding misstate these data.
hy: Preop enoxaparin in facelift = bleeding 16.2% vs 1.1%, no proven VTE benefit.

why.A: Correct — large bleeding increase (16.2 vs 1.1%) with no VTE benefit.
why.B: Wrong — bleeding rose sharply; VTE was not eliminated.
why.C: Wrong — enoxaparin increased, not reduced, bleeding.
why.D: Wrong — the ~6% figure is the breast-reconstruction cohort, not facelift.

stem: Intra-operative hypothermia is defined as a core temperature below what value, and why does it matter?

options.A: Below 36°C; it is linked to increased surgical-site infection and platelet dysfunction
options.B: Below 34°C; it is linked chiefly to triggering malignant hyperthermia
options.C: Below 35°C; its only real consequence is transient patient discomfort
options.D: Below 37°C; it actually improves hemostasis and lowers infection risk

exp: Hypothermia is a core temperature below 36°C; regional and general anesthesia blunt thermoregulation, so active warming (forced-air blankets, fluid warmers, raised ambient temperature) is needed. It matters because hypothermia is correlated with increased surgical-site infection and platelet dysfunction (impaired hemostasis). The 34°C/35°C/37°C options misstate the threshold or wrongly claim benefit, and hypothermia is not the MH trigger.
hy: Hypothermia <36°C → ↑SSI and platelet dysfunction; keep patients normothermic.

why.A: Correct — <36°C, associated with more SSI and platelet dysfunction.
why.B: Wrong — wrong threshold and MH is triggered by drugs, not cold.
why.C: Wrong — consequences extend well beyond discomfort.
why.D: Wrong — hypothermia worsens hemostasis and raises infection risk.

stem: The AORN describes three elements that combine to cause an operating-room fire. Which triad is correct?

options.A: An ignition source, a fuel source, and an oxidizer
options.B: Electrocautery, suction, and irrigation fluid
options.C: Heat, friction, and ambient static electricity
options.D: Alcohol prep, normal saline, and nitrogen gas

exp: The AORN fire triad is an ignition source, a fuel source, and an oxidizer — the three elements that must coincide for an operating-room fire (estimated ~100 occur per year). The distractor triads list specific items or unrelated physics terms rather than the conceptual triad. Recognizing the three categories drives prevention: control sparks, control fuels, and control pooled oxygen.
hy: OR-fire triad = ignition + fuel + oxidizer (AORN).
src: Neligan I.9 · Operating room fire prevention

why.A: Correct — ignition, fuel, and oxidizer is the AORN triad.
why.B: Wrong — lists devices, not the conceptual fire triad.
why.C: Wrong — generic physics terms, not the AORN categories.
why.D: Wrong — names specific materials rather than the three elements.

stem: Which combination correctly assigns the operating-room–fire elements?

options.A: Ignition = electrocautery/laser/frayed cords; fuel = drapes/hair/alcohol prep; oxidizer = pooled oxygen under drapes
options.B: Ignition = alcohol prep; fuel = oxygen under drapes; oxidizer = the electrocautery tip
options.C: Ignition = oxygen masks; fuel = saline irrigation; oxidizer = the surgical drapes
options.D: Ignition = surgical drapes; fuel = the electrocautery unit; oxidizer = the patient's hair

exp: Ignition sources include electrocautery, laser beams, and frayed cords; fuel sources include drapes, hair, clothing, padding, and alcohol-based prep; and oxidizers include oxygen delivered by nasal cannula or mask that pools under the drapes. The other options scramble these roles (e.g., calling oxygen a fuel or drapes an ignition source). Correct assignment is what lets the team interrupt the triad — e.g., letting alcohol prep dry and managing oxygen under drapes.
hy: Ignition = cautery/laser/cords; fuel = drapes/hair/alcohol prep; oxidizer = pooled O₂.
src: Neligan I.9 · Operating room fire prevention

why.A: Correct — assigns cautery→ignition, drapes/alcohol→fuel, pooled O₂→oxidizer.
why.B: Wrong — oxygen is the oxidizer and cautery the ignition, not as labeled.
why.C: Wrong — drapes are fuel and oxygen the oxidizer, not as labeled.
why.D: Wrong — cautery is ignition, not fuel; the assignments are scrambled.

stem: Fischer et al. used NSQIP breast-reduction data to study resident participation. What did propensity-matched analysis show?

options.A: Resident involvement was associated with longer operative time and higher major complications (OR ~2.18)
options.B: Resident involvement roughly halved overall surgical complication rates
options.C: Resident involvement had no measurable effect on any studied outcome
options.D: Resident involvement shortened operative time but raised wound infections only

exp: Using propensity matching on NSQIP breast-reduction data, Fischer et al. found resident involvement associated with prolonged operative time and higher rates of major surgical complication (OR 2.18, P=0.008). The signal is real but confounded — cohorts with and without residents differ in case mix — so it reflects association, not proven causation. The "halved," "no effect," and "wound-infection-only" options contradict the reported OR.
hy: Resident participation in breast reduction: longer OR time, major complications OR ~2.18.
src: Neligan I.9 · Patient safety in teaching hospitals

why.A: Correct — longer operative time and OR ~2.18 for major complications.
why.B: Wrong — complications rose, not fell.
why.C: Wrong — there was a measurable association (OR 2.18).
why.D: Wrong — the signal was major complications and operative time, not wound infection alone.

stem: Clayman and Seagle reviewed Florida office-surgery incidents (2000–2006) causing death, injury, or transfer. Regarding the 20 plastic surgery deaths, what did they find?

options.A: 11 were performed by board-certified plastic surgeons, and the most common cause of death after discharge was VTE
options.B: None were performed by board-certified surgeons, and bleeding was the leading cause
options.C: The true incidence could be precisely calculated and was reassuringly low
options.D: Anesthetic overdose was the leading cause and VTE was never implicated

exp: Clayman and Seagle's review of Florida office-surgery incidents found 20 deaths after plastic surgery procedures, of which 11 were performed by board-certified plastic surgeons, with VTE the most common cause of death after discharge. Importantly, the denominator (number of office procedures) was unrecorded, so a true incidence could not be calculated. The board-certification finding underscores that credentialing alone does not eliminate office-based risk.
hy: Florida office deaths: 20 total, 11 by board-certified surgeons, VTE the top post-discharge cause.

why.A: Correct — 11 of 20 by board-certified surgeons, VTE the leading post-discharge cause.
why.B: Wrong — many were board-certified, and VTE (not bleeding) led.
why.C: Wrong — the denominator was unknown, so incidence could not be calculated.
why.D: Wrong — VTE, not anesthetic overdose, was the leading cause.

stem: Large reviews of ambulatory surgery centers (Parina; Rohrich) found which pattern?

options.A: Plastic surgery can be done safely in ASCs (overall complications ~1%, VTE ~0.05–0.25%), though facilities vary significantly in VTE and readmission rates
options.B: ASCs showed uniformly high complication rates exceeding 10% across all facilities studied
options.C: ASCs recorded essentially no complications of any kind in the reviewed patient cohorts
options.D: ASCs were shown to be unsafe for plastic surgery and were recommended for closure

exp: Reviews by Parina (160,847 ASC patients; mean 30-day VTE 0.25%, abdominoplasty 0.7%, readmission 1.2%) and Rohrich (>26,000 cases; overall complications 0.98%, VTE 0.05%, two deaths) indicate plastic surgery can be performed safely in ambulatory surgery centers — while also showing that facilities vary significantly in VTE and readmission rates. The "uniformly >10%," "no complications," and "unsafe/close them" options overstate in either direction. The nuanced message is "safe overall, but facility-dependent."
hy: ASC plastic surgery is safe overall (~1% complications, low VTE) but facilities vary.

why.A: Correct — low overall complication/VTE rates with significant inter-facility variation.
why.B: Wrong — rates were ~1%, not uniformly >10%.
why.C: Wrong — complications occurred (~1%), not none.
why.D: Wrong — the data support safety, not closure.

stem: Why does fascial plication during abdominoplasty/DIEP raise DVT risk?

options.A: Plication raises intra-abdominal pressure (~2–6 mmHg in abdominoplasty, ~4–8 mmHg in DIEP), back-pressuring the IVC and causing common femoral vein stasis
options.B: Plication directly lacerates the wall of the common femoral vein during dissection
options.C: Plication lowers intra-abdominal pressure, pooling blood within the splanchnic bed
options.D: Plication compresses the aorta, reducing arterial perfusion to both lower limbs

exp: Abdominal-wall tightening procedures raise DVT risk because plication increases intra-abdominal pressure — by ~2–6 mmHg in abdominoplasty and ~4–8 mmHg in DIEP — which back-pressures the inferior vena cava, raising common femoral vein pressure and creating bilateral lower-extremity venous stasis and dilation. The mechanism is hemodynamic, not direct venous laceration, aortic compression, or a drop in intra-abdominal pressure. Hence plication should be used thoughtfully rather than routinely.
hy: Plication ↑IAP (2–6 mmHg abdominoplasty, 4–8 mmHg DIEP) → IVC back-pressure → femoral stasis.
src: Neligan I.9 · Procedures that tighten the abdominal wall

why.A: Correct — raised IAP transmits back-pressure to the IVC/femoral veins.
why.B: Wrong — the mechanism is pressure transmission, not vein laceration.
why.C: Wrong — plication raises, not lowers, intra-abdominal pressure.
why.D: Wrong — it is IVC back-pressure, not aortic compression.

stem: How does abdominoplasty VTE risk compare with the overall aesthetic population?

options.A: Higher — about 0.2% versus ~0.09% overall
options.B: Lower — about 0.02% versus ~0.09% overall
options.C: Identical to the overall aesthetic-population rate
options.D: Higher by 100-fold — about 9% versus ~0.09%

exp: Abdominoplasty carries higher VTE risk than the overall aesthetic population — about 0.2% versus ~0.09% — attributed to plication, longer operative times, and concurrent procedures. The "lower," "identical," and "100-fold/9%" options misstate this roughly two-fold elevation. It is a concrete number to quote when counseling abdominoplasty patients about thrombosis risk.
hy: Abdominoplasty VTE ~0.2% vs ~0.09% overall aesthetics.
src: Neligan I.9 · Procedures that tighten the abdominal wall

why.A: Correct — ~0.2% vs ~0.09%, a roughly two-fold increase.
why.B: Wrong — abdominoplasty risk is higher, not lower, than baseline.
why.C: Wrong — it exceeds the overall aesthetic rate.
why.D: Wrong — the elevation is ~2-fold, not 100-fold.

stem: For tumescent liposuction, lidocaine with epinephrine may be dosed up to what limit, compared with the usual systemic maximum?

options.A: Up to 35 mg/kg with epinephrine for liposuction, versus a general acceptable dose of 5–7 mg/kg
options.B: Up to 7 mg/kg for liposuction, versus 35 mg/kg in general use
options.C: Up to 100 mg/kg with epinephrine, versus 35 mg/kg generally
options.D: Up to 4.5 mg/kg, with or without added epinephrine

exp: For liposuction, lidocaine paired with epinephrine can be dosed up to 35 mg/kg, far above the general acceptable systemic dose of 5–7 mg/kg, because epinephrine vasoconstricts and the poorly vascularized fat absorbs lidocaine slowly. Risk can be further lowered in high-volume cases by reducing wetting-solution lidocaine concentration or using a superwet rather than tumescent technique. The reversed, 100 mg/kg, and 4.5 mg/kg options misstate these limits.
hy: Liposuction lidocaine up to 35 mg/kg with epi vs 5–7 mg/kg general.
src: Neligan I.9 · Liposuction and local anesthesia toxicity

why.A: Correct — 35 mg/kg with epinephrine for liposuction vs 5–7 mg/kg general.
why.B: Wrong — reverses the two doses.
why.C: Wrong — 100 mg/kg overstates the liposuction limit.
why.D: Wrong — 4.5 mg/kg is far below the epinephrine-paired liposuction limit.

stem: When lidocaine with epinephrine is infiltrated into fat, when do plasma levels peak, and how does this differ in vascular areas like the head and neck?

options.A: ~12 hours after infiltration in fat, versus ~6 hours in more vascular head/neck tissue
options.B: ~1 hour in fat, versus ~12 hours in the head and neck
options.C: ~6 hours in fat, versus ~24 hours in the head and neck
options.D: Immediately in both sites, with essentially no time difference

exp: When epinephrine-containing lidocaine is infiltrated into poorly vascularized fat, plasma levels peak ~12 hours after infiltration; in more vascular regions like the head and neck, absorption is faster and levels peak ~6 hours after infiltration. This delayed, prolonged absorption matters for multi-site cases, where late peaks can stack. The 1-hour, 6-vs-24-hour, and "immediate" options misstate this pharmacokinetic delay.
hy: Lidocaine+epi peaks ~12 h in fat, ~6 h in vascular head/neck.
src: Neligan I.9 · Liposuction and local anesthesia toxicity

why.A: Correct — ~12 h peak in fat, ~6 h in head/neck.
why.B: Wrong — fat absorption is slow (~12 h), not ~1 h.
why.C: Wrong — head/neck peaks earlier (~6 h), not at 24 h.
why.D: Wrong — there is a clear delayed peak, not an immediate one.

stem: Which sequence of plasma lidocaine concentrations matches the chapter's toxicity thresholds?

options.A: 3–6 μg/mL initial symptoms; >10 μg/mL CNS depression; >20 μg/mL respiratory depression and cardiac arrest
options.B: 1–2 μg/mL initial symptoms; >5 μg/mL cardiac arrest
options.C: 10–20 μg/mL initial symptoms; >50 μg/mL CNS depression
options.D: 3–6 mg/mL initial symptoms; >10 mg/mL cardiac arrest

exp: Lidocaine toxicity escalates with plasma level: 3–6 μg/mL produces initial symptoms (light-headedness, tinnitus, perioral numbness, metallic taste), concentrations above 10 μg/mL cause CNS depression, and above 20 μg/mL bring respiratory depression and cardiac arrest. The microgram-per-milliliter units and this ladder are the testable point; the mg/mL option is off by a thousand-fold, and the others misplace the cutoffs. Recognizing early symptoms allows intervention before cardiovascular collapse.
hy: Lidocaine: 3–6 μg/mL early Sx; >10 CNS depression; >20 respiratory/cardiac arrest.
src: Neligan I.9 · Liposuction and local anesthesia toxicity

why.A: Correct — matches the 3–6 / >10 / >20 μg/mL ladder.
why.B: Wrong — cardiac arrest at >5 μg/mL is too low.
why.C: Wrong — initial symptoms begin at 3–6, not 10–20 μg/mL.
why.D: Wrong — uses mg/mL, a 1000-fold unit error.

stem: Large-volume liposuction is defined by what aspirate volume, and what setting does the ASPS recommend?

options.A: >5000 mL in one setting; performed in a hospital or accredited/licensed facility with postoperative monitoring for fluid shifts
options.B: >1000 mL in one setting; safely performed in any office without monitoring
options.C: >10,000 mL in one setting; no special facility or monitoring required
options.D: >500 mL in one setting; mandatory postoperative ICU admission for all

exp: Large-volume liposuction is defined as removal of >5000 mL of aspirate in a single setting (some states set lower limits); ASPS recommends it be done only in a hospital or accredited/licensed facility with postoperative admission to monitor for both dehydration and fluid overload as fluid shifts from extra- to intravascular space. The >1000 mL "any office," >10,000 mL "no special setting," and >500 mL "ICU for all" options misstate the threshold or the setting. The fluid-shift monitoring is the safety rationale.
hy: Large-volume liposuction = >5000 mL → hospital/accredited facility with monitoring.
src: Neligan I.9 · Liposuction and local anesthesia toxicity

why.A: Correct — >5000 mL, accredited/hospital setting with fluid-shift monitoring.
why.B: Wrong — 1000 mL "any office" misstates threshold and setting.
why.C: Wrong — a setting is required; 10,000 mL is the wrong cutoff.
why.D: Wrong — 500 mL is far too low and universal ICU is not recommended.

stem: Gluteal fat grafting carries the highest mortality of any aesthetic procedure. What is the reported mortality range and the mechanism of death?

options.A: ~1:2351 to 1:6241, from pulmonary fat embolism when fat is injected intravascularly
options.B: ~1:50 to 1:100, from uncontrolled intraoperative hemorrhage
options.C: ~1:50,000, from local anesthetic systemic toxicity
options.D: ~1:2351 to 1:6241, from delayed wound infection and sepsis

exp: Gluteal fat grafting has higher mortality than any other aesthetic procedure, with published rates between ~1:2351 and ~1:6241, driven by pulmonary fat embolism when fat is inadvertently injected intravascularly. The mechanism is embolic, not hemorrhagic, anesthetic-toxic, or infectious, so the hemorrhage, local-anesthetic, and sepsis options are wrong even where they cite the right ratio. Fat embolism must be discussed at consent and considered in any unstable peri-operative patient.
hy: Gluteal fat grafting mortality ~1:2351–1:6241 from pulmonary fat embolism.

why.A: Correct — ~1:2351–1:6241 mortality from intravascular fat (pulmonary embolism).
why.B: Wrong — wrong ratio and mechanism (not hemorrhage).
why.C: Wrong — death is embolic, not from local anesthetic toxicity.
why.D: Wrong — correct ratio but wrong mechanism (embolism, not sepsis).

stem: Which technique is currently recommended to reduce fat-embolism death during gluteal fat grafting?

options.A: Subcutaneous-only injection with a constantly moving single-hole cannula ≥4.1 mm, avoiding the muscle and high fill pressures
options.B: Deep intramuscular injection to maximize graft take and contour
options.C: Submuscular injection through a fine (<2 mm) sharp needle for precision
options.D: A rapid high-pressure bolus injection to fill the buttock quickly

exp: Current care paradigms recommend subcutaneous-only fat injection using a constantly moving single-hole cannula ≥4.1 mm, with the cannula trajectory avoiding the muscle and avoiding excessively high graft-fill pressures — all to keep fat out of the gluteal venous system. Intramuscular, submuscular, fine-sharp-needle, and high-pressure-bolus techniques each raise the chance of intravascular injection and fatal embolism. These are the concrete, board-tested safety modifications.
hy: BBL safety = subcutaneous only, single-hole cannula ≥4.1 mm, avoid muscle and high pressure.

why.A: Correct — subcutaneous, ≥4.1 mm moving cannula, avoid muscle/high pressure.
why.B: Wrong — intramuscular injection is exactly what raises embolism risk.
why.C: Wrong — submuscular placement endangers the gluteal veins.
why.D: Wrong — high-pressure bolus injection increases intravascular delivery.

stem: In fatal gluteal fat embolism, by what anatomic route does intravascular fat reach the central circulation?

options.A: Through the superior or inferior gluteal veins to the iliac vein and then the central circulation
options.B: Through the lymphatics of the buttock into the thoracic duct
options.C: Directly across the subcutaneous capillary bed into the pulmonary arteries
options.D: Retrograde down the femoral artery into the popliteal circulation

exp: When fat is injected intravascularly during gluteal grafting, it travels through the superior or inferior gluteal veins to the iliac vein and then into the central circulation, lodging in the pulmonary arteries as a fatal embolus. The route is venous and regional, not lymphatic, transcapillary, or retrograde arterial. Understanding this anatomy is why avoiding the muscle (where these veins run) is central to safe technique.
hy: BBL fat embolism route: gluteal veins → iliac vein → central circulation.

why.A: Correct — gluteal veins to iliac vein to central circulation.
why.B: Wrong — the route is venous, not lymphatic/thoracic duct.
why.C: Wrong — emboli enter named gluteal veins, not via the capillary bed.
why.D: Wrong — it is antegrade venous flow, not retrograde femoral arterial.

stem: Which statement about postoperative VTE prevention is most accurate?

options.A: Early ambulation activates the calf-muscle pump and supersedes SCDs in the awake patient, while garments must be checked so they don't act as a venous tourniquet
options.B: SCDs in the awake, ambulating patient provide protection clearly superior to walking itself
options.C: Tight postoperative garments actively protect against DVT by continuously compressing the deep veins
options.D: Postoperative dehydration is protective because it concentrates the body's natural anticoagulant factors

exp: Postoperatively, early ambulation reactivates the calf-muscle pump and makes SCDs effectively obsolete in the awake, walking patient, while postoperative garments must be fitted and checked so they don't act as an iatrogenic venous tourniquet causing partial upstream blockage. SCDs help in the PACU as patients awaken but should not supplant walking. Tight garments do not protect against DVT, and dehydration increases (not decreases) stasis-related risk.
hy: Early ambulation > SCDs in awake patients; check garments for tourniquet effect.
src: Neligan I.9 · Postoperative VTE risk reduction

why.A: Correct — ambulation supersedes SCDs in awake patients; garments can constrict.
why.B: Wrong — walking is superior to SCDs once awake and mobile.
why.C: Wrong — tight garments can act as a venous tourniquet, not protect.
why.D: Wrong — dehydration worsens stasis and DVT risk.

stem: "Coach Class Syndrome" describes travel-associated DVT. What did level I evidence show about graduated compression stockings here?

options.A: 20–30 mmHg stockings cut asymptomatic DVT on long flights from ~4.5% to ~0.25%
options.B: Stockings raised DVT risk by themselves promoting lower-limb venous stasis
options.C: Stockings cut symptomatic, fatal pulmonary embolism from 4.5% down to 0%
options.D: Stockings had no measurable effect on the rate of travel-associated DVT

exp: Duplex studies of asymptomatic long-haul travelers ("Coach Class Syndrome") show ~4.5% develop an asymptomatic DVT on trans-Pacific flights, and level I evidence shows 20–30 mmHg graduated compression stockings cut that risk from ~4.5% to ~0.25% (1:400). The chapter notes this asymptomatic-DVT endpoint doesn't cleanly translate to symptomatic disease, but stockings are reasonable for patients flying around the time of surgery. The "raised risk," "fatal PE to 0%," and "no effect" options misstate the data.
hy: Travel DVT ~4.5% → ~0.25% with 20–30 mmHg compression stockings.
src: Neligan I.9 · Postoperative VTE risk reduction

why.A: Correct — stockings reduced asymptomatic DVT from ~4.5% to ~0.25%.
why.B: Wrong — stockings reduce, not raise, DVT risk.
why.C: Wrong — the endpoint was asymptomatic DVT, not fatal PE to zero.
why.D: Wrong — there was a clear protective effect.

stem: The VTEPS study identified which patients benefit from postoperative enoxaparin prophylaxis?

options.A: Inpatients with Caprini ≥7, in whom 60-day VTE fell (e.g., 8.5%→4.1% for Caprini >8), at the cost of ~0.7% more reoperative hematoma
options.B: All plastic surgery outpatients benefit equally, regardless of their calculated Caprini score
options.C: Only very-low-risk patients with Caprini ≤2 derived measurable VTE risk reduction
options.D: No patient subgroup showed benefit, so prophylaxis is never indicated after surgery

exp: The multicenter VTEPS study showed that plastic surgery inpatients with a Caprini score ≥7 have significant VTE risk reduction from postoperative enoxaparin for the inpatient stay — 60-day VTE falling from 8.5% to 4.1% in Caprini >8 and 2.6% to 1.2% in Caprini 7–8 — while routine anticoagulation carried a ~0.7% increased reoperative-hematoma risk. Benefit is therefore targeted to higher-risk patients, not to all outpatients, low-risk Caprini ≤2 patients, or no one. This score-driven approach is the model for judicious prophylaxis.
hy: VTEPS: Caprini ≥7 inpatients benefit (e.g., 8.5%→4.1%); ~0.7% more hematoma.
src: Neligan I.9 · Anticoagulation in postoperative patients

why.A: Correct — Caprini ≥7 inpatients benefit, with a small hematoma trade-off.
why.B: Wrong — benefit is not universal across outpatients regardless of score.
why.C: Wrong — low-risk Caprini ≤2 patients did not show the benefit.
why.D: Wrong — a high-risk subgroup clearly benefited.

stem: When postoperative enoxaparin is indicated, what does level I evidence from the FIVE trial support for dosing?

options.A: Weight-based dosing (0.5 mg/kg BID) optimizes pharmacokinetics over fixed "one size fits all" dosing
options.B: A single fixed 40 mg daily dose is pharmacokinetically superior for every patient
options.C: Dosing should be tripled during the first 24 hours and then stopped entirely
options.D: Subcutaneous unfractionated heparin is preferred over any enoxaparin regimen

exp: When postoperative anticoagulation is appropriate, level I evidence from the FIVE trial supports weight-based enoxaparin dosing (0.5 mg/kg BID) to optimize pharmacokinetics compared with fixed "one size fits all" dosing. Fixed 40 mg daily, tripling-then-stopping, and substituting unfractionated heparin are not the trial's conclusion. Weight-based dosing better matches drug exposure to body size, improving the risk–benefit balance.
hy: FIVE trial: weight-based enoxaparin 0.5 mg/kg BID beats fixed dosing.
src: Neligan I.9 · Anticoagulation in postoperative patients

why.A: Correct — weight-based 0.5 mg/kg BID optimizes pharmacokinetics.
why.B: Wrong — fixed 40 mg daily is the inferior comparator.
why.C: Wrong — no triple-then-stop regimen is supported.
why.D: Wrong — the trial supports weight-based enoxaparin, not switching to UFH.

stem: Reflecting the opioid crisis, what is the chapter's recommended analgesic paradigm for plastic surgery?

options.A: Multimodal, non-opioid-first regimens (an ERAS principle), reserving opioids rather than using them first-line
options.B: Opioid monotherapy aggressively titrated to patient comfort
options.C: Avoidance of essentially all analgesia in order to minimize prescribing
options.D: Routine high-dose opioids, with no meaningful role for adjuncts

exp: Reflecting the opioid crisis (over 40,000 opioid-attributed deaths in 2017; surgeons responsible for ~10% of opioid prescriptions), the chapter endorses a paradigm shift to multimodal, non-opioid-first analgesia — a central facet of Enhanced Recovery After Surgery (ERAS) — reserving opioids rather than using them first-line. Opioid monotherapy and routine high-dose opioids are exactly what this approach moves away from, and withholding all analgesia is neither intended nor humane. Over-prescribing fuels both addiction and diversion.
hy: Multimodal, opioid-sparing analgesia (ERAS) is the modern standard.

why.A: Correct — multimodal, non-opioid-first (ERAS) is the recommended paradigm.
why.B: Wrong — opioid monotherapy is what the shift moves away from.
why.C: Wrong — analgesia is provided, just opioid-sparing.
why.D: Wrong — adjuncts are central, not absent.

stem: Which statement about non-opioid adjuncts matches the chapter's figures?

options.A: NSAIDs reduce opioid use ~40%, and gabapentin reduces narcotic use ~35%
options.B: NSAIDs increase opioid use ~40%, while gabapentin reduces it ~35%
options.C: NSAIDs reduce opioid use only ~5%, and gabapentin has no opioid-sparing effect
options.D: Both NSAIDs and gabapentin increase postoperative narcotic requirements

exp: NSAIDs are a core multimodal agent that reduce opioid use by ~40% (with improved pain scores and no definitive bleeding association in plastic surgery), and gabapentin reduces narcotic use by ~35% in pooled analyses. The reversed and downgraded options contradict these figures, and both agents are opioid-sparing rather than opioid-increasing. Around-the-clock NSAIDs help establish a baseline level of pain control.
hy: NSAIDs ↓opioid use ~40%; gabapentin ↓narcotic use ~35%.
src: Neligan I.9 · Opioid management / multimodal analgesia

why.A: Correct — NSAIDs ~40% and gabapentin ~35% opioid-sparing.
why.B: Wrong — NSAIDs reduce, not increase, opioid use.
why.C: Wrong — both have substantial opioid-sparing effects, not ~5%/none.
why.D: Wrong — neither increases narcotic requirements.

stem: For an adult with normal liver function, the chapter's around-the-clock acetaminophen regimen is:

options.A: Up to 4000 mg/day in 1000 mg increments every 6 hours, avoiding concurrent combination narcotics
options.B: Up to 8000 mg/day in 2000 mg increments every 6 hours as needed
options.C: Up to 2000 mg/day, given only on an as-needed basis for breakthrough pain
options.D: Up to 4000 mg/day, always paired with a combination narcotic for synergy

exp: Acetaminophen has a favorable safety profile and, like NSAIDs, can be given around the clock: adults with normal liver function can receive up to 4000 mg/day in 1000 mg increments every 6 hours, and patients on scheduled acetaminophen should not also receive combination narcotics (which contain acetaminophen) to avoid overdose. The 8000 mg/day and "always paired with a narcotic" options are unsafe, and 2000 mg as-needed undertreats. It can even be started the night before or in the preoperative area since it has no bleeding risk.
hy: Acetaminophen ≤4000 mg/day (1000 mg q6h); avoid co-prescribed combination narcotics.
src: Neligan I.9 · Opioid management / multimodal analgesia

why.A: Correct — ≤4000 mg/day, 1000 mg q6h, avoid combination narcotics.
why.B: Wrong — 8000 mg/day exceeds the safe hepatic limit.
why.C: Wrong — 2000 mg as-needed undertreats versus scheduled dosing.
why.D: Wrong — pairing with combination narcotics risks acetaminophen overdose.

stem: Which statement about these adjuncts matches the chapter?

options.A: A single 8 mg IV dexamethasone dose reduces opioid use and PONV but is avoided in poorly controlled diabetes; liposomal bupivacaine can give ≥72 hours of relief
options.B: Dexamethasone is dosed daily for a week and is safest in poorly controlled diabetes
options.C: Liposomal bupivacaine lasts ~8 hours, shorter than plain bupivacaine's duration
options.D: Dexamethasone worsens postoperative nausea, so it is reserved for analgesia alone

exp: A single intra-operative 8 mg IV dexamethasone dose reduces postoperative opioid consumption and improves nausea/vomiting, but should be avoided in poorly controlled diabetes; separately, extended-release liposomal bupivacaine can provide 72 hours or more of pain relief versus the ~8–12 hours of plain local anesthetic. The daily-for-a-week, "safe in poorly controlled diabetes," "lasts 8 hours," and "worsens nausea" options each contradict the text. These adjuncts round out a procedure-specific multimodal plan.
hy: Dexamethasone single 8 mg IV (avoid uncontrolled DM); liposomal bupivacaine ≥72 h.
src: Neligan I.9 · Opioid management / multimodal analgesia

why.A: Correct — single 8 mg IV dexamethasone (avoid uncontrolled DM) and ~72 h liposomal bupivacaine.
why.B: Wrong — it is a single intra-operative dose and is avoided, not preferred, in poorly controlled diabetes.
why.C: Wrong — liposomal bupivacaine lasts ~72 h, longer than plain bupivacaine.
why.D: Wrong — dexamethasone improves, not worsens, postoperative nausea/vomiting.

stem: A patient reports persistent burning pain in a reconstructed limb months after the wound has healed. How is this pain best classified, and how does it differ from acute surgical pain?

options.A: Pathologic (neuropathic) pain — it persists beyond healing and is nonprotective/maladaptive, unlike physiologic nociceptive pain
options.B: Physiologic nociceptive pain — it serves a protective warning function and promotes healing
options.C: Referred visceral pain — it arises from an internal organ and is projected to the limb
options.D: Psychogenic pain — it has no neural basis and is purely an emotional response

exp: Pain that persists beyond the period of healing is pathologic (e.g., neuropathic) pain — it no longer serves a protective purpose and is maladaptive, in contrast to physiologic/nociceptive pain, which is acute, protective, and promotes healing. The chronic burning quality and persistence after the wound has healed identify it as pathologic. Multimodal analgesia may reduce the chance such persistent pain develops.
hy: Physiologic = protective acute; pathologic/neuropathic = persists past healing, maladaptive.
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — persistence beyond healing defines pathologic/neuropathic pain.
why.B: Wrong — nociceptive pain is acute and protective, not the chronic maladaptive picture here.
why.C: Wrong — referred visceral pain arises from organs, not a healed reconstructed limb.
why.D: Wrong — neuropathic pain has a clear neural basis; "no neural basis" is incorrect.

stem: Approximately what proportion of patients report acute pain after surgery, and what is the chapter's central strategy to address it?

options.A: ~80%, addressed by a multimodal, multi-targeted analgesic approach rather than single agents
options.B: ~20%, addressed by escalating opioid monotherapy to comfort
options.C: ~50%, addressed by routine general anesthesia extension into recovery
options.D: ~10%, addressed by withholding analgesia until pain is severe

exp: Approximately 80% of patients report acute pain after surgery, most of it moderate-to-severe, which is why the chapter advocates a multimodal, multi-targeted strategy (peripheral nociceptors, peripheral nerves, spinal and supraspinal sites) rather than single-agent therapy. Multimodal analgesia maximizes efficacy while limiting the adverse effects of any one drug class. Opioid monotherapy and withholding analgesia run counter to this approach.
hy: ~80% have acute postop pain → multimodal, opioid-sparing analgesia.
src: Neligan I.10 · Clinical consequences of inadequate pain relief
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — ~80% report acute pain; multimodal analgesia is the strategy.
why.B: Wrong — the figure is ~80%, and opioid monotherapy is what the chapter moves away from.
why.C: Wrong — wrong percentage and not the recommended approach.
why.D: Wrong — withholding analgesia until severe is explicitly poor practice.

stem: Per Table 10.1, inadequate postoperative pain control produces which pulmonary consequence?

options.A: Increased splinting with decreased ambulation and vital capacity → atelectasis, hypoventilation, and pulmonary infection
options.B: Bronchodilation with increased vital capacity and improved gas exchange
options.C: Central respiratory depression from unopposed vagal tone
options.D: Pleural effusion from increased pulmonary capillary pressure

exp: Per Table 10.1, inadequate pain control causes increased splinting with decreased ambulation and vital capacity, leading to atelectasis, hypoventilation, and pulmonary infection. Pain-driven guarding reduces effective ventilation. The bronchodilation, central depression, and effusion options describe the opposite or unrelated processes.
hy: Pain → splinting → atelectasis/hypoventilation/pneumonia.
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — splinting and reduced vital capacity drive atelectasis and infection.
why.B: Wrong — pain causes splinting and reduced vital capacity, not bronchodilation/improvement.
why.C: Wrong — the pulmonary effect is mechanical (splinting), not central vagal depression.
why.D: Wrong — Table 10.1 lists atelectasis/infection, not effusion from capillary pressure.

stem: Per Table 10.1, which organ-system effect is correctly paired with inadequate pain control?

options.A: Hematologic — decreased ambulation → venostasis and increased DVT risk
options.B: Renal — direct nephrotoxicity from circulating pain mediators
options.C: Hepatic — cholestasis and transaminitis from catecholamine surge
options.D: Endocrine — suppression of cortisol and a hypometabolic state

exp: Per Table 10.1, the hematologic consequence is decreased ambulation leading to venostasis and increased DVT risk — the link between poorly controlled pain and thromboembolism. The renal, hepatic, and endocrine pairings are not the table's listed responses (and the cortisol effect is actually an increase, driving hyperglycemia/catabolism, not suppression).
hy: Pain → ↓ambulation → venostasis → DVT (Table 10.1 hematologic row).
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — reduced ambulation causes venostasis and DVT, the hematologic row.
why.B: Wrong — Table 10.1 does not list direct renal nephrotoxicity from pain mediators.
why.C: Wrong — no cholestasis/transaminitis row appears in Table 10.1.
why.D: Wrong — Table 10.1 lists no endocrine row; the surgical stress response actually raises cortisol (hypermetabolic), the opposite of this option.

stem: Persistent pain one year after breast surgery (mastectomy, reduction, augmentation) has been reported in approximately what range, and what may reduce it?

options.A: 22–49%, potentially reduced by multimodal analgesia
options.B: 1–3%, essentially eliminated by opioid monotherapy
options.C: 70–90%, unaffected by any analgesic strategy
options.D: <1%, reduced only by avoiding all regional blocks

exp: Persistent pain one year after breast surgery has been reported in 22–49% of patients, with a multifactorial etiology (nerve injury, radiation, chemotherapy, phantom breast pain), and multimodal analgesia may reduce its incidence. The 1–3%, 70–90%, and <1% options misstate the cited range. This is a high-yield counseling figure for breast patients.
hy: Persistent post-breast-surgery pain 22–49% at 1 yr; multimodal analgesia may lower it.
src: Neligan I.10 · Clinical consequences of inadequate pain relief
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — 22–49% at one year, reducible by multimodal analgesia.
why.B: Wrong — far understates the incidence and overstates opioid benefit.
why.C: Wrong — 70–90% overstates it and ignores the multimodal effect.
why.D: Wrong — <1% is far below the cited 22–49%.

stem: Inadequate analgesia amplifies the surgical stress response. Which mechanism explains how this can impair wound healing in plastic surgery?

options.A: Surgical stress raises cortisol and humoral mediators → hyperglycemia, catabolism, and impaired immune function
options.B: Surgical stress lowers cortisol → hypoglycemia and excessive anabolism
options.C: Surgical stress directly lyses fibroblasts at the wound edge
options.D: Surgical stress increases tissue oxygen delivery, accelerating healing

exp: Inadequate analgesia heightens the surgical stress response: increased cortisol and humoral mediators produce hyperglycemia, catabolism, and impaired immune function and wound healing — particularly concerning for wound infection in plastic surgery. Adequate analgesia can mitigate this detrimental cascade. The hypoglycemia/anabolism, fibroblast-lysis, and increased-oxygen-delivery options invert or misstate the physiology.
hy: Poor analgesia → ↑cortisol/stress → hyperglycemia, catabolism, impaired immunity/healing.
src: Neligan I.10 · Clinical consequences of inadequate pain relief
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — the cortisol-driven stress response impairs immunity and healing.
why.B: Wrong — stress raises cortisol and causes catabolism, not the reverse.
why.C: Wrong — the mechanism is humoral/metabolic, not direct fibroblast lysis.
why.D: Wrong — the stress response impairs healing rather than accelerating it.

stem: What do hand-surgery data suggest about the role of acute postoperative prescribing in the opioid epidemic?

options.A: 8–13% of patients still used opioids at 90–120 days, implicating acute postoperative prescribing in excess use
options.B: Essentially no patients continued opioids past one week, exonerating surgical prescribing
options.C: Only patients with prior chronic pain continued opioids, so acute surgery plays no role
options.D: Continued use was confined to inpatients, not outpatient hand surgery

exp: Two hand-surgery studies found 8–13% of patients still using opioids at 90–120 days, supporting the view that opioid prescribing for acute postoperative pain — not just chronic pain — explains much of the excess prescribing. This reframes surgeons as central to opioid stewardship. The "no one continued," "only prior chronic pain," and "inpatients only" options contradict these data.
hy: 8–13% of hand-surgery patients still on opioids at 90–120 d → acute prescribing matters.
src: Neligan I.10 · Clinical consequences of inadequate pain relief
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — 8–13% persistent use implicates acute surgical prescribing.
why.B: Wrong — a meaningful fraction continued well past a week.
why.C: Wrong — opioid-naïve surgical patients developed persistent use.
why.D: Wrong — the hand-surgery cohorts were outpatients.

stem: A 2016 study of pediatric opioid prescriptions found which pattern?

options.A: 90% were prescribed by surgeons, with the average quantity sufficient for over 7 days
options.B: 90% were prescribed by pediatricians for non-surgical pain
options.C: Prescriptions averaged under one day's supply, indicating careful stewardship
options.D: Opioids were essentially never prescribed to children after surgery

exp: In a 2016 study of pediatric opioid prescriptions, 90% were written by surgeons and the average quantity dispensed was sufficient for over 7 days — evidence of overprescribing even in children. The pediatrician, under-one-day, and never-prescribed options contradict these findings. It underscores that surgical stewardship extends to pediatric practice.
hy: Pediatric opioids: 90% by surgeons, average supply >7 days.
src: Neligan I.10 · Clinical consequences of inadequate pain relief
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — 90% surgeon-prescribed, >7 days average supply.
why.B: Wrong — surgeons, not pediatricians, wrote most prescriptions.
why.C: Wrong — the average exceeded 7 days, not under one day.
why.D: Wrong — opioids were prescribed and over-supplied to children.

stem: For an adult without hepatic or renal disease, what is the maximum recommended daily acetaminophen dose, and its most significant serious toxicity?

options.A: 4 grams/day; hepatotoxicity at high doses, chronic use, or with alcohol
options.B: 8 grams/day; nephrotoxicity from tubular injury
options.C: 2 grams/day; methemoglobinemia at therapeutic doses
options.D: 6 grams/day; gastrointestinal ulceration and bleeding

exp: The maximum recommended daily acetaminophen dose in an adult without hepatic or renal disease is 4 grams, with dose reduction in liver insufficiency; its most significant serious adverse effect is hepatotoxicity at high doses, in chronic use, or with alcohol/other hepatotoxins. 8 g and 6 g exceed the safe ceiling, and 2 g understates it. Acetaminophen otherwise has a benign profile, which is why it anchors multimodal plans.
hy: Acetaminophen max 4 g/day; hepatotoxicity is the key serious risk.
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — 4 g/day ceiling; hepatotoxicity is the main serious toxicity.
why.B: Wrong — 8 g exceeds the limit; the key toxicity is hepatic, not renal.
