why.C: Wrong — 2 g understates the limit; methemoglobinemia is very rare, not at therapeutic doses.
why.D: Wrong — 6 g exceeds the limit; GI ulceration is an NSAID effect.

stem: Compared with opioids and NSAIDs, what makes acetaminophen a cornerstone of multimodal analgesia?

options.A: It has no effect on respiratory drive, platelets, or GI motility, and reduces opioid requirements
options.B: It is the most potent analgesic available and eliminates the need for any opioid
options.C: It has strong anti-inflammatory action superior to NSAIDs
options.D: It reliably increases platelet aggregation, lowering bleeding risk

exp: Acetaminophen has no effect on respiratory drive, platelets/hemostasis, or GI motility, and reduces opioid requirements, making it a safe backbone of every multimodal plan unless contraindicated. It is less potent than NSAIDs/Coxibs and lacks meaningful anti-inflammatory action, and it does not increase platelet aggregation. Its safety, not potency, is the reason it is ubiquitous.
hy: Acetaminophen = opioid-sparing, no platelet/respiratory/GI-motility effect.
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — clean side-effect profile plus opioid sparing.
why.B: Wrong — it is less potent than NSAIDs and does not eliminate opioid need.
why.C: Wrong — its anti-inflammatory activity is weak, not superior to NSAIDs.
why.D: Wrong — it has insignificant platelet effects, not pro-aggregation.

stem: When is intravenous acetaminophen specifically recommended as a first-line agent?

options.A: For pain and fever in adults and children when oral administration is not possible, giving rapid predictable plasma levels
options.B: Only as a second-line rescue agent after opioids fail
options.C: Exclusively for chronic neuropathic pain unresponsive to gabapentin
options.D: Only in patients with hepatic insufficiency, where it is safest

exp: IV acetaminophen produces rapid, predictable plasma concentrations and is recommended as a first-line agent for pain and fever in adults and children when oral administration is not possible. It is not merely a rescue agent, is not limited to neuropathic pain, and is not reserved for hepatic insufficiency (where dose reduction is actually advised). This makes it valuable perioperatively when patients are NPO.
hy: IV acetaminophen = first-line when oral route unavailable (rapid predictable levels).
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — first-line for pain/fever when oral dosing is not possible.
why.B: Wrong — it is first-line, not a last-resort rescue agent.
why.C: Wrong — it treats acute pain/fever broadly, not only neuropathic pain.
why.D: Wrong — in hepatic insufficiency the dose is reduced, not preferentially used.

stem: What is the primary analgesic mechanism of nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs?

options.A: Inhibition of COX-1 and COX-2, reducing thromboxane and prostaglandin synthesis
options.B: Antagonism of the NMDA receptor in the dorsal horn
options.C: Binding voltage-gated calcium channels to reduce substance P release
options.D: Agonism at spinal mu-opioid receptors

exp: NSAIDs work primarily by inhibiting cyclooxygenase (COX-1 and COX-2), reducing synthesis of thromboxanes and prostaglandins, which underlies their analgesic, antipyretic, and anti-inflammatory effects. NMDA antagonism is ketamine's mechanism, calcium-channel binding is the gabapentinoids', and mu-opioid agonism is the opioids'. The prostaglandin link also explains NSAID GI, renal, and platelet effects.
hy: NSAIDs = COX-1/COX-2 inhibition → ↓prostaglandins/thromboxanes.
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — COX inhibition reducing prostaglandins/thromboxanes.
why.B: Wrong — NMDA antagonism is ketamine's mechanism.
why.C: Wrong — calcium-channel binding describes gabapentinoids.
why.D: Wrong — mu-opioid agonism describes opioids, not NSAIDs.

stem: Per Table 10.2, which dosing rule applies to ketorolac?

options.A: Maximum single dose 30 mg and discontinue after 5 days, given the risk of GI ulceration
options.B: No ceiling dose, and it may be continued indefinitely
options.C: It is an oral-only COX-2 selective agent with no platelet effect
options.D: It must be loaded at 5 mg/kg and continued for 14 days

exp: Per Table 10.2, ketorolac has a maximum single dose of 30 mg and should be discontinued after 5 days because of its risk of GI upset and ulcers; the loading dose is 0.5–1 mg/kg (max 30 mg) with maintenance 0.5 mg/kg (max 15 mg) q6h, max daily 60 mg. It is not ceiling-free, not COX-2 selective, and is not loaded at 5 mg/kg for 14 days. The 5-day limit is the classic board point.
hy: Ketorolac: max single 30 mg, stop after 5 days.
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — 30 mg single-dose cap and 5-day limit per Table 10.2.
why.B: Wrong — ketorolac has clear dose and duration ceilings.
why.C: Wrong — it is a nonselective NSAID with platelet effects, not COX-2 selective oral-only.
why.D: Wrong — loading is 0.5–1 mg/kg and use is limited to 5 days, not 14.

stem: Per Table 10.2, which maximum adult daily dose is correctly matched?

options.A: Ibuprofen 3200 mg; naproxen 1500 mg; celecoxib 400 mg
options.B: Ibuprofen 800 mg; naproxen 4000 mg; celecoxib 50 mg
options.C: Ibuprofen 6000 mg; naproxen 200 mg; celecoxib 2000 mg
options.D: Ibuprofen 400 mg; naproxen 6000 mg; celecoxib 60 mg

exp: Per Table 10.2, the maximum adult daily doses are ibuprofen 3200 mg, naproxen 1500 mg, and celecoxib 400 mg (with acetaminophen and aspirin both 4000 mg). The other option sets transpose or invent values. Knowing the ceilings prevents inadvertent overdosing in multimodal regimens.
hy: Max daily: ibuprofen 3200, naproxen 1500, celecoxib 400 mg.
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — matches Table 10.2 maximum daily doses.
why.B: Wrong — transposes and understates/overstates the ceilings.
why.C: Wrong — invented values inconsistent with the table.
why.D: Wrong — these figures contradict the table entirely.

stem: Among NSAIDs, how does the risk of myocardial infarction differ between agents?

options.A: Diclofenac carries the greatest risk and naproxen the least
options.B: Naproxen carries the greatest risk and diclofenac the least
options.C: All NSAIDs carry an identical, dose-independent MI risk
options.D: NSAIDs uniformly protect against MI through platelet inhibition

exp: The chapter states the cardiovascular (MI) risk differs between NSAIDs, with diclofenac posing the greatest risk and naproxen the least; this risk is most concerning in underlying ischemic heart disease. The reversed and "identical/uniformly protective" options contradict the text. Naproxen's relatively favorable cardiovascular profile is the practical takeaway.
hy: NSAID MI risk: diclofenac highest, naproxen lowest.
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — diclofenac greatest MI risk, naproxen least.
why.B: Wrong — reverses the two drugs.
why.C: Wrong — the risk differs by agent, it is not identical.
why.D: Wrong — NSAIDs can raise MI risk, not uniformly protect.

stem: A patient with a documented sulfonamide allergy is being considered for a COX-2 inhibitor. What is the relevant caution?

options.A: Celecoxib has a sulfonamide-like structure and should not be given to patients with a known sulfa allergy
options.B: Celecoxib is a safe alternative precisely because it shares no structure with sulfonamides
options.C: Celecoxib is contraindicated only in patients with a penicillin allergy
options.D: Sulfa allergy mandates doubling the celecoxib dose to overcome cross-tolerance

exp: Celecoxib has a structure similar to sulfonamides and should not be given to patients with a known sulfa drug allergy. It is not a "safe because structurally unrelated" alternative, is not tied to penicillin allergy, and a sulfa allergy is a reason to avoid it, not to double it. This cross-reactivity is a frequently tested safety point.
hy: Celecoxib is sulfonamide-like → avoid in sulfa allergy.
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — celecoxib's sulfonamide structure contraindicates it in sulfa allergy.
why.B: Wrong — it does share structure with sulfonamides, so it is not safe here.
why.C: Wrong — the relevant cross-reactivity is sulfa, not penicillin.
why.D: Wrong — allergy is a reason to avoid, never to increase the dose.

stem: Which statement about NSAID adverse effects in the surgical patient is correct?

options.A: Renal insufficiency occurs especially with hypovolemia (bleeding/dehydration); platelet inhibition raises theoretical hematoma risk
options.B: NSAIDs improve renal perfusion in hypovolemic patients via prostaglandin synthesis
options.C: NSAIDs have no effect on platelet function or hemostasis
options.D: NSAIDs are absolutely contraindicated in all plastic surgery by proven hematoma data

exp: NSAIDs can precipitate renal insufficiency specifically in the setting of hypovolemia (from perioperative bleeding or dehydration) via prostaglandin inhibition, and their platelet inhibition creates a theoretical bleeding/hematoma risk. However, several well-designed studies show no increased hematoma with ibuprofen or ketorolac, so the risk is debated rather than proven absolute. The prostaglandin-improves-perfusion and "no platelet effect" options are incorrect.
hy: NSAID renal risk rises with hypovolemia; hematoma risk theoretical/debated.
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — hypovolemia-related renal risk plus platelet-mediated bleeding concern.
why.B: Wrong — prostaglandin inhibition worsens renal perfusion in hypovolemia.
why.C: Wrong — NSAIDs do impair platelet function.
why.D: Wrong — the data do not prove an absolute hematoma contraindication.

stem: What is the principal advantage of selective COX-2 inhibitors over traditional NSAIDs, and their key restriction?

options.A: Negligible platelet effect (low bleeding/hematoma risk), but they should be avoided in ischemic heart disease
options.B: Complete elimination of GI bleeding risk, and they are safest in ischemic heart disease
options.C: Superior analgesic potency proven over NSAIDs, with no cardiovascular concern
options.D: No renal side effects whatsoever compared with NSAIDs

exp: The major advantage of Coxibs is their negligible effect on platelet function, giving a low bleeding/hematoma risk and fewer GI ulcers than NSAIDs; however, because of thromboembolic/cardiovascular risk they should be avoided in ischemic heart disease. They reduce but do not eliminate GI bleeding, are not proven analgesically superior to NSAIDs, and have a renal side-effect rate similar to NSAIDs.
hy: Coxibs: negligible platelet/bleeding effect, but avoid in ischemic heart disease.
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — low bleeding risk but cardiovascular caution in ischemic heart disease.
why.B: Wrong — GI bleeding is reduced, not eliminated, and they are not safest in IHD.
why.C: Wrong — analgesic superiority over NSAIDs is not proven.
why.D: Wrong — renal side effects are similar to NSAIDs, not absent.

stem: Per Table 10.3, which pharmacologic property is correctly stated?

options.A: Etoricoxib has a long plasma half-life (~22 h) and duration of action (~20 h), unlike acetaminophen's 4–6 h duration
options.B: IV acetaminophen has only 50% bioavailability and an 8-hour half-life
options.C: Ketorolac IV has a 22-hour half-life and 20-hour duration
options.D: Ibuprofen has a 12-hour half-life and 24-hour duration

exp: Per Table 10.3, etoricoxib has a long plasma half-life (~22 h) and duration of action (~20 h), contrasting with acetaminophen's 4–6 h duration — relevant for dosing frequency. IV acetaminophen actually has 100% bioavailability and a ~2.7 h half-life; ketorolac IV has a 4–9 h half-life (~11 h duration), and ibuprofen a ~2 h half-life. The long-acting Coxib point is the table's standout.
hy: Etoricoxib ~22 h half-life / ~20 h duration (long-acting Coxib).
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — etoricoxib is long-acting versus acetaminophen's short duration.
why.B: Wrong — IV acetaminophen is 100% bioavailable with a ~2.7 h half-life.
why.C: Wrong — ketorolac IV half-life is ~4–9 h, not 22 h.
why.D: Wrong — ibuprofen's half-life is ~2 h, not 12 h.

stem: What is ketamine's mechanism and the feature that makes it attractive in a hemodynamically unstable plastic surgery patient?

options.A: Noncompetitive NMDA-receptor antagonist that preserves respiratory drive and supports hemodynamics via sympathomimetic effect
options.B: Pure mu-opioid agonist that provides analgesia but causes marked respiratory depression
options.C: COX-2 selective inhibitor that reduces inflammation without cardiac effects
options.D: GABA-A agonist that produces deep sedation with hypotension

exp: Ketamine is a noncompetitive NMDA-receptor antagonist; its hallmark advantages are preservation of respiratory drive (continuous spontaneous breathing, maintained airway tone) and hemodynamic support through a mild sympathomimetic effect (catecholamine release, noradrenaline-reuptake inhibition raising HR, stroke volume, and MAP). This makes it favorable in hemodynamically unstable patients. It is not an opioid agonist, COX-2 inhibitor, or GABA-A agonist.
hy: Ketamine = NMDA antagonist; preserves breathing + supports hemodynamics.
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — NMDA antagonist with respiratory and hemodynamic preservation.
why.B: Wrong — it is not a mu-opioid agonist and does not depress respiration.
why.C: Wrong — it is not a COX-2 inhibitor.
why.D: Wrong — it is not a GABA-A agonist and does not cause hypotension.

stem: At what dose does ketamine act as an analgesic adjunct, and why is that range favored in microsurgery?

options.A: Subanesthetic ~0.1 mg/kg/h — its sympathomimetic effect reduces vasopressor need, aiding flap perfusion, versus anesthetic doses of 1–5 mg/kg
options.B: Anesthetic 1–5 mg/kg continuous infusion, to keep the patient fully unconscious during the flap
options.C: 10–20 mg/kg boluses, to maximize the dissociative effect
options.D: A fixed 500 mg dose regardless of weight, for predictable plasma levels

exp: At subanesthetic doses (~0.1 mg/kg/h) ketamine improves analgesia and reduces opioid requirements; its sympathomimetic effect can lower perioperative vasopressor need, which is ideal for flap perfusion in microsurgery. Anesthetic doses are 1–5 mg/kg and carry more neuropsychiatric effects. The 1–5 mg/kg/h infusion, 10–20 mg/kg boluses, and fixed-500-mg options misstate the analgesic range.
hy: Ketamine analgesic dose ~0.1 mg/kg/h; sympathomimetic aids flap perfusion.
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — ~0.1 mg/kg/h subanesthetic dosing with flap-perfusion benefit.
why.B: Wrong — 1–5 mg/kg is the anesthetic (not analgesic-adjunct) range.
why.C: Wrong — 10–20 mg/kg would be grossly excessive.
why.D: Wrong — ketamine is weight-based, not a fixed 500 mg dose.

stem: Why is ketamine generally unsuitable as a sole analgesic agent?

options.A: Dose-dependent neuropsychiatric effects (dysphoria, hallucinations, vivid dreams) that are far less common at subanesthetic doses
options.B: Profound respiratory depression requiring routine intubation
options.C: Severe platelet inhibition that precludes any surgical use
options.D: Reliable, dangerous hypotension at every dose

exp: Ketamine's dose-dependent neuropsychiatric effects — dysphoria, hallucinations, vivid unpleasant dreams, catatonia/psychosis — make it unsuitable as a sole analgesic, though these are far less common at subanesthetic doses. It actually preserves respiratory drive (not profound depression), has no major platelet effect, and supports rather than lowers blood pressure. The psychiatric profile, not respiratory or hemodynamic risk, is the limiting factor.
hy: Ketamine's neuropsychiatric effects limit it to an adjunct, not a sole agent.
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — dysphoria/hallucinations limit sole use; less frequent at low dose.
why.B: Wrong — ketamine preserves respiratory drive.
why.C: Wrong — it does not cause severe platelet inhibition.
why.D: Wrong — it tends to raise, not lower, blood pressure.

stem: Gabapentin and pregabalin are GABA analogues, yet they do not act at GABA receptors. What is their actual mechanism?

options.A: Binding voltage-gated calcium channels of cortical/dorsal-horn neurons, attenuating glutamate, norepinephrine, and substance P release
options.B: Direct agonism at GABA-A and GABA-B receptors in the spinal cord
options.C: Irreversible inhibition of COX-1 and COX-2 enzymes
options.D: Antagonism of peripheral mu-opioid receptors

exp: Although structurally GABA analogues, gabapentin and pregabalin do not act at GABA-A or GABA-B receptors; instead they bind voltage-gated calcium channels of cortical and dorsal-horn neurons, attenuating release of glutamate, norepinephrine, and substance P (gabapentin may also activate descending inhibitory noradrenergic pathways). They are not COX inhibitors or opioid antagonists. This mechanism underlies their use in neuropathic pain.
hy: Gabapentinoids bind voltage-gated Ca channels (not GABA receptors).
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — voltage-gated calcium-channel binding reduces neurotransmitter release.
why.B: Wrong — they do not act via GABA-A/GABA-B receptors despite the name.
why.C: Wrong — COX inhibition is the NSAID mechanism.
why.D: Wrong — they are not opioid antagonists.

stem: The FDA placed a December 2019 "Black Box" warning on gabapentin for what reason?

options.A: Respiratory depression when combined with CNS depressants (opioids, benzodiazepines) or in COPD/elderly patients
options.B: A newly proven causal link to hepatic failure at therapeutic doses
options.C: An association with severe platelet dysfunction and surgical bleeding
options.D: Direct cardiotoxicity producing QT prolongation and arrhythmia

exp: The FDA's December 2019 Black Box warning on gabapentin reflects the risk of serious respiratory depression when it is combined with CNS depressants (opioids, benzodiazepines) or used in patients with COPD or advanced age; chronic-opioid patients are especially likely to die from respiratory depression with co-administered gabapentin. It is not based on hepatic failure, platelet dysfunction, or QT prolongation.
hy: Gabapentin Black Box (Dec 2019) = respiratory depression with CNS depressants/COPD/elderly.
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — respiratory depression with CNS depressants and in COPD/elderly.
why.B: Wrong — the warning is respiratory, not hepatic.
why.C: Wrong — it is not based on platelet/bleeding effects.
why.D: Wrong — it is not a QT/cardiotoxicity warning.

stem: Per Table 10.4, why is codeine an unreliable and potentially dangerous analgesic?

options.A: It is a prodrug requiring CYP2D6 conversion to morphine — ultra-rapid metabolizers overproduce it (black-box contraindication after pediatric tonsillectomy), poor metabolizers get no analgesia
options.B: It is a direct-acting opioid with predictable potency unaffected by metabolism
options.C: It cannot be combined with acetaminophen because of hepatotoxic synergy
options.D: It is contraindicated only in adults, never in children

exp: Per Table 10.4, codeine is a prodrug that must be converted by CYP2D6 to morphine: ultra-rapid metabolizers can produce excessive morphine (the basis of its black-box contraindication in pediatric tonsillectomy patients), while poor metabolizers get no analgesia. This pharmacogenetic variability makes it unreliable and unsafe. It is not a predictable direct-acting opioid, is often combined with acetaminophen, and is specifically dangerous in children.
hy: Codeine = CYP2D6 prodrug; ultra-rapid metabolizers → toxicity (peds tonsillectomy black box).
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — CYP2D6 prodrug with dangerous metabolizer variability.
why.B: Wrong — its potency is metabolism-dependent, not predictable.
why.C: Wrong — it is frequently combined with acetaminophen.
why.D: Wrong — it is specifically contraindicated in pediatric tonsillectomy.

stem: Per Table 10.4, which property characterizes fentanyl?

options.A: It can cause chest-wall rigidity from glottic closure and has a transmucosal dose of 10–15 mcg/kg
options.B: It causes prominent histamine release and vasodilation, like morphine
options.C: It has excellent oral bioavailability, so IV dosing is unnecessary
options.D: Its active metabolite reliably causes seizures at therapeutic doses

exp: Per Table 10.4, fentanyl can cause chest-wall rigidity from glottic closure and has a transmucosal dose of 10–15 mcg/kg; it is very effective for short painful procedures with minimal hemodynamic alteration. Histamine release/vasodilation characterizes morphine, fentanyl is given parenterally/transmucosally/transdermally (not orally for bioavailability), and the seizure-causing metabolite belongs to meperidine. Chest-wall rigidity is the classic fentanyl hazard.
hy: Fentanyl → chest-wall rigidity (glottic closure); transmucosal 10–15 mcg/kg.
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — chest-wall rigidity and a 10–15 mcg/kg transmucosal dose.
why.B: Wrong — histamine release/vasodilation describes morphine.
why.C: Wrong — fentanyl is not used orally for systemic bioavailability.
why.D: Wrong — the seizure-causing metabolite (normeperidine) belongs to meperidine.

stem: Per Table 10.4, which set of cautions applies to meperidine?

options.A: Catastrophic MAO-inhibitor interactions and a metabolite (normeperidine) that causes seizures; 12.5 mg effectively treats shivering
options.B: It is the inexpensive "gold standard" opioid with no significant drug interactions
options.C: It is an oral-only prodrug activated by CYP2D6
options.D: It is the preferred routine opioid because of its long, safe duration

exp: Per Table 10.4, meperidine has catastrophic interactions with MAO inhibitors and a metabolite, normeperidine, that produces seizures; a 12.5 mg dose effectively treats shivering, and it is not recommended for routine use. It is not the inexpensive "gold standard" (that descriptor is morphine's), is not an oral CYP2D6 prodrug (that is codeine), and is explicitly not preferred for routine analgesia. The normeperidine/MAOI hazards are the tested points.
hy: Meperidine: MAOI interaction, normeperidine seizures; 12.5 mg for shivering.
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — MAOI interaction, seizurogenic normeperidine, 12.5 mg for shivering.
why.B: Wrong — morphine, not meperidine, is the "gold standard" with few interactions.
why.C: Wrong — codeine is the CYP2D6 oral prodrug.
why.D: Wrong — meperidine is not recommended for routine use.

stem: Per Table 10.4, which statements about morphine are correct?

options.A: It causes histamine release/vasodilation (consider avoiding in asthmatics) and has poor oral bioavailability, so the oral dose is 3–5× the IV dose
options.B: It causes no histamine release and has near-complete oral bioavailability
options.C: It is an oral-only prodrug requiring hepatic activation
options.D: It is contraindicated by a black-box warning in all adults

exp: Per Table 10.4, morphine causes histamine release and vasodilation (so it is often avoided in asthmatics and in circulatory compromise) and has poor oral bioavailability, requiring an oral dose 3–5 times the IV dose. The "no histamine/near-complete bioavailability," "oral-only prodrug," and "black-box in all adults" options contradict the table. These are classic morphine pitfalls.
hy: Morphine: histamine release/vasodilation; oral dose 3–5× IV (poor bioavailability).
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — histamine release and the 3–5× oral-to-IV ratio.
why.B: Wrong — morphine does release histamine and has poor oral bioavailability.
why.C: Wrong — morphine is not a prodrug.
why.D: Wrong — there is no universal adult black-box for morphine.

stem: Patient-controlled analgesia is favored for several reasons, but doses must be reduced in certain patients. Which is correct?

options.A: PCA improves analgesia/satisfaction and reduces total exposure; reduce doses in obesity, OSA, the elderly, or with CNS depressants
options.B: PCA is unsafe and inferior to nurse-administered bolus dosing in all patients
options.C: PCA doses should be increased in OSA patients to overcome airway resistance
options.D: A continuous basal infusion is standard and recommended for all adults

exp: PCA improves analgesia and patient satisfaction and reduces side effects by lowering total opioid exposure, with an increased safety margin; per Table 10.5, doses should be reduced in patients at risk of opioid sensitivity (obesity, OSA, elderly, co-administered CNS depressants). It is not inferior to nurse-bolus dosing, doses should not be increased in OSA, and continuous basal infusions are rare/not routine in adults.
hy: PCA = safer titration; reduce doses in OSA/obesity/elderly/CNS depressants.
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — PCA benefits plus dose reduction in higher-risk groups.
why.B: Wrong — PCA is favored, not inferior to nurse boluses.
why.C: Wrong — OSA calls for lower, not higher, dosing.
why.D: Wrong — basal infusions are rare in adults, not standard.

stem: Obstructive sleep apnea (affecting ~9–24% of middle-aged adults) raises opioid risk. What management does the chapter recommend?

options.A: Reduce the opioid dose by half (or lengthen the interval), use continuous pulse oximetry, and start early positive-airway-pressure therapy
options.B: Increase opioid dosing to ensure comfort and avoid arousal
options.C: Avoid pulse oximetry, which causes nuisance alarms on the ward
options.D: Discharge OSA patients early, as they tolerate opioids better than others

exp: Because OSA (affecting ~9–24% of middle-aged adults) predisposes to opioid-related airway obstruction (opioids reduce genioglossus tone and arousal), the chapter recommends reducing the opioid dose by half or lengthening the interval, continuous pulse oximetry, longer PACU observation, and early postoperative positive-airway-pressure therapy. Increasing the dose, avoiding oximetry, and early discharge are the opposite of safe practice.
hy: OSA: halve opioid dose, continuous SpO₂, early PAP therapy.
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — dose reduction, monitoring, and early PAP therapy.
why.B: Wrong — increasing the dose worsens obstruction.
why.C: Wrong — continuous oximetry is specifically recommended.
why.D: Wrong — OSA patients are more, not less, opioid-sensitive.

stem: Which statement about opioids and the elderly surgical patient is supported by the chapter?

options.A: ~17% have postoperative cognitive dysfunction at 3 months (from anesthesia, not directly opioid dose); adjuncts can cut opioid use 15–55% in the first 24 h
options.B: Opioid dose is the proven direct cause of postoperative cognitive dysfunction
options.C: The elderly tolerate opioid side effects better than younger patients
options.D: Adjuncts such as acetaminophen have no opioid-sparing role in this group

exp: About 17% of elderly patients have postoperative cognitive dysfunction at 3 months, attributed to anesthesia (with opioid dose not a direct link, though side effects compound risk); adjuncts such as acetaminophen, NSAIDs, and α-2 agonists can reduce opioid administration by 15–55% in the first 24 hours. Opioid dose is not the proven direct cause, the elderly tolerate side effects worse, and adjuncts are central to their care.
hy: Elderly: 17% POCD at 3 mo; adjuncts cut opioids 15–55% in first 24 h.
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — 17% POCD at 3 months; adjuncts give 15–55% opioid sparing.
why.B: Wrong — opioid dose is not the proven direct cause of POCD.
why.C: Wrong — the elderly tolerate opioid effects worse, not better.
why.D: Wrong — acetaminophen and other adjuncts are key opioid-sparing tools.

stem: A patient on chronic buprenorphine presents for elective surgery. What does current evidence support?

options.A: Continue the home buprenorphine dose (tapering may cause harm); standard opioid regimens are less effective, so plan multimodal/regional analgesia
options.B: Always taper and stop buprenorphine well before surgery to restore opioid sensitivity
options.C: Buprenorphine has no bearing on perioperative analgesia and can be ignored
options.D: Replace buprenorphine with high-dose codeine the morning of surgery

exp: For patients on chronic buprenorphine, current evidence favors continuing the home dose (prior advice to taper preoperatively may cause harm); because buprenorphine tightly binds the opioid receptor with low activity, standard opioid regimens are less effective postoperatively, so a multimodal/regional plan and anesthesiology involvement are needed, with the dose sometimes increased. Routine preoperative tapering, ignoring it, or swapping to codeine are all incorrect.
hy: Chronic buprenorphine: continue home dose; plan multimodal/regional (standard opioids less effective).
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — continue buprenorphine and plan multimodal/regional analgesia.
why.B: Wrong — routine preoperative tapering may cause harm.
why.C: Wrong — buprenorphine strongly affects perioperative opioid efficacy.
why.D: Wrong — substituting high-dose codeine is not recommended.

stem: Which techniques reduce the pain of injecting local anesthesia with a sharp needle?

options.A: Buffer lidocaine/epinephrine with 8.4% sodium bicarbonate, warm the anesthetic, use 27–30-gauge needles, and inject slowly ahead of the needle
options.B: Refrigerate the anesthetic and use a large-bore needle for faster delivery
options.C: Acidify the solution and inject as rapidly as possible
options.D: Inject only after the area is fully numb from topical agents, never buffering

exp: The pain of sharp-needle local anesthesia injection can be reduced by buffering lidocaine/epinephrine with 8.4% sodium bicarbonate, warming the anesthetic, using small (27–30-gauge) needles, stabilizing the syringe, and injecting slowly with anesthetic kept ahead of the advancing needle. Refrigerating, acidifying, or injecting rapidly increase pain. These are practical, board-relevant techniques.
hy: Less injection pain: buffer (8.4% bicarb), warm, small needle, inject slowly ahead of needle.
src: Neligan I.10 · Minimizing pain with injection
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — buffering, warming, small needles, and slow injection.
why.B: Wrong — cold solution and large needles increase pain.
why.C: Wrong — acidic, rapid injection is more painful.
why.D: Wrong — buffering is specifically recommended, not avoided.

stem: For injecting hyaluronic-acid filler or local anesthetic over a wide sensitive area with minimal pain and ecchymosis, what technique is described?

options.A: Perforate the skin with a 27-gauge needle, then pass a fine blunt-tipped 30-gauge cannula subdermally
options.B: Use a large sharp 18-gauge needle to cover the area in fewer passes
options.C: Inject intradermally with a 25-gauge needle at high pressure
options.D: Apply only topical anesthetic, avoiding any cannula or needle

exp: The described technique perforates the skin with a 27-gauge needle, then introduces a fine blunt-tipped 30-gauge cannula subdermally, allowing wide-area injection of filler or local anesthetic with little pain and far less ecchymosis than a sharp needle tip. A large sharp 18-gauge needle, high-pressure intradermal injection, or topical-only approaches do not match the chapter. The blunt cannula minimizes vascular trauma.
hy: Blunt 30-G cannula (via 27-G skin nick) → wide-area injection, less pain/bruising.
src: Neligan I.10 · Minimizing pain with injection
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — 27-G perforation then a blunt 30-G cannula.
why.B: Wrong — a large sharp needle increases pain and bruising.
why.C: Wrong — high-pressure intradermal injection is not the technique.
why.D: Wrong — a cannula is used, not topical anesthetic alone.

stem: Which statements about liposomal bupivacaine (Exparel) are correct?

options.A: ~3% of the bupivacaine is free immediately (the rest released gradually); FDA-approved 2011 for wound infiltration and 2018 for interscalene block
options.B: 100% is released immediately, giving the same short duration as plain bupivacaine
options.C: It was FDA-approved in 2011 for spinal anesthesia and is contraindicated for infiltration
options.D: It contains no free drug, so it provides no immediate analgesic effect

exp: With liposomal bupivacaine (Exparel), about 3% of the bupivacaine is free at injection for an immediate effect while the rest is released gradually from vesicles for prolonged analgesia; the FDA approved it in 2011 for wound infiltration and in 2018 for interscalene nerve block. The "100% immediate," "2011 for spinal," and "no free drug" options misstate its kinetics or approvals.
hy: Liposomal bupivacaine: ~3% free immediately; FDA 2011 infiltration, 2018 interscalene.
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — 3% immediate free drug; 2011 and 2018 FDA approvals.
why.B: Wrong — only ~3% is free initially, giving extended (not short) duration.
why.C: Wrong — the 2011 approval was for infiltration, not spinal anesthesia.
why.D: Wrong — ~3% is free for an immediate effect, so it does act early.

stem: What does the chapter conclude about liposomal bupivacaine specifically in plastic surgery?

options.A: More recent well-designed (prospective, randomized, blinded) plastic surgery studies have not consistently shown a benefit
options.B: Randomized plastic surgery trials have definitively proven it superior to all alternatives
options.C: It has never been studied in any plastic surgery procedure
options.D: It is contraindicated in abdominoplasty and mastectomy

exp: While some well-designed studies in the broader surgical literature show benefit, the chapter notes that more recent well-designed plastic surgery studies (prospective, randomized, blinded) have not consistently found a benefit from liposomal bupivacaine. It has been studied in abdominoplasty, augmentation, and mastectomy (so "never studied" and "contraindicated" are wrong), and it is not definitively proven superior in plastic surgery. The honest verdict is "inconsistent evidence in plastic surgery."
hy: Liposomal bupivacaine benefit is inconsistent in rigorous plastic surgery trials.
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — recent rigorous plastic surgery trials show inconsistent benefit.
why.B: Wrong — it has not been definitively proven superior in plastic surgery.
why.C: Wrong — it has been studied in several plastic surgery procedures.
why.D: Wrong — it is described in abdominoplasty/mastectomy, not contraindicated.

stem: Per Table 10.6, what is the maximum subcutaneous/peripheral lidocaine dose, and what permits the higher end of the range?

options.A: 5–7 mg/kg, with the higher dose allowed only when epinephrine 1:200,000 is coadministered
options.B: 0.3–0.5 mg/kg, independent of epinephrine
options.C: 35 mg/kg regardless of epinephrine
options.D: 2–3 mg/kg, the same as bupivacaine

exp: Per Table 10.6, the maximum subcutaneous/peripheral lidocaine dose is 5–7 mg/kg, with the higher end allowed only when epinephrine 1:200,000 is coadministered (epinephrine slows absorption). The 0.3–0.5 mg/kg figure is for spinal bupivacaine/ropivacaine, 35 mg/kg is the tumescent liposuction figure, and 2–3 mg/kg is bupivacaine's limit. Epinephrine coadministration is the key that raises the ceiling.
hy: Lidocaine 5–7 mg/kg; higher end needs epinephrine 1:200,000.
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — 5–7 mg/kg, top of range with epinephrine 1:200,000.
why.B: Wrong — 0.3–0.5 mg/kg is the spinal amide dose, not subcutaneous lidocaine.
why.C: Wrong — 35 mg/kg is the tumescent figure, not the table's subcutaneous max.
why.D: Wrong — 2–3 mg/kg is bupivacaine's limit, not lidocaine's.

stem: Per Table 10.6, which maximum-dose pairing is correct?

options.A: Bupivacaine and ropivacaine 2–3 mg/kg (peripheral/epidural/subcutaneous); prilocaine total adult dose ≤600 mg
options.B: Bupivacaine 7 mg/kg; ropivacaine 35 mg/kg
options.C: Prilocaine has no ceiling; bupivacaine 12 mg/kg
options.D: Ropivacaine 0.1 mg/kg subcutaneous maximum

exp: Per Table 10.6, bupivacaine and ropivacaine share a 2–3 mg/kg ceiling for peripheral/epidural/subcutaneous use (0.3–0.5 mg/kg spinal), and the total adult prilocaine dose should not exceed 600 mg. The "bupivacaine 7 mg/kg," "prilocaine no ceiling," and "ropivacaine 0.1 mg/kg" options contradict the table. The shared bupivacaine/ropivacaine limit and prilocaine cap are the high-yield numbers.
hy: Bupivacaine/ropivacaine 2–3 mg/kg; prilocaine total ≤600 mg.
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — 2–3 mg/kg amides and a 600 mg prilocaine cap.
why.B: Wrong — bupivacaine is 2–3 mg/kg, not 7; ropivacaine is not 35.
why.C: Wrong — prilocaine has a 600 mg total ceiling.
why.D: Wrong — ropivacaine's subcutaneous limit is 2–3 mg/kg, not 0.1.

stem: Per Table 10.6, what is the maximum epinephrine dose for adults under general anesthesia with volatile agents, and for children?

options.A: 2–3 mcg/kg in adults and up to 10 mcg/kg in children
options.B: 10 mcg/kg in adults and 2–3 mcg/kg in children
options.C: 1 mg/kg in adults and 5 mg/kg in children
options.D: There is no dose limit when a volatile agent is used

exp: Per Table 10.6 footnotes, the maximum epinephrine dose for adults under general anesthesia with volatile agents is 2–3 mcg/kg, while infants and children tolerate up to 10 mcg/kg under volatile anesthesia. The reversed, 1 mg/kg, and "no limit" options are wrong. The lower adult ceiling reflects volatile-agent myocardial sensitization.
hy: Epinephrine under volatile GA: adults 2–3 mcg/kg, children up to 10 mcg/kg.
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — 2–3 mcg/kg adults, up to 10 mcg/kg children.
why.B: Wrong — reverses the adult and pediatric limits.
why.C: Wrong — 1 mg/kg is a thousand-fold too high.
why.D: Wrong — there is a defined limit under volatile anesthesia.

stem: What is the classic composition of Klein's tumescent solution?

options.A: 500 mg lidocaine + 1 mg epinephrine + 12.5 mEq bicarbonate in 1000 mL normal saline
options.B: 50 mg lidocaine + 10 mg epinephrine + 125 mEq bicarbonate in 100 mL saline
options.C: 5000 mg lidocaine + 1 mg epinephrine in 1000 mL Ringer's lactate
options.D: 1000 mg bupivacaine + 1 mg epinephrine in 500 mL normal saline

exp: Klein's classic tumescent ("Klein's Solution") formula is 500 mg lidocaine with 1 mg epinephrine and 12.5 mEq of bicarbonate in 1000 mL of normal saline; epinephrine prolongs analgesia, reduces bleeding, and slows local-anesthetic absorption. The other compositions misstate the drug amounts or carrier. This is a frequently tested eponymous recipe.
hy: Klein's solution = 500 mg lidocaine + 1 mg epi + 12.5 mEq bicarb / 1000 mL NS.
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — the canonical Klein's tumescent formula.
why.B: Wrong — the doses and volume are off by orders of magnitude.
why.C: Wrong — 5000 mg lidocaine and Ringer's carrier are incorrect.
why.D: Wrong — Klein's uses lidocaine, not bupivacaine.

stem: In tumescent anesthesia, how does the safe lidocaine-with-epinephrine dose compare with the usual systemic limit?

options.A: Standard limit ~7 mg/kg, but 35 mg/kg is routinely used and up to 55 mg/kg has been reported without complications during liposuction
options.B: The tumescent limit is lower (~2 mg/kg) because of rapid systemic absorption
options.C: Tumescent lidocaine is capped at 7 mg/kg with no exception
options.D: Up to 200 mg/kg is considered safe with epinephrine

exp: Although the standard reported lidocaine-with-epinephrine dose limit is 7 mg/kg, doses of 35 mg/kg are frequently used during liposuction and up to 55 mg/kg have been reported without complications, owing to slow absorption from dilute subcutaneous tumescent fluid with epinephrine. The "lower 2 mg/kg," "capped at 7," and "200 mg/kg" options are incorrect. Cardiac monitoring is advised above 35 mg/kg.
hy: Tumescent lidocaine: standard 7 mg/kg, but 35 (up to 55) mg/kg used in liposuction.
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — 7 mg/kg standard, 35–55 mg/kg in tumescent liposuction.
why.B: Wrong — tumescent dosing is far higher than 2 mg/kg.
why.C: Wrong — 35 mg/kg and above are used, exceeding 7 mg/kg.
why.D: Wrong — 200 mg/kg exceeds the reported safe range.

stem: Why can tumescent lidocaine toxicity present after the patient has been discharged?

options.A: Peak plasma lidocaine occurs ~10–14 h after infiltration (~6 h in highly vascular areas like above the clavicle)
options.B: Peak levels occur within 5 minutes, so any toxicity is seen intraoperatively
options.C: Lidocaine is not absorbed systemically from tumescent fluid
options.D: Peak levels occur ~72 h later, well after most symptoms would resolve

exp: Tumescent lidocaine reaches peak plasma concentration approximately 10–14 hours after infiltration (and ~6 hours in highly vascular areas such as above the clavicle), so toxicity can present hours later, well after the procedure and even after discharge. The "5 minutes," "not absorbed," and "72 hours" options misstate this delayed pharmacokinetics. This delayed peak is why extended monitoring is advised for high-volume cases.
hy: Tumescent lidocaine peaks ~10–14 h (≈6 h in vascular areas) → late/post-discharge toxicity.
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — 10–14 h peak (≈6 h in vascular areas).
why.B: Wrong — the peak is delayed by hours, not 5 minutes.
why.C: Wrong — tumescent lidocaine is absorbed systemically.
why.D: Wrong — the peak is ~10–14 h, not 72 h.

stem: What do the 2013 ASPS and AAD guidelines advise regarding bupivacaine in tumescent anesthesia?

options.A: They advise against the use of bupivacaine for tumescent anesthesia
options.B: They recommend bupivacaine as the preferred tumescent agent
options.C: They mandate combining bupivacaine with lidocaine for all cases
options.D: They permit bupivacaine only above 55 mg/kg

exp: The 2013 ASPS and AAD guidelines advise against the use of bupivacaine for tumescent anesthesia (lidocaine is the standard tumescent agent, with care in liver dysfunction since amides are hepatically metabolized). They do not recommend or mandate bupivacaine, nor permit it above 55 mg/kg. This guideline point is high-yield for liposuction safety.
hy: 2013 ASPS/AAD: avoid bupivacaine for tumescent anesthesia.
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — the guidelines advise against tumescent bupivacaine.
why.B: Wrong — bupivacaine is advised against, not preferred.
why.C: Wrong — no mandated lidocaine–bupivacaine combination.
why.D: Wrong — there is no 55 mg/kg bupivacaine allowance.

stem: What does the chapter say about the timing of epinephrine's maximal hemostatic effect?

options.A: Maximal hemostasis occurs ~25 minutes after administration (not the commonly held 7–10 min), with only minimal added effect after 15 minutes
options.B: Maximal hemostasis is instantaneous on injection
options.C: Maximal hemostasis occurs at 7–10 minutes, confirming the common belief
options.D: Epinephrine has no hemostatic effect and only prolongs analgesia

exp: Contrary to the common belief that epinephrine's hemostatic effect peaks at 7–10 minutes, the chapter states maximal hemostasis occurs about 25 minutes after administration, with only minimal additional effect after 15 minutes. Waiting ~25 minutes therefore optimizes the bloodless field. The "instantaneous," "7–10 min," and "no hemostatic effect" options are incorrect.
hy: Epinephrine's maximal hemostasis at ~25 min (not 7–10 min).
src: Neligan I.10 · Epinephrine in tumescent analgesia
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — ~25 min to maximal hemostasis, minimal gain after 15 min.
why.B: Wrong — the effect is not instantaneous.
why.C: Wrong — 7–10 min is the dispelled myth.
why.D: Wrong — epinephrine does have a hemostatic (vasoconstrictive) effect.

stem: Under general anesthesia with a volatile agent (isoflurane, sevoflurane, desflurane), at what epinephrine dose can adults develop arrhythmias, and in whom is epinephrine avoided?

options.A: As little as 2–3 mcg/kg in adults; avoid in pheochromocytoma, hyperthyroidism, severe hypertension, and cardiac/peripheral vascular disease
options.B: Only above 10 mcg/kg in adults; it is safe in pheochromocytoma
options.C: Only above 55 mcg/kg; there are no patient contraindications
options.D: Arrhythmias never occur with volatile agents at any epinephrine dose

exp: Under general anesthesia with a volatile agent (isoflurane, sevoflurane, desflurane), adults can develop arrhythmias with as little as 2–3 mcg/kg of epinephrine (children tolerate up to 10 mcg/kg); epinephrine should be avoided in pheochromocytoma, hyperthyroidism, severe hypertension, and cardiac or peripheral vascular disease. The "only above 10," "above 55," and "never" options contradict the text. Volatile agents sensitize the myocardium to catecholamines.
hy: Volatile GA: arrhythmias at 2–3 mcg/kg epi (adults); avoid in pheo/hyperthyroid/severe HTN/cardiac/PVD.
src: Neligan I.10 · Epinephrine in tumescent analgesia
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — 2–3 mcg/kg threshold plus the listed contraindications.
why.B: Wrong — arrhythmias can occur as low as 2–3 mcg/kg, and pheo is a contraindication.
why.C: Wrong — the threshold is far below 55 mcg/kg.
why.D: Wrong — arrhythmias do occur with volatile agents.

stem: Which sequence describes lidocaine systemic toxicity as plasma levels rise?

options.A: Early restlessness, metallic taste, tinnitus, perioral numbness and slurred speech → later muscle twitching, convulsions, CNS depression, and cardiac arrest
options.B: Early cardiac arrest → later perioral numbness and tinnitus
options.C: Only a localized rash, with no systemic or neurologic features
options.D: Early hypertension and bradycardia → later euphoria, with no CNS effects

exp: Lidocaine systemic toxicity progresses from early signs — restlessness, drowsiness, light-headedness, metallic taste, tinnitus, slurred speech, perioral numbness/tingling — to higher-level effects of shivering, muscle twitching, tremors, convulsions, CNS depression, coma, respiratory depression, and cardiac arrest. The reversed, "localized rash only," and "hypertension/euphoria" options misstate this classic CNS-then-cardiac sequence. Recognizing early symptoms allows intervention before collapse.
hy: Lidocaine toxicity: early CNS (perioral numbness, tinnitus, metallic taste) → seizures → cardiac arrest.
src: Neligan I.10 · Complications of tumescent anesthesia
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — early neurologic symptoms precede seizures and cardiac arrest.
why.B: Wrong — cardiac arrest is a late, not early, feature.
why.C: Wrong — toxicity is systemic/neurologic, not a localized rash.
why.D: Wrong — the early picture is excitatory CNS, not hypertension/euphoria.

stem: In local anesthetic systemic toxicity, what is the lipid-emulsion regimen the chapter specifies?

options.A: 20% Intralipid 1.5 mL/kg (may repeat up to 10 mL/kg), then infuse 0.25 mL/kg/h if cardiac effects persist
options.B: 20% Intralipid 15 mL/kg bolus once, with no infusion
options.C: 5% dextrose 1.5 mL/kg, then 0.25 mL/kg/h
options.D: 20% Intralipid 0.1 mL/kg, not to be repeated

exp: The chapter's local-anesthetic-toxicity protocol specifies 20% Intralipid 1.5 mL/kg (which may be repeated up to 10 mL/kg), followed by an infusion of 0.25 mL/kg/h if cardiac effects persist, alongside airway management and seizure suppression. The 15 mL/kg single bolus, dextrose substitution, and 0.1 mL/kg options misstate the regimen. Lipid emulsion is the cornerstone of LAST rescue.
hy: LAST: 20% Intralipid 1.5 mL/kg (repeat to 10 mL/kg), then 0.25 mL/kg/h.
src: Neligan I.10 · Complications of tumescent anesthesia
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — 1.5 mL/kg bolus (repeat to 10 mL/kg) then 0.25 mL/kg/h.
why.B: Wrong — 15 mL/kg as a single bolus misstates the dose.
why.C: Wrong — lipid emulsion, not dextrose, is used.
why.D: Wrong — the bolus is 1.5 mL/kg (repeatable up to 10 mL/kg), not 0.1 mL/kg.

stem: During resuscitation for local anesthetic toxicity, which actions are correct?

options.A: Manage arrhythmias per ACLS but do NOT use lidocaine, reduce the epinephrine dose to 1 mcg/kg, and do not use propofol as a lipid substitute for cardiac arrest
options.B: Give a standard ACLS lidocaine bolus to control the arrhythmia
options.C: Use full-dose epinephrine (1 mg) and propofol infusion as the lipid source
options.D: Withhold all benzodiazepines even for seizures

exp: During LAST resuscitation, arrhythmias are managed per ACLS but lidocaine must NOT be used, the epinephrine dose is reduced to 1 mcg/kg, and propofol should not be used as a lipid substitute for cardiac arrest; isolated seizures may be treated with benzodiazepines or propofol. Giving ACLS-dose lidocaine, full-dose epinephrine with propofol as the lipid, or withholding benzodiazepines for seizures are all wrong. These modifications distinguish LAST from standard arrest care.
hy: LAST: no lidocaine, epinephrine reduced to 1 mcg/kg, propofol not a lipid substitute.
src: Neligan I.10 · Complications of tumescent anesthesia
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — avoid lidocaine, reduce epinephrine to 1 mcg/kg, don't use propofol as lipid.
why.B: Wrong — lidocaine is specifically avoided in LAST.
why.C: Wrong — epinephrine is reduced and propofol is not the lipid source.
why.D: Wrong — benzodiazepines are used for seizures.

stem: What precautions does the chapter note for high-volume tumescent infiltration?

options.A: Warm the fluid to 37°C to avoid hypothermia, and watch for mild hyponatremia and hypokalemia in large-volume liposuction
options.B: Refrigerate the fluid to limit systemic absorption, accepting deliberate hypothermia
options.C: Add concentrated sodium to prevent hypernatremia
options.D: Electrolyte disturbances never occur with tumescent fluid

exp: For high-volume tumescent infiltration, the chapter advises warming the fluid to 37°C (large volumes of room-temperature fluid lower body temperature) and watching for mild hyponatremia and hypokalemia in large-volume liposuction. Refrigerating the fluid, adding concentrated sodium, and "no electrolyte disturbance" are incorrect. These precautions guard against hypothermia and electrolyte shifts.
hy: Warm tumescent fluid to 37°C; watch mild hyponatremia/hypokalemia in large-volume cases.
src: Neligan I.10 · Complications of tumescent anesthesia
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — warm to 37°C and monitor for hyponatremia/hypokalemia.
why.B: Wrong — fluid is warmed, not refrigerated; hypothermia is avoided.
why.C: Wrong — the issue is hyponatremia, not hypernatremia to correct.
why.D: Wrong — mild electrolyte disturbances can occur with large volumes.

stem: Why must tumescent volume be limited in patients with cardiac, pulmonary, or renal compromise?

options.A: High tumescent injection volumes can cause intravascular fluid overload with cardiac and pulmonary consequences
options.B: Tumescent fluid is hypertonic and causes cellular dehydration
options.C: The lidocaine itself directly causes pulmonary edema regardless of volume
options.D: Tumescent fluid cannot be absorbed, so overload is impossible

exp: High tumescent injection volumes can produce intravascular fluid overload with cardiac and pulmonary consequences, so in patients with cardiac, pulmonary, or renal compromise the administered volume must be limited (and other IV fluids reduced). Tumescent fluid is dilute and isotonic-ish, not hypertonic-dehydrating, the lidocaine alone does not cause volume overload, and the fluid is absorbed. Volume management is the safety principle.
hy: High tumescent volume → fluid overload; limit it in cardiac/pulmonary/renal compromise.
src: Neligan I.10 · Complications of tumescent anesthesia
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — volume overload risk mandates limiting tumescent volume in these patients.
why.B: Wrong — the risk is overload, not hypertonic cellular dehydration.
why.C: Wrong — the issue is fluid volume, not lidocaine-induced edema per se.
why.D: Wrong — tumescent fluid is absorbed, so overload is possible.

stem: What do the pectoral 1 (PEC 1) and pectoral 2 (PEC 2) blocks each target?

options.A: PEC 1 anesthetizes the medial and lateral pectoral nerves between pec major and minor; PEC 2 anesthetizes the intercostal nerves as they exit serratus anterior
options.B: PEC 1 blocks the intercostal nerves and PEC 2 blocks the brachial plexus trunks
options.C: Both blocks anesthetize only the long thoracic nerve
options.D: PEC 1 is a neuraxial block and PEC 2 is a spinal block

exp: The PEC 1 block anesthetizes the medial and lateral pectoral nerves as they run between pectoralis major and minor, while the PEC 2 block anesthetizes the intercostal nerves as they exit the serratus anterior — together covering the pectoralis muscle (PEC 1) and overlying breast/skin (PEC 2). They are peripheral, not neuraxial, blocks, and they target the pectoral/intercostal nerves, not the long thoracic nerve or brachial plexus trunks alone.
hy: PEC 1 = medial/lateral pectoral nerves; PEC 2 = intercostal nerves.
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — PEC 1 pectoral nerves, PEC 2 intercostal nerves.
why.B: Wrong — reverses the targets and adds the brachial plexus incorrectly.
why.C: Wrong — they do not target the long thoracic nerve.
why.D: Wrong — both are peripheral, not neuraxial/spinal, blocks.

stem: What is the typical local-anesthetic regimen for combined PEC blocks?

options.A: 10 mL of 0.5% bupivacaine for PEC 1 and 20 mL of 0.2% bupivacaine for PEC 2
options.B: 50 mL of 2% lidocaine for each block
options.C: 1 mL of 0.5% bupivacaine for PEC 1 and 2 mL for PEC 2
options.D: 20 mL of 5% lidocaine for PEC 1 only

exp: The typical combined-PEC regimen uses 10 mL of 0.5% bupivacaine for PEC 1 and 20 mL of 0.2% bupivacaine for PEC 2. The 50 mL of 2% lidocaine, 1–2 mL micro-volumes, and 5% lidocaine options are inconsistent with the described technique. The larger, more dilute PEC 2 volume reflects its broader intercostal spread.
hy: PEC 1 = 10 mL 0.5% bupivacaine; PEC 2 = 20 mL 0.2% bupivacaine.
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — 10 mL 0.5% (PEC 1) and 20 mL 0.2% (PEC 2).
why.B: Wrong — 50 mL of 2% lidocaine is not the described regimen.
why.C: Wrong — 1–2 mL volumes are far too small.
why.D: Wrong — 5% lidocaine for PEC 1 is incorrect.

stem: Which statement about the erector spinae plane (ESP) block is correct?

options.A: It was first described in 2016, is placed over the transverse process, and PEC blocks may be advantageous over ESP for breast surgery
options.B: It has been the gold-standard breast block since the 1980s
options.C: It is a neuraxial technique placed in the subarachnoid space
options.D: It reliably outperforms PEC blocks for breast surgery in trials

exp: The erector spinae plane (ESP) block was first described in 2016 and is placed just inferior to the erector spinae muscle over the transverse process; it provides lateral thoracic/abdominal-wall analgesia, but for breast surgery PEC blocks may have an advantage over ESP. It is not a decades-old gold standard, not neuraxial/subarachnoid, and does not reliably outperform PEC blocks. The 2016 origin and PEC-over-ESP point are the testable facts.
hy: ESP block = first described 2016; PEC may beat ESP for breast surgery.
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — 2016 origin, over the transverse process, PEC possibly superior for breast.
why.B: Wrong — it is recent (2016), not a long-standing gold standard.
why.C: Wrong — it is a fascial-plane, not subarachnoid/neuraxial, block.
why.D: Wrong — PEC may be advantageous over ESP, not the reverse.

stem: The transversus abdominis plane (TAP) block deposits local anesthetic in which plane, targeting which nerves?

options.A: Between the transversus abdominis and internal oblique, targeting the T10–L1 terminal intercostal branches
options.B: Between the skin and external oblique, targeting the genitofemoral nerve
options.C: Within the rectus sheath, targeting the lumbar plexus
options.D: In the subarachnoid space, targeting the cauda equina

exp: The TAP block deposits long-acting local anesthetic in the fascial plane between the transversus abdominis and internal oblique, blunting the afferent terminal branches of the intercostal nerves; in the traditional TAP, the T10–L1 terminal branches are targeted, producing abdominal-wall analgesia. The skin/external-oblique plane, rectus sheath, and subarachnoid options misplace the block. This relatively avascular plane gives slow clearance and prolonged analgesia.
hy: TAP = transversus abdominis/internal oblique plane; T10–L1 branches.
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — the transversus/internal-oblique plane targeting T10–L1.
why.B: Wrong — it is deeper than the external-oblique plane and targets intercostal branches.
why.C: Wrong — it is not a rectus-sheath/lumbar-plexus block.
why.D: Wrong — it is a fascial-plane, not subarachnoid, block.

stem: Which statement about TAP-block coverage is correct?

options.A: The subcostal TAP covers the supraumbilical abdomen (T7–9), and TAP blocks address abdominal-wall but not visceral pain
options.B: The TAP block reliably eliminates visceral pain from intra-abdominal procedures
options.C: The traditional TAP covers the entire thorax from T1 to T6
options.D: The TAP block provides complete lower-extremity analgesia

exp: The subcostal approach to the TAP block provides analgesia to the supraumbilical abdomen (T7–9), and TAP blocks address abdominal-wall pain but not the visceral pain of intra-abdominal procedures, so they are used as part of multimodal analgesia. The traditional TAP does not cover T1–T6 or the whole thorax, does not eliminate visceral pain, and gives no lower-extremity analgesia. Knowing it is somatic-wall-only is the key limitation.
hy: Subcostal TAP = T7–9 supraumbilical; TAP covers wall, not visceral pain.
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — subcostal TAP covers T7–9 and spares visceral pain.
why.B: Wrong — TAP does not relieve visceral pain.
why.C: Wrong — the traditional TAP targets T10–L1, not T1–T6.
why.D: Wrong — TAP gives abdominal-wall, not lower-extremity, analgesia.

stem: What is a key risk and a key advantage of the paravertebral block (PVB)?

options.A: Risk of pneumothorax (<1%) from the lung's proximity, but it usually avoids CNS complications such as hypotension and urinary retention
options.B: No risk of pneumothorax, but a high rate of spinal headache
options.C: It reliably causes bilateral analgesia and dense motor block
options.D: Its main advantage is that it requires no ultrasound and has no complications

exp: The paravertebral block carries a pneumothorax risk (<1%) because of the lung's proximity to the paravertebral space (ultrasound guidance helps avoid it), but a key advantage is that, unlike neuraxial techniques, it usually avoids central-nervous-system complications such as hypotension, urinary retention, and bradycardia, giving long-lasting unilateral dermatomal analgesia. The "no pneumothorax," "bilateral dense motor block," and "no complications" options are incorrect.
hy: PVB: pneumothorax <1%, but avoids the CNS side effects of neuraxial blocks.
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — <1% pneumothorax risk with avoidance of neuraxial CNS effects.
why.B: Wrong — there is a real (if small) pneumothorax risk.
why.C: Wrong — PVB gives ipsilateral analgesia, not bilateral dense motor block.
why.D: Wrong — it does carry complications including epidural spread and toxicity.

stem: Per Table 10.7, which is listed as a contraindication to epidural analgesia (beyond those shared with the paravertebral block)?

options.A: Elevated intracranial pressure, severe aortic or mitral stenosis, and severe hypovolemia
options.B: Controlled hypertension and mild anemia
options.C: Outpatient (ambulatory) surgical status
options.D: A history of postoperative nausea and vomiting

exp: Per Table 10.7, epidural-specific contraindications (beyond those shared with the paravertebral block, like coagulopathy and local infection) include elevated intracranial pressure, severe aortic or mitral stenosis, severe hypovolemia, and systemic infection. Controlled hypertension, mild anemia, ambulatory status, and a PONV history are not listed contraindications. The fixed-cardiac-output lesions and raised ICP are the high-yield additions.
hy: Epidural contraindications: ↑ICP, severe AS/MS, severe hypovolemia.
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — raised ICP, severe AS/MS, and severe hypovolemia per Table 10.7.
why.B: Wrong — controlled hypertension/mild anemia are not listed contraindications.
why.C: Wrong — ambulatory status is not a contraindication (indwelling catheters are limited to inpatients).
why.D: Wrong — a PONV history is not an epidural contraindication.

stem: Per Table 10.7, which statement about who performs these blocks is correct?

options.A: The TAP block may be performed by an anesthesiologist or a surgeon, whereas paravertebral and epidural blocks are performed by an anesthesiologist
options.B: All three blocks are performed only by surgeons
options.C: The epidural is routinely placed by the operating surgeon
options.D: The paravertebral block is performed only by a surgeon, never an anesthesiologist

exp: Per Table 10.7, the TAP block may be performed by an anesthesiologist or a surgeon, whereas the paravertebral and epidural blocks are performed by an anesthesiologist. The "all by surgeons," "epidural by the surgeon," and "PVB only by a surgeon" options contradict the table. This division reflects the technical and monitoring demands of neuraxial techniques.
hy: TAP = anesthesiologist or surgeon; PVB/epidural = anesthesiologist.
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — TAP by either, PVB/epidural by anesthesiologist.
why.B: Wrong — not all three are surgeon-performed.
why.C: Wrong — epidurals are placed by anesthesiologists.
why.D: Wrong — the PVB is performed by an anesthesiologist, not only a surgeon.

stem: Which contrast between spinal and epidural anesthesia is correct?

options.A: Spinal is a subarachnoid block best for short lower-extremity/pelvic cases; a thoracic epidural can spare lower-limb motor and micturition pathways, allowing early ambulation
options.B: Spinal anesthesia allows indefinite catheter infusion, while epidurals are single-shot only
options.C: The epidural deposits drug in the subarachnoid space and the spinal outside it
options.D: Spinal anesthesia is preferred for prolonged upper-extremity reconstruction

exp: Spinal anesthesia is a subarachnoid (intrathecal) block giving rapid deep anesthesia below the block, best suited to short lower-extremity or pelvic procedures; a thoracic epidural can spare lower-extremity motor nerves and micturition pathways, allowing early ambulation and avoiding prolonged urinary catheterization. Spinal is typically single-shot (epidurals allow catheter infusion), the epidural is outside the subarachnoid space, and neither is preferred for prolonged upper-extremity work.
hy: Spinal = subarachnoid (short lower-limb/pelvis); thoracic epidural spares motor/micturition.
src: Neligan I.10 · Spinal and epidural anesthesia
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — contrasts subarachnoid spinal with motor/micturition-sparing thoracic epidural.
why.B: Wrong — catheter infusion is an epidural feature; spinals are typically single-shot.
why.C: Wrong — the epidural is outside the subarachnoid space, the spinal within it.
why.D: Wrong — neither is the choice for prolonged upper-extremity reconstruction.

stem: Which physiologic complication of epidural anesthesia is correctly described?

options.A: Hypotension (incidence up to 33%) from efferent sympathetic blockade decreasing systemic vascular resistance
options.B: Hypertension from unopposed sympathetic outflow below the block
options.C: Diaphragmatic paralysis, since the epidural blocks phrenic innervation
options.D: Tachycardia from increased preload and cardiac output

exp: A common physiologic complication of epidural anesthesia is hypotension, with an incidence as high as 33%, arising from efferent sympathetic blockade that lowers systemic vascular resistance (and a preload drop with unopposed vagal tone reducing heart rate/cardiac output). It causes hypotension and bradycardia, not hypertension or tachycardia, and the diaphragm is spared (phrenic innervation is unaffected). These effects are usually mild and treatable.
hy: Epidural hypotension up to 33% from sympathectomy (↓SVR).
src: Neligan I.10 · Complications of epidural blocks
chname: Anesthesia and Pain Management in Plastic Surgery

why.A: Correct — sympathetic blockade lowers SVR, causing hypotension (up to 33%).
why.B: Wrong — the effect is hypotension, not hypertension.
why.C: Wrong — the diaphragm is spared; epidurals don't block phrenic innervation.
why.D: Wrong — bradycardia and reduced cardiac output occur, not tachycardia.

section: The best evidence – where do we find it?
stem: A resident is taught that "evidence-based medicine" means more than applying the latest trial result. By the chapter's definition, evidence-based medicine is the integration of which three elements?

options.A: Best available research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values
options.B: Randomized trial data, institutional protocols, and cost constraints
options.C: Expert consensus, regulatory guidelines, and surgeon preference
options.D: Systematic reviews, malpractice precedent, and hospital policy

exp: The chapter defines evidence-based medicine as the integration of the best available research evidence with clinical expertise and patient values — none of the three alone is sufficient. Research evidence supplies the data, clinical expertise interprets it for the individual, and patient values determine what outcomes matter to the person being treated. The other triads swap in plausible-sounding but non-canonical elements (protocols, cost, regulation, malpractice precedent) that are not the definition.
hy: EBM = best research evidence + clinical expertise + patient values.
src: Neligan I.11 · The best evidence – where do we find it?
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — the canonical triad of evidence, expertise, and patient values.
why.B: Wrong — protocols and cost constraints are not part of the definition.
why.C: Wrong — expert consensus and surgeon preference are weak evidence, not the EBM triad.
why.D: Wrong — malpractice precedent and hospital policy are administrative, not the definition.

section: The best evidence – where do we find it?
stem: When evaluating existing literature on a clinical question and finding the current evidence inadequate, what does the chapter describe as the second way to obtain best evidence for decision-making?

options.A: Produce new credible evidence using an appropriately designed study
options.B: Default to the most senior surgeon's accumulated personal experience
options.C: Adopt the technique most widely promoted by industry
options.D: Abandon the question as unanswerable until a guideline appears

exp: There are two ways to obtain best evidence: first, evaluate the existing literature and identify reports convincing on study design and clinically meaningful outcomes; when that current evidence is inadequate, the second choice is to produce new credible evidence with an appropriately designed study. Falling back on a single surgeon's experience, industry promotion, or abandoning the question all bypass the structured generation of evidence the chapter advocates. The aim is always to base decisions on the strongest obtainable evidence rather than authority or marketing.
hy: If existing evidence is inadequate → produce new credible evidence (don't default to authority).
src: Neligan I.11 · The best evidence – where do we find it?
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — the second route is generating new credible evidence by good study design.
why.B: Wrong — individual accumulated experience is the weak evidence EBM seeks to replace.
why.C: Wrong — industry promotion is not a source of credible evidence.
why.D: Wrong — the chapter pursues new evidence rather than abandoning the question.

stem: A surgeon frames a question: "In patients undergoing breast reduction, does preoperative antibiotic prophylaxis versus no prophylaxis reduce postoperative infection at 4 weeks?" The "4 weeks" component corresponds to which element of the PICOT framework?

options.A: Time horizon (T)
options.B: Intervention (I)
options.C: Outcome (O)
options.D: Comparison (C)

exp: PICOT structures a question as Patient problem, Intervention, Comparison, Outcome, and Time horizon — a standardized time point at which the outcome is measured. "At 4 weeks postoperatively" specifies when infection is assessed, so it is the Time horizon (T). The intervention is antibiotic prophylaxis, the comparison is no prophylaxis, and the outcome is infection rate; the time element is what is added to PICO to form PICOT.
hy: PICOT: the T = the standardized time point at which the outcome is measured.
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — "at 4 weeks" is the standardized outcome-measurement time point (T).
why.B: Wrong — the intervention is antibiotic prophylaxis, not the time element.
why.C: Wrong — the outcome is postoperative infection, not the time element.
why.D: Wrong — the comparison is "no prophylaxis," not the time element.

stem: In the PICO framework used to structure a well-formulated clinical question, the acronym stands for which set of components?

options.A: Patient problem, Intervention, Comparison, Outcome
options.B: Population, Incidence, Control, Observation
options.C: Problem, Indication, Cohort, Outcome
options.D: Patient, Investigation, Comparator, Objective

exp: PICO stands for Patient problem, Intervention, Comparison, and Outcome — a strategy for posing a focused, answerable clinical question that, when paired with PubMed Clinical Queries, improves search efficiency. Without this focus, an otherwise important question gets buried in irrelevant evidence. The other expansions mix in real research terms (population, incidence, control, observation, cohort) that are not the components of this particular acronym.
hy: PICO = Patient problem, Intervention, Comparison, Outcome (± Time = PICOT).
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — the standard expansion of the acronym.
why.B: Wrong — "incidence/observation" are not the PICO components.
why.C: Wrong — "indication/cohort" are not the PICO components.
why.D: Wrong — "investigation/objective" misstate Intervention and Outcome.

stem: A systematic review is conducted by clearly stated scientific methods to minimize bias. The statistical components of that review — summary effect estimates and tests of heterogeneity and publication bias — are specifically referred to by which term?

options.A: A meta-analysis
options.B: A network analysis
options.C: A narrative synthesis
options.D: A scoping review

exp: A systematic review is a methodologically rigorous synthesis of the literature designed to minimize bias; its quantitative statistical components — summary effect estimates and tests of heterogeneity and publication bias — are collectively termed a meta-analysis. Thus every meta-analysis sits within a systematic review's framework, but the term refers specifically to the pooled statistics. A network analysis, narrative synthesis, and scoping review are different review constructs and do not name the statistical pooling described.
hy: Meta-analysis = the statistical (pooling) component of a systematic review.
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — meta-analysis names the statistical components of a systematic review.
why.B: Wrong — a network analysis compares multiple interventions indirectly, a distinct technique.
why.C: Wrong — a narrative synthesis is qualitative, not the statistical pooling.
why.D: Wrong — a scoping review maps literature breadth without pooled effect estimates.

stem: Within the Cochrane handbook, which risk-of-bias tools are designated for randomized versus non-randomized studies of interventions, respectively?

options.A: RoB 2 for randomized trials; ROBINS-I for non-randomized studies
options.B: ROBINS-I for randomized trials; RoB 2 for non-randomized studies
options.C: GRADE for randomized trials; PRISMA for non-randomized studies
options.D: Newcastle–Ottawa for randomized trials; Jadad for non-randomized studies

exp: The Cochrane handbook designates the Cochrane Risk of Bias 2 (RoB 2) tool for randomized trials and the ROBINS-I tool for non-randomized studies of interventions. Matching the correct tool to the design is essential because randomized and observational studies are vulnerable to different biases. The reversed pairing is wrong, and GRADE/PRISMA and Newcastle–Ottawa/Jadad are different instruments (evidence grading, reporting, and older quality scales) not designated this way in the handbook.
hy: RoB 2 → randomized trials; ROBINS-I → non-randomized studies.
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — RoB 2 for randomized, ROBINS-I for non-randomized interventions.
why.B: Wrong — the assignments are reversed.
why.C: Wrong — GRADE grades certainty and PRISMA governs reporting, not risk of bias here.
why.D: Wrong — Newcastle–Ottawa and Jadad are separate quality scales, not the handbook's pairing.

stem: Several techniques exist to treat keloid scars but few head-to-head trials compare them all. Which analysis can estimate the relative effect of two interventions that were never directly compared, provided each was compared to a common third intervention?

options.A: Network meta-analysis
options.B: Traditional pairwise meta-analysis
options.C: Cross-sectional cohort study
options.D: Case–control study

exp: A network meta-analysis estimates effect sizes for all possible pairwise comparisons — including interventions never directly compared — by anchoring on common comparators (if A vs C and B vs C exist, A vs B can be inferred), allowing simultaneous comparison and ranking of multiple treatments. Traditional pairwise meta-analysis is limited to head-to-head comparisons and cannot rank several competing techniques, which is exactly the keloid-treatment problem. Cohort and case–control designs are primary observational studies, not evidence-synthesis methods.
hy: Network meta-analysis = indirect comparisons via a common comparator → ranks multiple treatments.
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — it estimates indirect comparisons and ranks multiple interventions.
why.B: Wrong — pairwise meta-analysis cannot compare interventions not tested head-to-head.
why.C: Wrong — a cross-sectional cohort estimates prevalence, not pooled treatment effects.
why.D: Wrong — a case–control study is a primary design, not an evidence-synthesis method.

stem: Per the ASPS levels-of-evidence table, a high-quality, adequately powered, single- or multi-centered randomized trial with proper allocation concealment and blinded outcome assessors represents which level of evidence?

options.A: Level I
options.B: Level II
options.C: Level III
options.D: Level IV

exp: Per Table 11.1, Level I is reserved for high-quality randomized trials — adequately powered, with proper allocation concealment and blinded outcome assessors — or systematic reviews of such trials. The qualifier "high-quality" plus the methodologic safeguards place it at Level I. A low-quality RCT would drop to Level II, retrospective/case–control designs to Level III, and case series to Level IV.
hy: Level I = high-quality (powered, concealed, blinded) RCT or SR of such trials.
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — a high-quality, concealed, blinded, powered RCT is Level I.
why.B: Wrong — Level II is the low-quality/underpowered RCT or prospective cohort.
why.C: Wrong — Level III is retrospective cohort or case–control.
why.D: Wrong — Level IV is a case series.

stem: Per the levels-of-evidence table, a retrospective cohort study or a case–control study corresponds to which level of evidence?

options.A: Level III
options.B: Level I
options.C: Level II
options.D: Level V

exp: Per Table 11.1, retrospective cohort studies and case–control studies (or systematic reviews of them) are classified as Level III evidence — below randomized designs and prospective cohorts because their backward-looking nature increases susceptibility to bias and confounding. Level I and II are reserved for randomized trials and prospective cohorts, and Level V for expert opinion or case reports.
hy: Level III = retrospective cohort or case–control studies.
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — retrospective cohort and case–control sit at Level III.
why.B: Wrong — Level I is high-quality RCTs.
why.C: Wrong — Level II is low-quality RCTs or prospective cohorts.
why.D: Wrong — Level V is expert opinion or case reports.

stem: Which pairing of study design to level of evidence is correct per the table?

options.A: A low-quality (underpowered, unblinded) RCT is Level II; a case series is Level IV
options.B: A case series is Level II; a prospective cohort study is Level IV
options.C: Expert opinion is Level III; a case–control study is Level I
options.D: A high-quality RCT is Level IV; a case report is Level I

exp: Reading across Table 11.1, a low-quality RCT (insufficient power, no/improper allocation concealment, unblinded assessors) is Level II, while a case series is Level IV — the correct two-step mapping. The other options misassign levels: a case series is never Level II, expert opinion is Level V (not III), and a high-quality RCT is Level I (not IV). Getting the ladder right matters because the level is shorthand for a study's bias risk.
hy: Low-quality RCT = II; retrospective/case–control = III; case series = IV; expert opinion/case report = V.
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research
