why.A: Correct — low-quality RCT is II and a case series is IV.
why.B: Wrong — a case series is IV, not II; a prospective cohort is II, not IV.
why.C: Wrong — expert opinion is V and case–control is III.
why.D: Wrong — a high-quality RCT is I and a case report is V.

section: Study design and levels of evidence
stem: The chapter cautions that a design's position atop the levels-of-evidence pyramid does not always mean better research. What ultimately determines research quality?

options.A: The effort to minimize bias, irrespective of the study design used
options.B: The total number of patients enrolled, irrespective of design
options.C: The impact factor of the journal that publishes it
options.D: Whether the study achieved a statistically significant result

exp: The chapter stresses that a design's rank on the evidence pyramid does not guarantee quality; for some questions (e.g., harm), a high-level design like an RCT is infeasible, forcing a lower-level design that can still be rigorous. Ultimately research quality is determined by the effort to minimize bias, irrespective of the design chosen. Enrollment size, journal prestige, and a significant result are all relevant considerations but none of them define quality the way bias-minimization does.
hy: Quality = degree of bias minimization, not the design's pyramid position.
src: Neligan I.11 · Study design and levels of evidence
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — bias minimization, not design rank, determines quality.
why.B: Wrong — sample size matters but does not by itself define quality.
why.C: Wrong — journal impact factor is unrelated to internal validity.
why.D: Wrong — statistical significance does not equal study quality.

stem: What is the distinguishing feature that makes the RCT the gold standard for comparing interventions?

options.A: Randomization, which balances both known and unknown confounders between groups
options.B: Blinding, which is reliably achievable in surgical trials
options.C: Its retrospective collection of exposure data
options.D: Its reliance on patient self-selection into treatment arms

exp: Randomization is the defining feature of the RCT: by randomly assigning the intervention it balances both known and unknown confounders across groups, which observational designs cannot guarantee. Blinding preserves that benefit but, as the chapter notes, is frequently impractical in surgical trials, so it is not the distinguishing feature. Retrospective exposure capture and patient self-selection describe observational designs, the very thing randomization is meant to avoid.
hy: Randomization balances known AND unknown confounders — the RCT's defining strength.
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — randomization controls known and unknown confounding.
why.B: Wrong — surgeon blinding is often impossible, so it isn't the defining feature.
why.C: Wrong — retrospective exposure capture characterizes cohort/case–control designs.
why.D: Wrong — self-selection is exactly what randomization eliminates.

section: Table 11.2 Four phases of clinical trials
stem: Per the four-phases table, a phase I clinical trial enrolls approximately how many subjects and serves what primary purpose?

options.A: 20–80 subjects, to evaluate safety, determine a safe dosage range, and identify side effects
options.B: 100–300 subjects, to determine effectiveness and further evaluate safety
options.C: 1000–3000 subjects, to confirm effectiveness and compare with standard treatments
options.D: A post-marketing population, to learn the treatment's long-term risks and benefits

exp: Per Table 11.2, phase I tests an experimental drug or treatment in roughly 20–80 people to evaluate safety, establish a safe dosage range/dose–response curve, and identify side effects — it is the first-in-human safety step, not an efficacy study. Effectiveness is the phase II question (100–300), confirmation is phase III (1000–3000), and post-marketing surveillance is phase IV.
hy: Phase I = 20–80 subjects, safety/dose-finding/side effects.
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — phase I enrolls 20–80 for safety and dose-finding.
why.B: Wrong — 100–300 for effectiveness describes phase II.
why.C: Wrong — 1000–3000 to confirm effectiveness describes phase III.
why.D: Wrong — post-marketing surveillance describes phase IV.

section: Table 11.2 Four phases of clinical trials
stem: Which description correctly matches the clinical-trial phase per the table?

options.A: Phase III enrolls 1000–3000 subjects to confirm effectiveness and compare to standard treatments; phase IV is post-marketing surveillance
options.B: Phase III is post-marketing surveillance; phase IV enrolls 1000–3000 subjects to confirm effectiveness
options.C: Phase II enrolls 1000–3000 subjects; phase III enrolls 100–300 subjects
options.D: Phase IV enrolls 20–80 subjects to determine a safe dose range

exp: Per Table 11.2, phase III enrolls 1000–3000 subjects to confirm effectiveness, monitor side effects, compare with commonly used treatments, and gather data for safe use; phase IV is post-marketing surveillance to learn more about risks, benefits, and optimal use after approval. The distractor pairings invert these phases or swap the phase II and III enrollment figures, all of which contradict the table.
hy: Phase III = 1000–3000 (confirm/compare); Phase IV = post-marketing.
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — phase III confirms effectiveness (1000–3000); phase IV is post-marketing.
why.B: Wrong — the phase III and IV descriptions are reversed.
why.C: Wrong — phase II is 100–300, not 1000–3000.
why.D: Wrong — 20–80 dose-finding is phase I, not phase IV.

stem: What ethical precondition must exist before patients can justifiably be randomized in an RCT?

options.A: Clinical equipoise — genuine uncertainty whether the intervention is beneficial or harmful versus the comparator
options.B: Prior proof that the experimental intervention is already superior
options.C: A documented surgeon preference for the experimental arm
options.D: Patient willingness to pay out of pocket for the experimental arm

exp: The ethical basis for an RCT is clinical equipoise — genuine uncertainty within the expert community as to whether a novel intervention is beneficial or harmful relative to placebo or the standard of care. Without equipoise it would be unethical to randomize patients to a treatment already believed inferior or superior. Pre-existing proof of superiority, surgeon preference, or willingness to pay are not the ethical precondition and, in the case of preference, actively undermine randomization.
hy: Equipoise = genuine uncertainty; the ethical prerequisite for randomizing patients.
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — equipoise (genuine uncertainty) is the ethical basis for an RCT.
why.B: Wrong — if superiority is already proven, randomizing is unethical.
why.C: Wrong — surgeon preference biases rather than justifies randomization.
why.D: Wrong — ability to pay is irrelevant to the ethics of randomization.

stem: To overcome the difficulty of blinding surgeons and the learning-curve problem in surgical trials, which design assigns two groups of surgeons — each already expert in one of the two techniques — to deliver only that technique?

options.A: The expertise-based randomized controlled trial (EB-RCT)
options.B: The crossover randomized trial
options.C: The cluster-randomized trial
options.D: The factorial randomized trial

exp: The expertise-based RCT (EB-RCT) assigns two distinct groups of surgeons — each relatively expert in one of the two techniques — so that patients randomized to surgery A are operated on by A-experts and those randomized to B by B-experts. This design overcomes the impracticality of blinding surgeons and the learning-curve confounder that plague conventional surgical RCTs, and removes bias from surgeons who personally favor one technique. It was first proposed by Van der Linden in 1980. Crossover, cluster, and factorial designs address different problems (within-subject comparison, group-level randomization, multiple simultaneous interventions).
hy: EB-RCT: surgeons assigned to their own area of expertise — fixes blinding + learning curve.
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — the EB-RCT matches surgeons to their expert technique.
why.B: Wrong — a crossover trial gives each subject both treatments sequentially.
why.C: Wrong — cluster randomization assigns groups, not surgeon-expertise arms.
why.D: Wrong — a factorial trial tests two or more interventions simultaneously.

stem: In a systematic review of 173 plastic surgery RCTs comparing surgical interventions, what did the authors find regarding blinding and sample size?

options.A: Surgeon blinding was impractical in 97% of trials, and 82% randomized fewer than 100 patients (median 43)
options.B: Surgeon blinding was achieved in 97% of trials, and most enrolled over 1000 patients
options.C: Every trial was double-blinded and adequately powered
options.D: Blinding was impractical in only 3%, and the median enrollment was 430 patients

exp: In the cited systematic review of 173 plastic surgery RCTs comparing surgical interventions, blinding the surgeon was impractical in almost every trial (97%), and the trials were typically small — 82% (141/173) randomized fewer than 100 patients, with a median of 43 (so half had fewer than ~22 per arm). These twin problems (unblindable surgeons and underpowering) are why specialized designs like the EB-RCT and multicenter collaboration are advocated. The distractors invert the blinding figure or inflate the sample sizes.
hy: 173-RCT review: surgeon blinding impractical 97%; 82% enrolled <100 (median 43).
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — 97% could not blind the surgeon; 82% randomized fewer than 100 (median 43).
why.B: Wrong — blinding was impractical, not achieved, and trials were small.
why.C: Wrong — the trials were largely not blinded and underpowered.
why.D: Wrong — the figures (3%, median 430) are fabricated inversions.

stem: A surgeon wants to know whether textured breast implants cause BIA-ALCL. Why is an RCT not an appropriate design to answer this harm question?

options.A: It would be unethical to randomize patients to a suspected harmful exposure, events rarer than 1 in 100 are logistically prohibitive, and RCTs often underreport harm
options.B: An RCT cannot include a control group when the outcome is harm
options.C: Randomization introduces more confounding than observation when studying harm
options.D: Questions of harm can only be answered with Level V evidence by definition

exp: The chapter gives three reasons RCTs are ill-suited to questions of harm: it is unethical to randomize patients to an exposure suspected of causing harm; studying an event occurring less often than 1 in 100 requires prohibitively large samples and long follow-up; and RCTs frequently fail to adequately report harm data. For these reasons observational designs (cohort, case–control) are used to investigate harm such as BIA-ALCL. The other options misstate RCT mechanics (control groups are feasible; randomization reduces, not adds, confounding) or invent a rule.
hy: RCTs poor for harm: unethical to randomize, rare events too costly, harm underreported.
src: Neligan I.11 · Limitations of RCTs: investigating harm
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — the three reasons are ethics, rarity/cost, and poor harm reporting.
why.B: Wrong — RCTs can have control groups; that is not the issue.
why.C: Wrong — randomization reduces confounding rather than adding it.
why.D: Wrong — no rule restricts harm questions to Level V evidence.

stem: How does a cohort study differ fundamentally from a randomized controlled trial?

options.A: Exposure is determined by patient or physician preference (or happenstance) rather than by randomization
options.B: It begins by selecting subjects on the basis of their outcome
options.C: It can only ever be conducted retrospectively
options.D: It always yields Level I evidence

exp: A cohort study resembles an RCT but lacks randomization: exposure status is determined by patient or physician preference, or sometimes happenstance, rather than by random assignment. It can be conducted prospectively or retrospectively and follows groups to compare outcomes. Beginning with the outcome characterizes a case–control study, not a cohort, and a cohort is not automatically Level I evidence.
hy: Cohort = RCT without randomization; exposure set by preference/happenstance.
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — lack of randomization (exposure by preference) defines the cohort.
why.B: Wrong — starting from the outcome describes a case–control study.
why.C: Wrong — cohorts can be prospective or retrospective.
why.D: Wrong — cohorts are not automatically Level I evidence.

stem: A cohort evaluated at a single point in time, frequently used to estimate the prevalence of disease, is best described as which type of study?

options.A: A cross-sectional cohort study
options.B: A case–control study
options.C: A retrospective cohort study
options.D: A randomized controlled trial

exp: When a cohort is evaluated at a single point in time — often to estimate the prevalence of disease — it is called a cross-sectional cohort study. Evaluating exposures and risk over time requires at least two time points, which is the longitudinal cohort. Case–control and retrospective cohort designs are defined differently, and a randomized trial involves assignment of an intervention rather than a snapshot of prevalence.
hy: Cross-sectional cohort = single time point → prevalence.
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — a single-time-point cohort estimating prevalence is cross-sectional.
why.B: Wrong — case–control starts from disease status, not a prevalence snapshot.
why.C: Wrong — retrospective cohort examines past exposures over time, not one point.
why.D: Wrong — an RCT assigns an intervention rather than measuring prevalence.

stem: What is the defining starting point of a case–control study?

options.A: Initial identification of cases by the presence of disease/outcome, compared with controls lacking it
options.B: Random assignment of an exposure to two groups
options.C: Following exposed and unexposed cohorts forward in time
options.D: Selecting subjects on the basis of their exposure status

exp: A case–control study is defined by starting with the outcome: cases are selected because they have the disease/outcome, and controls because they do not, after which prior exposures are compared between the groups. Random assignment of exposure defines an RCT; following exposed and unexposed groups forward defines a (prospective) cohort; selecting by exposure status also describes a cohort. This backward-from-outcome logic is what makes case–control efficient for rare diseases.
hy: Case–control begins with cases (outcome present) vs controls (outcome absent).
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — selection is by presence/absence of the outcome.
why.B: Wrong — random assignment of exposure is an RCT.
why.C: Wrong — following cohorts forward in time is a cohort study.
why.D: Wrong — selecting by exposure status defines a cohort, not case–control.

stem: What is the major strength of the case–control design, and its corresponding weakness?

options.A: Strength: studying rare or long-latency conditions; weakness: it cannot assess incidence or prevalence of disease
options.B: Strength: establishing causation directly; weakness: it requires very large samples
options.C: Strength: prospective data collection; weakness: prohibitive cost
options.D: Strength: randomization of exposure; weakness: very short follow-up

exp: The case–control design's major strength is efficiency in studying rare conditions or outcomes that take a long time to develop, because investigators start with the cases rather than waiting for the outcome to accrue. Its principal weakness is that it cannot assess incidence or prevalence of disease and is more susceptible to bias. It does not establish causation directly, does not require huge samples (an advantage for rare disease), and is not prospective or randomized.
hy: Case–control: best for rare/long-latency disease; cannot give incidence or prevalence.
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — strong for rare disease; cannot measure incidence/prevalence.
why.B: Wrong — it suggests association, not direct causation, and is efficient for small numbers.
why.C: Wrong — data are typically retrospective, not prospective.
why.D: Wrong — there is no randomization in a case–control study.

stem: A surgeon assembles a large consecutive series of patients who received a new flap. How does this differ from a case report, and what concern does a nonconsecutive case report raise?

options.A: A consecutive case series can inform indications and outcomes; nonconsecutive case reports raise sampling bias such as patient self-selection or recall bias
options.B: Case reports are higher-level evidence than case series because they are more detailed
options.C: A case series requires randomization, whereas a case report does not
options.D: A case series is by definition Level I evidence, a case report Level II

exp: A large consecutive collection of patients (a case series) can offer useful information on indications, contraindications, and expected outcomes; when cases are nonconsecutive (a case report), sampling bias such as patient self-selection or surgeon recall bias threatens the validity of inferred indications and outcomes. Case reports nonetheless hold value for presenting new ideas, techniques, and rare adverse events. Case reports are not higher evidence than series, require no randomization, and are low on the evidence hierarchy (IV–V), not Level I/II.
hy: Consecutive series → indications/outcomes; nonconsecutive case report → sampling/recall bias.
src: Neligan I.11 · Case series and case reports
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — consecutive series inform outcomes; nonconsecutive reports risk sampling bias.
why.B: Wrong — case reports are lower-level evidence than a consecutive series.
why.C: Wrong — neither design uses randomization.
why.D: Wrong — series/reports are Level IV–V, not Level I–II.

stem: When no evidence exists for a novel surgical innovation, the chapter recommends a specific progression of study designs. What is the correct sequence?

options.A: Case report → case series → comparative studies (cohort or preferably RCT)
options.B: Randomized controlled trial → meta-analysis → case report
options.C: Case–control study → case report → expert opinion
options.D: Systematic review → case series → case report

exp: When no evidence exists, the chapter's practical progression is to begin with a case report, advance to a case series, and — if the innovation is adopted widely — proceed to comparative studies (cohort studies or, preferably, RCTs). This staged build mirrors the growth of evidence and avoids jumping to an RCT before the technique is even described. The other sequences run the hierarchy backwards or in nonsensical order.
hy: No evidence → case report → case series → comparative (cohort/RCT).
src: Neligan I.11 · Case series and case reports
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — report → series → comparative studies is the recommended build.
why.B: Wrong — starting with an RCT ignores the need for initial description.
why.C: Wrong — this sequence descends the hierarchy illogically.
why.D: Wrong — a systematic review cannot precede the primary studies it synthesizes.

stem: What distinguishes an efficacy study from an effectiveness study in surgical outcomes research?

options.A: Efficacy evaluates outcomes under ideal, controlled conditions; effectiveness evaluates outcomes under real-world conditions
options.B: Efficacy evaluates real-world outcomes; effectiveness evaluates ideal-condition outcomes
options.C: Efficacy studies enroll heterogeneous populations; effectiveness studies use strict inclusion criteria
options.D: They are interchangeable terms describing the same study type

exp: Surgical outcomes research spans a continuum from efficacy to effectiveness. Efficacy studies ask whether an intervention works under ideal, controlled circumstances — highly selected homogeneous populations, trained staff, standardized interventions. Effectiveness studies ask whether it works in the real world — heterogeneous populations, minimal exclusions, usual providers, concurrent interventions. The reversed and "interchangeable" options contradict this distinction, which underlies why trial results may not reflect community practice.
hy: Efficacy = ideal conditions; effectiveness = real-world conditions.
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — efficacy is ideal-condition, effectiveness is real-world.
why.B: Wrong — the definitions are reversed.
why.C: Wrong — heterogeneous populations characterize effectiveness, not efficacy.
why.D: Wrong — the two terms are distinct, not interchangeable.

stem: The carotid endarterectomy example contrasting randomized-trial hospitals with non-trial hospitals illustrated which lesson about trials versus real-world practice?

options.A: Real-world mortality was substantially higher than in the trials and was inversely proportional to a center's case volume
options.B: Real-world mortality matched the trial mortality almost exactly
options.C: Trial mortality was higher than community-practice mortality
options.D: Mortality rose directly with increasing center volume

exp: After the carotid endarterectomy trials reported very low mortality, real-world mortality proved substantially higher than the trials — even at institutions that had participated — and was inversely proportional to a center's procedural volume (higher volume, lower mortality). The lesson is that efficacy under trial conditions may overstate real-world effectiveness, supporting the value of population-based database research. The distractors claim matching or directly proportional relationships that the figure refutes.
hy: Trial mortality < real-world; real-world mortality inversely related to center volume.
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — real-world mortality was higher and inversely related to volume.
why.B: Wrong — real-world results did not match the trials.
why.C: Wrong — trial mortality was lower, not higher, than community practice.
why.D: Wrong — the relationship to volume was inverse, not direct.

stem: What does a P-value represent?

options.A: The probability of obtaining a result at least as extreme as observed, assuming the null hypothesis is true
options.B: The probability that the null hypothesis itself is true
options.C: The probability that the study's findings will reproduce
options.D: The magnitude of the treatment effect observed

exp: The P-value is the probability of obtaining a result at least as extreme as the one observed for a statistical test, assuming the null hypothesis (no difference) is true. A small P-value therefore implies the data are unlikely under the null, suggesting a true difference exists. Crucially it is not the probability that the null is true, not a measure of reproducibility, and not the effect size — common misinterpretations the chapter warns against.
hy: P-value = P(result this extreme | null true) — not P(null is true).
src: Neligan I.11 · Power and the P-value
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — probability of data this extreme given a true null.
why.B: Wrong — that is the common misinterpretation the chapter rejects.
why.C: Wrong — reproducibility is not what a P-value quantifies.
why.D: Wrong — the P-value is not the effect size.

stem: The conventional threshold P < 0.05 corresponds to the alpha, or type I error. What does committing a type I error mean?

options.A: Rejecting a true null hypothesis — falsely concluding a difference exists
options.B: Failing to reject a false null hypothesis — missing a difference that truly exists
options.C: Achieving adequate statistical power for the study
options.D: Overestimating the magnitude of the treatment effect

exp: The threshold for statistical significance (typically 0.05 or 0.01) is the alpha, or type I error — the probability of rejecting a true null hypothesis and falsely concluding that a difference exists (a false positive). Failing to reject a false null (missing a real difference) is the type II/β error. Power and effect size are separate concepts. Confusing these errors leads to misreading "significant" results.
hy: Type I (α) error = false positive (reject a true null).
src: Neligan I.11 · Power and the P-value
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — type I error is falsely concluding a difference exists.
why.B: Wrong — that describes the type II (β) error.
why.C: Wrong — power is the complement of the type II error, not type I.
why.D: Wrong — effect size is a separate quantity.

stem: An underpowered plastic surgery study fails to detect a difference that genuinely exists between two groups. This represents which statistical error, and it reflects what?

options.A: A type II (β) error, reflecting a lack of statistical power
options.B: A type I (α) error, reflecting a false-positive result
options.C: Selection bias from non-random sampling
options.D: Confounding from an unmeasured third variable

exp: Failing to detect a difference that truly exists is a type II (beta) error, and it happens when a study lacks adequate statistical power — typically from too small a sample size, a common problem in low-volume plastic surgery research. A type I error is the opposite (false positive). Selection bias and confounding are distinct threats to validity, not the specific error of missing a real effect.
hy: Type II (β) error = missing a true difference → driven by low power/small n.
src: Neligan I.11 · Power and the P-value
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — missing a real difference is a type II error reflecting low power.
why.B: Wrong — type I error is a false positive, the opposite situation.
why.C: Wrong — selection bias is a sampling problem, not this error.
why.D: Wrong — confounding is a distinct validity threat.

stem: Chung et al. reported that more than 80% of "negative studies" in the Journal of Hand Surgery were underpowered to detect a 25% difference between groups. Below what power value did these studies fall, with insufficient sample size as the primary cause?
exp: Chung et al. found that more than 80% of "negative studies" in the Journal of Hand Surgery had powers below 0.80 to detect a 25% difference between groups, with a greater than 20% risk of missing a true difference; the primary culprit was insufficient sample size. Power of 0.80 (i.e., a 20% type II error rate) is the conventional minimum acceptable threshold. The other values (0.50, 0.95, 0.05) are not the cited power floor.
hy: Conventional minimum power = 0.80; most "negative" hand studies fell below it.
src: Neligan I.11 · Power and the P-value
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — studies fell below the 0.80 power threshold.
why.B: Wrong — 0.50 is not the conventional power floor cited.
why.C: Wrong — 0.95 is a confidence level, not the power threshold here.
why.D: Wrong — 0.05 is the alpha threshold, not power.

stem: Per the chapter and the sample-size table, the P-value is affected by which two factors, such that a true difference can fail to reach significance?

options.A: The effect size (difference between groups) and the sample size
options.B: The journal's impact factor and the number of co-authors
options.C: The chosen alpha and the title of the study
options.D: The standard deviation alone, independent of sample size

exp: The P-value is influenced by both the effect size (the magnitude of difference between groups) and the sample size; for a given effect, a small sample may fail to reach significance while a large one may achieve it (illustrated in the chapter's sample-size table, where identical complication rates are non-significant at small n). Recognizing this dependence is key to not over- or under-interpreting a P-value. The other options name irrelevant factors or wrongly isolate the standard deviation from sample size.
hy: P-value depends on effect size AND sample size.
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — effect size and sample size jointly determine the P-value.
why.B: Wrong — impact factor and author count are irrelevant.
why.C: Wrong — the study title has no bearing on the P-value.
why.D: Wrong — standard deviation matters only together with sample size.

stem: What is the key difference between a clinical registry and administrative claims data?

options.A: Registries are designed to collect detailed clinical and treatment data; claims data are collected for billing and lack clinical detail
options.B: Claims data provide detailed clinical information; registries exist only for billing
options.C: Registries capture only a single episode of care; claims data follow patients longitudinally
options.D: Both are restricted to mortality and length of stay only

exp: Per Table 11.4, clinical registries are purpose-built to collect detailed clinical and treatment information (an advantage for nuanced research), whereas administrative/claims ("discharge") data are gathered for billing, typically represent only one episode of care, and lack clinical detail — limiting outcomes mostly to mortality, length of stay, and charges. The reversed description is wrong, the "single episode" feature belongs to claims rather than registries, and not both are limited to mortality/LOS.
hy: Registry = detailed clinical data; claims data = billing-derived, clinically sparse.
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — registries hold detailed clinical data; claims data are billing-based.
why.B: Wrong — the descriptions are reversed.
why.C: Wrong — single-episode capture is a claims-data feature, not a registry one.
why.D: Wrong — registries are not limited to mortality and length of stay.

stem: The Plastic Surgery Registries Network (PSRN) of the ASPS and Plastic Surgery Foundation comprises five registries. Which set correctly lists them?

options.A: TOPS, NBIR, GRAFT, PROFILE, ASPS-QCDR
options.B: SEER, NCCN, NSQIP, NIS, TOPS
options.C: TOPS, SEER, GRAFT, NSQIP, EQ-5D
options.D: NBIR, PROFILE, Dartmouth Atlas, CosmetAssure, SCIP

exp: Per Table 11.4, the Plastic Surgery Registries Network (PSRN) of the ASPS and Plastic Surgery Foundation comprises five registries: TOPS (Tracking Operations and Outcomes for Plastic Surgeons), NBIR (National Breast Implant Registry), GRAFT (autologous fat transfer), PROFILE (breast implants and ALCL), and the ASPS-QCDR. The other lists mix in cancer registries (SEER, NCCN), surgical-quality programs (NSQIP), administrative samples (NIS), utility instruments (EQ-5D), and databases (Dartmouth Atlas, CosmetAssure) that are not part of the PSRN.
hy: PSRN's five: TOPS, NBIR, GRAFT, PROFILE, ASPS-QCDR.
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — the five PSRN registries.
why.B: Wrong — SEER/NCCN/NSQIP/NIS are non-PSRN databases.
why.C: Wrong — SEER, NSQIP, and EQ-5D are not PSRN registries.
why.D: Wrong — Dartmouth Atlas, CosmetAssure, and SCIP are not PSRN registries.

stem: Which registry is a collaboration of the ASPS, the Plastic Surgery Foundation, and the FDA created specifically to study whether breast implants are associated with anaplastic large cell lymphoma?

options.A: PROFILE
options.B: NBIR
options.C: GRAFT
options.D: TOPS

exp: PROFILE (Patient Registry and Outcomes For breast Implants and anaplastic large cell Lymphoma etiology and Epidemiology) is the ASPS/PSF/FDA collaboration created specifically to understand the role of breast implants in the etiology of anaplastic large cell lymphoma and to determine whether an association exists. NBIR is the general breast-implant safety registry, GRAFT tracks fat grafting, and TOPS is the general operations/outcomes registry — none is the dedicated implant-ALCL registry.
hy: PROFILE = ASPS/PSF/FDA registry for breast implants and ALCL.
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — PROFILE is the dedicated breast-implant/ALCL registry.
why.B: Wrong — NBIR is the broad breast-implant safety registry.
why.C: Wrong — GRAFT tracks autologous fat transfer.
why.D: Wrong — TOPS is the general plastic surgery operations registry.

stem: Why can administrative claims data not be used to evaluate cosmetic surgery outcomes, and which database has been used instead?

options.A: Cosmetic procedures are self-pay and therefore not captured in claims; CosmetAssure, a complication-insurance database, has been used
options.B: Cosmetic procedures are illegal to bill, so SEER has been used instead
options.C: Claims data exclude all surgical procedures, so NSQIP has been used instead
options.D: Cosmetic claims are too detailed to process, so the Dartmouth Atlas has been used

exp: Claims data cannot evaluate cosmetic surgery because these procedures are self-pay and thus not captured in insurance/billing records. To study cosmetic outcomes, researchers have turned to CosmetAssure — a nationally sold insurance policy covering complications of cosmetic surgery, which creates a financial incentive to report covered complications (though its clinical detail is limited and it lacks patient-reported outcomes). SEER (cancer), NSQIP (surgical quality), and the Dartmouth Atlas (variation) do not fill this role, and the rationales given for them are false.
hy: Cosmetic = self-pay → absent from claims; CosmetAssure used instead.
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — self-pay cosmetic cases are absent from claims; CosmetAssure is used.
why.B: Wrong — cosmetic surgery is legal to bill privately; SEER is a cancer registry.
why.C: Wrong — claims data do capture surgical procedures generally.
why.D: Wrong — the Dartmouth Atlas studies variation, not cosmetic complications.

stem: Procedures such as hysterectomy and prostatectomy show wide regional rate variation, whereas appendectomy and cholecystectomy do not. What does high rate variability for a procedure suggest?

options.A: The optimal management strategy has not yet been established or universally adopted
options.B: The procedure is being systematically overpriced
options.C: The procedure is always being overused
options.D: The procedure is contraindicated in those regions

exp: Small-area variation analysis (pioneered by Wennberg and Gittelsohn, 1973; exemplified by the Dartmouth Atlas) shows that procedures with well-established risks and benefits vary little across regions, while high variability signals that the optimal management has not yet been established or adopted. Variation itself does not tell us which rate is correct — only that more definitive evaluation is needed. It is not evidence of overpricing, universal overuse, or contraindication.
hy: High procedure-rate variation → optimal management not yet established/adopted.
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — variability flags unsettled optimal management.
why.B: Wrong — variation is about practice patterns, not price.
why.C: Wrong — high variation does not by itself prove overuse.
why.D: Wrong — variation does not imply the procedure is contraindicated.

stem: Per the volume–outcome table (Roohan et al.), 5-year survival after breast cancer treatment differed between low- and high-volume hospitals by approximately how much?

options.A: As much as a 30% difference, favoring high-volume centers
options.B: No measurable difference between volume strata
options.C: About 5%, favoring low-volume centers
options.D: About 50%, favoring low-volume centers

exp: Per Table 11.5 (Roohan et al.), 5-year survival risk ratios after breast cancer treatment rose from 1.00 at high-volume hospitals (>150 cases/yr) to 1.60 at very-low-volume hospitals (1–10 cases/yr) — about a 30% difference in survival between low- and high-volume centers. This volume–outcome relationship (also shown by Birkmeyer for surgeon volume and mortality, and for autologous breast reconstruction) supports referral to higher-volume centers. The other options deny or invert the gradient.
hy: ~30% better 5-yr survival at high- vs low-volume centers (RR 1.00 vs 1.60).
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — about a 30% survival difference favoring high-volume centers (RR 1.60 very-low vs 1.00 high).
why.B: Wrong — a clear volume gradient existed.
why.C: Wrong — the advantage favors high-volume, not low-volume, centers.
why.D: Wrong — the difference was ~30%, not 50% favoring low volume.

stem: Preferences may be quantified as "values" or "utilities" depending on the elicitation method. Which statement is correct?

options.A: Values are measured under conditions of certainty (rating scale, time trade-off); utilities are measured under uncertainty (standard gamble)
options.B: Values are measured under uncertainty; utilities are measured under certainty
options.C: Both values and utilities are measured only by the standard gamble
options.D: Utilities use the rating scale, whereas values use the standard gamble

exp: Preferences are termed values when measured under conditions of certainty (e.g., rating scale, time trade-off) and utilities when measured under conditions of uncertainty (the standard gamble). Both quantify the desirability of a health state, and the most common preference metric derived is the QALY. The reversed pairing and the claims that both come only from the standard gamble (or that the methods are swapped) contradict the definitions.
hy: Values = certainty (rating scale/TTO); utilities = uncertainty (standard gamble).
src: Neligan I.11 · Utilities and preference-based measures
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — values under certainty, utilities under uncertainty.
why.B: Wrong — the certainty/uncertainty assignment is reversed.
why.C: Wrong — the standard gamble measures utilities, not both.
why.D: Wrong — the rating scale measures values; the standard gamble measures utilities.

stem: On the utility scale used to derive QALYs, what do the anchors 0.0 and 1.0 represent, and can a health state score below zero?

options.A: 0.0 = death and 1.0 = perfect health; some states judged worse than death can score negative
options.B: 0.0 = perfect health and 1.0 = death; negative scores are impossible
options.C: 0.0 = mild disability and 1.0 = severe disability; scores are never negative
options.D: 0.0 = death and 1.0 = perfect health; but scores can never be negative

exp: Preference-based measures run from 0.0 (death) to 1.0 (perfect health); importantly, some health states are considered worse than death and can therefore carry negative scores. These utilities feed QALY calculations and cost-utility analysis, allowing comparison across diverse conditions on a common scale. The options that reverse the anchors or deny negative values misstate the scale.
hy: Utility scale: 0.0 = death, 1.0 = perfect health; states worse than death can be negative.
src: Neligan I.11 · Utilities and preference-based measures
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — 0 = death, 1 = perfect health, with possible negative (worse-than-death) values.
why.B: Wrong — the anchors are reversed and negatives are possible.
why.C: Wrong — the anchors are death and perfect health, not disability grades.
why.D: Wrong — negative scores ARE possible for states worse than death.

stem: The standard gamble technique for measuring utilities is considered to adhere most closely to which theory?

options.A: The von Neumann–Morgenstern utility theory
options.B: Bayesian decision theory
options.C: The Donabedian model of quality
options.D: Porter's theory of value-based competition

exp: The standard gamble — in which a patient chooses between their current health state and a gamble between perfect health and death, varying the probabilities to a point of indifference — is considered to adhere most closely to the von Neumann–Morgenstern utility theory, the formal theory of decision-making under uncertainty. Bayesian decision theory, the Donabedian quality model, and Porter's value framework are unrelated to standard-gamble utility measurement.
hy: Standard gamble ↔ von Neumann–Morgenstern utility theory.
src: Neligan I.11 · Utilities and preference-based measures
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — the standard gamble is grounded in von Neumann–Morgenstern theory.
why.B: Wrong — Bayesian decision theory is a different framework.
why.C: Wrong — Donabedian's model concerns quality of care, not utility elicitation.
why.D: Wrong — Porter's value theory addresses value-based competition, not utilities.

stem: Which set comprises widely used preference (utility) classification systems that convert generic health-status responses into a utility value?

options.A: Quality of Well-Being Scale (QWB), Health Utilities Index (HUI), and EuroQol (EQ-5D)
options.B: BREAST-Q, FACE-Q, and BODY-Q
options.C: CONSORT, STROBE, and PRISMA
options.D: SEER, NCCN, and NSQIP

exp: Widely used preference (utility) classification systems convert responses on a generic health-status instrument into a single utility via population-derived algorithms; the examples named are the Quality of Well-Being Scale (QWB), the Health Utilities Index (HUI), and the EuroQol (EQ-5D). BREAST-Q/FACE-Q/BODY-Q are condition-specific PROMs (not generic utility systems), CONSORT/STROBE/PRISMA are reporting guidelines, and SEER/NCCN/NSQIP are databases/registries.
hy: Utility classification systems: QWB, HUI, EQ-5D.
src: Neligan I.11 · Utilities and preference-based measures
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — QWB, HUI, and EQ-5D are generic utility classification systems.
why.B: Wrong — BREAST-Q/FACE-Q/BODY-Q are condition-specific PROMs.
why.C: Wrong — CONSORT/STROBE/PRISMA are reporting guidelines.
why.D: Wrong — SEER/NCCN/NSQIP are databases/registries.

stem: A cost–benefit analysis (CBA) is distinguished from the other economic evaluations by how it expresses outcomes. How?

options.A: Outcomes are converted to monetary units using a willingness-to-pay approach (dollars expended for dollars gained)
options.B: Outcomes are expressed as cost per quality-adjusted life year
options.C: Outcomes are expressed as cost per natural clinical unit
options.D: Only the costs of interventions are compared, with no outcome measure

exp: A cost–benefit analysis converts outcomes into monetary units using a willingness-to-pay approach, so both costs and consequences are expressed in dollars and results read as dollars expended for dollars gained. This is what distinguishes CBA from cost-effectiveness analysis (cost per natural clinical unit), cost-utility analysis (cost per QALY), and a bare cost analysis (costs only). Monetizing health outcomes is both CBA's hallmark and its main controversy.
hy: CBA = outcomes monetized via willingness-to-pay (dollars for dollars).
src: Neligan I.11 · Types of economic studies
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — CBA monetizes outcomes using willingness-to-pay.
why.B: Wrong — cost per QALY is cost-utility analysis.
why.C: Wrong — cost per natural unit is cost-effectiveness analysis.
why.D: Wrong — comparing costs only is a cost analysis.

stem: What distinguishes a cost-effectiveness analysis (CEA) from a cost–utility analysis (CUA)?

options.A: CEA measures cost per natural clinical unit (e.g., per successful flap); CUA measures cost per QALY, incorporating patient preference
options.B: CEA uses QALYs; CUA uses natural clinical units
options.C: CEA compares only costs; CUA compares only outcomes
options.D: CEA converts outcomes to dollars; CUA ignores outcomes entirely

exp: Cost-effectiveness analysis expresses results as cost per natural clinical unit directly tied to the intervention's goal (e.g., cost per successful flap or per successful replantation), whereas cost-utility analysis — the preferred method when outcomes of compared interventions differ — uses health utilities converted to QALYs and thus incorporates patient preference (cost per QALY). They share the cost perspective but differ in the outcome measure. The other options confuse CEA/CUA with cost–benefit (dollar outcomes) or bare cost analysis.
hy: CEA = cost per natural unit; CUA = cost per QALY (patient preference).
src: Neligan I.11 · Types of economic studies
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — CEA uses natural units; CUA uses preference-weighted QALYs.
why.B: Wrong — the QALY/natural-unit assignment is reversed.
why.C: Wrong — comparing only costs (or only outcomes) describes neither CEA nor CUA.
why.D: Wrong — monetizing outcomes is cost–benefit analysis; CUA does not ignore outcomes.

stem: Why is a cost analysis (CA) considered only a partial economic evaluation?

options.A: It compares only the costs of interventions, with no evaluation of their outcomes
options.B: It evaluates outcomes but deliberately ignores costs
options.C: It uses QALYs without any monetary conversion
options.D: It can only be applied to cosmetic, self-pay procedures

exp: A cost analysis compares only the costs of interventions and performs no evaluation of their outcomes, which is why it is regarded as only a partial economic evaluation — frequently mislabeled "cost-effectiveness" in the plastic surgery literature. A true economic evaluation integrates both cost and outcomes (CEA, CUA, or CBA). The other options describe outcome-only or QALY-based analyses, or wrongly restrict CA to cosmetic surgery.
hy: Cost analysis = costs only, no outcomes → partial economic evaluation.
src: Neligan I.11 · Types of economic studies
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — CA compares costs without evaluating outcomes.
why.B: Wrong — CA ignores outcomes, it does not ignore costs.
why.C: Wrong — using QALYs would make it a cost-utility analysis.
why.D: Wrong — CA is not restricted to cosmetic procedures.

stem: In cost–utility analysis, what cost-per-QALY threshold is typically used to judge whether a new intervention should be adopted?
exp: In cost–utility analysis, a threshold of approximately $50,000 per QALY is conventionally used to judge whether a new intervention should be adopted — interventions below this cost per QALY are generally considered worth adopting. The other figures are off by one or more orders of magnitude and do not reflect the chapter's cited threshold. This single number is a high-yield board fact.
hy: Adoption threshold ≈ $50,000 per QALY.
src: Neligan I.11 · Types of economic studies
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — $50,000/QALY is the conventional adoption threshold cited.
why.B: Wrong — $5,000 is an order of magnitude too low.
why.C: Wrong — $500,000 is an order of magnitude too high.
why.D: Wrong — $1,000,000 far exceeds the cited threshold.

stem: In Thoma et al.'s analysis, the DIEP flap was more costly than the free TRAM flap yet was found to be more cost-effective. What principle does this illustrate about comparative effectiveness research?

options.A: CER identifies the treatment with the best value (benefits relative to cost), which is not necessarily the lowest-cost option
options.B: CER always selects the least expensive intervention available
options.C: Cost-effectiveness and lowest cost are synonymous concepts
options.D: A more expensive option can never be the more cost-effective one

exp: Comparative effectiveness research weighs benefits against costs to identify the treatment with the best value, which is not necessarily the cheapest: in Thoma et al.'s study the DIEP flap cost more than the free TRAM yet delivered enough additional benefit to be the more cost-effective option. CER therefore looks at value, not cost alone. The distractors equate cost-effectiveness with lowest cost or wrongly assert a costlier option can never be cost-effective.
hy: Cost-effective ≠ cheapest; CER picks best value (benefit relative to cost).
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — CER identifies best value, not necessarily lowest cost.
why.B: Wrong — CER does not automatically choose the cheapest option.
why.C: Wrong — cost-effectiveness and lowest cost are not synonymous.
why.D: Wrong — a costlier option can indeed be more cost-effective (the DIEP example).

stem: Among the four common perspectives in economic evaluation (societal, patient, employer, payer), how is the societal perspective distinguished?

options.A: It includes all benefits and costs to the whole of society, such as work productivity and caregiver costs, not just one patient
options.B: It includes only the individual patient's out-of-pocket costs and benefits
options.C: It includes only the payer's reimbursement costs and benefits
options.D: It includes only the employer's gains from increased worker productivity

exp: The societal perspective is the broadest of the four common viewpoints: it counts all benefits and costs to the whole of society — including work productivity and caregiver costs — rather than to a single patient. The patient perspective counts only the individual's costs/benefits, the payer perspective only the payer's, and the employer perspective only the employer's productivity-related costs and benefits. (A second panel recommended the third-party payer and society as the most important perspectives.)
hy: Societal perspective = all costs/benefits to society (productivity, caregiver costs).
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — the societal view captures all of society's costs and benefits.
why.B: Wrong — out-of-pocket-only is the patient perspective.
why.C: Wrong — reimbursement-only is the payer perspective.
why.D: Wrong — productivity-only is the employer perspective.

stem: A researcher preparing manuscripts for a randomized trial, an observational study, a systematic review, and an economic evaluation should follow which EQUATOR reporting guidelines, respectively?

options.A: CONSORT, STROBE, PRISMA, CHEERS
options.B: PRISMA, CONSORT, CHEERS, STROBE
options.C: STROBE, CHEERS, CONSORT, PRISMA
options.D: CHEERS, PRISMA, STROBE, CONSORT

exp: The EQUATOR network houses design-specific reporting guidelines: CONSORT for randomized trials, STROBE for observational studies, PRISMA for systematic reviews, and CHEERS for economic evaluations. Matching the manuscript to the right checklist improves transparency and completeness of reporting. The other orderings scramble these assignments.
hy: CONSORT (RCT), STROBE (observational), PRISMA (systematic review), CHEERS (economic).
src: Neligan I.11 · Preconditions to credible research
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — the standard EQUATOR mapping by study type.
why.B: Wrong — CONSORT is for trials, not observational studies.
why.C: Wrong — PRISMA is for systematic reviews, not trials.
why.D: Wrong — CHEERS is for economic evaluations, not trials.

section: What do we do with the outcomes?
stem: Donabedian's 1966 framework for assessing quality of care is built on which three categories?

options.A: Structure, Process, and Outcome
options.B: Input, Throughput, and Output
options.C: Efficacy, Effectiveness, and Efficiency
options.D: Access, Cost, and Quality

exp: Donabedian's 1966 model — still foundational today — assesses quality of care across three categories: Structure (the setting where care occurs, e.g., a dedicated microsurgical team), Process (the activities of patients and clinicians in giving/receiving care, e.g., a standardized replantation pathway), and Outcome (the effects of care on health status). The other triads are management or educational frameworks, not Donabedian's quality model.
hy: Donabedian: Structure, Process, Outcome.
src: Neligan I.11 · What do we do with the outcomes?
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — Structure, Process, Outcome is Donabedian's triad.
why.B: Wrong — input/throughput/output is a generic systems model.
why.C: Wrong — efficacy/effectiveness/efficiency are evaluation concepts, not the triad.
why.D: Wrong — access/cost/quality is a different health-policy framing.

stem: The chapter highlights a gap between evidence and practice. On average, how long does it take for new research knowledge to reach widespread practice, and what fraction of recommended care did US patients actually receive?

options.A: 17–24 years; 54.9% of recommended care
options.B: 2–3 years; 95% of recommended care
options.C: 30–40 years; 25% of recommended care
options.D: 5–10 years; 80% of recommended care

exp: The chapter cites a large evidence-to-practice gap: it takes an average of 17–24 years for new research knowledge (such as RCT findings) to be integrated into widespread practice, and a landmark study found US patients received only 54.9% of recommended care overall. These figures motivate knowledge translation efforts and quality-improvement initiatives. The other paired values are fabricated.
hy: ~17–24 yr lag to widespread practice; patients get ~54.9% of recommended care.
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — 17–24 years and 54.9% are the cited figures.
why.B: Wrong — the lag is far longer and the care fraction far lower than 95%.
why.C: Wrong — neither 30–40 years nor 25% matches the text.
why.D: Wrong — 5–10 years and 80% are not the cited values.

stem: A "core outcome set" is an agreed minimum list of outcomes to be measured in all studies of a condition. Which initiative is central to developing core outcome sets, and which provides systematic reviews of outcome-measurement instruments?

options.A: COMET develops core outcome sets; COSMIN reviews outcome-measurement instruments
options.B: COSMIN develops core outcome sets; COMET reviews outcome-measurement instruments
options.C: CONSORT develops core outcome sets; PRISMA reviews them
options.D: EQUATOR develops core outcome sets; CHEERS reviews them

exp: A core outcome set is an agreed minimum list of outcomes to be measured in all studies of a given condition, developed through systematic review, stakeholder interviews, and consensus methods such as Delphi surveys; the COMET (Core Outcome Measures in Effectiveness Trials) initiative is central to developing these. Separately, COSMIN (Consensus-Based Standards for the Selection of Health Measurement Instruments) provides systematic reviews of outcome-measurement instruments to help select valid, reliable, responsive measures. CONSORT/PRISMA/EQUATOR/CHEERS are reporting frameworks, not these.
hy: COMET = core outcome sets; COSMIN = reviews of outcome-measurement instruments.
src: Neligan I.11 · Preconditions to credible research
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — COMET develops core outcome sets; COSMIN reviews measurement instruments.
why.B: Wrong — the two initiatives' roles are reversed.
why.C: Wrong — CONSORT and PRISMA are reporting guidelines.
why.D: Wrong — EQUATOR hosts guidelines; CHEERS is an economic checklist.

stem: In Cabana et al.'s landmark systematic review, the sequence of behavior change in a physician's clinical practice was divided into which three domains?

options.A: Knowledge, attitude, and behavior
options.B: Structure, process, and outcome
options.C: Awareness, agreement, and adoption
options.D: Cognitive, affective, and psychomotor

exp: In Cabana et al.'s landmark systematic review, the sequence of behavior change in a physician's practice was divided into three domains — knowledge, attitude, and behavior — each of which can encounter its own barriers to applying evidence (e.g., lack of awareness or familiarity at the knowledge stage; lack of outcome expectancy or self-efficacy at the attitude stage; external constraints at the behavior stage). Donabedian's structure/process/outcome is a quality model, and the other triads are not Cabana's domains.
hy: Cabana: knowledge → attitude → behavior (each with its own barriers).
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — knowledge, attitude, behavior are Cabana's three domains.
why.B: Wrong — structure/process/outcome is Donabedian's quality model.
why.C: Wrong — awareness/agreement/adoption is a different adoption framing.
why.D: Wrong — cognitive/affective/psychomotor are learning domains, not Cabana's.

stem: How does pay-for-performance (P4P) differ from the federal pay-for-reporting (P4R) Physician Quality Reporting Initiative?

options.A: P4P links financial incentives to the quality of care delivered; P4R compensates physicians for reporting regardless of their performance
options.B: P4P pays physicians regardless of performance; P4R ties payment strictly to outcomes
options.C: Both tie reimbursement exclusively to risk-adjusted mortality
options.D: P4P applies only to cosmetic surgery; P4R only to reconstructive surgery

exp: Pay-for-performance (P4P, a form of value-based purchasing and part of the Affordable Care Act) ties reimbursement to the quality of care delivered — metric-driven outcomes, best practices, and patient satisfaction. By contrast, the federal pay-for-reporting (P4R) Physician Quality Reporting Initiative compensates physicians for participating in quality reporting regardless of how they perform on the measures. The reversed and "mortality-only" or specialty-restricted options misstate both programs.
hy: P4P pays for performance/quality; P4R pays for reporting regardless of performance.
src: Neligan I.11 · Public reporting and pay-for-performance
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — P4P rewards performance; P4R rewards reporting itself.
why.B: Wrong — the two models are reversed.
why.C: Wrong — neither is tied exclusively to mortality.
why.D: Wrong — the programs are not split by cosmetic vs reconstructive surgery.

stem: A Cochrane systematic review of patient decision aids (Stacey et al.) found which effect compared with usual care?

options.A: Patients felt more knowledgeable and had reduced decisional conflict, with no adverse effect on health outcomes or satisfaction
options.B: Patients ended up less knowledgeable and had greater decisional conflict
options.C: Decision aids reliably improved patient satisfaction, anxiety, and HRQL
options.D: Decision aids fully replaced the patient–physician encounter

exp: The Cochrane review by Stacey et al. found that, compared with usual care, patients exposed to decision aids felt more knowledgeable about their options and had reduced decisional conflict from feeling uninformed or unclear about their values, with no adverse effects on health outcomes or satisfaction; more patients also participated actively in decisions. However, RCTs did not demonstrate that decision aids improve outcomes such as satisfaction, anxiety, or HRQL, and they supplement rather than replace the patient–physician encounter. The distractors invert these findings.
hy: Decision aids ↑ knowledge, ↓ decisional conflict; no harm, but no proven outcome benefit.
src: Neligan I.11 · Decision aids for patients
chname: Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Services Research

why.A: Correct — more knowledge and less decisional conflict, without adverse effects.
why.B: Wrong — knowledge improved and conflict fell, the opposite of this option.
why.C: Wrong — trials did not show reliable gains in satisfaction, anxiety, or HRQL.
why.D: Wrong — decision aids supplement, not replace, the clinical encounter.

stem: By the US FDA definition, a patient-reported outcome (PRO) is best described as which of the following?

options.A: Any report of the status of a patient's health condition that comes directly from the patient, without interpretation by a clinician or anyone else
options.B: A clinician's structured assessment of a patient's health status recorded at each visit
options.C: An objective clinical endpoint such as flap survival or postoperative infection rate
options.D: A caregiver's proxy report of the patient's functional status over time

exp: The FDA defines a PRO as any report of the status of a patient's health condition that comes directly from the patient, without interpretation of the patient's response by a clinician or anyone else. The defining feature is that the information is unmediated — it captures the patient's own perspective. A clinician's structured assessment is a clinician-reported outcome, an objective endpoint like flap survival is a clinical/biometric outcome, and a caregiver's account is a proxy report — none is a PRO by this definition.
hy: PRO = patient's own report, no clinician interpretation (FDA).

why.A: Correct — direct, uninterpreted patient report is the FDA definition.
why.B: Wrong — a clinician's assessment is a clinician-reported outcome.
why.C: Wrong — flap survival/infection are objective clinical outcomes, not PROs.
why.D: Wrong — a caregiver account is a proxy report, not a patient report.

stem: In psychometric terms, what is a patient-reported outcome measure (PROM)?

options.A: A scientifically designed questionnaire in which responses are collected directly from patients and quantified
options.B: The smallest score change a patient perceives as beneficial
options.C: A diagram of the expected relationships between domains and the overall concept
options.D: The ability of an instrument to detect clinically important change over time

exp: Per Table 12.1, a PROM is a scientifically designed questionnaire in which responses are collected directly from patients, without clinician interpretation, recording and quantifying the outcomes that matter most to patients (HRQL, satisfaction, functional status). It is the instrument used to capture a PRO. The smallest beneficial score change is the MID, a diagram of domain relationships is the conceptual framework, and the ability to detect change is responsiveness.
hy: PROM = scientifically designed, patient-completed questionnaire quantifying patient-important outcomes.

why.A: Correct — a patient-completed, scientifically designed questionnaire is the PROM.
why.B: Wrong — the smallest beneficial change defines the MID.
why.C: Wrong — a diagram of domain relationships is the conceptual framework.
why.D: Wrong — detecting clinically important change defines responsiveness.

stem: In a value-based healthcare (VBHC) system, how is "value" achieved?

options.A: By measuring health outcomes against the cost of delivering the care
options.B: By maximizing the number of procedures performed per unit time
options.C: By minimizing length of stay regardless of the outcome achieved
options.D: By prioritizing surgeon reimbursement over measured outcomes

exp: In value-based healthcare the "value" is achieved by measuring health outcomes against the cost of delivering the care — a well-functioning VBHC system ideally lowers cost while raising quality, benefiting patients, providers, payers, suppliers, and society. Maximizing procedure volume, minimizing length of stay irrespective of outcome, or prioritizing reimbursement all describe volume- or cost-only thinking, which is the opposite of value-based care.
hy: VBHC value = health outcomes ÷ cost of delivering care.

why.A: Correct — value is outcomes measured against the cost of care.
why.B: Wrong — maximizing volume is the fee-for-service mindset VBHC replaces.
why.C: Wrong — cutting length of stay regardless of outcome ignores the outcome half of value.
why.D: Wrong — prioritizing reimbursement over outcomes is not value-based.

stem: Why does the chapter argue that traditional "hard" outcomes (survival, reoperation, complication rates) are insufficient on their own in plastic surgery?

options.A: Most plastic surgery procedures are not life-saving but aim to improve HRQL, satisfaction, and appearance — outcomes hard endpoints miss
options.B: Hard outcomes cannot be measured reliably in surgical patients
options.C: Hard outcomes are unavailable for reconstructive procedures
options.D: Patients are unable to perceive complications, so hard outcomes are invalid

exp: Traditional "hard" outcomes (survival, reoperation, complication rates) may not correlate with what matters to patients day to day; most plastic and reconstructive procedures are not life-saving but are performed to improve health-related quality of life, satisfaction, and appearance/function — dimensions hard endpoints simply do not capture. This gap is why PROMs gained traction in the field. Hard outcomes are measurable and available, and patients can perceive complications, so those options misstate the rationale.
hy: Plastic surgery aims at HRQL/satisfaction/appearance → needs PROMs, not just hard endpoints.

why.A: Correct — non-life-saving, quality-of-life goals require patient-reported measures.
why.B: Wrong — hard outcomes can be measured reliably; that is not the issue.
why.C: Wrong — hard outcomes are available for reconstructive procedures.
why.D: Wrong — patients can perceive complications; this is not the rationale.

stem: The very first PROMs in plastic surgery are considered "ad hoc" questionnaires. What is their defining limitation?

options.A: Their items were chosen by clinical experts with rarely any psychometric evaluation or patient input
options.B: They were developed using overly rigorous Rasch analysis that patients could not complete
options.C: They were validated only in international multilanguage samples
options.D: They measured a single concept too narrowly to be useful

exp: The earliest plastic surgery PROMs were "ad hoc" questionnaires: their items were deemed relevant by clinical experts, but a psychometric evaluation of the instrument's performance was rarely done and patient input was usually absent. As a result they often asked questions patients did not understand or considered irrelevant, and did not perform as consistently as experts assumed. They were not over-rigorous, not internationally validated, and not faulted for measuring a single concept too narrowly.
hy: Ad hoc questionnaires = expert-chosen items, little/no psychometrics or patient input.

why.A: Correct — expert-chosen items, scant psychometric testing or patient input.
why.B: Wrong — they lacked rigor; they were not over-rigorous Rasch instruments.
why.C: Wrong — international multilanguage validation describes modern PROMs.
why.D: Wrong — narrow single-concept focus is a modern concept-driven feature, not their flaw.

stem: For what purpose can ad hoc questionnaires still be appropriately used?

options.A: Merely exploratory work — to get a basic sense of answers to the questions posed
options.B: Determining treatment effectiveness in formal clinical research
options.C: Setting reimbursement in value-based healthcare systems
options.D: Creating evidence-based clinical practice guidelines

exp: Ad hoc questionnaires can be useful if the goal is merely exploratory — to get a basic sense of answers to the questions posed. Because they are not psychometrically validated, their data are not appropriate for determining treatment effectiveness in research or clinical care, for creating guidelines, or for setting reimbursement in value-based systems. Drawing firm conclusions from such flawed instruments risks mistaking a bad questionnaire for an ineffective treatment.
hy: Ad hoc questionnaires: exploratory use only — not for effectiveness, guidelines, or reimbursement.

why.A: Correct — exploratory sensing of answers is their legitimate use.
why.B: Wrong — they are not appropriate for determining treatment effectiveness.
why.C: Wrong — they cannot set reimbursement in value-based healthcare.
why.D: Wrong — they should not be used to create clinical practice guidelines.

stem: Instruments developed with Classical Test Theory (CTT) carried which characteristic limitation?

options.A: They could detect large differences between groups but not smaller, clinically relevant change within an individual patient
options.B: They could detect individual-level change but never group differences
options.C: They required computerized adaptive testing to score
options.D: They were inherently unidimensional and concept-driven

exp: Classical Test Theory instruments were generally validated to detect large differences between groups (e.g., drug vs placebo) but were not capable of detecting smaller, clinically relevant changes within an individual patient; many also relied on a total score and were developed without patient input. This individual-level insensitivity is the limitation modern IRT/RMT methods address. CTT does not require CAT, is not inherently unidimensional, and does not detect individual change better than group change.
hy: CTT: good for group differences, poor at individual-level change; total-score reliant.

why.A: Correct — CTT detects group differences but misses individual change.
why.B: Wrong — CTT detects group differences; the gap is at the individual level.
why.C: Wrong — CAT is a modern RMT-enabled feature, not a CTT requirement.
why.D: Wrong — concept-driven unidimensionality is a modern approach, not CTT's.

stem: Modern PROMs use Item Response Theory (IRT), commonly via Rasch Measurement Theory (RMT). How does the IRT/RMT approach differ from CTT?

options.A: It focuses on individual items within the instrument and yields interval-level measurement, rather than relying on the overall test score
options.B: It abandons psychometric validation in favor of expert consensus
options.C: It can only compare two groups, not individual patients
options.D: It scores instruments solely by summing raw item responses

exp: Item Response Theory (a mathematical framework, commonly implemented as Rasch Measurement Theory) focuses on the individual items within an instrument rather than the overall test score, and produces interval-level measurement estimates — true measurements like a ruler — that allow examination of change at the individual patient level. It does not abandon validation, is not limited to two-group comparison, and does not score by simple raw-sum.
hy: IRT/RMT = item-focused, interval-level measurement (not total-score based).

why.A: Correct — IRT/RMT focuses on items and yields interval-level measurement.
why.B: Wrong — RMT increases, not abandons, psychometric rigor.
why.C: Wrong — RMT enables individual-level (not only two-group) measurement.
why.D: Wrong — raw summation is the CTT-style total-score approach RMT improves on.

stem: A key advantage of PROMs built with RMT is described using the analogy of a "Rasch ruler." What does this convey?

options.A: The numbers generated represent interval-level measurements, allowing examination of change at the individual patient level
options.B: The instrument can only rank patients ordinally, not measure magnitude
options.C: Every patient must answer all items for any score to be valid
options.D: Scores are categorical and cannot detect change over time

exp: The "Rasch ruler" analogy conveys that RMT generates interval-level measurement estimates — the numbers are actual measurements, like marks on a ruler — which permits examining change at the level of the individual patient rather than just ranking patients. The other options describe ordinal-only ranking, mandatory completion of all items, or categorical scores, all of which contradict the interval-measurement advantage.
hy: Rasch ruler = interval-level scores → individual-patient change measurable.

why.A: Correct — interval-level measurement enabling individual change tracking.
why.B: Wrong — RMT provides interval, not merely ordinal, measurement.
why.C: Wrong — CAT-capable RMT does not require answering every item.
why.D: Wrong — scores are interval measurements, not categorical.

stem: Modern PROM development takes a "concept-driven" approach. What does this mean for a scale?

options.A: Each scale should measure a single concept of interest (i.e., be unidimensional)
options.B: Each scale should combine several distinct concepts into one total score
options.C: Scales should be designed without patient input to stay objective
options.D: Scales should always be as long as possible to maximize content

exp: A concept-driven approach ensures each scale measures a single concept of interest — it is unidimensional — so that improvement or deterioration can be attributed to a specific aspect of health. Legacy PROMs that combined multiple concepts into one "total score" made it unclear which aspect changed, now seen as a design flaw. Combining concepts, excluding patient input, and maximizing length all contradict the concept-driven principle.
hy: Concept-driven = each scale unidimensional (one concept), not a mixed total score.

why.A: Correct — concept-driven means each scale is unidimensional.
why.B: Wrong — combining concepts into a total score is the old flaw being corrected.
why.C: Wrong — patient input is central to concept-driven development.
why.D: Wrong — length is minimized where possible, not maximized.

stem: PROMs are divided into two main categories. Which contrast is correct?

options.A: Generic PROMs allow comparison across many conditions but are less sensitive to condition-specific change; disease-specific PROMs detect small important changes but cannot compare across conditions
options.B: Generic PROMs detect small condition-specific change; disease-specific PROMs compare across all conditions
options.C: Generic and disease-specific PROMs are interchangeable in scope
options.D: Disease-specific PROMs are always shorter and broader than generic ones

exp: Generic PROMs (e.g., SF-36, PROMIS-29) have broad scope and allow comparison across many different conditions, but they are often not sensitive enough to detect change in a specific condition after a targeted intervention. Disease-specific PROMs (e.g., BREAST-Q) cover a specific population's concerns and can detect small but important condition-related changes, but they cannot compare health states across different diseases. The other options reverse these properties or call them interchangeable.
hy: Generic = broad/comparable but insensitive; disease-specific = sensitive but not cross-comparable.

why.A: Correct — generic compares broadly; disease-specific detects condition change.
why.B: Wrong — the sensitivity/comparability properties are reversed.
why.C: Wrong — the two categories differ in scope and are not interchangeable.
why.D: Wrong — disease-specific PROMs are narrower, not broader, than generic.

stem: Which pairing of PROM to category is correct per the chapter?

options.A: SF-36 and PROMIS-29 are generic PROMs; the BREAST-Q is a condition-specific PROM
options.B: SF-36 is condition-specific; the BREAST-Q is generic
options.C: PROMIS-29 and BREAST-Q are both generic PROMs
options.D: SF-36 and BREAST-Q are both condition-specific PROMs

exp: Per the chapter, SF-36 and PROMIS-29 are generic PROMs applicable across many conditions, whereas the BREAST-Q is a condition-specific PROM focused on breast surgery patients. The other pairings miscategorize SF-36 as condition-specific, BREAST-Q as generic, or PROMIS-29 as anything other than generic. Knowing which tools are generic vs disease-specific guides their correct use in research and care.
hy: Generic: SF-36, PROMIS-29; disease-specific: BREAST-Q.

why.A: Correct — SF-36/PROMIS-29 generic; BREAST-Q disease-specific.
why.B: Wrong — SF-36 is generic and BREAST-Q is disease-specific.
why.C: Wrong — BREAST-Q is disease-specific, not generic.
why.D: Wrong — SF-36 is generic, not disease-specific.

stem: When undertaking clinical research with PROMs, what does the chapter recommend?

options.A: Incorporate both a generic and a disease-specific PROM as outcome measures
options.B: Use only a generic PROM to keep the questionnaire short
options.C: Use only a disease-specific PROM since generic ones add nothing
options.D: Avoid PROMs altogether in favor of clinician-rated scales

exp: Because generic and disease-specific PROMs have complementary strengths — broad comparability versus condition-specific sensitivity — the chapter recommends incorporating both as outcome measures when undertaking clinical research. Using only one forgoes either cross-condition comparison or sensitivity to the intervention, and abandoning PROMs in favor of clinician-rated scales loses the patient perspective entirely.
hy: In research, use BOTH a generic and a disease-specific PROM.

why.A: Correct — both types together capture comparability and sensitivity.
why.B: Wrong — generic alone misses condition-specific change.
why.C: Wrong — generic PROMs add cross-condition comparability, so they are not useless.
why.D: Wrong — clinician-rated scales lose the patient perspective PROMs provide.

stem: Per Table 12.2, which describes the BREAST-Q?

options.A: A 2008 condition-specific PROM (Pusic, Klassen et al.) with pre- and postoperative modules and a 0–100 scale for breast reconstruction, reduction, augmentation, and breast-conserving surgery
options.B: A 1992 generic 36-item health survey scored so that lower scores represent worse outcomes
options.C: A 5-item generic instrument across mobility, self-care, usual activity, pain, and anxiety
options.D: A hand-specific questionnaire assessing wrist pain and function

exp: Per Table 12.2, the BREAST-Q (2008; Pusic, Klassen, Scott, et al.) is a condition-specific PROM with pre- and postoperative questionnaires for breast reconstruction, reduction mammaplasty, augmentation, and breast-conserving surgery, scored on a 0–100 scale. The other descriptions belong to the generic SF-36 (1992, 36-item, lower = worse), the generic EQ-5D (5 items), and a hand/wrist instrument.
hy: BREAST-Q (2008, Pusic/Klassen): 0–100, breast recon/reduction/augmentation/BCS modules.

why.A: Correct — the BREAST-Q's authors, year, modules, and 0–100 scale.
why.B: Wrong — that is the generic SF-36.
why.C: Wrong — five dimensions describe the EQ-5D.
why.D: Wrong — wrist pain/function describes a hand PROM, not the BREAST-Q.

stem: Per Table 12.2, the FACE-Q (2013, Pusic/Klassen et al.) was developed for what purpose?

options.A: To assess the impact and effectiveness of facial aesthetic procedures across scales such as satisfaction with facial appearance and HRQL
options.B: To assess outcomes of breast reconstruction and reduction
options.C: To assess severity of carpal tunnel syndrome symptoms
options.D: To provide a generic health-status profile across seven domains

exp: Per Table 12.2, the FACE-Q (2013; Pusic, Klassen, Scott, Cano) assesses the impact and effectiveness of facial aesthetic procedures, examining scales such as satisfaction with facial appearance, health-related quality of life, negative sequelae, and satisfaction with the process of care.
