why.A: Correct — most common skin cancer, median ~68, male predominance.
why.B: Wrong — BCC is far more common than melanoma and not a childhood tumor.
why.C: Wrong — there is a male, not female, predominance and older age.
why.D: Wrong — BCC rarely metastasizes/kills; it is not the top skin-cancer killer.

stem: Nodular BCC is the most common subtype (~50% of BCCs). Which clinical description is classic?

options.A: A shiny, pearly papule/nodule with arborizing telangiectasias and a rolled border that may ulcerate ("rodent ulcer")
options.B: An erythematous scaly patch on the trunk with subclinical lateral spread
options.C: A scar-like indurated plaque with ill-defined borders
options.D: A rapidly growing crateriform nodule with a central keratotic core

exp: Nodular BCC, the most common subtype (~50%), classically presents as a shiny, pearly papule or nodule with a smooth surface, arborizing telangiectasias, and an elevated rolled border that may ulcerate centrally ("rodent ulcer"). The other descriptions are superficial BCC (B, trunk patch with lateral spread), morpheaform BCC (C, scar-like), and keratoacanthoma (D, crateriform keratotic core).
hy: Nodular BCC = pearly papule, arborizing telangiectasia, rolled border, rodent ulcer.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — the classic pearly, telangiectatic, rolled-border nodule.
why.B: Wrong — an erythematous trunk patch is superficial BCC.
why.C: Wrong — a scar-like plaque is morpheaform BCC.
why.D: Wrong — a crateriform keratotic nodule is a keratoacanthoma.

stem: Compared with other BCC subtypes, superficial BCC has which characteristic profile?

options.A: Favors the trunk, presents at a younger mean age (mid-50s), grows primarily horizontally, and has notable subclinical lateral spread causing recurrence after routine surgery
options.B: Favors the central face, presents in the elderly, and grows deeply with perineural invasion
options.C: Is the most aggressive subtype with frequent metastasis
options.D: Presents as a pearly nodule with arborizing telangiectasia on the nose

exp: Superficial BCC favors the trunk, presents at a younger mean age (mid-50s) and is the most common subtype in younger patients; it grows primarily horizontally and has subclinical lateral spread, which accounts for its significant recurrence rate after routine surgical treatment. Option B (central face, deep/perineural) and D (pearly nodule) describe nodular BCC, and C wrongly calls superficial BCC the most aggressive/metastatic subtype.
hy: Superficial BCC: trunk, younger, horizontal growth, subclinical lateral spread → recurrence.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — trunk, mid-50s, horizontal spread with recurrence risk.
why.B: Wrong — central-face deep growth describes nodular/aggressive subtypes.
why.C: Wrong — superficial BCC is not the most aggressive/metastatic.
why.D: Wrong — a pearly telangiectatic nodule is nodular BCC.

stem: Morpheaform (sclerosing) BCC is important to recognize because it:

options.A: Resembles a scar or plaque of morphea with ill-defined borders and behaves more aggressively with extensive local destruction
options.B: Is a soft pedunculated plaque on the lower back that never recurs
options.C: Is a pigmented nodule easily confused with melanoma but is indolent
options.D: Is a superficial erythematous patch confined to the epidermis

exp: Morpheaform (sclerosing) BCC presents as a slightly elevated to depressed area of induration, light pink to white, with ill-defined borders that may resemble a scar or plaque of morphea; an elevated pearly border is typically absent, and its biologic behavior is more aggressive with extensive local destruction. Option B describes fibroepithelioma of Pinkus, C a pigmented nodular BCC, and D superficial BCC.
hy: Morpheaform BCC = scar-like, ill-defined, aggressive local destruction.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — scar-like induration with aggressive local behavior.
why.B: Wrong — a soft pedunculated back plaque is fibroepithelioma of Pinkus.
why.C: Wrong — a pigmented indolent nodule is pigmented nodular BCC.
why.D: Wrong — an epidermis-confined patch is superficial BCC.

stem: Which BCC variant is correctly described?

options.A: Fibroepithelioma of Pinkus — a soft, skin-colored to pink sessile plaque favoring the lower back, which some consider a trichoblastoma variant
options.B: Fibroepithelioma of Pinkus — an aggressive ulcerating tumor of the central face
options.C: Fibroepithelioma of Pinkus — a pigmented nodule of the eyelid
options.D: Fibroepithelioma of Pinkus — the in-situ form of squamous cell carcinoma

exp: Fibroepithelioma of Pinkus is a rare BCC variant presenting as a skin-colored or pink, soft, sessile plaque (or pedunculated papulonodule) favoring the lower back, often in patients with multiple superficial BCCs; some experts regard it as a variant of trichoblastoma rather than true BCC. The other options wrongly cast it as an aggressive facial tumor, an eyelid nodule, or SCC in situ.
hy: Fibroepithelioma of Pinkus = soft sessile lower-back plaque; ?trichoblastoma variant.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — sessile lower-back plaque, possible trichoblastoma variant.
why.B: Wrong — it is not an aggressive central-face ulcerating tumor.
why.C: Wrong — it is not an eyelid pigmented nodule.
why.D: Wrong — it is a BCC variant, not squamous in situ.

stem: Metastatic BCC is rare. Which histopathologic patterns are most associated with aggressive behavior and metastatic potential?

options.A: Morpheaform, infiltrating, metatypical (basosquamous), and perineural growth
options.B: Nodular and superficial patterns
options.C: Fibroepithelioma of Pinkus and infundibulocystic patterns
options.D: Pigmented nodular pattern only

exp: Although metastatic BCC is rare, it arises preferentially from tumors with aggressive histopathologic patterns — morpheaform, infiltrating, metatypical (basosquamous) — and perineural growth is an indicator of aggressive disease; metastases involve regional nodes, lungs, bone, and skin. Nodular and superficial patterns (B), fibroepithelioma/infundibulocystic (C), and pigmented nodular alone (D) are not the aggressive metastasizing patterns.
hy: Aggressive/metastasizing BCC: morpheaform, infiltrating, metatypical, perineural.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — morpheaform/infiltrating/metatypical + perineural growth.
why.B: Wrong — nodular and superficial are the indolent common types.
why.C: Wrong — fibroepithelioma/infundibulocystic are not aggressive metastasizers.
why.D: Wrong — pigmentation alone does not confer metastatic potential.

stem: Which histopathologic features are characteristic of basal cell carcinoma?

options.A: Aggregations of basaloid keratinocytes with peripheral palisading and peritumoral retraction (clefting) within a fibromyxoid stroma
options.B: Keratin pearls and intercellular bridges with eosinophilic glassy cytoplasm
options.C: Nests of melanocytes with pagetoid upward spread
options.D: Full-thickness keratinocyte atypia confined to the epidermis

exp: All BCCs share aggregations of basaloid keratinocytes with peripheral palisading of nuclei and characteristic peritumoral retraction (clefting) between tumor and the surrounding fibromyxoid stroma. Keratin pearls and intercellular bridges (B) characterize SCC, melanocytic nests with pagetoid spread (C) characterize melanoma, and full-thickness keratinocyte atypia confined to the epidermis (D) is SCC in situ.
hy: BCC histo: basaloid nests, peripheral palisading, peritumoral retraction clefts.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — palisading basaloid nests with retraction clefts.
why.B: Wrong — keratin pearls/intercellular bridges are SCC features.
why.C: Wrong — pagetoid melanocytic nests are melanoma.
why.D: Wrong — full-thickness epidermal atypia is SCC in situ.

stem: Basosquamous (metatypical) carcinoma is clinically important because:

options.A: It has features of both BCC and cSCC, behaves more like an SCC (more aggressive, greater recurrence/metastasis risk), and constitutes ~1% of keratinocyte carcinomas
options.B: It is the most indolent BCC subtype and never metastasizes
options.C: It is a benign adnexal tumor mistaken for BCC
options.D: It is identical in behavior to a superficial BCC

exp: Basosquamous (metatypical, keratotic) carcinoma has histopathologic features of both BCC and cSCC and tends to behave more like an SCC — more aggressive, with greater likelihood of recurrence and metastasis; it is estimated to constitute ~1% of keratinocyte carcinomas. It is therefore not indolent (B), not a benign adnexal mimic (C), and not equivalent to superficial BCC (D).
hy: Basosquamous = BCC+SCC features, SCC-like aggressive behavior, ~1% of KCs.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — mixed histology, SCC-like behavior, ~1% of KCs.
why.B: Wrong — it is aggressive, not the most indolent subtype.
why.C: Wrong — it is a true carcinoma, not a benign adnexal mimic.
why.D: Wrong — it behaves far more aggressively than superficial BCC.

stem: Compared with nodular BCC, the micronodular (and infiltrating) pattern is notable for:

options.A: Smaller basaloid aggregations that infiltrate the dermis with subclinical spread and higher recurrence rates
options.B: Larger, well-circumscribed nodules with low recurrence
options.C: Confinement to the epidermis without dermal involvement
options.D: Spontaneous regression in most cases

exp: Micronodular (and infiltrating) BCC is composed of smaller basaloid aggregations than nodular BCC that infiltrate the dermis, producing destructive behavior with subclinical spread and high recurrence rates; clinically it can be indistinguishable from nodular BCC. It is not a well-circumscribed low-recurrence tumor (B), not epidermis-confined (C), and does not regress spontaneously (D).
hy: Micronodular/infiltrating BCC: small infiltrative islands, subclinical spread, high recurrence.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — small infiltrating nests with subclinical spread/recurrence.
why.B: Wrong — it is infiltrative with high, not low, recurrence.
why.C: Wrong — it invades the dermis, not just the epidermis.
why.D: Wrong — it does not characteristically regress.

stem: Trichoblastoma is a key histopathologic simulant of BCC. Which feature helps distinguish trichoblastoma from BCC?

options.A: Clefts located within the stroma (not peritumoral), follicular germs/papillae, and PHLDA1 positivity
options.B: Peritumoral retraction clefts and absence of any follicular differentiation
options.C: Full-thickness epidermal atypia with keratin pearls
options.D: Pagetoid spread of atypical melanocytes

exp: Trichoblastoma simulates BCC histologically, but distinguishing features include clefts located within the stroma (rather than peritumoral, as in BCC), the presence of follicular germs and papillae, and PHLDA1 positivity (a follicular stem-cell marker stronger/more diffuse than in BCC). Option B describes BCC itself, C describes SCC, and D describes melanoma.
hy: Trichoblastoma vs BCC: stromal (not peritumoral) clefts, follicular germs, PHLDA1+.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — stromal clefts, follicular germs, PHLDA1 positivity.
why.B: Wrong — peritumoral retraction clefts characterize BCC, not trichoblastoma.
why.C: Wrong — keratin pearls/full-thickness atypia are SCC.
why.D: Wrong — pagetoid melanocytes are melanoma.

stem: What are characteristic dermoscopic findings of basal cell carcinoma?

options.A: Well-focused arborizing vessels with foci of microulceration, plus blue-gray ovoid nests/globules, leaf-like structures, and spoke-wheel areas — with absence of a pigment network
options.B: A prominent pigment network with brown reticular lines
options.C: A "strawberry pattern" of erythematous pseudonetwork around follicular openings
options.D: Parallel ridge pattern on acral skin

exp: The dermoscopic hallmark of BCC is well-focused arborizing vessels with foci of microulceration; additional high-specificity findings include large blue-gray ovoid nests, multiple blue-gray globules, leaf-like structures, and spoke-wheel areas, with absence of a pigment network. A prominent pigment network (B) suggests a melanocytic lesion, the "strawberry pattern" (C) is a facial AK finding, and the parallel ridge pattern (D) is an acral-melanoma sign.
hy: BCC dermoscopy: arborizing vessels + blue-gray nests/globules, leaf-like/spoke-wheel; no pigment network.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — arborizing vessels with blue-gray structures, no network.
why.B: Wrong — a pigment network points to a melanocytic lesion.
why.C: Wrong — the strawberry pattern is a facial AK feature.
why.D: Wrong — the parallel ridge pattern indicates acral melanoma.

stem: What surgical excision margins are recommended for a low-risk cSCC and for a non-morpheaform BCC <2 cm in diameter, respectively?

options.A: cSCC ~4–6 mm; non-morpheaform BCC <2 cm ~4 mm (adequate in ~98% of cases)
options.B: cSCC ~1 mm; BCC ~1 mm
options.C: cSCC ~2 cm; BCC ~2 cm
options.D: Both require ≥15 mm regardless of tumor type

exp: Low-risk cSCC should be excised with a ~4–6 mm margin, and for non-morpheaform BCCs <2 cm in diameter, 4-mm margins are adequate in ~98% of cases. The 1-mm (B) and 2-cm (C) figures are far off, and routine ≥15 mm margins (D) are not required (the 5–15 mm range is a European Mohs-unavailable recommendation for higher-risk BCC, not the routine standard).
hy: Margins: low-risk cSCC 4–6 mm; non-morpheaform BCC <2 cm = 4 mm (~98% adequate).
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — 4–6 mm for low-risk cSCC; 4 mm for small non-morpheaform BCC.
why.B: Wrong — 1-mm margins are inadequate.
why.C: Wrong — 2-cm margins are excessive for these tumors.
why.D: Wrong — ≥15 mm is not the routine requirement.

stem: Mohs micrographic surgery is preferred for high-risk and certain BCCs. Which statement is supported by the chapter's cited recurrence data?

options.A: In one series, Mohs had a 5-year recurrence rate of ~1%, superior to excision (~10%), curettage and electrodesiccation (~8%), radiation (~9%), and cryotherapy (~7.5%)
options.B: Mohs had the highest recurrence rate of all modalities
options.C: Mohs is contraindicated for morpheaform or recurrent BCCs
options.D: Mohs and cryotherapy have identical recurrence rates of ~25%

exp: Mohs micrographic surgery gives the highest cure rate: in one series, BCCs treated with Mohs had a 5-year recurrence of only ~1%, superior to excision (~10%), curettage and electrodesiccation (~8%), radiation therapy (~9%), and cryotherapy (~7.5%). Mohs is therefore not the worst (B), is preferred (not contraindicated) for morpheaform/recurrent BCC (C), and recurrence rates are low, not ~25% (D).
hy: Mohs 5-yr recurrence ~1% < excision 10%/C&E 8%/RT 9%/cryo 7.5%.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — Mohs ~1% vs higher recurrence with other modalities.
why.B: Wrong — Mohs has the lowest, not highest, recurrence.
why.C: Wrong — Mohs is preferred for morpheaform/recurrent BCC.
why.D: Wrong — recurrence is ~1–10%, not ~25%, across these modalities.

stem: Curettage with electrodesiccation is frequently used for BCCs. Which statement is correct?

options.A: Cure rates as high as 97–98% are reported for carefully selected lesions; it can also be used for SCC in situ and well-differentiated primary SCCs <1 cm, but is poor for lesions extending into deep dermis or hair-bearing sites
options.B: It is the treatment of choice for morpheaform BCC of the nose
options.C: It provides full histologic margin control like Mohs
options.D: It is preferred for tumors with perineural invasion

exp: Curettage with electrodesiccation can achieve cure rates as high as 97–98% for carefully selected lesions, and can be used for smaller SCCs in situ and well-differentiated primary SCCs <1 cm; however, lesions extending into the deep dermis or in terminal-hair-bearing sites risk incomplete removal (follicular extension). It is not appropriate for morpheaform BCC of the nose (B), provides no margin control (C, unlike Mohs), and is not for perineural-invasion tumors (D).
hy: C&E: 97–98% in select lesions; SCC in situ / well-diff SCC <1 cm; avoid deep/hair-bearing.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — high cure in select lesions, with stated limitations.
why.B: Wrong — morpheaform facial BCC needs Mohs, not C&E.
why.C: Wrong — C&E gives no histologic margin control.
why.D: Wrong — perineural-invasion tumors require aggressive margin-controlled surgery.

stem: Regarding radiation therapy for keratinocyte carcinomas, which statement is correct?

options.A: It is useful for non-surgical candidates and as adjuvant therapy for high-risk cSCC with perineural involvement, but is avoided for verrucous carcinoma (risk of anaplastic transformation), morpheaform BCC (radioresistant), and BCNS (induces new tumors)
options.B: It is first-line for all BCCs and produces scars that improve over time
options.C: It is the preferred treatment for verrucous carcinoma
options.D: It is especially recommended in basal cell nevus syndrome

exp: Radiation therapy is an option for patients who are poor surgical candidates and is used as adjuvant therapy for high-risk cSCC, especially with perineural involvement, to reduce recurrence; it is avoided for verrucous carcinoma (concern for anaplastic transformation), morpheaform BCC (relatively radioresistant), and BCNS (X-rays can mutate the second PTCH allele and induce new tumors). Radiation scars also worsen over time. Options B–D state the opposite of these caveats.
hy: RT: adjuvant for PNI; avoid in verrucous carcinoma, morpheaform BCC, and BCNS.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — adjuvant for high-risk PNI; the three avoidance settings.
why.B: Wrong — RT is not first-line for all BCCs, and its scars worsen with time.
why.C: Wrong — verrucous carcinoma is generally not irradiated.
why.D: Wrong — radiation is avoided, not recommended, in BCNS.

stem: Which statement about field/destructive therapies for AKs is correct?

options.A: Cryosurgery is a mainstay for AKs, and photodynamic therapy is FDA-approved for multiple AKs (with daylight PDT useful for field cancerization) — though one RCT found topical 5-FU more effective than PDT, imiquimod, or ingenol mebutate
options.B: PDT is contraindicated for actinic keratoses
options.C: Cryosurgery cannot be used for AKs
options.D: Topical 5-FU was the least effective AK therapy in that RCT

exp: Cryosurgery is a mainstay for AKs (and can treat some BCCs/SCCs), and photodynamic therapy is FDA-approved for multiple AKs, with daylight PDT particularly useful in field cancerization; however, a randomized trial in immunocompetent patients found topical 5-FU more effective than PDT, imiquimod, or ingenol mebutate for AKs. Option B/C wrongly exclude PDT or cryosurgery for AKs, and D inverts the RCT result.
hy: Cryo & PDT are AK mainstays, but topical 5-FU beat PDT/imiquimod/ingenol in an RCT.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — cryo/PDT for AKs, with 5-FU most effective in the RCT.
why.B: Wrong — PDT is FDA-approved for AKs, not contraindicated.
why.C: Wrong — cryosurgery is a mainstay for AKs.
why.D: Wrong — 5-FU was the most, not least, effective in that trial.

stem: 5-Fluorouracil is a mainstay topical agent. For which indication is it primarily used, and what is its relative efficacy for AKs?

options.A: Primarily for AKs (and occasionally superficial BCC / SCC in situ); in a randomized immunocompetent trial it was more effective than other standard AK therapies
options.B: Primarily for morpheaform BCC, where it is curative
options.C: Primarily for melanoma in situ
options.D: It is ineffective for AKs and used only as a moisturizer

exp: 5-Fluorouracil is used primarily for AKs (and occasionally for superficial BCCs and selected cSCCs in situ); in a randomized trial of immunocompetent patients it was more effective than other standard AK therapies. It is not curative for morpheaform BCC (B), not a melanoma-in-situ treatment (C), and is an active antimetabolite, not a mere moisturizer (D).
hy: Topical 5-FU: mainstay for AK (most effective in RCT); also superficial BCC/SCC in situ.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — primarily AK, most effective among standard topicals.
why.B: Wrong — it does not cure morpheaform BCC.
why.C: Wrong — it is not a treatment for melanoma in situ.
why.D: Wrong — it is a cytotoxic antimetabolite, not a moisturizer.

stem: Imiquimod's mechanism and approved use include which of the following?

options.A: A Toll-like receptor 7 agonist; the 5% formulation is FDA-approved for superficial BCC, and 2.5%/3.75%/5% formulations for non-hypertrophic facial/scalp AKs
options.B: A Src-kinase inhibitor approved only for invasive SCC
options.C: An antimetabolite identical in mechanism to 5-fluorouracil
options.D: A hedgehog pathway inhibitor approved for metastatic BCC

exp: Imiquimod is a Toll-like receptor 7 agonist; the 5% formulation is FDA-approved for superficial BCC, and the 2.5%, 3.75%, and 5% formulations are approved for non-hypertrophic AKs of the face and scalp. Its major side effect is a local inflammatory skin reaction. It is not a Src-kinase inhibitor (B, that is tirbanibulin), not an antimetabolite like 5-FU (C), and not a hedgehog inhibitor (D, that is vismodegib/sonidegib).
hy: Imiquimod = TLR7 agonist; 5% for superficial BCC; 2.5/3.75/5% for facial/scalp AK.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — TLR7 agonist with these BCC/AK approvals.
why.B: Wrong — Src-kinase/tubulin inhibition is tirbanibulin.
why.C: Wrong — imiquimod is an immunomodulator, not an antimetabolite.
why.D: Wrong — hedgehog inhibition describes vismodegib/sonidegib.

stem: Tirbanibulin, recently approved as a 1% ointment for AKs, has which mechanism and dosing?

options.A: Inhibits Src kinase signaling and tubulin polymerization; applied once daily for 5 consecutive days, with complete clearance in ~44–54% in phase III trials
options.B: A Toll-like receptor 7 agonist applied twice weekly for 16 weeks
options.C: A topical NSAID applied twice daily for 90 days
options.D: An oral retinoid taken daily for 1 year

exp: Tirbanibulin, recently approved as a 1% ointment for AKs, inhibits Src kinase signaling and tubulin polymerization; it is applied once daily for 5 consecutive days (face and scalp), with complete clearance in ~44–54% of participants in two phase III trials. Option B describes imiquimod, C describes diclofenac, and D describes an oral retinoid — not tirbanibulin.
hy: Tirbanibulin = Src/tubulin inhibitor, 1% ointment ×5 days, ~44–54% clearance.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — Src/tubulin inhibitor, 5-day course, 44–54% clearance.
why.B: Wrong — twice-weekly ×16 weeks TLR7 agonist is imiquimod.
why.C: Wrong — a 90-day topical NSAID is diclofenac.
why.D: Wrong — an oral daily retinoid is not tirbanibulin.

stem: In a randomized controlled trial comparing imiquimod with surgery for BCCs, what were the approximate clearance rates?

options.A: ~82.5% with imiquimod versus ~98% with surgery
options.B: ~98% with imiquimod versus ~50% with surgery
options.C: Equal (~70%) for both
options.D: ~30% with imiquimod versus ~30% with surgery

exp: In a randomized controlled trial of 501 patients with BCCs, tumor clearance was ~82.5% in the imiquimod group versus ~98% in the surgery group — surgery was more effective, but imiquimod cleared most lesions, supporting its role for selected superficial tumors. The reversed (B), equal (C), and uniformly-low (D) figures contradict the trial.
hy: BCC RCT: imiquimod ~82.5% vs surgery ~98% clearance.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — ~82.5% imiquimod vs ~98% surgery.
why.B: Wrong — surgery, not imiquimod, had the higher clearance.
why.C: Wrong — the arms were not equal.
why.D: Wrong — both arms cleared far more than 30%.

stem: Regarding intralesional treatment of NMSC, which statement is correct?

options.A: Intralesional 5-FU (max ~50–100 mg/lesion/session) or methotrexate (check renal function; max ~1 mL/lesion) can achieve response rates around 80% in selected patients
options.B: Intralesional corticosteroids are the standard cytostatic agent for NMSC
options.C: Intralesional therapy is first-line for all high-risk SCCs
options.D: Methotrexate requires no assessment of renal function before injection

exp: Intralesional therapy of NMSC can use cytostatics — methotrexate, bleomycin, or 5-FU — or immunomodulators (interferon alpha-2b), with response rates around 80% in selected patients; per Table 108.9, intralesional 5-FU is given at ~50–100 mg per lesion per session, and methotrexate requires checking renal function with a maximum of ~1 mL per lesion. Corticosteroids are not the cytostatic agent (B), it is not first-line for all high-risk SCC (C), and MTX does require renal assessment (D).
hy: Intralesional 5-FU 50–100 mg / MTX (check renal, ≤1 mL); ~80% response.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — the 5-FU/MTX dosing and ~80% response in selected cases.
why.B: Wrong — corticosteroids are not the cytostatic injected for NMSC.
why.C: Wrong — it is for selected patients, not first-line for all high-risk SCC.
why.D: Wrong — renal function must be checked before MTX.

stem: For advanced (locally advanced or metastatic) cSCC not amenable to surgery or radiation, which systemic therapy is first-line, and what is its approximate efficacy?

options.A: Anti-PD-1 immune checkpoint inhibitors (e.g. cemiplimab), with overall response rates of ~44–45% and >40% response reported across studies
options.B: Hedgehog pathway inhibitors such as vismodegib, with response rates ~6%
options.C: BRAF/MEK inhibitor combinations, with response rates ~90%
options.D: Intralesional corticosteroids

exp: For advanced cSCC not curable by surgery or radiation, anti-PD-1 immune checkpoint inhibitors are first-line: cemiplimab produced overall response rates of ~44% (locally advanced) and ~45% (metastatic), and response rates >40% have been reported with anti-PD-1 antibodies generally. Hedgehog inhibitors (B) are for BCC, BRAF/MEK (C) is for melanoma, and corticosteroids (D) are not a systemic antitumor therapy here.
hy: Advanced cSCC: anti-PD-1 (cemiplimab) ORR ~44–45%.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — cemiplimab/anti-PD-1 with ~44–45% ORR.
why.B: Wrong — hedgehog inhibitors treat BCC, not cSCC.
why.C: Wrong — BRAF/MEK therapy targets BRAF-mutant melanoma.
why.D: Wrong — intralesional steroids are not systemic cSCC therapy.

stem: Beyond first-line checkpoint blockade, which additional facts about advanced cSCC therapy are correct?

options.A: Cemiplimab as neoadjuvant therapy for stage II–IV cSCC produced a pathologic complete response rate of ~51%; cetuximab (an EGFR inhibitor) is a potential second-line option
options.B: Cetuximab is a hedgehog inhibitor used first-line
options.C: Neoadjuvant cemiplimab produced no pathologic responses
options.D: Chemotherapy outperforms anti-PD-1 antibodies in advanced cSCC

exp: Cemiplimab used as neoadjuvant therapy for stage II–IV cSCC produced a pathologic complete response rate of ~51%; for second-line systemic therapy, the EGFR inhibitor cetuximab (with chemotherapy or radiation) is a potential option. Cetuximab is not a first-line hedgehog inhibitor (B), neoadjuvant cemiplimab did produce responses (C), and anti-PD-1 antibodies outperform chemotherapy in advanced cSCC (D).
hy: Neoadjuvant cemiplimab cSCC pCR ~51%; cetuximab (EGFR) = second-line.
src: Bologna Ch 108, Treatment of advanced cSCC
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — ~51% pCR neoadjuvant; cetuximab second-line.
why.B: Wrong — cetuximab is an EGFR inhibitor, not first-line hedgehog.
why.C: Wrong — neoadjuvant cemiplimab achieved a high pCR.
why.D: Wrong — anti-PD-1 beat chemotherapy in advanced cSCC.

stem: Hedgehog pathway inhibitors are the key systemic treatment for advanced BCC. Which statement is correct?

options.A: Vismodegib and sonidegib inhibit Smoothened (SMO); in the vismodegib phase I trial ~6% had a complete and ~48% a partial response, and across studies ORRs were ~30% (metastatic) and ~40–45% (locally advanced)
options.B: They inhibit Patched (PTCH1) and are used for advanced cSCC
options.C: They are anti-PD-1 antibodies used for melanoma
options.D: They are EGFR inhibitors with no role in BCC

exp: Vismodegib and sonidegib are hedgehog pathway inhibitors that act by Smoothened (SMO) antagonism for advanced BCC; in the vismodegib phase I trial, ~6% had a complete and ~48% a partial response, and across later studies metastatic-BCC ORR was ~30% and locally advanced ~40–45%. They inhibit SMO, not Patched (B), are not anti-PD-1 antibodies (C), and are not EGFR inhibitors (D).
hy: Vismodegib/sonidegib = SMO inhibitors for advanced BCC (ORR ~30% met, ~40–45% LA).
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — SMO inhibitors with the cited response rates.
why.B: Wrong — they antagonize SMO, not Patched, and treat BCC.
why.C: Wrong — they are small-molecule SMO inhibitors, not anti-PD-1.
why.D: Wrong — they are not EGFR inhibitors.

stem: Which adverse effect is among the most common with vismodegib/sonidegib, and what is a critical safety warning?

options.A: Dysgeusia (>40% of patients), muscle spasms, and alopecia are common; both drugs are teratogenic (can cause birth defects or fetal death)
options.B: They are completely free of side effects and safe in pregnancy
options.C: The main side effect is severe immunosuppression requiring prophylactic antibiotics
options.D: They cause hypertension as the predominant adverse effect

exp: Per Table 108.10, common adverse effects of vismodegib/sonidegib include dysgeusia (>40% of patients, from altered tongue-epithelium turnover), muscle spasms, and alopecia; critically, both drugs are teratogenic and can cause birth defects or fetal death, mandating strict contraception. They are not side-effect-free (B), do not cause predominant immunosuppression (C), and hypertension is not the main effect (D).
hy: Hedgehog inhibitors: dysgeusia >40%, muscle spasms, alopecia; teratogenic.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — dysgeusia/spasms/alopecia, and teratogenicity warning.
why.B: Wrong — adverse effects are common and include teratogenicity.
why.C: Wrong — immunosuppression is not the hallmark toxicity.
why.D: Wrong — hypertension is not the predominant side effect.

stem: The anti-PD-1 antibody cemiplimab is FDA-approved for advanced BCC in which setting?

options.A: For locally advanced or metastatic BCC previously treated with a hedgehog pathway inhibitor (or when one is inappropriate); second-line use achieved an ORR of ~31%
options.B: As first-line therapy for all superficial BCCs
options.C: Only for BCCs that have never received hedgehog inhibitors and are surgically resectable
options.D: It has no approved role in BCC

exp: The anti-PD-1 antibody cemiplimab is FDA-approved for locally advanced or metastatic BCC in patients previously treated with a hedgehog pathway inhibitor (or for whom one is inappropriate); as second-line therapy after hedgehog-inhibitor resistance it achieved an ORR of ~31%. It is not first-line for superficial BCC (B), not restricted to hedgehog-naïve resectable tumors (C), and does have an approved BCC role (D).
hy: Cemiplimab in BCC: after hedgehog inhibitor (or if inappropriate); ORR ~31%.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — approved post-hedgehog-inhibitor, ORR ~31%.
why.B: Wrong — it is not first-line for superficial BCC.
why.C: Wrong — it is used precisely after hedgehog-inhibitor therapy.
why.D: Wrong — cemiplimab is FDA-approved in advanced BCC.

stem: For chemoprevention in high-risk patients, which agents and roles are correctly matched?

options.A: Low-dose acitretin to reduce the number of cSCCs and nicotinamide (niacinamide) to reduce AKs/NMSCs in immunocompetent patients
options.B: Systemic corticosteroids to reduce cSCCs
options.C: Oral hydrochlorothiazide to reduce AKs
options.D: High-dose vitamin C to eliminate BCCs

exp: For chemoprevention in high-risk patients, the most commonly used agents are low-dose acitretin to reduce the number of cSCCs and nicotinamide (niacinamide) to reduce AKs and NMSCs (in immunocompetent patients; benefit was not seen in one transplant study). Systemic corticosteroids (B) would worsen risk via immunosuppression, HCTZ (C) increases NMSC risk, and high-dose vitamin C (D) is not an established preventive.
hy: Chemoprevention: acitretin (↓cSCC), nicotinamide (↓AK/NMSC).
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — acitretin and nicotinamide are the cited chemopreventives.
why.B: Wrong — systemic steroids immunosuppress and would not reduce cSCC.
why.C: Wrong — HCTZ raises, not lowers, NMSC risk.
why.D: Wrong — high-dose vitamin C is not an established preventive.

stem: What does the evidence show about sunscreen for primary prevention of keratinocyte carcinoma?

options.A: Consistent sunscreen use decreases the incidence of SCC, but has no effect or only a weak effect on BCC incidence
options.B: Sunscreen prevents BCC but increases SCC
options.C: Sunscreen has been proven to have no effect on any skin cancer
options.D: Sunscreen prevents melanoma but increases both BCC and SCC

exp: Consistent sunscreen use has been shown to decrease the incidence of SCC, but to have no effect or only a weak effect on BCC incidence, in both immunocompetent individuals and organ transplant recipients — reflecting the different roles of cumulative (SCC) versus intermittent (BCC) UV exposure. Options B–D misstate this evidence.
hy: Sunscreen reduces SCC; little/no effect on BCC incidence.
chname: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma

why.A: Correct — sunscreen lowers SCC but has weak/no effect on BCC.
why.B: Wrong — it reduces SCC and does not increase it.
why.C: Wrong — it has a demonstrated effect on SCC.
why.D: Wrong — the cited evidence concerns SCC/BCC, and sunscreen does not increase them.
